异苑 - Garden of Marvels

    Home

    异苑 南朝宋　刘敬叔

    　　●卷一 [1]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [1]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 異苑 - Refers to a collection of strange or unusual stories, often categorized under the broader genre of '志怪' (records of strange things).
    • 南朝宋 - Refers to the Southern Dynasty of Song, one of the dynasties in the Southern and Northern Dynasties period.
    • 劉敬叔 - The author of the text, a scholar and writer during the Southern Dynasty of Song.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 異苑 - This term is used to denote a collection of supernatural and extraordinary tales, which were popular during the Southern and Northern Dynasties period. These stories often served as moral and cultural reflections of the time.
    • 南朝宋 - The Southern Dynasty of Song was a significant period in Chinese history, known for its cultural and literary achievements. It was one of the dynasties that ruled the southern part of China during the division of the country into northern and southern regions.
    • 劉敬叔 - He was a prominent figure in the literary circles of his time, known for his contributions to the genre of '志怪' literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 異苑 - The title of the work.
    • 南朝宋 - The dynasty during which the work was written.
    • 劉敬叔 - The author of the work.

    Strange Tales from the South (異苑)
    Southern Dynasty of Song (南朝宋)
    By Liu Jingshu (劉敬叔)

    Chapter One (●卷一)

    This translation aims to capture the essence and cultural significance of the original text while maintaining clarity and coherence in English. [1]

    古语有之曰，古者有夫妻，荒年菜食而死，俱化成青绛，故俗呼美人虹。郭云：红为雩，俗呼为美人。
    　　晋义熙初，晋陵薛愿有虹。饮其釜澳，须臾翕响便竭。愿辇酒灌之，随投随涸，便吐金满釜，于是灾弊日祛而丰富岁臻。
    　　太原温湛婢见一妪向婢流涕，无孔窍，婢骇怖，告湛。湛遂抽刀逐之，化成一物如紫虹形，宛然长舒，上没霄汉。
    　　长沙王道怜子义庆在广陵卧疾。食次，忽有白虹入室，就饮其粥。义庆掷器于阶，遂作风雨，声振于庭户，良久不见。
    　　衡阳山、九嶷山皆有舜庙，每太守修理、祭祀洁敬则闻弦歌之声。汉章帝时，零陵文学奚景于冷道县祠下得笙，白玉管，舜时西王母献。
    　　衡山有三峰，极秀。其一名华盖，又名紫盖。天景明澈，辄有一双白鹤回翔其上。一峰名石，下有石室，中常闻讽诵声，清响亮彻。一峰名芙蓉，最为疏桀，自非清霁素朝，不可望见。峰上有泉飞氵爪，如一幅绢，分映青林，直注山下。 [2]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [2]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 古語有之曰 - Indicates a traditional saying or proverb, often used to introduce a piece of wisdom or historical anecdote.
    • 荒年菜食而死 - Refers to starving to death during a famine, emphasizing the hardship and scarcity of food.
    • 俱化成青絳 - Means 'both turned into green and purple,' likely referring to a transformation into a form of natural phenomenon, possibly a rainbow.
    • 故俗呼美人虹 - Explains why the phenomenon is called 'beautiful woman rainbow,' suggesting a cultural or folkloric interpretation.
    • 郭云 - A reference to Guo, likely a historical or literary figure, providing additional commentary or explanation.
    • 紅為雩 - Indicates that red is associated with a ritual or ceremony, specifically the雩 (rain-making) ceremony.
    • 俗呼為美人 - Another folkloric reference, indicating that the phenomenon is commonly referred to as 'beautiful woman.'
    • 晉義熙初 - Refers to the early years of the Jinyi era, a period in the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 晉陵薛願有虹 - Describes a specific event involving a rainbow in the home of Xue Yuan, a person from Jinling.
    • 飲其釜澳 - Suggests a magical or supernatural interaction with the rainbow.
    • 翕響便竭 - Describes the rapid disappearance of the rainbow.
    • 願輦酒灌之 - Indicates a ritual act to summon or appease the rainbow.
    • 隨投隨涸 - Describes the continuous and immediate effect of the ritual.
    • 吐金滿釜 - Indicates a miraculous outcome.
    • 災弊日祛而豐富歲臻 - Describes the positive impact of the event.
    • 太原溫湛婢見一嫗向婢流涕 - Describes a supernatural encounter.
    • 無孔竅 - Describes the unusual nature of the old woman.
    • 婢駭怖 - Indicates the maid's reaction to the encounter.
    • 告湛 - Indicates the maid's action.
    • 湛遂抽刀逐之 - Indicates Wen Zhan's response.
    • 化成一物如紫虹形 - Describes the transformation.
    • 宛然長舒 - Describes the appearance of the transformed object.
    • 上沒霄漢 - Indicates the height and grandeur of the transformation.
    • 長沙王道憐子義慶在廣陵臥疾 - Describes a specific event involving Wang Dao's son, Yi Qing, who was ill in Guangling.
    • 忽有白虹入室 - Indicates a supernatural occurrence.
    • 就飲其粥 - Suggests a direct interaction with the rainbow.
    • 義慶擲器於階 - Indicates Yi Qing's reaction.
    • 遂作風雨 - Describes the subsequent natural phenomenon.
    • 聲振於庭戶 - Indicates the intensity of the storm.
    • 良久不見 - Indicates the end of the phenomenon.
    • 衡陽山、九嶷山皆有舜廟 - Describes the presence of temples dedicated to Shun on Mount Hengyang and Mount Jiuyi.
    • 每太守修理、祭祀潔敬則聞弦歌之聲 - Means 'whenever the governor repairs and performs a clean and respectful sacrifice, the sound of stringed instruments and singing can be heard,' indicating a miraculous or divine response.
    • 漢章帝時 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Zhang of Han.
    • 零陵文學奚景於冷道縣祠下得笙 - Describes a specific event involving Xiji, a literary figure from Lingling, finding a flute at a temple in Lengdao County.
    • 白玉管 - Means 'white jade tube,' describing the flute.
    • 舜時西王母獻 - Means 'presented by the Queen Mother of the West during Shun's time,' indicating the origin of the flute.
    • 衡山有三峰 - Describes the three peaks of Mount Heng.
    • 極秀 - Means 'extremely beautiful,' describing the peaks.
    • 其一名華蓋，又名紫蓋 - Names the first peak as 'Huagai' and 'Zigai,' indicating its beauty and grandeur.
    • 天景明澈，輒有一雙白鶴回翔其上 - Means 'when the sky is clear and bright, a pair of white cranes often circle above it,' indicating a natural phenomenon.
    • 一峰名石，下有石室 - Names the second peak as 'Shiwei' and describes a stone room at its base.
    • 中常聞誦聲，清響亮徹 - Means 'one often hears the sound of recitation, clear and resonant,' indicating a spiritual or cultural presence.
    • 一峰名芙蓉，最為疏桀 - Names the third peak as 'Furong' and describes it as the most remote and rugged.
    • 自非清霽素朝，不可望見 - Means 'unless it is a clear and bright morning, it cannot be seen,' indicating the difficulty of viewing the peak.
    • 峰上有泉飛氵爪，如一幅絹，分映青林，直注山下 - Describes a waterfall on the peak, comparing it to a piece of silk and describing its descent through the green forest.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 古語有之曰 - Indicates a traditional saying or proverb, often used to introduce a piece of wisdom or historical anecdote.
    • 荒年菜食而死 - Refers to starving to death during a famine, emphasizing the hardship and scarcity of food.
    • 俱化成青絳 - Likely refers to a transformation into a form of natural phenomenon, possibly a rainbow.
    • 故俗呼美人虹 - Suggests a cultural or folkloric interpretation of the phenomenon.
    • 郭云 - A reference to Guo, likely a historical or literary figure, providing additional commentary or explanation.
    • 紅為雩 - Indicates that red is associated with a ritual or ceremony, specifically the雩 (rain-making) ceremony.
    • 俗呼為美人 - Another folkloric reference, indicating that the phenomenon is commonly referred to as 'beautiful woman.'
    • 晉義熙初 - Refers to the early years of the Jinyi era, a period in the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 晉陵薛願有虹 - Describes a specific event involving a rainbow in the home of Xue Yuan, a person from Jinling.
    • 飲其釜澳 - Suggests a magical or supernatural interaction with the rainbow.
    • 翕響便竭 - Describes the rapid disappearance of the rainbow.
    • 願輦酒灌之 - Indicates a ritual act to summon or appease the rainbow.
    • 隨投隨涸 - Describes the continuous and immediate effect of the ritual.
    • 吐金滿釜 - Indicates a miraculous outcome.
    • 災弊日祛而豐富歲臻 - Describes the positive impact of the event.
    • 太原溫湛婢見一嫗向婢流涕 - Describes a supernatural encounter.
    • 無孔竅 - Describes the unusual nature of the old woman.
    • 婢駭怖 - Indicates the maid's reaction to the encounter.
    • 告湛 - Indicates the maid's action.
    • 湛遂抽刀逐之 - Indicates Wen Zhan's response.
    • 化成一物如紫虹形 - Describes the transformation.
    • 宛然長舒 - Describes the appearance of the transformed object.
    • 上沒霄漢 - Indicates the height and grandeur of the transformation.
    • 長沙王道憐子義慶在廣陵臥疾 - Describes a specific event involving Wang Dao's son, Yi Qing, who was ill in Guangling.
    • 忽有白虹入室 - Indicates a supernatural occurrence.
    • 就飲其粥 - Suggests a direct interaction with the rainbow.
    • 義慶擲器於階 - Indicates Yi Qing's reaction.
    • 遂作風雨 - Describes the subsequent natural phenomenon.
    • 聲振於庭戶 - Indicates the intensity of the storm.
    • 良久不見 - Indicates the end of the phenomenon.
    • 衡陽山、九嶷山皆有舜廟 - Describes the presence of temples dedicated to Shun on Mount Hengyang and Mount Jiuyi.
    • 每太守修理、祭祀潔敬則聞弦歌之聲 - Means 'whenever the governor repairs and performs a clean and respectful sacrifice, the sound of stringed instruments and singing can be heard,' indicating a miraculous or divine response.
    • 漢章帝時 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Zhang of Han.
    • 零陵文學奚景於冷道縣祠下得笙 - Describes a specific event involving Xiji, a literary figure from Lingling, finding a flute at a temple in Lengdao County.
    • 白玉管 - Means 'white jade tube,' describing the flute.
    • 舜時西王母獻 - Means 'presented by the Queen Mother of the West during Shun's time,' indicating the origin of the flute.
    • 衡山有三峰 - Describes the three peaks of Mount Heng.
    • 極秀 - Means 'extremely beautiful,' describing the peaks.
    • 其一名華蓋，又名紫蓋 - Names the first peak as 'Huagai' and 'Zigai,' indicating its beauty and grandeur.
    • 天景明澈，輒有一雙白鶴回翔其上 - Means 'when the sky is clear and bright, a pair of white cranes often circle above it,' indicating a natural phenomenon.
    • 一峰名石，下有石室 - Names the second peak as 'Shiwei' and describes a stone room at its base.
    • 中常聞誦聲，清響亮徹 - Means 'one often hears the sound of recitation, clear and resonant,' indicating a spiritual or cultural presence.
    • 一峰名芙蓉，最為疏桀 - Names the third peak as 'Furong' and describes it as the most remote and rugged.
    • 自非清霽素朝，不可望見 - Means 'unless it is a clear and bright morning, it cannot be seen,' indicating the difficulty of viewing the peak.
    • 峰上有泉飛氵爪，如一幅絹，分映青林，直注山下 - Describes a waterfall on the peak, comparing it to a piece of silk and describing its descent through the green forest.

    A Traditional Saying goes: "In ancient times, there was a couple who, during a famine, died of starvation after eating only vegetables. Both of them transformed into a green and purple phenomenon, hence the common name 'Beautiful Woman Rainbow.' Guo said: 'Red is associated with the rain-making ritual, and it is commonly called 'Beautiful Woman.'"

    During the early years of the Jinyi era, Xue Yuan of Jinling had a rainbow. It drank from the dregs of the pot, and in a moment, it sucked up the sound and vanished. Xue Yuan loaded wine and poured it over it, and as soon as it was poured, it dried up, then spewed gold filling the pot. Thus, disasters were gradually eliminated, and prosperity increased year by year.

    Wen Zhan of Taiyuan had a maid who saw an old woman weeping towards her, with no holes or openings. The maid was terrified and reported to Wen Zhan. Wen Zhan then drew a knife and chased after her, and she transformed into something like a purple rainbow, gracefully stretching out and rising up to the sky.

    Wang Dao's son, Yi Qing, was ill in Guangling. While eating, suddenly a white rainbow entered the room and drank his porridge. Yi Qing threw the utensils onto the steps, and then there was a storm, with the sound reverberating through the hall and doorways. After a long time, it disappeared.

    Mount Hengyang and Mount Jiuyi both have temples dedicated to Shun. Whenever the governor repairs and performs a clean and respectful sacrifice, the sound of stringed instruments and singing can be heard. During the reign of Emperor Zhang of Han, Xiji, a literary figure from Lingling, found a flute at a temple in Lengdao County. It was a white jade tube, presented by the Queen Mother of the West during Shun's time.

    Mount Heng has three peaks, all extremely beautiful. The first is named Huagai and Zigai, the second is named Shiwei, with a stone room at its base where one often hears the sound of recitation, clear and resonant. The third is named Furong, the most remote and rugged, and can only be seen on a clear and bright morning. On the peak, there is a waterfall that flows like a piece of silk, dividing through the green forest and cascading down the mountain. [2]

    长沙罗县有屈原自投之川，山明水净，异于常处。民为立庙在汨潭之西岸侧，盘石马迹尚存，相传云原投川之日，乘白骥而来。
    　　寻阳姑石山在江之坻。初，桓玄至西下，令人登。之中岭，便闻长啸声，甚清澈。及至峰顶，见一人箕踞石上。
    　　会稽天台山虽非遐远，自非卒生忘形则不能跻也。赤城阻其径，瀑布激其冲，石有莓苔之险，渊有不测之深。
    　　乌程卞山，本名土山，有项籍庙，自号卞王，因改名山。山足有一石柜，高数尺。陈郡殷康常往开之，风雨晦冥，乃止。
    　　钓矶山者，陶侃尝钓于此，山下水中得一织梭，还挂壁上。有顷，雷雨，梭变成赤龙，从空而去。其山石上犹有侃迹存焉。
    　　乘矶山，下临清川。昔有渔父宿于川，夜半，闻水中有弦歌之音，宫商和畅，清弄谐密。 [3]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [3]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 自投之川 - Literal expression meaning 'threw oneself into the river,' used to describe suicide or a dramatic act of self-sacrifice.
    • 山明水净 - Descriptive phrase meaning 'the mountains are clear and the waters are clean,' often used to depict a serene and beautiful natural setting.
    • 民为立庙 - Means 'the people built a temple,' indicating a place of worship or remembrance.
    • 盘石马迹 - Refers to a flat stone with horse tracks, suggesting a physical remnant of a legendary event.
    • 乘白骥而来 - Metaphorical expression meaning 'came riding a white horse,' often used to describe a heroic or noble figure.
    • 初，桓玄至西下 - Means 'at first, when Huán Xuán came westward,' introducing a historical event.
    • 长啸声 - Refers to a loud, drawn-out sound, often associated with a call or cry.
    • 箕踞石上 - Posture where one sits with legs spread out, often used to describe a relaxed or defiant stance.
    • 自非卒生忘形 - Means 'unless one is completely detached from form,' suggesting a state of spiritual or mental transcendence.
    • 赤城阻其径 - Refers to a red city blocking the path, likely a metaphor for a natural obstacle.
    • 瀑布激其冲 - Means 'waterfalls striking the path,' describing a dramatic natural feature.
    • 莓苔之险 - Refers to the danger posed by moss and algae, suggesting slippery or treacherous terrain.
    • 不测之深 - Means 'depths that cannot be fathomed,' indicating a mysterious or dangerous environment.
    • 弦歌之音 - Refers to the sound of stringed instruments and singing, often associated with a harmonious and pleasant atmosphere.
    • 宫商和畅 - Means 'the tones of the pentatonic scale are harmonious and smooth,' describing a beautiful musical performance.
    • 清弄谐密 - Means 'the clear playing is harmonious and close,' emphasizing the quality of the music.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 屈原 - A famous poet and statesman from the Warring States period, known for his patriotism and tragic end.
    • 汨罗江 - The river where Qu Yuan is said to have drowned himself, now a site of pilgrimage and remembrance.
    • 桓玄 - A powerful general and regent during the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his ambition and eventual downfall.
    • 赤城 - A mountain in Zhejiang Province, often associated with spiritual and natural beauty.
    • 天台山 - A famous mountain in Zhejiang Province, known for its scenic beauty and cultural significance.
    • 项籍庙 - A temple dedicated to Xiang Yu, a famous warlord from the late Qin dynasty.
    • 卞王 - A title given to Xiang Yu, meaning 'King of Bian.'
    • 陶侃 - A famous general and statesman from the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his military prowess and administrative skills.
    • 赤龙 - A mythical creature in Chinese culture, often associated with power and transformation.

    Proper Nouns

    • 屈原 - A famous poet and statesman from the Warring States period.
    • 汨罗江 - The river where Qu Yuan is said to have drowned himself.
    • 桓玄 - A powerful general and regent during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 赤城 - A mountain in Zhejiang Province.
    • 天台山 - A famous mountain in Zhejiang Province.
    • 项籍 - A famous warlord from the late Qin dynasty.
    • 卞王 - A title given to Xiang Yu.
    • 陶侃 - A famous general and statesman from the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 乌程卞山 - A mountain in Wuxing County, Zhejiang Province.
    • 陈郡殷康 - A person from Chen County.
    • 钓矶山 - A mountain where Tao Kan, a famous general, once fished.
    • 乘矶山 - A mountain near the river where a fisherman had a mystical experience.

    In the Luo County of Changsha, there is the river where Qu Yuan threw himself, with its clear waters and bright mountains, unlike any other place. The people built a temple on the western bank of the Mi River, and the flat stone with horse tracks still remains. It is said that on the day Qu Yuan threw himself into the river, he came riding a white horse.

    The Guishi Mountain of Xunyang is located on the bank of the river. At first, when Huán Xuán came westward, he ordered someone to climb the mountain. As they reached the middle of the mountain, they heard a long, clear cry. Upon reaching the summit, they saw a person sitting with his legs spread out on a stone.

    The TianTai Mountain of Kuaiji, though not far away, cannot be ascended unless one is completely detached from form. The Red City blocks the path, and waterfalls strike the way, with the danger of moss and algae, and depths that cannot be fathomed.

    The Bian Mountain of Wucheng, originally named Earth Mountain, has a temple dedicated to Xiang Ji, who called himself King of Bian, and thus the mountain was renamed. At the foot of the mountain is a stone box, several feet high. Chen County's Yin Kang often tried to open it, but when he did, it was met with wind, rain, and darkness, so he stopped.

    The Diaoji Mountain is where Tao Kan once fished. Below the mountain in the water, he found a shuttle, which he hung on the wall. Soon after, there was a thunderstorm, and the shuttle transformed into a red dragon, flying away from the sky. The tracks of Tao Kan still remain on the stones of the mountain.

    The Chengji Mountain overlooks a clear river below. Once, a fisherman stayed by the river and, in the middle of the night, heard the sound of stringed instruments and singing in the water, with the tones of the pentatonic scale being harmonious and smooth, and the clear playing being harmonious and close. [3]

    百丈山上有石房，内有石案，置石书二卷。
    　　永宁县涛山有河，水色红赤，有自然石桥，多鱼獭异禽。阴雨时尝闻兆角声甚亮。
    　　凉州西有沙山，俗云昔有覆师于此者，积尸数万，从是有大风吹沙覆其上，遂成山阜，因名沙山，时闻有鼓角声。
    　　吴孙权赤乌八年，遣校尉陈勋漕句容，中道凿破瑶，掘得一黑物，无有首尾，形如数百斛舡，长数十丈，蠢蠢而动。有顷，悉融液成汁，时人莫能识得此。之后，遂获泉源，或谓是水脉。每至大旱，余渎皆竭，惟此巨流焉。
    　　东乡太湖，吴庚申岁，于此有一军士五百人将破堰，先以酒肉祈神，约令水涸。夜梦人云：“塘水速竭，若见巨鳞，慎勿杀也。又有铜釜，并不可发。”明，往尺水翕然而尽，得白鱼，形状非常。小人贪利，剖而治之，见昨所祭余食充溢肠内。须臾，复得釜，又取发，水便暴出，五百人一时没溺，唯督监得存，具说事状。于今犹名此湖为五百陂。 [4]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [4]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 石书 - Refers to inscriptions or books made of stone.
    • 自然石桥 - A naturally formed bridge, possibly a rock formation.
    • 蠢蠢而动 - Describes the movement of a creature as sluggish or slow.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 百丈山 - A mountain in China, likely in a region known for its natural features.
    • 永宁县 - A county in China, possibly in the southern part of the country.
    • 涛山 - A mountain in Yongning County, known for its river and natural formations.
    • 凉州 - An ancient region in northwest China, known for its desert landscapes.
    • 吴孙权赤乌八年 - The 8th year of the Chih Wu era (245 AD) during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 句容 - A city in Jiangsu Province, known for its historical significance.
    • 东乡太湖 - A lake in the eastern region, possibly in Jiangsu Province.
    • 吴庚申岁 - A specific year in the Chinese zodiac, likely referring to a significant event.

    Proper Nouns

    • 百丈山 - Mount Bai Zhang
    • 永宁县 - Yongning County
    • 涛山 - Tao Mountain
    • 凉州 - Liangzhou
    • 吴孙权 - Sun Quan
    • 赤乌八年 - 8th year of the Chih Wu era (245 AD)
    • 校尉陈勋 - General Chen Xun
    • 句容 - Jurong
    • 东乡太湖 - Dongxiang Taihu Lake
    • 吴庚申岁 - Gengshen year

    On Mount Bai Zhang there are stone houses, inside which there are stone tables, on which are placed two stone books.

    In Yongning County, Tao Mountain has a river with red and reddish water, a natural stone bridge, and many unusual aquatic animals. During rainy weather, one can often hear the sound of horns being blown.

    To the west of Liangzhou, there is a sand mountain. Folklore says that in ancient times, an army was defeated here, leaving tens of thousands of corpses. From then on, strong winds carried sand over the area, eventually forming a mountain. It was named Sand Mountain, and sometimes one can hear the sound of drums and horns.

    During the 8th year of the Chih Wu era (245 AD) under the reign of Sun Quan, General Chen Xun was sent to Jurong to transport grain. On the way, they broke through a rock and discovered a black object with no head or tail, shaped like a large boat, about dozens of zhang long, moving sluggishly. After a while, it melted into a liquid, and no one could identify it. Later, a spring source was discovered, and it was believed to be a water vein. During severe droughts, other streams dried up, but this large flow remained.

    In the eastern region, Taihu Lake, during the Gengshen year, a group of five hundred soldiers were about to break a dam. They first offered wine and meat to the gods and promised that the water would dry up. That night, they dreamed of a voice saying, "The pond water will quickly dry up. If you see a large fish, be careful not to kill it. There is also a copper pot, which should not be disturbed." The next morning, the water suddenly dried up, and they found a white fish with an unusual shape. The soldiers, greedy for profit, cut and cooked the fish, finding the food they had offered the previous day filling its intestines. Soon after, they found another copper pot and disturbed it, causing the water to suddenly surge. All five hundred soldiers drowned at once, except for the supervisor, who survived and recounted the events. To this day, the lake is still known as the Five Hundred Pools. [4]

    永嘉郡有百簿濑，郡人断水捕鱼，宰生祷祭，以祈多获。逾时了无所得，众侣忿怨，弃业将罢。其夕并梦见一老公云“诸君且可小停，要思其宜。”夜忽闻有跳跃声，惊起共看，乃是大鱼，М以为脍，顿获百簿，故因以百簿名濑。
    　　晋吴隶为鱼塞于湖，有大鱼化为人，语隶云：“晚有大鱼攻塞，切勿杀。”隶许之。须臾，有大鱼至，群鱼从之，隶同侣误杀大鱼。是夕，风雨晦冥，鱼悉飞上木间，因号为飞鱼径。
    　　兰陵昌虑县阝城有华山，山上有井，鸟巢其中，金喙黑色而团翅。此鸟见则大水，井又不可窥，窥者不盈一岁辄死。
    　　浔阳昙椿世居长沙，宅有古井，每夜辄闻有如炮竹声，相承谓之龙叱。
    　　句容县有延陵季子庙，庙前井及渎恒自涌沸，故曰沸井。于今犹然，亦曰沸潭。
    　　陈郡谢晦字宣明，宅南路上有古井，以元嘉二年汲者忽见二龙甚分明，行道住观，莫不嗟异。有人入井，始知是砖，隐起作龙形。 [5]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [5]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 永嘉郡有百簿濑 - A place name, '永嘉郡' refers to a region in Zhejiang Province, and '百簿濑' is a specific location within it.
    • 宰生祷祭 - '宰生' means 'slaughtering a living creature,' and '祷祭' means 'praying and sacrificing.'
    • 逾时了无所得 - '逾时' means 'after a long time,' and '了无所得' means 'nothing was obtained.'
    • 众侣忿怨，弃业将罢 - '众侣' means 'the group of people,' '忿怨' means 'angry and resentful,' '弃业' means 'abandoning the business,' and '将罢' means 'about to give up.'
    • 其夕并梦见一老公云 - '其夕' means 'that night,' '并梦' means 'all had a dream,' and '一老公' means 'an old man.'
    • 诸君且可小停，要思其宜 - '诸君' means 'you all,' '且可' means 'may just,' '小停' means 'take a short break,' '要思' means 'think carefully,' and '其宜' means 'what is appropriate.'
    • 夜忽闻有跳跃声 - '夜忽闻' means 'suddenly heard at night,' and '跳跃声' means 'jumping sounds.'
    • 惊起共看 - '惊起' means 'woke up in surprise,' and '共看' means 'looked together.'
    • 乃是大鱼 - '乃是' means 'it turned out to be,' and '大鱼' means 'a big fish.'
    • 以为脍 - '以为' means 'made into,' and '脍' means 'minced meat.'
    • 顿获百簿 - '顿获' means 'suddenly obtained,' and '百簿' means 'a hundred pounds.'
    • 晋吴隶为鱼塞于云湖 - '晋吴隶' refers to a person named Wu Li from the Jin dynasty, '为鱼塞' means 'created a fish barrier,' and '云湖' is a lake.
    • 有大鱼化为人 - '有大鱼' means 'a big fish,' '化为人' means 'transformed into a human.'
    • 晚有大鱼攻塞 - '晚' means 'later,' '有大鱼' means 'a big fish,' '攻塞' means 'attacked the barrier.'
    • 须臾 - '须臾' means 'in a moment.'
    • 隶同侣误杀大鱼 - '隶同侣' means 'Wu Li and his companions,' '误杀' means 'mistakenly killed,' and '大鱼' means 'the big fish.'
    • 风雨晦冥 - '风雨' means 'wind and rain,' '晦冥' means 'dark and gloomy.'
    • 鱼悉飞上木间 - '鱼悉' means 'all the fish,' '飞上' means 'flew up,' and '木间' means 'among the trees.'
    • 因号为飞鱼径 - '因号' means 'therefore called,' '为' means 'as,' '飞鱼径' means 'Flying Fish Path.'
    • 兰陵昌虑县有华山 - '兰陵昌虑县' is a county in Shandong Province, '有华山' means 'has a Huashan.'
    • 山上有井 - '山上有井' means 'there is a well on the mountain.'
    • 鸟巢其中 - '鸟巢' means 'bird's nest,' '其中' means 'inside it.'
    • 金喙黑色而团翅 - '金喙' means 'golden beak,' '黑色' means 'black,' '而' means 'and,' '团翅' means 'round wings.'
    • 此鸟见则大水 - '此鸟' means 'this bird,' '见则' means 'when seen,' '大水' means 'flood.'
    • 井又不可窥 - '井又' means 'the well also,' '不可窥' means 'cannot be looked into.'
    • 窥者不盈一岁辄死 - '窥者' means 'those who look into it,' '不盈一岁' means 'not living for a year,' '辄死' means 'dying immediately.'
    • 浔阳昙椿世居长沙 - '浔阳昙椿' is a person, '世居' means 'lived in,' '长沙' is a city.
    • 宅有古井 - '宅' means 'house,' '有古井' means 'has an ancient well.'
    • 每夜辄闻有如炮竹声 - '每夜' means 'every night,' '辄闻' means 'always hear,' '有如炮竹声' means 'sounds like firecrackers.'
    • 相承谓之龙叱 - '相承' means 'passed down,' '谓之' means 'called it,' '龙叱' means 'dragon's roar.'
    • 句容县有延陵季子庙 - '句容县' is a county in Jiangsu Province, '有延陵季子庙' means 'has the Temple of Yanling Jizi.'
    • 庙前井及渎恒自涌沸 - '庙前井' means 'the well in front of the temple,' '及渎' means 'and the stream,' '恒自涌沸' means 'always boiling up.'
    • 故曰沸井 - '故曰' means 'therefore called,' '沸井' means 'Boiling Well.'
    • 于今犹然 - '于今' means 'even now,' '犹然' means 'still.'
    • 亦曰沸潭 - '亦曰' means 'also called,' '沸潭' means 'Boiling Pond.'
    • 陈郡谢晦字宣明 - '陈郡' is a region, '谢晦' is a person, '字宣明' means 'with the courtesy name Xuanming.'
    • 宅南路上有古井 - '宅南路上' means 'on the road south of the house,' '有古井' means 'has an ancient well.'
    • 以元嘉二年汲者忽见二龙甚分明 - '以元嘉二年' means 'in the second year of the Yuanjia era,' '汲者' means 'those who draw water,' '忽见' means 'suddenly saw,' '二龙' means 'two dragons,' '甚分明' means 'very clearly.'
    • 行道住观，莫不嗟异 - '行道' means 'passersby,' '住观' means 'stopped to watch,' '莫不' means 'all,' '嗟异' means 'expressed amazement.'
    • 有人入井，始知是砖，隐起作龙形 - '有人' means 'someone,' '入井' means 'went into the well,' '始知' means 'found out,' '是砖' means 'it was bricks,' '隐起' means 'rising up,' '作龙形' means 'in the shape of a dragon.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 永嘉郡 - A region in Zhejiang Province during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 晋吴隶 - A person named Wu Li from the Jin dynasty.
    • 云湖 - A lake in the region.
    • 兰陵昌虑县 - A county in Shandong Province.
    • 华山 - A mountain in the county.
    • 浔阳昙椿 - A person who lived in Changsha.
    • 长沙 - A city in Hunan Province.
    • 句容县 - A county in Jiangsu Province.
    • 延陵季子庙 - The Temple of Yanling Jizi, a historical figure.
    • 陈郡 - A region in Henan Province.
    • 元嘉二年 - The second year of the Yuanjia era, a period in the Liu Song dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 永嘉郡 - A region in Zhejiang Province during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 晋吴隶 - A person named Wu Li from the Jin dynasty.
    • 云湖 - A lake in the region.
    • 兰陵昌虑县 - A county in Shandong Province.
    • 华山 - A mountain in the county.
    • 浔阳昙椿 - A person who lived in Changsha.
    • 长沙 - A city in Hunan Province.
    • 句容县 - A county in Jiangsu Province.
    • 延陵季子庙 - The Temple of Yanling Jizi, a historical figure.
    • 陈郡 - A region in Henan Province.
    • 元嘉二年 - The second year of the Yuanjia era, a period in the Liu Song dynasty.

    The Yongjia Prefecture has a place called Bai Bu Lai, where the locals block the water to catch fish, slaughtering live creatures and praying and sacrificing to seek a bountiful catch. After a long time, they got nothing, and the group became angry and resentful, about to give up their business. That night, they all had a dream of an old man who said, "You all may just take a short break and think carefully about what is appropriate." Suddenly at night, they heard jumping sounds, woke up in surprise and looked together, and it turned out to be a big fish, which they made into minced meat and suddenly obtained a hundred pounds, so they named the place Bai Bu Lai after the hundred pounds.

    During the Jin dynasty, Wu Li created a fish barrier in Yun Lake, and a big fish transformed into a human, telling Wu Li, "Later, a big fish will attack the barrier; do not kill it." Wu Li agreed. In a moment, a big fish arrived, followed by a group of fish, and Wu Li and his companions mistakenly killed the big fish. That night, with wind and rain and dark and gloomy weather, all the fish flew up among the trees, and it was therefore called the Flying Fish Path.

    The Lanling Changlu County has a city with a Huashan, on which there is a well, inside of which there is a bird's nest. The bird has a golden beak, black color, and round wings. When this bird is seen, there will be a flood, and the well also cannot be looked into; those who look into it do not live for a year and die immediately.

    Xunyang Tan Chun lived in Changsha, and his house has an ancient well. Every night, they always hear sounds like firecrackers, and it was passed down to be called the dragon's roar.

    The Jurong County has the Temple of Yanling Jizi, and in front of the temple, there is a well and a stream that always boil up, so it is called the Boiling Well. Even now, it is still the same, and it is also called the Boiling Pond.

    Chen Prefecture's Xie Hui, with the courtesy name Xuanming, has an ancient well on the road south of his house. In the second year of the Yuanjia era, those who drew water suddenly saw two dragons very clearly, and passersby stopped to watch, all expressing amazement. Someone went into the well, and found out it was bricks, rising up in the shape of a dragon. [5]

    元嘉初武溪蛮入射鹿，逐入石穴，才容人。蛮人入穴，见其傍有梯，因上梯，豁然开朗，桑果蔚然，行人翱翔，亦不以怪。此蛮于路斫树为记，其后茫然，无复仿佛。
    　　河东毋丘俭字仲恭，尝征沃沮，使王颀穷其东界。耆老云：曾有一破船随波流出。在海岸边有一人，项中复有面。生得之，与语，不相通，不食而死。又得一布衣从海中浮出其身，如中国人衣，但两袖顿长三丈。

    　　●卷二
    　　魏时殿前大钟无故大鸣，人皆异之，以问张华。华曰：“此蜀郡铜山崩，故钟鸣应之耳。”寻蜀郡上其事，果如华言。
    　　晋武帝时，吴郡临平岸崩出一石，鼓打之无声，以问张华。华云：“可取蜀中桐材刻作鱼形打之则鸣矣。”于是如言，音闻数十里。
    　　晋中朝有人畜铜澡盘，晨夕恒鸣如人扣。乃问张华，华曰：“此盘与洛钟宫商相应。宫中朝暮撞钟，故声相应耳。可错令轻则韵乖，鸣自止也。”如其言，后不复鸣。 [6]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [6]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元嘉初 - Refers to the early years of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, specifically the Yuanjia era (424-453 AD).
    • 武溪蛮 - Refers to a group of non-Han Chinese people living in the Wuxi area, likely referring to the Miao or Yao ethnic groups.
    • 豁然开朗 - A classical idiom meaning 'suddenly becoming bright and clear,' often used to describe a sudden realization or a pleasant surprise.
    • 桑果蔚然 - Describes a lush and abundant scene of mulberry trees and fruit trees.
    • 行人翱翔 - Refers to people moving about freely and comfortably.
    • 项中复有面 - Describes a person with another face on their neck, a mythical or supernatural phenomenon.
    • 耆老云 - Refers to elderly people who say or report something.
    • 错令轻则韵乖 - Means 'if you make it lighter, the sound will be out of tune.'
    • 钟鸣应之耳 - Means 'the bell is responding to it,' suggesting a supernatural connection.
    • 洛钟宫商相应 - Refers to the musical tuning of the palace bell in Luoyang, where the '宫' (gong) and '商' (shang) tones are in harmony.
    • 朝暮撞钟 - Means 'striking the bell in the morning and evening,' a common practice in ancient Chinese rituals.
    • 无不利於事也 - A general statement that nothing is unhelpful in matters, suggesting that all these roles or pursuits are valuable in their own right.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉 - The era name of Emperor Wen of Song, indicating the historical period.
    • 武溪蛮 - Refers to a specific group of non-Han Chinese people in the Wuxi region.
    • 毋丘俭 - A prominent official during the Three Kingdoms period, known for his military campaigns.
    • 吴郡临平岸 - Refers to a location in Wu County, near the mouth of the Yangtze River.
    • 毋丘俭字仲恭 - His courtesy name, which is a common practice in ancient China.
    • 晋武帝 - Refers to Emperor Sima Yan of the Jin dynasty, who unified China after the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 张华 - A famous scholar and statesman of the Jin dynasty, known for his wisdom and knowledge.
    • 洛钟 - The palace bell in Luoyang, a significant symbol of imperial authority and ritual.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元嘉 - The era name of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 武溪蛮 - A group of non-Han Chinese people.
    • 毋丘俭 - A historical figure from the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 吴郡临平岸 - A location in Wu County.
    • 晋武帝 - Emperor Sima Yan of the Jin dynasty.
    • 张华 - A famous scholar and statesman of the Jin dynasty.
    • 洛钟 - The palace bell in Luoyang.
    • 毋丘俭字仲恭 - His courtesy name.
    • 晋中朝 - Refers to the central government during the Jin dynasty.

    Translation

    In the early years of the Yuanjia era, a group of Wuxi barbarians entered a place where deer were being shot and followed the deer into a narrow cave that could only accommodate a person. The barbarians entered the cave and saw a ladder beside it. They climbed the ladder and suddenly found themselves in a bright and open space, with lush mulberry and fruit trees, and people moving about freely without finding it strange. These barbarians marked their path by cutting trees along the way, but later they became disoriented and lost their way.

    In the eastern region of Hedong, there was a man named Wu Qiu Jian, whose courtesy name was Zhong Gong. He once led a campaign against the Wōju people and sent Wang Qi to explore the eastern boundary. The elderly people reported that there had been a broken ship that floated out with the waves. On the coast, there was a man with another face on his neck. When they captured him, he could not communicate and died without eating. They also found a cloth garment floating out of the sea, which looked like clothing worn by Chinese people, but the sleeves were unusually long, extending three zhang (a traditional Chinese unit of length).

    In the second chapter:

    During the Wei dynasty, a large bell in front of the palace rang without reason, and people were puzzled by it. They consulted Zhang Hua, who said, "This is because the copper mountains in Shu Commandery have collapsed, and the bell is responding to it." Soon after, Shu Commandery reported the incident, and it turned out to be exactly as Zhang Hua had said.

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, a stone emerged from the collapsed bank of the Linping shore in Wu County. When struck with a drum, it made no sound. They consulted Zhang Hua, who said, "You can take the camphor wood from Shu Commandery, carve it into the shape of a fish, and strike it, and it will make a sound." They followed his advice, and the sound could be heard for dozens of li (a traditional Chinese unit of distance).

    During the Jin dynasty, there was a person in the central government who kept a copper basin that rang constantly like someone knocking on it every morning and evening. They consulted Zhang Hua, who said, "This basin is in tune with the palace bell in Luoyang. The palace bell is struck in the morning and evening, so the sound of the basin is in harmony with it. If you make the basin lighter, the sound will be out of tune, and it will stop ringing." They followed his advice, and the basin stopped ringing afterward. [6]

    豫章有石黄，白色而理疏，以水灌之便热，加鼎于上，炊足以熟。冷则灌之，雷焕。以问张华，华曰：“此燃石也。”
    　　元康中有人入嵩高山下得竹简一枚，上有两行科斗书。台中外传以相示，莫有知者。司空张华以问博士东，曰：“此明帝显节陵中策文也。”检校果然。
    　　晋惠帝元康五年，武库火，烧汉高祖斩白蛇剑、孔子履、王莽头等三物。中书监张茂先惧难作，列兵陈卫。咸见此剑穿屋飞去，莫知所向。
    　　晋康帝建元中，有渔父垂钓，得一金锁。引锁尽，见金牛，急挽出牛，断，犹得锁长二尺。
    　　晋太元中，桂阳临武徐孙江行，见岸有钱溢出，即辇着船中，须臾悉变成土。
    　　晋义熙中，庞猗为宜都太守，御人牧马于野，见一铜炉，上舀带锁而行，持归以呈猗。遂槛盛逸下荆州无都北乃，忽风雨，有叫声，火光烛天，径来趋船，失炉所在。 [7]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [7]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 豫章有石黄 - Descriptive phrase indicating that the region of Yuzhang (modern-day Nanchang) has a type of stone called 'shihuang'.
    • 白色而理疏 - Describes the stone as white in color and having a loose or porous texture.
    • 以水灌之便热 - Literal expression meaning that when water is poured over the stone, it becomes hot.
    • 加鼎于上，炊足以熟 - Literal expression meaning that placing a tripod (鼎) on top of the stone and cooking food will make it ready.
    • 冷则灌之 - Literal expression meaning that when the stone cools down, it should be poured with water again.
    • 此燃石也 - Literal expression meaning 'this is a burning stone'.
    • 元康中有人入嵩高山下得竹简一枚 - Descriptive phrase indicating that during the Yuan Kang period, someone entered the foot of Mount Song and found a bamboo slip.
    • 上有两行科斗书 - Literal expression meaning that there were two lines of script on the bamboo slip.
    • 台中外传以相示 - Literal expression meaning that officials in the palace passed the bamboo slip around to show each other.
    • 莫有知者 - Literal expression meaning 'no one knew'.
    • 司空张华以问博士东 - Literal expression meaning that Zhang Hua, the Grand Works Minister, asked the scholar Dong .
    • 此明帝显节陵中策文也 - Literal expression meaning 'this is the inscription from the Xianjie Tomb of Emperor Ming'.
    • 检校果然 - Literal expression meaning 'upon checking, it turned out to be true'.
    • 晋惠帝元康五年，武库火 - Descriptive phrase indicating that in the fifth year of the Yuan Kang period of Emperor Huai of Jin, the armory caught fire.
    • 烧汉高祖斩白蛇剑、孔子履、王莽头等三物 - Literal expression meaning that the armory fire burned three items: the sword of Han Gaozu (Emperor Gao) that cut the white snake, the shoes of Confucius, and the head of Wang Mang.
    • 中书监张茂先惧难作，列兵陈卫 - Literal expression meaning that Zhang Maoxian, the Chief Clerk of the Secretariat, feared trouble and arranged soldiers for protection.
    • 咸见此剑穿屋飞去，莫知所向 - Literal expression meaning that everyone saw the sword pass through the roof and fly away, and no one knew where it went.
    • 晋康帝建元中，有渔父垂钓，得一金锁 - Descriptive phrase indicating that during the Jianyuan period of Emperor Kang of Jin, a fisherman caught a golden lock while fishing.
    • 引锁尽，见金牛 - Literal expression meaning that when the fisherman pulled the lock out, he saw a golden ox.
    • 急挽出牛，断，犹得锁长二尺 - Literal expression meaning that he quickly pulled out the ox, which broke, leaving a lock two feet long.
    • 晋太元中，桂阳临武徐孙江行，见岸有钱溢出 - Descriptive phrase indicating that during the Taoyuan period of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, Xu Sunjiang from Linwu, Guiyang, saw money overflowing from the bank.
    • 即辇着船中，须臾悉变成土 - Literal expression meaning that he immediately loaded it onto his boat, and soon it all turned into earth.
    • 晋义熙中，庞猗为宜都太守，御人牧马于野 - Descriptive phrase indicating that during the Yixi period of Emperor An of Jin, Pang Yi served as the Prefect of Yidu and kept people and horses in the fields.
    • 见一铜炉，上舀带锁而行 - Literal expression meaning that he saw a copper stove with a handle and a lock, moving.
    • 持归以呈猗 - Literal expression meaning that he brought it back to show Pang Yi.
    • 遂槛盛逸下荆州无都北乃 - Literal expression meaning that he then placed it in a cage and sent it to the north of Jingzhou, which was not the capital.
    • 忽风雨，有叫声，火光烛天，径来趋船，失炉所在 - Literal expression meaning that suddenly there was wind and rain, with a sound, and fire lit up the sky, coming straight to the ship, and the stove was lost.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 豫章 - A historical region in present-day Jiangxi Province, known for its rich natural resources.
    • 石黄 - A type of stone, possibly a mineral or a natural phenomenon.
    • 雷焕 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or a local official.
    • 张华 - A prominent scholar and official during the Western Jin Dynasty, known for his knowledge and wisdom.
    • 元康 - The reign title of Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 嵩高山 - Mount Song, one of the Five Great Mountains in China, known for its historical and cultural significance.
    • 竹简 - Ancient writing material made of bamboo strips.
    • 科斗书 - An ancient script, possibly a form of seal script.
    • 台中外传 - Refers to officials in the palace or government.
    • 司空 - A high-ranking official in the government, often responsible for civil engineering and construction.
    • 博士 - A learned scholar, often involved in education and research.
    • 明帝显节陵 - The Xianjie Tomb of Emperor Ming, a significant historical site.
    • 武库 - The armory, a place where weapons and military equipment were stored.
    • 汉高祖斩白蛇剑 - The sword used by Emperor Gao of Han to cut a white snake, a legendary event.
    • 孔子履 - The shoes of Confucius, a revered figure in Chinese history.
    • 王莽头 - The head of Wang Mang, a historical figure who briefly usurped the Han throne.
    • 晋惠帝元康五年 - The fifth year of the Yuan Kang period of Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 晋康帝建元中 - The Jianyuan period of Emperor Kang of Jin.
    • 晋太元中 - The Taoyuan period of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 晋义熙中 - The Yixi period of Emperor An of Jin.
    • 宜都太守 - The Prefect of Yidu, a local administrative position.

    Proper Nouns

    • 豫章 - A historical region in present-day Jiangxi Province.
    • 石黄 - A type of stone.
    • 雷焕 - A historical figure.
    • 张华 - A prominent scholar and official during the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 元康 - The reign title of Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 嵩高山 - Mount Song.
    • 竹简 - Ancient writing material made of bamboo strips.
    • 科斗书 - An ancient script.
    • 台中外传 - Officials in the palace or government.
    • 司空 - A high-ranking official in the government.
    • 博士 - A learned scholar.
    • 明帝显节陵 - The Xianjie Tomb of Emperor Ming.
    • 武库 - The armory.
    • 汉高祖斩白蛇剑 - The sword used by Emperor Gao of Han to cut a white snake.
    • 孔子履 - The shoes of Confucius.
    • 王莽头 - The head of Wang Mang.
    • 晋惠帝元康五年 - The fifth year of the Yuan Kang period of Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 晋康帝建元中 - The Jianyuan period of Emperor Kang of Jin.
    • 晋太元中 - The Taoyuan period of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 晋义熙中 - The Yixi period of Emperor An of Jin.
    • 宜都太守 - The Prefect of Yidu.

    Yuzhang has a type of stone called "shihuang," which is white in color and has a loose texture. When water is poured over it, it becomes hot, and when a tripod is placed on top, cooking food will make it ready. When the stone cools down, it should be poured with water again. This was observed by Leihuan, who asked Zhang Hua about it. Zhang Hua replied, "This is a burning stone."

    During the Yuan Kang period, someone entered the foot of Mount Song and found a bamboo slip. There were two lines of script on the slip. Officials in the palace passed it around to show each other, but no one knew what it was. Zhang Hua, the Grand Works Minister, asked the scholar Dong , who said, "This is the inscription from the Xianjie Tomb of Emperor Ming." Upon checking, it turned out to be true.

    In the fifth year of the Yuan Kang period of Emperor Huai of Jin, the armory caught fire, burning three items: the sword of Han Gaozu (Emperor Gao) that cut the white snake, the shoes of Confucius, and the head of Wang Mang. Zhang Maoxian, the Chief Clerk of the Secretariat, feared trouble and arranged soldiers for protection. Everyone saw the sword pass through the roof and fly away, and no one knew where it went.

    During the Jianyuan period of Emperor Kang of Jin, a fisherman caught a golden lock while fishing. When he pulled the lock out, he saw a golden ox. He quickly pulled out the ox, which broke, leaving a lock two feet long.

    During the Taoyuan period of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, Xu Sunjiang from Linwu, Guiyang, saw money overflowing from the bank. He immediately loaded it onto his boat, and soon it all turned into earth.

    During the Yixi period of Emperor An of Jin, Pang Yi served as the Prefect of Yidu and kept people and horses in the fields. He saw a copper stove with a handle and a lock, moving. He brought it back to show Pang Yi. He then placed it in a cage and sent it to the north of Jingzhou, which was not the capital. Suddenly there was wind and rain, with a sound, and fire lit up the sky, coming straight to the ship, and the stove was lost. [7]

    义熙中，新野黄舒耕田得一舡金，卜者云：“三年勿用，长守富也。”舒不能从，遂成土壤。
    　　晋时钱塘浙江有樟林桁大船，每有乘者，辄漂荡摇扬而不可禁。常鸣鼓，钱塘江头凌浪如故，惟船吏章粤能相制伏。及粤死，遂废去。
    　　海西太和中，会稽山阴县起仓，凿得两大船，船中有钱，皆轮文。时日向暮，凿者驰以告官，官夜遣防守甚严。至明旦失钱所在，惟有船存，视其状，悉有钱处。
    　　苻坚建元年中，长安樵人于城内见金鼎，走白坚。坚遣载取，到化为铜，鼎入门，又变成大铎。
    　　西河有钟在水中，晦朔辄鸣，声响悲激，羁客闻而凄怆。
    　　越崔门会元县有元马河，有铜钫船，河畔有祠，中有碧珠，若不祭祀，取之不祥。
    　　长山朱郭夫妻采澡涧滨，见二铜釜，沿流而下。取之而归。有员盖满中铜器，光辉曜目，自然作声。郭惧，运盖北山埋之，而后卖釜与人。共载出为货，船无故自覆，失釜所在。 [8]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [8]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 三年勿用 - A divination result suggesting that the gold should not be used for three years.
    • 长守富也 - The divination result indicating that the gold will bring long-term wealth.
    • 遂成土壤 - The gold eventually became part of the soil, implying it was lost or disappeared.
    • 漂荡摇扬 - Describes the ship's movement, suggesting it is unstable or dangerous.
    • 凌浪如故 - To navigate the waves as usual, implying the ship behaves normally despite the warnings.
    • 惟船吏章粤能相制伏 - Only the ship's official named Zhang Yue could control the ship.
    • 晦朔辄鸣 - The bell rings on the first and last days of the lunar month.
    • 声响悲激 - The sound is sorrowful and intense.
    • 若不祭祀，取之不祥 - If not properly worshipped, taking the pearl would be unlucky.
    • 自然作声 - The objects make a sound naturally.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 義熙中 - The reign period of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song (403–424 AD).
    • 新野 - A historical place in Henan Province.
    • 卜者 - Diviners were common in ancient China, often consulted for guidance.
    • 錢塘浙江 - The Qiantang River is a significant geographical feature in Zhejiang Province.
    • 海西太和中 - The reign period of Emperor Taihe of Liu Song (402–420 AD).
    • 会稽山阴县 - A historical county in Shanyin, modern-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang Province.
    • 越崔门会元县 - A historical county in Huaiyuan, modern-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 元马河 - A historical river in the region.
    • 西河有钟 - A bell in the western river, possibly a local landmark.
    • 碧珠 - Green pearls were considered valuable and often associated with religious or cultural significance.
    • 樵人 - Woodcutters were common in ancient China, often involved in discovering natural resources.
    • 长安 - The capital of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 苻坚 - Emperor of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 建元年中 - The reign period of Emperor Fu Jian (357–385 AD).

    Proper Nouns

    • 義熙中 - The reign period of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song (403–424 AD).
    • 新野 - A historical place in Henan Province.
    • 卜者 - Diviners were common in ancient China.
    • 錢塘浙江 - The Qiantang River in Zhejiang Province.
    • 海西太和中 - The reign period of Emperor Taihe of Liu Song (402–420 AD).
    • 会稽山阴县 - A historical county in Shanyin, modern-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang Province.
    • 越崔门会元县 - A historical county in Huaiyuan, modern-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 元马河 - A historical river in the region.
    • 西河有钟 - A bell in the western river.
    • 碧珠 - Green pearls were considered valuable.
    • 樵人 - Woodcutters were common in ancient China.
    • 长安 - The capital of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 苻坚 - Emperor of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 建元年中 - The reign period of Emperor Fu Jian (357–385 AD).

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song (403–424 AD), Huang Shu of Xinye found a shipload of gold while farming. A diviner advised him, "Do not use it for three years; it will bring long-term wealth." Huang Shu did not follow the advice, and the gold eventually became part of the soil.

    During the Jin dynasty, a large ship was discovered in the Qiantang River near Zhanglin. Anyone who boarded the ship would experience uncontrollable drifting and swaying. The ship's official, Zhang Yue, was the only one who could control it. After Zhang Yue's death, the ship became uncontrollable.

    During the reign of Emperor Taihe of Liu Song (402–420 AD), two large ships with coins were discovered while building a granary in Shanyin County. The coins had patterns on them. As night fell, the workers rushed to inform the authorities, who sent guards to protect the site. By the next morning, the coins were missing, but the ships remained. Upon inspection, the ships appeared to be full of coins.

    During the reign of Emperor Fu Jian of the Former Qin dynasty (357–385 AD), a woodcutter in Chang'an found a golden tripod inside the city walls. He reported it to the emperor, who sent someone to retrieve it. Upon arrival, the tripod had turned into copper. When it entered the palace, it transformed into a large bell.

    A bell in the western river would ring on the first and last days of the lunar month, producing a sorrowful and intense sound that made travelers feel sad.

    In Huaiyuan County, there was a river called Yuanma with a copper ship. By the riverbank was a shrine containing a green pearl. If not properly worshipped, taking the pearl would be unlucky.

    In Changshan, Zhuo and his wife were collecting herbs by the stream when they saw two copper cauldrons floating downstream. They took them home. The cauldrons had lids full of copper objects that shone brightly and made a natural sound. Zhuo was afraid and buried the lid in the north mountain. He then sold the cauldrons to others. They loaded them onto a boat to sell as goods, but the boat capsized without reason, and the cauldrons were lost. [8]

    上党侯亮之于江都城下获一石磨，下有铜马。
    　　弘农杨子阳闻土中有声怕，掘得玉犭屯，长可尺许，屋栋间乃自漏秫米。如此三年，昼夜不息，米坠既止。忽有一青蛇长数尺，住在梁上，每落粪，辄成碎银子。阳获银米，遂为富儿。锻银作器化卖，倍售余家，市者随以破灭。
    　　永康王旷井上有洗石，时见赤气，后有二胡人寄宿，忽求买之。旷怪所以，未及度钱，子妇孙氏睹二黄鸟斗于石上，疾往掩取，变成黄金。胡人不知，索市愈急。既得，撞破，内空段有二鸟处。
    　　西域句夷国山上有石骆驰，腹下出水。以金铁及手承取，即便对过，惟瓠芦盛之者则得饮之，令人身体香净而升仙。其国神秘，不可数遇。
    　　月支国有佛发，盛以琉璃罂。
    　　南康归美山石城内有甘橘橙柚，就食其实，任意取足。脱持归者，便遇大蛇，或颠仆失径，家人啖之辄病。 [9]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [9]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 上黨侯亮 - A historical figure or title.
    • 弘農楊子陽 - A historical figure or title.
    • 永康王曠 - A historical figure or title.
    • 胡人 - Refers to people from the Western regions.
    • 西域句夷國 - A mythical or historical Western country.
    • 月支國 - A historical country in Central Asia.
    • 南康歸美山石城 - A mythical or historical place in southern China.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 上黨侯亮 - A historical figure or title.
    • 弘農楊子陽 - A historical figure or title.
    • 永康王曠 - A historical figure or title.
    • 胡人 - Refers to people from the Western regions.
    • 西域句夷國 - A mythical or historical Western country.
    • 月支國 - A historical country in Central Asia.
    • 南康歸美山石城 - A mythical or historical place in southern China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 上黨侯亮 - A historical figure or title.
    • 弘農楊子陽 - A historical figure or title.
    • 永康王曠 - A historical figure or title.
    • 胡人 - Refers to people from the Western regions.
    • 西域句夷國 - A mythical or historical Western country.
    • 月支國 - A historical country in Central Asia.
    • 南康歸美山石城 - A mythical or historical place in southern China.

    The Marquis Liang of Shangdang discovered a stone mill near the city of Jiangdu, with a copper horse underneath.

    Yang Ziyang of Hongnong heard a sound coming from the earth and dug up a jade fox-like creature, about a foot long, which leaked millet from the roof beam. This continued for three years, day and night, until the millet stopped falling. Suddenly, a green snake about three feet long appeared on the beam, and its droppings turned into small pieces of silver. Yang Ziyang gained wealth from the silver millet and became a wealthy man. He forged silver into vessels and sold them, doubling the price of other merchants' goods, and those who bought them subsequently met with misfortune.

    Wang Kuang of Yongkang had a washing stone on his well, which sometimes emitted a red aura. Later, two Hu people stayed overnight and suddenly asked to buy it. Wang Kuang was puzzled and had not yet paid them, but his wife Sun saw two yellow birds fighting on the stone and quickly caught them, turning them into gold. The Hu people did not know about this and continued to insist on buying it. When they got it, they smashed it open and found a hollow space where the birds had been.

    In the Western country of Gouyi, there was a stone camel on a mountain that produced water from its belly. If one caught the water with gold, iron, or their hands, it would pass through without being retained; only if one used a gourd to collect it could one drink it, and doing so would make one's body fragrant and pure, leading to immortality. The country was mysterious and could not be encountered frequently.

    In the Month Country, there was a Buddha's hair, preserved in a glass jar.

    In the Shicheng of Guimei Mountain in Nankang, there were sweet oranges, tangerines, and pomelos. One could freely eat the fruits, taking as much as one wished. If one took them home, one would encounter a large snake, or lose one's way, and if one's family ate them, they would fall ill. [9]

    阳羡县小吏吴龛于溪中见五色浮石，因取内床头，至夜化成女子。
    　　河内司马元允元嘉中为新釜令，丧官。月旦设祭，柑化而为鸢。
    　　晋惠帝元康二年，巴西郡界竹生花，紫色结实如麦，外皮青，中赤白，味甚甘。
    　　晋太元中，南郡忻陵县有枣树，一年忽生桃、李、枣三种花子。
    　　汉兴平元年九月，桑再椹时，刘玄德军于沛，年荒谷贵，士众皆饥，仰以为粮。
    　　北方有白桑椹，长数寸，食之甘美。
    　　建安有竹，节中有人，长尺许，头足皆具。
    　　元嘉四年东阳流道先家中筋竹林忽生连理，野人无知，谓之祸祟，欲斫杀之。
    　　汉安帝元初三年，平陆有瓜异处同蒂，共生一瓜，时以为嘉瓜。
    　　晋武帝太康八年六月，王浚园生瓜三茎一实。
    　　晋安平有越王余{艹У}，长尺许，白者似骨，黑者如角。古云越王行海，曾于舟中作筹，有余者弃之于水生焉。 [10]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [10]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 五色浮石 - A stone that appears in five colors, which is unusual and possibly supernatural.
    • 化成女子 - Transformed into a woman, indicating a supernatural transformation.
    • 柑化而为鸢 - Transformed into an eagle, indicating a supernatural transformation.
    • 桑再椹时 - Mulberry trees bearing fruit twice, an unusual and significant event.
    • 仰以为粮 - Relied on it as food, indicating a practical use of the unusual event.
    • 白桑椹 - White mulberries, which are unusual in color.
    • 建安有竹 - A type of bamboo found in Jian'an, with a specific characteristic.
    • 节中有人 - People inside the joints, indicating a supernatural or unusual phenomenon.
    • 连理 - Two trees growing together as one, symbolizing good fortune.
    • 祸祟 - Disaster or bad omen, indicating a negative interpretation of the unusual event.
    • 嘉瓜 - Auspicious gourd, indicating a positive interpretation of the unusual event.
    • 三茎一实 - Three stems with one fruit, indicating an unusual and significant event.
    • 越王余{艹У} - A type of plant or object associated with the Yue kings, with specific characteristics.
    • 白者似骨，黑者如角 - White ones look like bones, black ones like horns, describing the appearance of the plant or object.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 阳羡县 - Yangxian County, a place in ancient China.
    • 小吏 - A low-ranking official or clerk.
    • 河内司马元允 - Sima Yuanyun, a historical figure from the Northern Wei dynasty.
    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 新釜令 - A local official in Xinfu, a place in ancient China.
    • 晋惠帝元康二年 - The second year of the Yuankang era during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 巴西郡界 - The border region of Bazhui Commandery, a place in ancient China.
    • 汉兴平元年 - The first year of the Xingping era during the reign of Emperor Xian of Han.
    • 沛 - Pei, a place in ancient China.
    • 北方 - The northern region of China.
    • 建安 - Jian'an, a place in ancient China.
    • 元嘉四年 - The fourth year of the Yuankang era during the reign of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 东阳 - Dongyang, a place in ancient China.
    • 汉安帝元初三年 - The third year of the Yuanchu era during the reign of Emperor An of Han.
    • 平陆 - Pinglu, a place in ancient China.
    • 晋武帝太康八年 - The eighth year of the Taikang era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin.
    • 王浚园 - Wang Jun's garden, a place associated with a historical figure.
    • 晋安平 - Anping, a place in ancient China.
    • 越王 - The rulers of the state of Yue, an ancient kingdom in China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 阳羡县 - Yangxian County.
    • 吴龛 - Wu Kan.
    • 河内司马元允 - Sima Yuanyun.
    • 元嘉 - Yuankang era.
    • 新釜令 - Local official in Xinfu.
    • 晋惠帝 - Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 元康二年 - Second year of the Yuankang era.
    • 巴西郡 - Bazhui Commandery.
    • 汉兴平元年 - First year of the Xingping era.
    • 沛 - Pei.
    • 北方 - Northern region.
    • 建安 - Jian'an.
    • 元嘉四年 - Fourth year of the Yuankang era.
    • 东阳 - Dongyang.
    • 汉安帝 - Emperor An of Han.
    • 平陆 - Pinglu.
    • 晋武帝 - Emperor Wu of Jin.
    • 太康八年 - Eighth year of the Taikang era.
    • 王浚 - Wang Jun.
    • 晋安平 - Anping.
    • 越王 - Rulers of the state of Yue.

    A Clerk from Yangxian County, Wu Kan, saw a five-colored floating stone in a stream and took it home, where it transformed into a woman by night.
    In the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, Sima Yuanyun, a local official in Xinfu, lost his position. During a sacrifice on the first day of the month, a tangerine transformed into an eagle.
    In the second year of the Yuankang era during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin, bamboo in the border region of Bazhui Commandery produced flowers, purple fruits, and was sweet.
    In the Taoyuan era, a jujube tree in Xinling County suddenly produced flowers of three different kinds in one year.
    In the first year of the Xingping era, mulberry trees bore fruit twice. Liu Bei's army in Pei relied on them for food during a famine.
    In the north, there were white mulberries that grew several inches long and were very sweet.
    In Jian'an, there was a type of bamboo with people inside its joints, about a foot long, with heads and feet.
    In the fourth year of the Yuankang era, a bamboo grove in Dongyang suddenly grew together. The locals, unaware of its significance, considered it a bad omen and wanted to cut it down.
    In the third year of the Yuanchu era during the reign of Emperor An of Han, a gourd in Pinglu grew from two different places but shared one stem, and it was considered auspicious.
    In the eighth year of the Taikang era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, a gourd in Wang Jun's garden grew from three stems but had one fruit.
    In Anping, there was a plant or object associated with the Yue kings, about a foot long, with white ones looking like bones and black ones like horns. It was said that the Yue kings threw them into the water when not needed, and they grew there. [10]

    薯蓣一名山芋，根既可入药，又复可食。野人谓之土{艹诸}。若欲掘取，默然则获，唱名者便不可得。人有植者，随所种之物而像之也。
    　　人参一名土精，生上党者隹。人形皆具，能作儿啼。昔，有人掘之，始下铧，便闻土中呻吟声，寻音而取，果得人参。
    　　交州诸菌以叶涂人躯，便举体菌生。生既遍，便就朽烂，肌肉消腐。
    　　隋县永阳有山，壁立千仞，岩上有石室，古名为神农窟。窟前有百药丛茂，莫不毕备。又别有异物藤花，形似菱菜，朝紫中绿，晡黄暮青，夜赤五色迭耀。

    　　●卷三
    　　晋太康二年冬，大寒。南洲人见二白鹤语于桥下曰：“今兹寒不减尧崩年也。”于是飞去。
    　　宾国王买得一鸾，欲其鸣不可，致饣希金繁，飨珍羞，对之愈戚，三年不鸣。夫人曰：“尝闻鸾见类则鸣，何不悬镜照之？”王从其言，鸾睹影悲鸣，冲霄一奋而绝。 [11]

    Translation

    The yam, also known as the mountain yam, has roots that can be used both as medicine and for food. Common people call it tu zhu. If one wishes to dig it up, they must do so silently to find it; if they call out its name, it will not be found. If someone plants it, it will grow according to what was planted.

    Ginseng, also known as the earth spirit, is best when grown in Shangdang. It has a human-like shape and can make a child's cry. In the past, someone digging for it heard a groaning sound from the earth and followed the sound to find the ginseng.

    In Jiaozhou, various mushrooms can be applied to a person's body with their leaves, causing mushrooms to grow all over the body. Once they have grown all over, they begin to decay, causing the flesh to rot.

    In Yongyang County, Sui County, there is a mountain that stands thousands of feet high, with a rock face and a stone cave on it, anciently named the Shennong Cave. In front of the cave, there are many medicinal herbs growing in abundance, all present. There is also a unique vine flower that resembles water chestnuts, changing colors throughout the day and night: purple in the morning, green in the middle of the day, yellow in the afternoon, blue at dusk, and red at night, with the five colors alternating in brilliance.

    Volume Three

    In the second year of the Tai Kang era of the Jin Dynasty, during a severe winter, people in Nan Zhou saw two white cranes talking under a bridge, saying, "This cold is no less than the year of Emperor Yao's death." Then they flew away.

    The king of the country of Xianbi bought a phoenix, but it would not sing. He provided it with fine food and precious delicacies, but it became sadder and would not sing for three years. His wife said, "I have heard that phoenixes sing when they see their kind. Why not hang a mirror in front of it?" The king followed her advice, and the phoenix, seeing its reflection, mourned and flew up into the sky, never to return. [11]

    张华有白鹦鹉，华每出行，还辄说僮仆善恶。后寂无言，华问其故，答曰：“见藏瓮中，何由得知？”公后在外，令唤鹦鹉，鹦鹉曰：“昨夜梦恶，不宜出户。”公犹强之，至庭为所搏，教其啄脚，仅而获免。
    　　有鹦鹉飞集他山，山中禽兽，辄相贵重。鹦鹉自念虽乐，不可久也，便去。后数月，山中大火，鹦鹉遥见，便入水濡，羽飞而洒之。天神言：“汝虽有志意，何足云也？”对曰：“虽知不能救，然尝侨居是山，禽兽行善，皆为兄弟，不忍见耳。”天神嘉感，即为灭火。
    　　五月五日剪鸲鹆舌，教令学人语声，尤清越，虽鹦鹉不能过也。
    　　晋司空桓豁在荆州，有参军五月五日剪鸲鹆舌，每教令学人语，遂无所不名，与人相顾问。参军善弹琵琶，鸲鹆每听辄移时。
    　　山鸡爱其毛羽，映水则舞。魏武时南方献之。帝欲其鸣舞而无由，公子苍舒令置大镜其前，鸡鉴形而舞，不知止，遂乏死。韦仲将为之赋其事。 [12]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [12]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 还辄说僮仆善恶 - A set phrase meaning 'whenever he returned, he would tell about the good and bad of the servants,' indicating a habit of Zhang Hua.
    • 见藏瓮中，何由得知 - A rhetorical question expressing the parrot's inability to speak, literally 'It was hidden in a jar, how could it know?'
    • 令唤鹦鹉 - An instruction, literally 'He ordered the parrot to be called.'
    • 昨夜梦恶，不宜出户 - The parrot's response to being called, literally 'Last night I had a bad dream, it's not suitable to go out.'
    • 至庭为所搏 - Describes the parrot's fate, literally 'It went to the courtyard and was attacked by a hawk.'
    • 教其啄脚，仅而获免 - Shows Zhang Hua's quick thinking, literally 'He taught it to peck at the hawk's leg, and it barely escaped.'
    • 虽乐，不可久也 - The parrot's reasoning for leaving, literally 'Although it was happy, it couldn't stay long.'
    • 后数月，山中大火 - Sets the scene for the parrot's heroic act, literally 'After several months, a big fire broke out in the mountain.'
    • 鹦鹉遥见，便入水濡羽飞而洒之 - Describes the parrot's actions, literally 'The parrot saw from afar and immediately went into the water to wet its feathers and flew to sprinkle water.'
    • 汝虽有志意，何足云也 - A dismissive response from the deity, literally 'You may have good intentions, but what is that worth?'
    • 虽知不能救，然尝侨居是山，禽兽行善，皆为兄弟，不忍见耳 - The parrot's explanation, literally 'Although I know I can't save them, I have lived here as a guest, and the birds and beasts here are like brothers, I can't bear to see them.'
    • 天神嘉感，即为灭火 - Shows the positive outcome, literally 'The deity was moved and immediately put out the fire.'
    • 五月五日剪鸲鹆舌 - Introduces a new story, literally 'On the fifth day of the fifth month, the tongue of a magpie was cut.'
    • 每教令学人语声，遂无所不名 - Describes the magpie's abilities, literally 'It was taught to learn human speech, and it eventually could imitate all sounds.'
    • 参军善弹琵琶，鸲鹆每听辄移时 - Shows the magpie's interest in music, literally 'The military officer was good at playing the pipa, and the magpie would listen for a long time.'
    • 山鸡爱其毛羽，映水则舞 - Describes the peafowl's behavior, literally 'The peafowl loved its feathers and would dance when it saw its reflection in the water.'
    • 魏武时南方献之 - Introduces the peafowl to the emperor, literally 'During the time of Wei Wu, the south presented it to him.'
    • 帝欲其鸣舞而无由 - Sets up the problem, literally 'The emperor wanted it to sing and dance but had no way to do so.'
    • 公子苍舒令置大镜其前，鸡鉴形而舞，不知止，遂乏死 - Describes the peafowl's tragic end, literally 'The young lord Cangshu ordered a large mirror to be placed in front of it, and the peafowl saw its reflection and danced, not knowing when to stop, and eventually died.'
    • 韦仲将为之赋其事 - Indicates the literary response to the event, literally 'Wei Zhongjiang wrote a poem about the incident.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 张华 - Zhang Hua, a famous scholar and official during the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 鹦鹉 - Parrots, known for their ability to mimic human speech.
    • 鸲鹆 - Magpies, known for their ability to mimic sounds.
    • 魏武 - Cao Cao, a powerful warlord and statesman during the late Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 公子苍舒 - Cangshu, a son of Cao Cao.
    • 韦仲将 - Wei Zhongjiang, a poet and official during the Wei dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张华 - Zhang Hua, a famous scholar and official during the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 鹦鹉 - Parrots, known for their ability to mimic human speech.
    • 鸲鹆 - Magpies, known for their ability to mimic sounds.
    • 魏武 - Cao Cao, a powerful warlord and statesman during the late Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 公子苍舒 - Cangshu, a son of Cao Cao.
    • 韦仲将 - Wei Zhongjiang, a poet and official during the Wei dynasty.

    Zhang Hua had a white parrot. Whenever he went out, he would tell about the good and bad of the servants upon his return. Later, the parrot became silent, and Zhang Hua asked it why. The parrot replied, "It was hidden in a jar, how could I know?" Later, when Zhang Hua was out, he ordered the parrot to be called. The parrot said, "I had a bad dream last night, it's not suitable to go out." Despite this, Zhang Hua still forced it to go. It went to the courtyard and was attacked by a hawk. Zhang Hua taught it to peck at the hawk's leg, and it barely escaped.

    There was a parrot that flew to another mountain. The birds and beasts in the mountain valued it highly. The parrot thought to itself that although it was happy, it couldn't stay long, so it left. After several months, a big fire broke out in the mountain. The parrot saw from afar and immediately went into the water to wet its feathers and flew to sprinkle water. The deity said, "You may have good intentions, but what is that worth?" The parrot replied, "Although I know I can't save them, I have lived here as a guest, and the birds and beasts here are like brothers, I can't bear to see them." The deity was moved and immediately put out the fire.

    On the fifth day of the fifth month, the tongue of a magpie was cut. It was taught to learn human speech, and it eventually could imitate all sounds, even clearer than a parrot.

    During the Jin dynasty, the Military Governor of Jingzhou, Huán Huō, had a military officer who cut the tongue of a magpie on the fifth day of the fifth month. He taught it to learn human speech, and it eventually could imitate all sounds. It would listen to the military officer play the pipa for a long time.

    The peafowl loved its feathers and would dance when it saw its reflection in the water. During the time of Wei Wu, the south presented it to him. The emperor wanted it to sing and dance but had no way to do so. The young lord Cangshu ordered a large mirror to be placed in front of it, and the peafowl saw its reflection and danced, not knowing when to stop, and eventually died. Wei Zhongjiang wrote a poem about the incident. [12]

    晋义熙三年，朱猗戍寿阳，婢炊衤，忽有群鸟集灶，竞来啄啖，驱逐不去。有猎犬咋杀两鸟，余鸟因共咋杀犬，又啖其肉，唯余骨存。
    　　杜鹃始阳相催而鸣，先鸣者吐血死。常有人山行，见一群寂然，聊学其声，使呕血死。初鸣，先听其声者，主离别。厕上听其声不祥。厌之法，当为大声以应之。
    　　晋兖州刺史沛国宋处宗尝买得一长鸣鸡，爱养甚至，恒笼置窗间，鸡遂作人语，与处宗谈论，极有言致。终日不辍，处宗由此玄言大进。
    　　晋义熙中，羌主姚毗于洛阳阴沟取砖，得一双雄鹅，并金色交颈，长鸣，声闻于九皋。养之此沟。
    　　傅承为江夏守，有一双鹅失之三年，忽引导得三十余头来向承家。
    　　武陵龙阳虞德流寓溢阳，止主人夏蛮舍中。忽见有白纸一幅，长尺余，标蛮女头，乃起扳取。俄顷有虎到户而退，寻见何老母，标如初。德又取之。如斯三返，乃具以语蛮，于是相与执杖伺候。须臾虎至，即格杀之。同县黄期具说如此。 [13]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [13]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋义熙三年 - Indicates the year 407 AD during the Jin Dynasty, specifically the reign of Emperor An.
    • 婢炊衤 - Describes a female servant cooking, where '婢' means servant and '炊衤' refers to cooking or preparing food.
    • 忽有群鸟集灶 - Means 'suddenly a group of birds gathered around the stove,' indicating an unusual occurrence.
    • 竞来啄啖 - Describes the birds coming to peck and eat, emphasizing their sudden and aggressive behavior.
    • 驱逐不去 - Means 'unable to drive away,' indicating the persistence of the birds despite attempts to remove them.
    • 咋杀两鸟 - Means 'bit and killed two birds,' describing the actions of the dog.
    • 余鸟因共咋杀犬 - Means 'the remaining birds then bit and killed the dog,' indicating a retaliatory action.
    • 又啖其肉，唯余骨存 - Means 'then they ate its meat, leaving only bones,' describing the aftermath of the attack.
    • 始阳相催而鸣 - Means 'beginning to call each other to sing,' indicating the start of the breeding season for the cuckoo.
    • 先鸣者吐血死 - Means 'those who sing first will die spitting blood,' indicating a fatal consequence for early singers.
    • 常有人山行 - Means 'there was once a man who was traveling in the mountains,' setting the scene for a supernatural event.
    • 见一群寂然 - Means 'saw a group of birds sitting quietly,' describing the initial observation.
    • 聊学其声 - Means 'tried to imitate their sound,' indicating the man's attempt to mimic the birds.
    • 使呕血死 - Means 'caused him to vomit blood and die,' indicating the fatal consequence of the imitation.
    • 初鸣，先听其声者，主离别 - Means 'when they first sing, those who hear them first will experience separation,' indicating a superstitious belief.
    • 厕上听其声不祥 - Means 'hearing their sound in the toilet is inauspicious,' indicating a specific place where hearing the sound is considered bad luck.
    • 厌之法 - Means 'a method to ward off evil,' indicating a ritual or practice to counteract negative effects.
    • 当为大声以应之 - Means 'one should make a loud sound to respond to them,' indicating the recommended action.
    • 晋兖州刺史沛国宋处宗 - Refers to Cao Cao's official, Song Chuzong, who lived in the state of Pei.
    • 尝买得一长鸣鸡 - Means 'once bought a rooster that could crow for a long time,' indicating the rooster's unique ability.
    • 爱养甚至 - Means 'took great care of it,' indicating the level of care and affection.
    • 恒笼置窗间 - Means 'always kept it in a cage by the window,' indicating the rooster's living conditions.
    • 鸡遂作人语 - Means 'the rooster began to speak like a human,' indicating the rooster's unusual behavior.
    • 与处宗谈论，极有言致 - Means 'spoke with Song Chuzong, showing great eloquence,' indicating the quality of the conversation.
    • 终日不辍 - Means 'continued throughout the day,' indicating the duration of the interaction.
    • 处宗由此玄言大进 - Means 'Song Chuzong's understanding of profound language greatly improved,' indicating the positive impact on Song Chuzong.
    • 羌主姚毗于洛阳阴沟取砖 - Refers to Yao Bi, the leader of the Qiang people, taking bricks from the underground canal in Luoyang.
    • 得一双雄鹅，并金色交颈 - Means 'found a pair of golden geese with their necks intertwined,' indicating the rarity and beauty of the geese.
    • 长鸣，声闻于九皋 - Means 'they called out loudly, and their sound could be heard nine miles away,' indicating the loudness and reach of their calls.
    • 养之此沟 - Means 'they were raised in this canal,' indicating the location where the geese were kept.
    • 傅承为江夏守 - Refers to Fu Cheng, the governor of Jiangxia.
    • 有一双鹅失之三年 - Means 'lost a pair of geese for three years,' indicating the duration of the loss.
    • 忽引导得三十余头来向承家 - Means 'suddenly led more than thirty geese to Fu Cheng's house,' indicating a miraculous return.
    • 武陵龙阳虞德流寓溢阳 - Refers to Yu De, a man from Longyang, Wuling, who was living in Yiyang.
    • 止主人夏蛮舍中 - Means 'stayed at the house of a man named Xia Man,' indicating the location where Yu De was staying.
    • 忽见有白纸一幅，长尺余，标蛮女头 - Means 'suddenly saw a white sheet of paper about a foot long, marked with a woman's head,' indicating a supernatural sign.
    • 乃起扳取 - Means 'got up to take it,' indicating Yu De's reaction to the sign.
    • 俄顷有虎到户而退 - Means 'soon after, a tiger came to the door and retreated,' indicating the immediate danger.
    • 寻见何老母，标如初 - Means 'soon saw an old woman named He, marked similarly,' indicating a second supernatural sign.
    • 德又取之 - Means 'Yu De took it again,' indicating his repeated action.
    • 如斯三返 - Means 'this happened three times,' indicating the repetition of the events.
    • 乃具以语蛮 - Means 'then told Xia Man about it,' indicating the communication of the events.
    • 于是相与执杖伺候 - Means 'they then prepared to wait with weapons,' indicating the preparation for action.
    • 须臾虎至，即格杀之 - Means 'soon the tiger arrived, and they killed it immediately,' indicating the successful outcome.
    • 同县黄期具说如此 - Refers to Huang Qi, a fellow villager, who also reported similar events.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋义熙三年 - The year 407 AD during the Jin Dynasty, a period of political instability and cultural development.
    • 晋兖州刺史沛国宋处宗 - Cao Cao's official, Song Chuzong, who was known for his literary and administrative skills.
    • 羌主姚毗 - Yao Bi, the leader of the Qiang people, a nomadic group that often interacted with Chinese states.
    • 洛阳阴沟 - An underground canal in Luoyang, a significant city in Chinese history.
    • 武陵龙阳 - A region in Hunan Province, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 溢阳 - A place name, possibly referring to a small town or village.
    • 夏蛮 - A man named Xia Man, likely a local inhabitant or landowner.
    • 何老母 - An old woman named He, possibly a local resident.
    • 同县黄期 - A fellow villager named Huang Qi, who also reported similar events.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sequence describes a series of events: the servant cooking, the birds gathering, pecking, and not being driven away.
    • This sequence describes the dog killing two birds, the remaining birds killing the dog, and the birds eating the dog's meat.
    • This sequence describes the cuckoos calling each other to sing, with the first singers dying from spitting blood.
    • This sequence describes a man traveling in the mountains, seeing a group of birds, imitating their sound, and dying from vomiting blood.
    • This clause describes the superstition that hearing the cuckoos' first call predicts separation.
    • This clause describes the superstition that hearing the cuckoos' call in the toilet is inauspicious.
    • This clause describes the method to ward off evil by making a loud sound.
    • This long sequence describes Song Chuzong's experience with the talking rooster and its impact on his understanding of profound language.
    • This sequence describes Yao Bi taking bricks from the underground canal in Luoyang, finding a pair of golden geese, and raising them in the canal.
    • This sequence describes Fu Cheng losing a pair of geese for three years and then suddenly having more than thirty geese led to his house.
    • This sequence describes Yu De seeing a supernatural sign, reacting to it, and then seeing a second sign, repeating the process three times.
    • This sequence describes Yu De telling Xia Man about the events, preparing to wait with weapons, and then killing a tiger that arrived.
    • This clause refers to Huang Qi, a fellow villager, who also reported similar events.

    In the third year of the Yixi era during the Jin Dynasty, Zhu Yi was stationed in Shouyang. While a female servant was cooking, suddenly a group of birds gathered around the stove, coming to peck and eat, and could not be driven away. A hunting dog bit and killed two of the birds, and the remaining birds then bit and killed the dog, eating its meat until only bones remained.

    The cuckoos began to call each other to sing, and those who sang first would die spitting blood. Once, a man was traveling in the mountains and saw a group of birds sitting quietly. He tried to imitate their sound, which caused him to vomit blood and die. When the cuckoos first sing, those who hear them first will experience separation. Hearing their sound in the toilet is inauspicious. To ward off evil, one should make a loud sound to respond to them.

    During the Yixi era of the Jin Dynasty, Song Chuzong, the governor of Yanzhou from the state of Pei, once bought a rooster that could crow for a long time. He took great care of it, always keeping it in a cage by the window. The rooster began to speak like a human, conversing with Song Chuzong, showing great eloquence. They continued throughout the day, and Song Chuzong's understanding of profound language greatly improved.

    During the Yixi era of the Jin Dynasty, Yao Bi, the leader of the Qiang people, took bricks from the underground canal in Luoyang and found a pair of golden geese with their necks intertwined. They called out loudly, and their sound could be heard nine miles away. They were raised in this canal.

    Fu Cheng, the governor of Jiangxia, once lost a pair of geese for three years. Suddenly, more than thirty geese were led to Fu Cheng's house.

    Yu De, a man from Longyang, Wuling, who was living in Yiyang, stayed at the house of a man named Xia Man. Suddenly, he saw a white sheet of paper about a foot long, marked with a woman's head. He got up to take it. Soon after, a tiger came to the door and retreated. Soon, he saw an old woman named He, marked similarly. Yu De took it again. This happened three times. He then told Xia Man about it, and they prepared to wait with weapons. Soon the tiger arrived, and they killed it immediately. A fellow villager named Huang Qi also reported similar events. [13]

    彭城刘广雅以晋太元元年为京府佐，被使还都，路经竹里亭，于逻宿。此逻多虎，刘极自防卫，系马于户前，手执戟布于地上。中霄与士庶同睡，虎乘间跳入，跨越人畜，独取刘而去。
    　　晋太元末，徐桓以太元中出门，仿佛见一女子，因言曲相调，便要桓入草中。桓悦其色，乃随去，女子忽然变成虎，负桓着背上，径向深山。其家左右寻觅，惟见虎迹，旬日，虎送桓下著门外。
    　　扶南王范寻常畜虎五六头及鳄鱼十头。若有讼，未知曲直，便投与鱼虎，鱼虎不食则为有理。秽貊之人祭虎为神，将有以也。
    　　永初中，邵都梁冯恭醉卧于山路。夜有虎来，以头枕其背。恭中宵辗转，以手搏之，复大寝。向晓始醒，犹见虎蹲在脚后，若有宿命，非智力所及也。 [14]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [14]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 以晋太元元年 - Indicates the specific year of the reign period of Emperor Xian of Jin (376 AD), using the era name 'Taiyuan.'
    • 被使还都 - Means 'sent on a mission back to the capital,' where '被使' indicates being sent on an official mission.
    • 逻宿 - Refers to staying at a checkpoint or way station, which was common during travel.
    • 系马于户前 - Means 'tied the horse in front of the door,' indicating a practical action taken for safety.
    • 手执戟布于地上 - Means 'held the halberd spread out on the ground,' describing a defensive posture.
    • 中霄与士庶同睡 - Means 'slept with the common people in the middle of the night,' indicating a communal sleeping arrangement.
    • 虎乘间跳入 - Means 'the tiger took advantage of the opportunity to jump in,' describing the sudden intrusion.
    • 跨越人畜 - Means 'jumped over people and animals,' indicating the tiger's movement through the group.
    • 独取刘而去 - Means 'took Liu alone and left,' highlighting the specific target of the attack.
    • 晋太元末 - Indicates the end of the Taiyuan period of Emperor Xian of Jin (around 397 AD).
    • 仿佛见一女子 - Means 'vaguely saw a woman,' indicating a hazy or indistinct vision.
    • 曲相调 - Means 'flirted or teased in a playful manner.'
    • 要桓入草中 - Means 'invited Huan into the grass,' indicating a seductive gesture.
    • 桓悦其色 - Means 'Huan was pleased by her appearance,' describing his attraction.
    • 忽然变成虎 - Means 'suddenly turned into a tiger,' indicating a supernatural transformation.
    • 负桓着背上 - Means 'carried Huan on its back,' describing the tiger's action.
    • 径向深山 - Means 'headed directly toward the deep mountains,' indicating the direction of the tiger's movement.
    • 惟见虎迹 - Means 'only saw tiger tracks,' indicating the aftermath of the incident.
    • 旬日 - Means 'about ten days,' indicating a period of time.
    • 送桓下著门外 - Means 'dropped Huan off outside the door,' indicating the tiger's return.
    • 扶南王范常 - Refers to the ruler of Funan (an ancient kingdom in Southeast Asia) named Fan.
    • 畜虎五六头及鳄鱼十头 - Means 'kept five or six tigers and ten crocodiles,' indicating the ruler's menagerie.
    • 若有所讼 - Means 'if there is a dispute,' indicating a legal or moral conflict.
    • 未知曲直 - Means 'it is unknown who is right or wrong,' indicating the uncertainty in the dispute.
