    • 投与鱼虎 - Means 'throw it to the fish or tiger,' indicating a method of determining the outcome.
    • 鱼虎不食则为有理 - Means 'if the fish or tiger does not eat it, then the person is in the right,' indicating a superstition.
    • 秽貊之人祭虎为神 - Means 'the people of Hui Mo worship the tiger as a god,' indicating a cultural practice.
    • 永初中 - Indicates the beginning of the Yongchun period of Emperor An of Eastern Jin (402 AD).
    • 邵都梁冯恭 - Refers to a person named Feng Gong from Shao Duliang (a place in ancient China).
    • 醉卧于山路 - Means 'drunk and lying down on a mountain road,' indicating a state of inebriation.
    • 夜有虎来 - Means 'a tiger came at night,' indicating a nocturnal encounter.
    • 以头枕其背 - Means 'placed its head on his back,' indicating the tiger's position.
    • 中宵辗转 - Means 'turned restlessly in the middle of the night,' indicating a disturbed sleep.
    • 以手搏之 - Means 'struck it with his hand,' indicating a defensive action.
    • 复大寝 - Means 'fell into a deep sleep again,' indicating a return to sleep.
    • 向晓始醒 - Means 'woke up just before dawn,' indicating the time of waking.
    • 若有宿命 - Means 'as if by predestined fate,' indicating a sense of inevitability.
    • 非智力所及也 - Means 'beyond the reach of intelligence or skill,' indicating the inexplicable nature of the event.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋太元元年 - The first year of the Taiyuan era of Emperor Xian of Jin (376 AD).
    • 晋太元末 - The end of the Taiyuan era of Emperor Xian of Jin (around 397 AD).
    • 永初中 - The beginning of the Yongchun era of Emperor An of Eastern Jin (402 AD).
    • 扶南王范常 - Refers to the ruler of Funan (an ancient kingdom in Southeast Asia) named Fan.
    • 秽貊之人 - Refers to the people of Hui Mo (an ancient ethnic group in Northeast China).
    • 祭虎为神 - Indicates a cultural practice of worshipping the tiger as a deity.

    Proper Nouns

    • 刘广雅 - A person's name.
    • 徐桓 - A person's name.
    • 邵都梁冯恭 - A person's name.
    • 扶南王范常 - A person's name and title.
    • 竹里亭 - A place name.
    • 永初中 - A place name.
    • 扶南 - A place name.
    • 秽貊 - A place name.

    Dongguan Liu Guangya, during the first year of the Taiyuan era of the Jin dynasty (376 AD), served as an assistant to the capital governor. He was sent on a mission back to the capital, passing through Zhuliteng, where he stayed at a checkpoint. This checkpoint was known for its many tigers. Liu took extreme precautions to protect himself, tying his horse in front of the door and holding his halberd spread out on the ground. In the middle of the night, he slept with the common people. A tiger took advantage of the opportunity to jump in, leaping over people and animals, and took Liu alone and left.

    At the end of the Taiyuan era of the Jin dynasty (around 397 AD), Xu Huan went out during the Taiyuan period and vaguely saw a woman. They flirted and teased each other, and the woman invited Huan into the grass. Huan was pleased by her appearance and followed her. Suddenly, the woman transformed into a tiger and carried Huan on its back, heading directly toward the deep mountains. His family searched for him and only found tiger tracks. After about ten days, the tiger dropped Huan off outside the door.

    The ruler of Funan, named Fan, usually kept five or six tigers and ten crocodiles. If there was a dispute and it was unknown who was right or wrong, they would throw the case to the fish or tiger. If the fish or tiger did not eat it, then the person was considered to be in the right. The people of Hui Mo worship the tiger as a god, which may explain this practice.

    During the beginning of the Yongchun era of the Eastern Jin dynasty (402 AD), Feng Gong from Shao Duliang was drunk and lying down on a mountain road. A tiger came at night and placed its head on his back. Feng turned restlessly in the middle of the night, striking the tiger with his hand, and then fell into a deep sleep again. He woke up just before dawn and still saw the tiger crouching behind his feet, as if by predestined fate, beyond the reach of intelligence or skill. [14]

    熊兽藏于山穴，穴里不得见秽及伤残，见则舍穴外死人。欲捕者便令一人卧其藏内，余伴执杖隐在岩侧，熊辄共兴出人，不致伤损，傍人仍得聘其矛。
    　　熊无穴，或居大树孔中。东土呼熊为子路，以物击树，云：“子路可起。”于是便下，不呼则不动也。
    　　元嘉初，青州刘幡射得一鹿，剖腹藏，以草塞之，蹶然起走。幡从而拔塞，须臾复还倒。如此三焉，幡密求此种类，治伤痍多愈。
    　　始兴郡阳山县有人行田，忽遇一象，以鼻卷之，遥入深山。见一象脚有巨刺，此人牵挽得出，病者即起，相与躅陆，状若欢喜。前象复载人就一污湿地，以鼻掘出数条长牙，送还本处。彼境田稼常为象所困，其象俗呼为大客，因语云：“我田稼在此，恒为大客所犯。若念我者，勿复见侵。”便见踯躅如有驯解，于是一家业田绝无其患。 [15]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [15]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 熊獸藏於山穴 - Literal description of bears hiding in mountain caves.
    • 穴里不得見秽及伤残 - The cave must be clean and free from damage.
    • 見則舍穴外死人 - If a bear sees a dead body, it will leave the cave.
    • 欲捕者便令一人卧其藏内 - Method of capturing bears by having someone lie in the cave.
    • 余伴执杖隐在岩侧 - Companions hide with sticks near the rock.
    • 熊辄共兴出人，不致伤损 - The bear will come out and not cause harm.
    • 傍人仍得聘其矛 - Companions can then use their spears.
    • 无穴，或居大树孔中 - Bears sometimes live in tree holes.
    • 东土呼熊为子路 - In the eastern regions, bears are called 'Zilu'.
    • 以物击树，云：“子路可起。” - Strike the tree and say, 'Zilu, get up'.
    • 于是便下，不呼则不动也 - The bear will come down if called but not otherwise.
    • 元嘉初，青州刘幡射得一鹿 - In the early Yuanjia period, Liu Fan in Qingzhou shot a deer.
    • 剖腹藏，以草塞之 - He cut open the deer's belly and stuffed it with grass.
    • 蹶然起走 - The deer suddenly stood up and ran.
    • 幡从而拔塞，须臾复还倒 - Liu Fan followed and pulled out the grass, and the deer returned and fell down.
    • 如此三焉 - This happened three times.
    • 幡密求此种类，治伤痍多愈 - Liu Fan secretly sought out this kind and found that many injuries were healed.
    • 始兴郡阳山县有人行田 - In Yangshan County of Shixing Prefecture, someone was working in the fields.
    • 忽遇一象 - Suddenly, he met an elephant.
    • 以鼻卷之，遥入深山 - The elephant picked him up with its trunk and took him far into the mountains.
    • 见一象脚有巨刺 - He saw that the elephant's foot had a large thorn.
    • 此人牵挽得出 - He pulled the thorn out.
    • 病者即起，相与躅陆，状若欢喜 - The sick elephant stood up, and they walked together, appearing happy.
    • 前象复载人就一污湿地 - The elephant carried him to a muddy place.
    • 以鼻掘出数条长牙 - It used its trunk to dig out several long tusks.
    • 送还本处 - It returned them to their original place.
    • 彼境田稼常为象所困 - In that area, crops were often damaged by elephants.
    • 其象俗呼为大客 - Elephants were commonly called 'Big Guest'.
    • 因语云：“我田稼在此，恒为大客所犯。若念我者，勿复见侵。” - He said, 'My crops are here, and they are often damaged by Big Guest. If you remember me, do not invade them again.'
    • 便见踯躅如有驯解 - The elephant then walked as if tamed.
    • 于是一家业田绝无其患 - From then on, there was no more trouble with the elephant.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Liu Song, a period in the Southern Dynasties.
    • 青州 - A region in northern China.
    • 始兴郡阳山县 - A specific location in the Lingnan region.
    • 子路 - A historical figure, a disciple of Confucius, used here as a nickname for bears in the eastern regions.
    • 大客 - A colloquial term for elephants, possibly indicating a cultural perception of elephants as guests or visitors.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Liu Song.
    • 青州 - A region in northern China.
    • 始兴郡阳山县 - A specific location in the Lingnan region.
    • 子路 - A historical figure, a disciple of Confucius.
    • 大客 - A colloquial term for elephants.

    Bears hide in mountain caves, and the caves must be clean and free from damage. If a bear sees a dead body, it will leave the cave. To capture a bear, one person lies inside the cave while the others hide with sticks near the rock. The bear will come out and not cause harm, allowing the companions to use their spears.

    Bears sometimes live in tree holes. In the eastern regions, bears are called "Zilu." By striking the tree and saying, "Zilu, get up," the bear will come down if called but not otherwise.

    In the early Yuanjia period, Liu Fan in Qingzhou shot a deer, cut open its belly, and stuffed it with grass. The deer suddenly stood up and ran. Liu Fan followed and pulled out the grass, and the deer returned and fell down. This happened three times. Liu Fan secretly sought out this kind and found that many injuries were healed.

    In Yangshan County of Shixing Prefecture, someone was working in the fields and suddenly met an elephant. The elephant picked him up with its trunk and took him far into the mountains. He saw that the elephant's foot had a large thorn and pulled it out. The sick elephant stood up, and they walked together, appearing happy. The elephant then carried him to a muddy place, used its trunk to dig out several long tusks, and returned them to their original place. In that area, crops were often damaged by elephants, which were commonly called "Big Guest." He said, "My crops are here, and they are often damaged by Big Guest. If you remember me, do not invade them again." The elephant then walked as if tamed, and from then on, there was no more trouble with the elephant. [15]

    晋义熙十三年，余为长沙景王骠骑参军，在西州得一黄牛，时将货之，便昼夜衔草不食，淹泪瘦瘠。
    　　苻坚为慕容冲所袭，坚驰马堕而落涧，追兵几及。计无由出，马即踟蹰临涧，垂缰与坚。坚不能及，马又跪而受焉。坚援之，得登岸而走庐江。
    　　晋隆安初，东海何澹之屡入关中。后还，得一犬，壮大非常，每出入辄已知处。澹之后抱疾，犬亦疾，寻及于亡。
    　　楚王与群臣猎于梦，纵良犬逐狡兔，三日而获。之其肠似铁，良工曰：“可以为剑。”
    　　西域有鼠王国，鼠之大者如狗，中者如兔，小者如常。大鼠头悉已白，然带金环枷。商估：有经过其国，不先祈祀者，则啮人衣裳也。得沙门咒愿，更获无他。释道安昔至西方，亲见如此。俗谚云：鼠得死人目睛则为王。 [16]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [16]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋义熙十三年 - Refers to the 13th year of the Yongxi era of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, a specific date in Chinese history.
    • 余为长沙景王骠骑参军 - Means 'I was the military advisor (骠骑参军) to Prince Jing of Changsha,' a formal title indicating a high-ranking position.
    • 得一黄牛 - Literally 'got a yellow ox,' but in this context, it refers to acquiring a yellow ox.
    • 便昼夜衔草不食 - Means 'it would chew grass all day and night without eating,' describing the ox's unusual behavior.
    • 淹泪瘦瘠 - Means 'it became thin and emaciated from shedding tears,' a metaphorical expression indicating the ox's emotional distress.
    • 苻坚为慕容冲所袭 - Means 'Fu Jian was attacked by Murong Chong,' referring to a historical event.
    • 坚驰马堕而落涧 - Means 'Fu Jian was thrown off his horse and fell into a ravine,' describing the physical action.
    • 追兵几及 - Means 'the pursuing troops almost caught up with him,' indicating the urgency of the situation.
    • 计无由出 - Means 'there was no way out,' describing Fu Jian's predicament.
    • 马即踟蹰临涧，垂缰与坚 - Means 'the horse hesitated at the edge of the ravine, lowering its reins for Fu Jian,' showing the horse's loyalty.
    • 坚不能及，马又跪而受焉 - Means 'Fu Jian could not reach it, and the horse knelt to accept him,' further emphasizing the horse's assistance.
    • 坚援之，得登岸而走庐江 - Means 'Fu Jian grabbed the reins, climbed to the shore, and fled to Lujiang,' describing the resolution of the situation.
    • 晋隆安初，东海何澹之屡入关中 - Means 'In the early years of the Long'an era, He Danzhi of Donghai frequently entered Guanzhong,' providing a historical context.
    • 后还，得一犬 - Means 'After returning, he got a dog,' describing the acquisition of a dog.
    • 壮大非常 - Means 'it was unusually large and strong,' describing the dog's physical attributes.
    • 每出入辄已知处 - Means 'whenever it went out or came in, it always knew where to go,' indicating the dog's intelligence.
    • 澹之后抱疾，犬亦疾 - Means 'He Danzhi later fell ill, and the dog also fell ill,' describing a parallel illness.
    • 寻及于亡 - Means 'soon after, he died,' indicating the outcome.
    • 楚王与群臣猎于梦 - Means 'The King of Chu and his ministers hunted in the clouds of Meng,' a poetic description of a hunting trip.
    • 纵良犬逐狡兔 - Means 'they released a good dog to chase a cunning rabbit,' describing the hunting activity.
    • 三日而获 - Means 'they caught it after three days,' indicating the duration of the hunt.
    • 之其肠似铁 - Means 'its intestines were as hard as iron,' describing the rabbit's unusual physical characteristic.
    • 良工曰：‘可以为剑。’ - Means 'a skilled craftsman said, 'It can be used to make a sword,''' indicating the potential use of the rabbit's intestines.
    • 西域有鼠王国 - Means 'In the Western Regions, there is a kingdom of mice,' introducing a fantastical setting.
    • 鼠之大者如狗，中者如兔，小者如常 - Means 'the largest mice are as big as dogs, the medium-sized ones as big as rabbits, and the smallest ones as usual,' describing the sizes of the mice.
    • 大鼠头悉已白，然带金环枷 - Means 'the largest mice have completely white heads, but wear golden rings and shackles,' describing the appearance of the largest mice.
    • 商估：有经过其国，不先祈祀者，则啮人衣裳也 - Means 'Merchants: if anyone passes through their country without first offering sacrifices, the mice will bite their clothes,' describing the superstition.
    • 得沙门咒愿，更获无他 - Means 'If they receive a monk's blessing, they will not suffer any harm,' indicating a protective measure.
    • 释道安昔至西方，亲见如此 - Means 'The monk Dao'an once went to the West and personally saw this,' providing a historical reference.
    • 俗谚云：鼠得死人目睛则为王 - Means 'A folk saying goes: 'If a mouse gets a dead person's eyeball, it becomes a king,''' describing a local belief.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋义熙十三年 - Refers to the 13th year of the Yongxi era of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (417 AD).
    • 长沙景王 - Refers to Prince Jing of Changsha, a member of the Jin imperial family.
    • 骠骑参军 - A high-ranking military advisor in the Jin dynasty.
    • 苻坚 - A prominent ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 慕容冲 - A prince of the Later Yan dynasty who rebelled against Fu Jian.
    • 卢江 - A place name in the Jin dynasty, likely referring to a region in modern-day Anhui province.
    • 晋隆安初 - Refers to the early years of the Long'an era of Emperor An of Jin (397-399 AD).
    • 东海何澹之 - A historical figure from Donghai (modern-day Lianyungang, Jiangsu province).
    • 关中 - Refers to the Guanzhong region, a historical area in Shaanxi province.
    • 楚王 - Refers to the King of Chu, a title used during the Warring States period.
    • 云梦 - A region in Hubei province known for its hunting grounds.
    • 西域 - Refers to the Western Regions, a term used to describe the areas west of the Hexi Corridor.
    • 鼠王国 - A fantastical kingdom of mice, likely a mythological or folkloric reference.
    • 释道安 - A famous Buddhist monk who traveled to India and brought back Buddhist scriptures.
    • 西方 - Refers to the West, likely India in this context.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋义熙十三年 - The 13th year of the Yongxi era of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 长沙景王 - Prince Jing of Changsha.
    • 骠骑参军 - A high-ranking military advisor.
    • 苻坚 - A ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 慕容冲 - A prince of the Later Yan dynasty.
    • 卢江 - A place name in the Jin dynasty.
    • 晋隆安初 - The early years of the Long'an era of Emperor An of Jin.
    • 东海何澹之 - A historical figure from Donghai.
    • 关中 - The Guanzhong region.
    • 楚王 - The King of Chu.
    • 云梦 - A region in Hubei province.
    • 西域 - The Western Regions.
    • 鼠王国 - A fantastical kingdom of mice.
    • 释道安 - A famous Buddhist monk.
    • 西方 - The West, likely India in this context.

    In the 13th year of the Yongxi era of the Jin dynasty, I served as the military advisor to Prince Jing of Changsha. While stationed in the Western Capital, I acquired a yellow ox. At the time, I intended to sell it, but the ox would chew grass all day and night without eating, becoming thin and emaciated from shedding tears.

    Fu Jian, the ruler of the Former Qin, was attacked by Murong Chong. Fu Jian was thrown off his horse and fell into a ravine, with pursuing troops almost catching up with him. With no way out, the horse hesitated at the edge of the ravine, lowering its reins for Fu Jian. Fu Jian could not reach it, and the horse knelt to accept him. Fu Jian grabbed the reins, climbed to the shore, and fled to Lujiang.

    In the early years of the Long'an era of the Jin dynasty, He Danzhi of Donghai frequently entered Guanzhong. After returning, he got a dog that was unusually large and strong. Whenever the dog went out or came in, it always knew where to go. He Danzhi later fell ill, and the dog also fell ill, soon after which he died.

    The King of Chu and his ministers hunted in the clouds of Meng. They released a good dog to chase a cunning rabbit, and after three days, they caught it. Its intestines were as hard as iron, and a skilled craftsman said, "It can be used to make a sword."

    In the Western Regions, there is a kingdom of mice. The largest mice are as big as dogs, the medium-sized ones as big as rabbits, and the smallest ones as usual. The largest mice have completely white heads, but wear golden rings and shackles. Merchants: if anyone passes through their country without first offering sacrifices, the mice will bite their clothes. If they receive a monk's blessing, they will not suffer any harm. The monk Dao'an once went to the West and personally saw this. A folk saying goes: "If a mouse gets a dead person's eyeball, it becomes a king." [16]

    拱鼠形如常鼠，行田野中，见人即拱手而立，人近欲搏之，跳跃而去。泰川有之。
    　　义鼠形如鼠，短尾，每行递相咬尾。三五为群，惊之则散，俗云见之者当有吉兆。成都有之。
    　　唐鼠形如鼠，稍长，青黑色，腹边有余物如肠，时亦污落，亦名易肠鼠。昔仙人唐拔宅升天，鸡犬皆去，唯鼠坠下死，而肠出数寸，三年易之，俗呼为唐鼠。城固川中有之。
    　　前废帝景和中，东阳大水，永康蔡喜夫避雨南陇，夜有大鼠，形如犭屯子，浮水而来，径伏喜夫奴床角。怒愍而不犯，每食辄以余饭与之。水势既退，喜夫得返故居，鼠以前脚捧青囊，囊有三寸许珠，留置奴床前，啾啾状如欲语。从此去来不绝，亦能隐形，又知人祸福。后同县吕庆祖牵狗野猎暂过，遂啮杀之。 [17]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [17]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 拱鼠 - A type of mouse that behaves uniquely, characterized by its gesture of bowing when it encounters people.
    • 行田野中 - Means 'walking in the fields,' indicating the mouse's habitat.
    • 見人即拱手而立 - Describes the mouse's behavior of bowing when it sees a person.
    • 人近欲搏之，跳跃而去 - Means 'when people approach to catch it, it jumps away,' describing the mouse's reaction to being approached.
    • 義鼠 - A type of mouse considered to bring good fortune.
    • 短尾 - Means 'short-tailed,' describing a physical characteristic of the mouse.
    • 每行递相咬尾 - Means 'each time they walk, they bite each other's tails,' indicating a peculiar behavior.
    • 三五为群 - Means 'three or five in a group,' describing the social structure of these mice.
    • 惊之则散 - Means 'when startled, they scatter,' indicating their reaction to being frightened.
    • 俗云见之者当有吉兆 - Means 'it is said that seeing them brings good omens,' indicating the cultural belief associated with these mice.
    • 唐鼠 - A type of mouse named after the legendary figure Tang, known for its unique appearance and behavior.
    • 稍长 - Means 'slightly longer,' describing a physical characteristic of the mouse.
    • 青黑色 - Means 'dark blue-black,' describing the color of the mouse.
    • 腹边有余物如肠，时亦污落 - Means 'there is an extra substance on its abdomen like intestines, sometimes falling off,' indicating a unique physical feature.
    • 亦名易肠鼠 - Means 'also called the changing-intestine mouse,' indicating another name for this type of mouse.
    • 昔仙人唐拔宅升天，鸡犬皆去，唯鼠坠下死，而肠出数寸，三年易之 - A reference to the legend of the immortal Tang, where his house was lifted to heaven, and only a mouse fell and died, with its intestines protruding and changing every three years.
    • 成都有之 - Means 'there are such mice in Chengdu,' indicating the geographical location where these mice are found.
    • 前废帝景和中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Jianhe of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 东阳大水 - Means 'a great flood in Dongyang,' indicating a historical event.
    • 永康蔡喜夫避雨南陇 - Means 'Cai Xifu from Yongkang took shelter from the rain in the southern hills,' indicating a specific event and location.
    • 夜有大鼠，形如犭屯子，浮水而来 - Means 'at night, a large mouse, shaped like a wildcat, came floating on the water,' describing the appearance and behavior of the mouse.
    • 径伏喜夫奴床角 - Means 'it lay down at the corner of Cai Xifu's servant's bed,' indicating the mouse's location.
    • 怒愍而不犯 - Means 'it was angry and pitiful but did not attack,' describing the mouse's behavior.
    • 每食辄以余饭与之 - Means 'each time Cai Xifu ate, he gave the leftover food to the mouse,' indicating the mouse's interaction with Cai Xifu.
    • 水势既退，喜夫得返故居 - Means 'after the floodwaters receded, Cai Xifu returned to his old home,' indicating the outcome of the flood.
    • 鼠以前脚捧青囊，囊有三寸许珠 - Means 'the mouse held an emerald bag with its front paws, and the bag contained pearls about three inches in size,' describing the mouse's actions.
    • 留置奴床前，啾啾状如欲语 - Means 'it left the bag at the servant's bed and made a sound like it wanted to speak,' indicating the mouse's behavior.
    • 从此去来不绝 - Means 'from then on, it came and went without interruption,' indicating the mouse's continued presence.
    • 亦能隐形 - Means 'it could also become invisible,' indicating a supernatural ability.
    • 又知人祸福 - Means 'it also knew people's fortunes and misfortunes,' indicating the mouse's supernatural knowledge.
    • 后同县吕庆祖牵狗野猎暂过 - Means 'later, Lü Qingzu from the same county, who was hunting in the wild with a dog, passed by,' indicating a subsequent event.
    • 遂啮杀之 - Means 'the dog bit and killed it,' indicating the outcome of the encounter.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 拱鼠 - A type of mouse with unique behavioral characteristics, often associated with folklore.
    • 義鼠 - A type of mouse believed to bring good fortune, reflecting traditional beliefs in auspicious animals.
    • 唐鼠 - Named after the legendary figure Tang, who is said to have had a mouse that exhibited extraordinary behavior, linking the mouse to a famous legend.
    • 前废帝景和中 - Refers to the reign of Emperor Jianhe, a specific period in Chinese history.
    • 东阳大水 - A historical event that occurred during the reign of Emperor Jianhe, involving a significant flood in Dongyang.
    • 成都有之 - Indicates that these types of mice are found in Chengdu, a city with a rich cultural and historical background.

    Proper Nouns

    • 拱鼠 - A type of mouse with unique behavioral characteristics.
    • 義鼠 - A type of mouse believed to bring good fortune.
    • 唐鼠 - A type of mouse named after the legendary figure Tang.
    • 前废帝 - Refers to Emperor Jianhe of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 景和 - The era name of Emperor Jianhe.
    • 东阳 - A place name, referring to the region where the flood occurred.
    • 永康 - A place name, referring to the hometown of Cai Xifu.
    • 蔡喜夫 - A person, the main character in the story.
    • 吕庆祖 - A person, the hunter who encountered the mouse.

    The Arch Mouse resembles a common mouse in shape, walking through the fields. When it sees people, it bows and stands still. When people approach to catch it, it jumps away. There are such mice in Taichuan.

    The Good Fortune Mouse resembles a mouse, with a short tail. Each time they walk, they bite each other's tails. They gather in groups of three or five, and when startled, they scatter. It is said that seeing them brings good omens. There are such mice in Chengdu.

    The Tang Mouse resembles a mouse, slightly longer, dark blue-black in color, with an extra substance on its abdomen like intestines, sometimes falling off. It is also called the Changing-Intestine Mouse. In ancient times, the immortal Tang had his house lifted to heaven, and all his chickens and dogs went with him, except for a mouse that fell and died, with its intestines protruding and changing every three years. This is why it is called the Tang Mouse. There are such mice in Chengdu and Chenggu.

    During the reign of Emperor Jianhe, a great flood occurred in Dongyang. Cai Xifu from Yongkang took shelter from the rain in the southern hills. At night, a large mouse, shaped like a wildcat, came floating on the water and lay down at the corner of Cai Xifu's servant's bed. It was angry and pitiful but did not attack. Each time Cai Xifu ate, he gave the leftover food to the mouse. After the floodwaters receded, Cai Xifu returned to his old home. The mouse held an emerald bag with its front paws, and the bag contained pearls about three inches in size. It left the bag at the servant's bed and made a sound like it wanted to speak. From then on, it came and went without interruption, and it could also become invisible. It also knew people's fortunes and misfortunes. Later, Lü Qingzu from the same county, who was hunting in the wild with a dog, passed by, and the dog bit and killed it. [17]

    貂出句丽国，常有一物共居穴。或见之，形貌类人，长三尺，能制貂，爱乐刀子。其俗：人欲得貂皮，以刀投穴口。此物夜出穴，置皮刀边，须人持皮去，乃敢取刀。
    　　吴孙皓时，临海得毛人。《山海经》云：山精如人而有毛，此蒋山精也，故《抱朴子》曰：山之精，形如小儿而独足，足向后，喜来犯人，其名曰支。知而呼之，即当自却耳。一名曰超空，可燕呼之。又或如鼓，赤色，一足，其名曰浑。又或如人，长九尺，衣裘戴笠，名曰金累。又或如龙，有五色赤角，名曰飞龙。见之皆可呼其名，不敢为害。《玄中记》山精如人，一足长三四尺，食山蟹，夜出昼藏。 [18]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [18]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 貂出句麗國 - Literal meaning 'sable fur comes from the country of Goguryeo.'
    • 常有一物共居穴 - Literal meaning 'there is always an animal that lives in the same cave with it.'
    • 或見之，形貌類人，長三尺 - Literal meaning 'sometimes one sees it, its appearance resembles a human, and it is three feet long.'
    • 能制貂，愛樂刀子 - Literal meaning 'it can control the sable, and it loves knives.'
    • 其俗：人欲得貂皮，以刀投穴口 - Literal meaning 'the custom is: when people want sable fur, they throw a knife at the cave entrance.'
    • 此物夜出穴，置皮刀邊，須人持皮去，乃敢取刀 - Literal meaning 'this creature comes out of the cave at night, places the fur next to the knife, waits for someone to take the fur away, and then dares to take the knife.'
    • 吳孫皓時，臨海得毛人 - Literal meaning 'during the reign of Sun Hao of Wu, a hairy person was found by the sea.'
    • 《山海經》云 - Literal meaning 'The *Classic of Mountains and Seas* says.'
    • 山精如人而有毛 - Literal meaning 'the mountain spirit looks like a human but has hair.'
    • 此蒋山精也 - Literal meaning 'this is the mountain spirit of Mount Jiang.'
    • 故《抱朴子》曰 - Literal meaning 'therefore, *Baopu Zi* says.'
    • 山之精，形如小儿而独足 - Literal meaning 'the mountain spirit has the shape of a child and only one foot.'
    • 足向后，喜来犯人 - Literal meaning 'its foot is backward, and it likes to attack people.'
    • 其名曰支 - Literal meaning 'its name is *Zhi*.'
    • 知而呼之，即当自却耳 - Literal meaning 'if you know its name and call it, it will retreat on its own.'
    • 一名曰超空，可燕呼之 - Literal meaning 'another name is *Chao Kong*, which can be called in a relaxed manner.'
    • 又或如鼓，赤色，一足，其名曰浑 - Literal meaning 'or it might look like a drum, red in color, with one foot, and its name is *Hun*.'
    • 又或如人，长九尺，衣裘戴笠，名曰金累 - Literal meaning 'or it might look like a human, nine feet tall, wearing fur and a hat, and its name is *Jinlei*.'
    • 又或如龙，有五色赤角，名曰飞龙 - Literal meaning 'or it might look like a dragon, with five colors and red horns, and its name is *Feilong*.'
    • 见之皆可呼其名，不敢为害 - Literal meaning 'when you see them, you can call their names, and they will not harm you.'
    • 《玄中记》山精如人，一足长三四尺，食山蟹，夜出昼藏 - Literal meaning 'The *Xuanzhong Ji* says the mountain spirit looks like a human, has one foot, is three to four feet long, eats mountain crabs, comes out at night, and hides during the day.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 句麗國 - Refers to Goguryeo, an ancient Korean kingdom.
    • 吳孫皓 - Refers to Sun Hao, the last emperor of the Wu state during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 《山海經》 - Refers to the *Classic of Mountains and Seas*, an ancient Chinese text containing mythological and geographical information.
    • 《抱朴子》 - Refers to *Baopu Zi*, a text on Taoist philosophy and alchemy.
    • 《玄中记》 - Refers to *Xuanzhong Ji*, another ancient Chinese text on mythology and geography.
    • 山精 - Refers to mountain spirits or supernatural beings associated with mountains.
    • 支 - A specific name for a type of mountain spirit.
    • 超空 - Another name for a type of mountain spirit.
    • 浑 - A specific name for a type of mountain spirit.
    • 金累 - A specific name for a type of mountain spirit.
    • 飞龙 - A specific name for a type of mountain spirit.

    Proper Nouns

    • 句麗國 - Goguryeo.
    • 吳孫皓 - Sun Hao.
    • 《山海經》 - *Classic of Mountains and Seas*.
    • 《抱朴子》 - *Baopu Zi*.
    • 《玄中记》 - *Xuanzhong Ji*.
    • 支 - *Zhi*.
    • 超空 - *Chao Kong*.
    • 浑 - *Hun*.
    • 金累 - *Jinlei*.
    • 飞龙 - *Feilong*.

    Sables come from the country of Goguryeo, and there is always an animal that lives in the same cave with them. Sometimes one sees it, its appearance resembling a human, and it is three feet long. It can control the sable and loves knives. The custom is: when people want sable fur, they throw a knife at the cave entrance. This creature comes out of the cave at night, places the fur next to the knife, waits for someone to take the fur away, and then dares to take the knife.

    During the reign of Sun Hao of Wu, a hairy person was found by the sea. The Classic of Mountains and Seas says: the mountain spirit looks like a human but has hair. This is the mountain spirit of Mount Jiang. Therefore, Baopu Zi says: the mountain spirit has the shape of a child and only one foot. Its foot is backward, and it likes to attack people. Its name is Zhi. If you know its name and call it, it will retreat on its own. Another name is Chao Kong, which can be called in a relaxed manner. Or it might look like a drum, red in color, with one foot, and its name is Hun. Or it might look like a human, nine feet tall, wearing fur and a hat, and its name is Jinlei. Or it might look like a dragon, with five colors and red horns, and its name is Feilong. When you see them, you can call their names, and they will not harm you. The Xuanzhong Ji says the mountain spirit looks like a human, has one foot, is three to four feet long, eats mountain crabs, comes out at night, and hides during the day. [18]

    陆机尝饷张华。于时宾客满座，华发器便曰：“此龙肉也。”众未之信，华曰：“试以苦酒濯之，必有异。”既而五色光起，机还问主，果云园中茅积下得一鱼，质状非常，乃以作，过美，故以相献。
    　　张永家地有泉出，小龙在焉。从此遂为富室。逾年，因雨腾跃而去，于是生赀，日不暇给。俗说云与龙共居，不知神龙效矣。
    　　晋太元中，东阳西寺七佛屋龛下有一物出，头如鹿，有法献道人迫而观之，于是吐沫喷洒，气若雾。至元嘉十四年四月七日，此头复出，寻觅其处，亦无孔穴，年年有声，殷若小雷。
    　　晋义熙中，江陵赵姥以酤酒为业，居室内地忽自隆起，姥察为异，朝夕以酒酹之。尝有一物出，头似驴而地初无孔穴。及姥死，邻人闻土下有声如哭。后人掘地，见一异物，蠢蠢而动，不测大小，须臾失之，俗谓之土龙。 [19]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [19]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 陸機嘗饋張華 - Lu Ji once presented a dish to Zhang Hua. The term '饋' indicates a formal offering, while '' refers to a specific type of dish.
    • 此龍肉也 - This is dragon meat. An idiomatic expression used to describe something extraordinary or valuable.
    • 試以苦酒濯之 - Try washing it with bitter wine. A ritualistic or testing procedure, where '苦酒' (bitter wine) is used to reveal the true nature of the dish.
    • 五色光起 - Five-colored light arose. Describes a supernatural or magical phenomenon.
    • 質狀非常 - Its appearance was very unusual. Indicates that the fish had an extraordinary or supernatural appearance.
    • 逾年 - After a year. Indicates a significant passage of time.
    • 殷若小雷 - Deep like a small thunder. A vivid description of a sound that is both deep and resonant.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陸機 - Lu Ji, a famous literary figure of the Western Jin dynasty, known for his literary and political achievements.
    • 張華 - Zhang Hua, a prominent statesman and scholar of the Western Jin dynasty, known for his wisdom and literary talent.
    • 五經 - The Five Classics of Confucianism: the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), and *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals). These are foundational texts in Chinese culture.
    • 龍肉 - In Chinese folklore, dragon meat is often associated with extraordinary power or value.
    • 苦酒 - Bitter wine, often used in rituals or as a test to reveal the true nature of something.
    • 小雷 - A metaphorical description of a sound that is both deep and resonant, often used to describe supernatural or extraordinary phenomena.
    • 土龍 - A mythical creature that emerges from the earth, often associated with fertility or supernatural power.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陸機 - Lu Ji, a famous literary figure of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 張華 - Zhang Hua, a prominent statesman and scholar of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 五經 - The Five Classics of Confucianism: the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), and *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals).

    Lu Ji once presented a dish to Zhang Hua. At that time, guests filled the room, and upon seeing the dish, Zhang Hua remarked, "This is dragon meat." The guests did not believe him, so Zhang Hua said, "Try washing it with bitter wine, and there will certainly be something unusual." Soon after, five-colored light arose. Lu Ji then asked the owner of the dish, who confirmed that it was made from a fish found under a pile of straw in the garden, which had an extraordinary appearance. The fish was so exquisite that it was offered to Zhang Hua.

    In the household of Zhang Yong, a spring emerged from the ground, and a small dragon was found there. From then on, the family became wealthy. After a year, during a rainstorm, the dragon leapt and vanished, and the family's wealth diminished rapidly. Folklore says that living with a dragon brings prosperity, but it is unknown if this was due to the influence of the divine dragon.

    During the Taiyuan period of the Jin dynasty, a creature emerged from beneath the shrine of the Seven Buddhas in the West Temple of Dongyang. It had a head like a deer. A monk named Fajian approached to observe it, and the creature spat out foam and mist. On the 7th day of the 4th month of the 14th year of the Yuangang era, the head reappeared. Upon searching the area, there were no visible holes or cavities. Year after year, a deep sound like a small thunder could be heard.

    During the Yixi period of the Jin dynasty, an old woman named Zhao from Jiangling made a living selling wine. One day, the ground inside her home suddenly rose, and she considered it an omen. She began offering wine to the ground daily. A creature emerged, with a head like a donkey, and there were no visible holes or cavities in the ground. After the old woman died, neighbors heard sounds like crying from beneath the ground. Later, when people dug up the area, they found an unusual creature that moved sluggishly, its size unknown. It disappeared shortly after, and people called it an earth dragon. [19]

    赵牙行船于阖庐，见水际有大槎，人牵不动。牙往举得之，以着船，船破，槎变为龙，浮水而去。
    　　永阳人李增行经大溪，见二蛟浮于水上，发矢射之，一蛟中焉。增归，因复出，市有女子，素服衔泪，持所射箭。增怪而问焉，女答曰：“何用问焉！为暴若是。”便以相还授矢而灭。增恶而骤走，未达家，暴死于路。
    　　荆州上明浦沔水隈，潭极深，常有蛟杀人，浴汲死者不脱岁。升平中，陈郡邓遐字应遥为襄阳太守，素勇健，愤而入水，觅蛟得之，便举拳曳着岸，欲斫杀。母语云：“蛟是神物，宁忍杀之？今可咒，令勿复为患。”遐咒而放焉。自兹迄今，遂无此患。一云遐拔剑入水，蛟绕其足。遐自挥剑，截蛟数段，流血水丹，勇冠当时，于后遂无蛟患。 [20]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [20]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 趙牙行船于阖庐 - Describes Zhao Ya navigating a boat in Hélú, likely a place name. Requires context to fully understand its significance.
    • 見水際有大槎 - "見" means 'to see,' "水際" refers to the water's edge, and "大槎" likely refers to a large log or raft. Sets up the scene of discovery.
    • 人牽不動 - "人牽" means 'people pulling,' and "不動" means 'not moving.' Describes the difficulty in moving the large log.
    • 牙往舉得之 - "牙往舉" means 'Zhao Ya went and lifted it,' and "得之" means 'obtained it.' Describes Zhao Ya's action of lifting the log.
    • 以着船 - "以" means 'to use,' "着" means 'to attach,' and "船" means 'boat.' Describes the act of attaching the log to the boat.
    • 船破 - "船破" means 'the boat broke.' Describes the consequence of attaching the log to the boat.
    • 槎變為龍 - "槎變為" means 'the log transformed into,' and "龍" means 'dragon.' Describes the transformation of the log into a dragon.
    • 浮水而去 - "浮水" means 'floating on the water,' and "而去" means 'and went away.' Describes the dragon floating away.
    • 永陽人李增行經大溪 - "永陽人" means 'a person from Yongyang,' "李增" is the name 'Li Zeng,' "行經" means 'traveling through,' and "大溪" means 'a large stream.' Introduces Li Zeng and his journey.
    • 見二蛟浮於水上 - "見" means 'to see,' "二蛟" means 'two dragons,' and "浮於水上" means 'floating on the water.' Describes Li Zeng seeing two dragons on the water.
    • 發矢射之 - "發矢" means 'to shoot an arrow,' and "射之" means 'at it.' Describes Li Zeng shooting an arrow at one of the dragons.
    • 一蛟中焉 - "一蛟" means 'one dragon,' "中焉" means 'was hit.' Describes the dragon being hit by the arrow.
    • 增歸，因復出 - "增歸" means 'Li Zeng returned,' "因復出" means 'and then went out again.' Describes Li Zeng's return and subsequent departure.
    • 市有女子，素服含淚 - "市" means 'market,' "有女子" means 'there was a woman,' "素服" means 'wearing plain clothes,' and "含淚" means 'with tears in her mouth.' Describes a woman in the market wearing plain clothes and crying.
    • 持所射箭 - "持" means 'holding,' "所射箭" means 'the arrow that was shot.' Describes the woman holding the arrow.
    • 增怪而問焉 - "增怪" means 'Li Zeng was surprised,' "而問焉" means 'and asked her.' Describes Li Zeng's surprise and his question to the woman.
    • 女答曰 - "女答" means 'the woman answered,' "曰" means 'said.' Introduces the woman's response.
    • 何用問焉 - "何用" means 'why bother,' "問焉" means 'to ask her.' The woman's response, indicating that Li Zeng's question is unnecessary.
    • 為暴若是 - "為暴" means 'to be violent,' "若是" means 'like this.' Describes the woman's statement about the dragon's violence.
    • 便以相還授矢而滅 - "便以" means 'then with,' "相還" means 'returned,' "授矢" means 'gave the arrow,' "而滅" means 'and disappeared.' Describes the woman returning the arrow and then disappearing.
    • 增惡而驟走 - "增惡" means 'Li Zeng was disgusted,' "而驟走" means 'and hurriedly ran away.' Describes Li Zeng's reaction and his hasty departure.
    • 未達家，暴死於路 - "未達家" means 'before reaching home,' "暴死" means 'died suddenly,' "於路" means 'on the road.' Describes Li Zeng's sudden death on the road.
    • 荊州上明浦沔水隈 - "荊州" means 'Jingzhou,' "上明浦" means 'Shangming Bay,' "沔水隈" means 'the banks of the Mian River.' Describes the location.
    • 潭極深 - "潭" means 'pool,' "極深" means 'very deep.' Describes the depth of the pool.
    • 常有蛟殺人 - "常有" means 'often has,' "蛟" means 'dragon,' "殺人" means 'killing people.' Describes the frequent occurrence of dragons killing people.
    • 浴汲死者不脫歲 - "浴汲" means 'bathing and drawing water,' "死者" means 'the dead,' "不脫歲" means 'not leaving the year.' Describes the continuous deaths due to the dragons.
    • 升平中 - "升平" means 'Shengping,' likely a reference to the Shengping era, and "中" means 'during.' Sets the time frame.
    • 陳郡鄧遐字應遙為襄陽太守 - "陳郡" means 'Chen Prefecture,' "鄧遐" is the name 'Deng Xia,' "字應遙" means 'with the courtesy name Yingyao,' "為" means 'as,' "襄陽太守" means 'Governor of Xiangyang.' Introduces Deng Xia and his position.
    • 素勇健 - "素" means 'always,' "勇健" means 'brave and strong.' Describes Deng Xia's character.
    • 憤而入水 - "憤" means 'angry,' "而入水" means 'and entered the water.' Describes Deng Xia's action of entering the water in anger.
    • 覓蛟得之 - "覓" means 'to seek,' "蛟" means 'dragon,' "得之" means 'found it.' Describes Deng Xia finding the dragon.
    • 便舉拳曳着岸 - "便" means 'then,' "舉拳" means 'lifted his fist,' "曳" means 'dragged,' "着岸" means 'onto the shore.' Describes Deng Xia dragging the dragon onto the shore.
    • 欲斫殺 - "欲" means 'wanted to,' "斫殺" means 'cut and kill.' Describes Deng Xia's intention to kill the dragon.
    • 母語云 - "母語" means 'mother said,' "云" means 'that.' Introduces Deng Xia's mother's words.
    • 蛟是神物 - "蛟" means 'dragon,' "是" means 'is,' "神物" means 'a divine creature.' Describes the dragon as a divine creature.
    • 寧忍殺之 - "寧" means 'would,' "忍" means 'bear,' "殺之" means 'to kill it.' Describes the mother's reluctance to kill the dragon.
    • 今可咒，令勿復為患 - "今" means 'now,' "可咒" means 'can be cursed,' "令" means 'to make,' "勿復為患" means 'not to cause more trouble.' Describes the mother's suggestion to curse the dragon to prevent further harm.
    • 遐咒而放焉 - "遐咒" means 'Deng Xia cursed,' "而放焉" means 'and released it.' Describes Deng Xia cursing the dragon and releasing it.
    • 自茲迄今 - "自茲" means 'since then,' "迄今" means 'until now.' Describes the time frame since the event.
    • 遂無此患 - "遂" means 'then,' "無此患" means 'no longer had this problem.' Describes the resolution of the dragon problem.
    • 一云 - "一云" means 'one says,' indicating an alternative account.
    • 遐拔劍入水 - "遐拔劍" means 'Deng Xia drew his sword,' "入水" means 'into the water.' Describes Deng Xia drawing his sword and entering the water.
    • 蛟繞其足 - "蛟繞" means 'the dragon wrapped around,' "其足" means 'his feet.' Describes the dragon wrapping around Deng Xia's feet.
    • 遐自揮劍 - "遐自" means 'Deng Xia himself,' "揮劍" means 'waved his sword.' Describes Deng Xia waving his sword.
    • 截蛟數段 - "截" means 'cut,' "蛟數段" means 'the dragon into several pieces.' Describes Deng Xia cutting the dragon into several pieces.
    • 流血水丹 - Describes the blood flowing and the water turning red.

    Proper Nouns

    • 趙牙 - Name of a person, likely Zhao Ya.
    • 阖庐 - Place name, likely Hélú.
    • 永陽 - Place name, likely Yongyang.
    • 李增 - Name of a person, likely Li Zeng.
    • 大溪 - Place name, likely a large stream.
    • 荆州 - Place name, likely Jingzhou.
    • 上明浦 - Place name, likely Shangming Bay.
    • 沔水隈 - Place name, likely the banks of the Mian River.
    • 升平 - Era name, likely Shengping.
    • 陳郡 - Place name, likely Chen Prefecture.
    • 鄧遐 - Name of a person, likely Deng Xia.
    • 應遙 - Courtesy name of Deng Xia.
    • 襄陽太守 - Title, likely Governor of Xiangyang.

    Zhao Ya was navigating a boat in Hélú when he saw a large log at the water's edge that people could not pull. Zhao Ya went to lift it and obtained it, attaching it to his boat. The boat broke, and the log transformed into a dragon, floating away on the water.

    A man from Yongyang named Li Zeng was traveling through a large stream when he saw two dragons floating on the water. He shot an arrow at one of them, and it was hit. Li Zeng returned home and then went out again. In the market, there was a woman wearing plain clothes with tears in her mouth, holding the arrow that had been shot. Li Zeng was surprised and asked her. The woman replied, "Why bother asking? It was so violent." She then returned the arrow and disappeared. Li Zeng was disgusted and hurriedly ran away, dying suddenly on the road before reaching home.

    In the vicinity of Shangming Bay in Jingzhou, near the banks of the Mian River, there was a very deep pool that often had dragons killing people, and those who bathed or drew water from it died continuously. During the Shengping era, Deng Xia, a brave and strong man from Chen Prefecture with the courtesy name Yingyao, served as the Governor of Xiangyang. He became angry and entered the water, finding the dragon. He lifted his fist and dragged it onto the shore, intending to cut it up and kill it. His mother said, "The dragon is a divine creature; would you bear to kill it? Now you can curse it, so that it will not cause more trouble." Deng Xia cursed it and released it. Since then, there has been no more trouble from the dragon. Another account says that Deng Xia drew his sword and entered the water, and the dragon wrapped around his feet. Deng Xia himself waved his sword, cutting the dragon into several pieces, with blood turning the water red. His bravery was unparalleled at the time, and after this, there were no more dragon troubles. [20]

    鲁国中牟县蒙山上有寺废久，民欲架屋者，辄大蛇数十丈出来惊人，故莫得安焉。
    　　新野苏卷与妇佃于野舍，每至饮时，辄有一物来，其形似蛇，长七尺五寸，色甚光采。卷异而饷之，遂经数载，产业加厚。妇后密打杀，即得能食病，日进三斛饣卞，犹不为饱，少时而死。
    　　晋中朝武库内封闭甚密。忽有雉ず，时人咸谓为怪。张司空云：“此必蛇之所化耳。”即使搜库中，雉侧果得蛇蜕。
    　　司马︷之字道援，善射雉。太元中，将媒下翳，此媒屡ず，野雉亦应。试令寻觅，所应者头翅已成雉，半身故是蛇。
    　　晋太元中，汝南人入山伐竹，见一竹中蛇形已成，上枝叶如故。又吴郡桐庐人常伐余遗竹，见一竹竿雉头，颈尽就身，犹未变。此亦竹为蛇、蛇为雉也。
    　　丹阳钟忠以元嘉冬月晨行，见有一蛇长二尺许，文色似青，琉璃头，有双角，白如玉。感而畜之，于是赀业日登。经年蛇自亡去。忠及二子相继殒毙。此蛇来吉去凶，其唯龙乎？ [21]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [21]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 廢久 - Abandoned for a long time, indicating the ruinous state of the temple.
    • 架屋者 - Refers to people attempting to build houses on the site.
    • 輒大蛇 - Always a large snake appears, indicating a habitual action.
    • 出來惊人 - To come out and startle people, describing the snake's sudden appearance and its frightening effect.
    • 莫得安焉 - No one can be at peace here, indicating the persistent disturbance caused by the snake.
    • 新野苏卷与妇佃于野舍 - Farming in a rural cottage, describing their occupation and living situation.
    • 每至饮时 - Every time during drinking time, indicating a regular occurrence during meals.
    • 其形似蛇 - Its shape like a snake, describing the appearance of the creature.
    • 卷异而饷之 - Qian found it strange and fed it, indicating Qian's reaction and action.
    • 遂经数载 - Thus through several years, indicating the duration of the unusual occurrence.
    • 產業加厚 - Property or business increased greatly, indicating a significant improvement in their wealth.
    • 婦後密打殺 - The wife later secretly killed, indicating the wife's secret action.
    • 即得能食病 - Immediately got appetite sickness, indicating the immediate onset of illness.
    • 日進三斛饣卞 - Daily intake of three measures of food, indicating the large amount of food consumed.
    • 猶不為飽 - Still not satisfied, indicating the persistent hunger.
    • 少時而死 - Shortly after died, indicating the rapid decline and death.
    • 晉中朝武庫內封閉甚密 - During the Jin dynasty, in the military warehouse, very tightly sealed, indicating the secure storage of weapons.
    • 忽有雉ず - Suddenly appeared, pheasant-like creature, indicating the sudden appearance of an unusual animal.
    • 時人咸謂為怪 - People of the time all called it a strange thing, indicating the widespread perception of the event as unusual.
    • 張司空雲 - Zhang Siguang, a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.
    • 此必蛇之所化耳 - This must be the transformation of a snake, only, indicating Zhang's belief that the pheasant-like creature was a transformed snake.
    • 即使搜庫中 - Immediately ordered to search in the warehouse, indicating the search conducted in the warehouse.
    • 雉側果得蛇蜕 - Near the pheasant-like creature, found indeed, snake skin, indicating the discovery of the snake's shed skin.
    • 司馬︷之字道援 - Sima Dao Yuan, his courtesy name.
    • 善射雉 - Skilled in shooting pheasants, indicating his skill in hunting pheasants.
    • 太元中 - During the era name of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, indicating the time period.
    • 將媒下翳 - Using a decoy, lowering a net, indicating the method of hunting.
    • 此媒屢ず - This decoy frequently appeared, indicating the frequent appearance of the pheasant-like creature.
    • 野雉亦應 - Wild pheasants also responded, indicating the response of wild pheasants to the decoy.
    • 試令尋覓 - Tried to order to search for, indicating the attempt to find the source of the response.
    • 所應者頭翅已成雉 - What responded, head and wings had become a pheasant, indicating the transformation.
    • 半身故是蛇 - Half body was still a snake, indicating the remaining part of the creature was still a snake.
    • 元嘉冬月晨行 - During the era name of Emperor Wen of Liu Song, winter month, morning walk, indicating the time and place of the event.
    • 見有一蛇長二尺許 - Saw a snake about two feet long, indicating the size of the snake.
    • 文色似青 - Patterned color like blue, indicating the snake's appearance.
    • 琉璃頭 - Lapis lazuli or glass-like head, indicating the snake's head.
    • 有雙角 - Has two horns, indicating the snake's features.
    • 白如玉 - White like jade, indicating the snake's color.
    • 感而畜之 - Was moved and kept it, indicating the action of keeping the snake.
    • 於是赀業日登 - Therefore, property and business daily increased, indicating the growth in wealth.
    • 經年蛇自亡去 - For a year, the snake died and left, indicating the snake's death and departure.
    • 忠及二子相繼殞殞 - Zhong and his two sons one after another died, indicating their deaths.
    • 此蛇來吉去凶 - Brought good fortune, left bad luck, indicating the snake's influence.
    • 其唯龍乎 - Is it not a dragon, indicating the speculation that the snake might be a dragon.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 中牟縣蒙山 - Refers to a specific location in ancient China, emphasizing the geographical setting.
    • 新野 - Refers to a county in Henan Province, providing a specific location.
    • 晉中朝 - Refers to the Jin dynasty, a period in Chinese history.
    • 太元 - An era name of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, indicating the time period.
    • 元嘉 - An era name of Emperor Wen of Liu Song, indicating the time period.
    • 司馬︷之 - Refers to Sima Dao Yuan, a historical figure known for his hunting skills.
    • 張司空 - Refers to Zhang Siguang, a high-ranking official during the Jin dynasty.

    On the mountain of Meng in Zhongmou County of the state of Lu, there stood an ancient temple that had been abandoned for a long time. Whenever locals attempted to build houses on the site, a large snake, measuring several tens of feet, would suddenly emerge, startling everyone and preventing them from settling there.

    In Xinye, there was a man named Su Qian, who, along with his wife, lived in a rural cottage and farmed the land. Every time they had their meal, a creature would appear, resembling a snake, about seven chi and five cun in length, with a very bright and colorful appearance. Su Qian found this strange and fed it. Over several years, their property and business flourished. However, his wife secretly killed the creature, and immediately afterward, Su Qian developed an insatiable appetite, consuming three measures of food daily yet still feeling unsatisfied. Soon after, he died.

    During the reign of the Jin dynasty, the military warehouse was tightly sealed. Suddenly, a pheasant-like creature appeared inside. People at the time all considered it a strange occurrence. Zhang Siguang, a high-ranking official, remarked, "This must be the transformation of a snake." He immediately ordered a search of the warehouse, and near the pheasant-like creature, they found the shed skin of a snake.

    Sima Dao Yuan, whose courtesy name was Dao Yuan, was skilled in hunting pheasants. During the Taiyuan period, he used a decoy and lowered a net. The decoy frequently attracted the pheasant-like creature, and wild pheasants also responded. When he tried to find the source of the response, he discovered that the head and wings had transformed into a pheasant, but the rest of the body was still a snake.

    During the Taiyuan period of the Jin dynasty, a man from Runan entered the mountains to cut bamboo and saw a snake inside a bamboo stalk, its shape already formed, with the branches and leaves remaining unchanged. In Wuchang, a man from Tonglu often cut down leftover bamboo and saw a pheasant's head on a bamboo pole, with the neck fully formed but still not transformed. This is also an example of bamboo turning into a snake and a snake turning into a pheasant.

    In Danyang, there was a man named Zhong Zhong, who, during the winter month of the Yuanjia period, went out in the morning and saw a snake about two feet long, with a patterned blue color, a lapis lazuli-like head, two horns, and white as jade. Moved by this, he kept the snake, and his wealth and business grew daily. After a year, the snake died and left. Zhong Zhong and his two sons died one after another. This snake brought good fortune when it came and bad luck when it left, perhaps it was a dragon. [21]

    昔有田父耕地，值见伤蛇在焉。有一蛇衔草著疮上，经日伤蛇走。田父取其草余叶以治疮皆验。本不知草名，因以蛇衔为名。《抱朴子》云蛇衔能续已断之指如故，是也。
    　　海曲有物名蛇公，形如覆莲花，正白。
    　　吴孙权时，永康县有人入山，遇一大龟，即束之以归。龟便言曰：“游不量时，为君所得。”人甚怪之，担出欲上吴王。夜泊越里，缆舟于大桑树。宵中树忽呼龟曰：“劳乎元绪，奚事尔耶？”龟曰：“我被拘系，方见烹霍。虽然，尽南山之樵不能溃我。”树曰：“诸葛元逊博识，必致相苦。今求如我之徒，计从安簿？”龟曰：“子明无多辞，祸将及尔。”树寂而止。既至建业，权命煮之，焚柴万车，语犹如故。诸葛恪曰：“燃以老桑树乃熟。”献者乃说龟树共言，权使人伐桑树煮之，龟乃立烂。今烹龟犹多用桑薪，野人故呼龟为元绪。 [22]

    Once upon a time, there was a farmer plowing his field, and he chanced upon an injured snake there. A snake brought some grass and placed it on the wound. After several days, the injured snake left. The farmer picked up the remaining grass and used it to treat wounds, and it was effective. He did not know the name of the grass, so he named it "snake-bitten grass." According to Baopu Zi, the snake-bitten grass can regenerate severed fingers, and this is true.

    There is an animal called "snake king" in the sea. It looks like an inverted lotus flower, pure white in color.

    During the reign of Sun Quan in Wu, someone from Yongkang County entered a mountain and encountered a large turtle. He tied it up and took it home. The turtle spoke and said, "I didn't measure my time, and I was captured by you." The man was greatly surprised and took the turtle out, intending to present it to King Wu. At night, they stopped at a place called Yue Li and tied their boat to a large mulberry tree. In the middle of the night, the tree suddenly called out to the turtle, "Are you tired, Yuanxun? What are you doing?" The turtle replied, "I am being restrained, and I am about to be cooked. Even if you use all the firewood from Mount Nanshan, you cannot cook me." The tree said, "Zhuge Yuanxun is well-versed, and he will certainly cause trouble for you. Now, if you find someone like me, we can devise a plan to save ourselves." The turtle said, "Subhuti, do not speak much, for disaster will soon befall you." The tree fell silent. Once they arrived in Jianye, Sun Quan ordered the turtle to be cooked, using a vast amount of firewood, but the turtle remained unchanged. Zhuge Ke said, "Burn it with an old mulberry tree, and it will cook." The man who presented the turtle explained that the turtle and the tree had spoken together, and Sun Quan had people cut down the mulberry tree to cook the turtle, which then quickly fell apart. Now, when cooking turtles, people often use mulberry wood, and the common folk call the turtle "Yuanxun." [22]

    元嘉初，益州刺史遣三人入山伐樵，路迷，或见一龟大如车轮，四足各摄一小龟而行。又有百余黄龟从其后。三人叩头请示出路，龟乃伸头，若有意焉。因共随逐，即得出路。一人无故取小龟，割以为霍食之，须臾暴死，惟不啖者无恙。
    　　舀鱼，凡诸鱼欲产舀，辄以头冲其腹。舀鱼自欲生者，亦更相撞触。故世人谓为众鱼之生母也。 [23]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [23]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元嘉初 - Refers to the early years of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song (424-453 AD).
    • 益州刺史 - Refers to the governor of Yizhou (modern-day Sichuan).
    • 三人入山伐樵 - Means 'three people went into the mountains to cut firewood.'
    • 或见一龟大如车轮 - Means 'someone saw a turtle as large as a cart wheel.'
    • 四足各摄一小龟而行 - Means 'each of its four feet carried a small turtle.'
    • 又有百余黄龟从其后 - Means 'there were also more than a hundred yellow turtles following behind.'
    • 三人叩头请示出路 - Means 'the three people knelt and asked the turtle to show them the way out.'
    • 龟乃伸头，若有意焉 - Means 'the turtle then extended its head, as if it had an intention.'
    • 因共随逐，即得出路 - Means 'they followed it and immediately found their way out.'
    • 一人无故取小龟，割以为霍食之 - Means 'one person took a small turtle without reason, cut it up, and ate it.'
    • 须臾暴死 - Means 'soon after, he died suddenly.'
    • 惟不啖者无恙 - Means 'only those who did not eat it remained unharmed.'
    • 舀鱼 - Refers to a type of fish.
    • 凡诸鱼欲产舀，辄以头冲其腹 - Means 'when other fish want to give birth, they bump their heads against the belly of the 舀鱼.'
    • 舀鱼自欲生者，亦更相撞触 - Means 'the 舀鱼 itself, when it wants to give birth, also bumps and collides with others.'
    • 故世人谓为众鱼之生母也 - Means 'thus, people call it the 'birth mother of all fish.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉初 - The early years of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 益州刺史 - The governor of Yizhou, a region known for its rich cultural and natural resources.
    • 龟 - Turtles are often associated with longevity and wisdom in Chinese culture.
    • 舀鱼 - A specific type of fish, possibly a local species known for its unique behavior.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元嘉 - The era name of Emperor Wu of Liu Song.
    • 益州 - The name of the region, modern-day Sichuan.
    • 龟 - The general term for turtle.
    • 舀鱼 - The specific name of the fish.

    In the early years of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song, the governor of Yizhou sent three people into the mountains to cut firewood. They got lost, and one of them saw a turtle as large as a cart wheel, with each of its four feet carrying a small turtle. There were also more than a hundred yellow turtles following behind. The three people knelt and asked the turtle to show them the way out. The turtle then extended its head, as if it had an intention. They followed it and immediately found their way out. One person took a small turtle without reason, cut it up, and ate it. Soon after, he died suddenly. Only those who did not eat it remained unharmed.

    The 舀鱼 is a type of fish. When other fish want to give birth, they bump their heads against the belly of the 舀鱼. The 舀鱼 itself, when it wants to give birth, also bumps and collides with others. Thus, people call it the "birth mother of all fish." [23]

    晋义熙五年，卢循自广州下，泊船江西，众多疫死。事平之后，人往蔡州，见死人发变而为。今上镇西参军与司马张逝瞻河际有一棺，棺头有，众试令拨看，都是发，亦有未即化者。一说云，生以秫潘沐，死则发变为。又昔有人食，不能无，死后改棺，且满棺中。且即也。
    　　司马休遣文武千余人迎家人违南郡。值风泊船上岸伐薪，见聚肉有数百斤，乃割取还，以镬煮之，汤欲热，皆变成数千虾墓。
    　　蝴蝶变作。
    　　鹦鹉螺形似鸟，故以为名。常脱壳而游，朝出则有虫，类如蜘蛛，入其壳中螺。夕还则此虫出。庾阐所谓鹦鹉内游，寄居负壳者也。
    　　晋明帝尝欲肆{生月}闭而不谋，乃屏曲室，去左右，下帷草诏。有大苍蝇触帐而入，萃于笔端，须臾亡去。帝窃异焉，令人寻看，即蝇所集处辄传有诏，喧然已遍矣。
    　　有小虫形色如大豆，咒令叩头，又咒令吐血，皆从所教，如似请放稽颡。辄七十而有声，故俗呼为叩头虫也。 [24]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [24]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 泊船江西 - Anchor the boat on the Yangtze River.
    • 众多疫死 - Many died from an epidemic.
    • 事平之后 - After the matter was settled.
    • 见死人发变而为 - The hair of the dead changed into a type of fungus or mold.
    • 今上镇西参军 - A military officer of the Western Garrison during the current emperor's reign.
    • 司马张逝 - A name, possibly a reference to a historical figure.
    • 一棺，棺头有 - A coffin with a type of fungus or mold on its head.
    • 一说云，生以秫潘沐，死则发变为 - A theory suggests that if one bathes with a mixture of millet and water while alive, their hair will turn into fungus after death.
    • 又昔有人食，不能无 - Another story tells of someone who, despite eating, could not avoid having fungus.
    • 死后改棺，且满棺中 - After death, when the coffin was changed, it was full of fungus.
    • 且即也 - The character '且' is likely a variant or typo of ',' referring to a type of fungus.
    • 司马休遣文武千余人迎家人违南郡 - '司马休' is a name, possibly a historical figure; '文武千余人' means thousands of soldiers and officers; '迎家人' means to welcome family members; '违南郡' means to avoid or bypass Nanyang.
    • 值风泊船上岸伐薪 - '值风' means encountering a wind; '泊船' means to anchor a boat; '上岸伐薪' means to go ashore and chop wood.
    • 见聚肉有数百斤 - Saw a pile of meat weighing hundreds of pounds.
    • 乃割取还 - Cut it up and brought it back.
    • 以镬煮之 - Cooked it in a large pot.
    • 汤欲热 - The soup was about to heat up.
    • 皆变成数千虾墓 - All turned into thousands of shrimp.
    • 蝴蝶变作 - Butterflies transformed into another type of insect (possibly a moth).
    • 鹦鹉螺形似鸟 - The nautilus shell resembles a bird.
    • 故以为名 - Therefore, it is named after a bird.
    • 常脱壳而游 - It often leaves its shell to move around.
    • 朝出则有虫，类如蜘蛛，入其壳中螺 - In the morning, it leaves its shell, and a spider-like creature enters the shell.
    • 夕还则此虫出 - In the evening, the creature returns to the shell.
    • 庾阐所谓鹦鹉内游，寄居负壳者也 - As stated by Yu Chan, the nautilus is like a parrot that lives inside its shell.
    • 晋明帝尝欲肆{生月}闭而不谋 - Emperor Ming of Jin once wanted to do something without consulting others.
    • 乃屏曲室，去左右，下帷草诏 - He secluded himself in a small room, dismissed his attendants, and drafted an edict behind a curtain.
    • 有大苍蝇触帐而入，萃于笔端 - A large green fly touched the curtain and flew into the room, landing on the tip of the brush.
    • 须臾亡去 - Soon after, it flew away.
    • 帝窃异焉 - The emperor secretly found this strange.
    • 令人寻看 - Ordered someone to look for it.
    • 即蝇所集处辄传有诏 - Wherever the fly had landed, it was said that an edict had been issued.
    • 喧然已遍矣 - The news spread quickly.
    • 有小虫形色如大豆 - A small insect with the color and shape of a soybean.
    • 咒令叩头，又咒令吐血 - Chanted spells to make it bow its head and then to make it spit blood.
    • 皆从所教 - It followed the instructions.
    • 如似请放稽颡 - It seemed to be asking for forgiveness.
    • 辄七十而有声 - It made a sound after seventy times.
    • 故俗呼为叩头虫也 - Therefore, it is commonly called the 'bowing bug.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋义熙五年 - The year 409 AD, a significant period in the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 司马休 - Possibly a historical figure, but not well-documented in standard historical texts.
    • 庾阐 - Yu Chan, a historian and writer from the Eastern Jin period.
    • 晋明帝 - Emperor Ming of Jin (296–325), the second emperor of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 鹦鹉螺 - The nautilus, a marine mollusk known for its spiral shell.
    • 叩头虫 - A type of insect, possibly a reference to a specific species with unique behaviors.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋义熙五年 - The year 409 AD.
    • 司马休 - A name, possibly a historical figure.
    • 庾阐 - Yu Chan, a historian and writer from the Eastern Jin period.
    • 晋明帝 - Emperor Ming of Jin (296–325).
    • 鹦鹉螺 - The nautilus.
    • 叩头虫 - A type of insect.

    During the fifth year of the Yixi era of the Jin dynasty (409 AD), Lu Xun descended from Guangzhou and anchored his ships on the Yangtze River. Many of his men died from an epidemic. After the situation was resolved, people traveled to Cai Prefecture and saw that the hair of the dead had transformed into a type of fungus. A military officer of the Western Garrison and General Zhang Shi, looking across the river, noticed a coffin with a type of fungus on its head. They tested it by probing, and found that it was all hair, some of which had not yet fully transformed. One theory suggests that if one bathes with a mixture of millet and water while alive, their hair will turn into fungus after death. Another story tells of someone who, despite eating, could not avoid having fungus. After their death, when the coffin was changed, it was full of fungus.

    General Shi Xiu sent thousands of soldiers and officers to welcome his family, bypassing Nanyang. They encountered a wind and anchored their boats to go ashore and chop wood. They saw a pile of meat weighing hundreds of pounds and cut it up, bringing it back to cook in a large pot. As the soup was about to heat up, it all turned into thousands of shrimp.

    Butterflies transformed into another type of insect (possibly a moth).

    The nautilus shell resembles a bird, hence its name. It often leaves its shell to move around. In the morning, it leaves its shell, and a spider-like creature enters the shell. In the evening, the creature returns to the shell. As stated by Yu Chan, the nautilus is like a parrot that lives inside its shell.

    Emperor Ming of Jin once wanted to do something without consulting others. He secluded himself in a small room, dismissed his attendants, and drafted an edict behind a curtain. A large green fly touched the curtain and flew into the room, landing on the tip of the brush. Soon after, it flew away. The emperor secretly found this strange and ordered someone to look for it. Wherever the fly had landed, it was said that an edict had been issued, and the news spread quickly.

    There was a small insect with the color and shape of a soybean. Spells were chanted to make it bow its head and then to make it spit blood. It followed the instructions, seeming to ask for forgiveness. After seventy times, it made a sound. Therefore, it is commonly called the "bowing bug." [24]

    缢女虫也一名蚬，长寸许，头赤身黑，恒吐丝自悬。昔齐东郭姜既乱崔杼之室，庆封杀其二子，姜亦自经。俗传此妇骸化为虫，故以缢女名虫。

    　　●卷四
    　　蜀郡临邛县有火井，汉室之隆则炎赫弥炽。暨桓灵之际，火势渐微。诸葛亮一瞰而更盛，至景曜元年，人以烛投即灭。其年蜀并于魏。
    　　孙钟富春人，坚父也。与母居，至孝笃性，种瓜为业。忽有三年少容服妍丽，诣钟乞瓜。钟为设食出瓜，礼敬殷勤。三人临去，曰：“我等司命郎，感君接见之厚，欲连世封侯，欲数世天子。”钟曰：“数世天子故当所乐。”因为钟定墓地，出门悉化成白鹊。一云，孙坚丧父，行葬地，忽有一人曰：“君欲百世诸侯乎？欲四世帝乎？”笑曰：“欲帝。”此人因指一处，喜悦而没。坚异而从之。时富春有沙涨暴出，及坚为监丞，邻党相送于上，父老谓曰：此沙狭而长子，后将为长沙矣。果起义兵于长沙。 [25]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [25]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 一名 - Classical expression meaning 'also known as' or 'another name for.'
    • 恒吐丝自悬 - Literal phrase meaning 'constantly spitting silk to hang itself,' describing the behavior of the insect.
    • 昔齐东郭姜既乱崔杼之室 - Complex phrase referring to a historical event where Lady Dongguo of Qi disrupted the household of Cui Zhu. '昔' means 'in the past,' '既' means 'already,' and '乱' means 'to disrupt' or 'to disturb.'
    • 庆封杀其二子 - Historical reference where King Qing of Qi killed two of Lady Dongguo's sons. '杀' means 'to kill,' and '其二子' means 'her two sons.'
    • 姜亦自经 - Phrase meaning 'Lady Dongguo also hanged herself.' '自经' is a euphemism for suicide by hanging.
    • 俗传此妇骸化为虫 - Phrase meaning 'it is said that the bones of this woman transformed into an insect.' '俗传' means 'it is said' or 'according to popular belief.'
    • 以缢女名虫 - Phrase meaning 'and the insect was named after the hanged woman.' '以...名' means 'to name after.'
    • 蜀郡临邛县有火井 - Phrase meaning 'in Linqiong County of Shu Commandery, there is a fire well.' '火井' refers to a natural gas well that produces flames.
    • 汉室之隆则炎赫弥炽 - Phrase meaning 'when the Han dynasty was at its height, the flames burned even more fiercely.' '隆' means 'prosperous' or 'flourishing,' '炎赫' means 'fiery' or 'burning brightly,' and '弥炽' means 'even more intense.'
    • 暨桓灵之际，火势渐微 - Phrase meaning 'by the time of Emperors Huan and Ling, the fire had become less intense.' '暨' means 'by the time of,' '桓灵' refers to Emperors Huan and Ling of the Eastern Han dynasty, and '渐微' means 'gradually weakened.'
    • 诸葛亮一瞰而更盛 - Phrase meaning 'when Zhuge Liang took a look at it, the fire became even stronger.' '一瞰' means 'to take a look,' and '更盛' means 'became even stronger.'
    • 至景曜元年，人以烛投即灭 - Phrase meaning 'by the first year of Jingyao, when people threw candles into it, the fire went out.' '景曜元年' refers to the first year of the Jingyao era during the Shu Han dynasty, and '即灭' means 'went out immediately.'
    • 其年蜀并于魏 - Phrase meaning 'that year, Shu was annexed by Wei.' '并于' means 'was annexed by.'
    • 孙钟富春人，坚父也 - Phrase meaning 'Sun Zhong was from Fuchun and was the father of Sun Jian.' '富春' is the place name Fuchun, and '坚父' means 'father of Sun Jian.'
    • 种瓜为业 - Phrase meaning 'he made a living growing melons.' '种瓜' means 'growing melons,' and '为业' means 'as a profession.'
    • 诣钟乞瓜 - Phrase meaning 'came to Sun Zhong to beg for melons.' '诣' means 'to visit,' and '乞' means 'to beg.'
    • 礼敬殷勤 - Phrase meaning 'with great respect and hospitality.' '礼敬' means 'respect,' and '殷勤' means 'hospitality' or 'kindness.'
    • 司命郎 - Term referring to officials who manage life and death, often associated with deities or spirits.
    • 感君接见之厚 - Phrase meaning 'grateful for your generous reception.' '感' means 'grateful,' '君' means 'you,' '接见' means 'reception,' and '厚' means 'generous.'
    • 欲连世封侯 - Phrase meaning 'wanting to be enfeoffed as a marquis for generations.' '连世' means 'for generations,' and '封侯' means 'to be enfeoffed as a marquis.'
    • 欲数世天子 - Phrase meaning 'wanting to have emperors for several generations.' '数世' means 'several generations,' and '天子' means 'emperor.'
    • 钟曰：‘数世天子故当所乐。’ - Phrase meaning 'Sun Zhong replied, 'Having emperors for several generations is indeed what one desires.' '故当' means 'indeed,' and '所乐' means 'what one desires.'
    • 因为钟定墓地 - Phrase meaning 'they then designated a burial site for Sun Zhong.' '因为' means 'therefore,' and '定墓地' means 'designate a burial site.'
    • 出门悉化成白鹊 - Phrase meaning 'as they left, everything turned into white magpies.' '出门' means 'as they left,' and '悉化成' means 'all turned into.'
    • 一云 - Phrase meaning 'another version says.' '一云' is a common phrase used to introduce alternative accounts or legends.
    • 孙坚丧父，行葬地 - Phrase meaning 'Sun Jian, after his father's death, went to choose a burial site.' '丧父' means 'after his father's death,' and '行葬地' means 'went to choose a burial site.'
    • 忽有一人曰 - Phrase meaning 'suddenly, a man appeared and said.' '忽' means 'suddenly,' and '曰' means 'said.'
    • 君欲百世诸侯乎？欲四世帝乎？ - Phrase meaning 'Do you want to be a feudal lord for a hundred generations? Or do you want to have emperors for four generations?' '百世' means 'a hundred generations,' '诸侯' means 'feudal lords,' '四世' means 'four generations,' and '帝' means 'emperor.'
    • 笑曰：‘欲帝。’ - Phrase meaning 'he laughed and said, 'I want to be an emperor.' '笑曰' means 'laughed and said,' and '欲帝' means 'want to be an emperor.'
    • 此人因指一处 - Phrase meaning 'the man then pointed to a place.' '因指' means 'then pointed,' and '一处' means 'a place.'
    • 喜悦而没 - Phrase meaning 'he was delighted and disappeared.' '喜悦' means 'delighted,' and '没' means 'disappeared.'
    • 异而从之 - Phrase meaning 'Sun Jian found it strange and followed him.' '异' means 'found strange,' and '从之' means 'followed him.'
    • 时富春有沙涨暴出 - Phrase meaning 'at that time, a sandbar suddenly emerged in Fuchun.' '沙涨' means 'sandbar,' and '暴出' means 'suddenly emerged.'
    • 及坚为监丞，邻党相送于上 - Phrase meaning 'when Sun Jian became a supervisor, his neighbors and relatives accompanied him up.' '监丞' means 'supervisor,' and '邻党相送于上' means 'neighbors and relatives accompanied him up.'
    • 父老谓曰 - Phrase meaning 'the elders said to him.' '父老' means 'elders,' and '谓曰' means 'said to him.'
    • 此沙狭而长子，后将为长沙矣 - Phrase meaning 'this narrow and long sandbar will become Changsha in the future.' '狭而长子' means 'narrow and long,' and '后将为长沙矣' means 'will become Changsha in the future.'
    • 果起义兵于长沙 - Phrase meaning 'indeed, he raised an army in Changsha.' '果' means 'indeed,' and '起义兵' means 'raised an army.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 齐东郭姜 - Lady Dongguo of Qi, a historical figure who disrupted the household of Cui Zhu.
    • 崔杼之室 - The household of Cui Zhu, a prominent noble family in the State of Qi.
    • 庆封 - King Qing of Qi, who ordered the execution of Lady Dongguo's sons.
    • 诸葛亮 - Zhuge Liang, a famous statesman and military strategist of the Shu Han dynasty.
    • 景曜元年 - The first year of the Jingyao era during the Shu Han dynasty.
    • 蜀郡临邛县 - Linqiong County in Shu Commandery, known for its natural gas wells.
    • 汉室之隆 - The height of the Han dynasty, a period of prosperity and power.
    • 桓灵之际 - The reigns of Emperors Huan and Ling of the Eastern Han dynasty, a period of decline.
    • 孙钟 - Sun Zhong, the father of Sun Jian, a general of the Eastern Han dynasty.

    The Hanged Woman Insect, also known as the Clam, is about an inch long, with a red head and a black body. It constantly spits silk to hang itself. In the past, Lady Dongguo of Qi disrupted the household of Cui Zhu. King Qing of Qi ordered the execution of her two sons, and Lady Dongguo also hanged herself. It is said that the bones of this woman transformed into an insect, hence the insect was named after the hanged woman.

    Chapter Four

    In Linqiong County of Shu Commandery, there is a fire well. When the Han dynasty was at its height, the flames burned even more fiercely. By the time of Emperors Huan and Ling, the fire had become less intense. When Zhuge Liang took a look at it, the fire became even stronger. By the first year of Jingyao, when people threw candles into it, the fire went out. That year, Shu was annexed by Wei.

    Sun Zhong was from Fuchun and was the father of Sun Jian. He lived with his mother, showing great filial piety and dedication. He made a living growing melons. Suddenly, three young men with beautiful attire came to Sun Zhong to beg for melons. Sun Zhong prepared food and offered them melons, showing great respect and hospitality. As they were leaving, the three men said, "We are officials who manage life and death, grateful for your generous reception. We wish to have you enfeoffed as a marquis for generations and to have emperors for several generations." Sun Zhong replied, "Having emperors for several generations is indeed what one desires." They then designated a burial site for Sun Zhong, and as they left, everything turned into white magpies. Another version says that after Sun Jian's father's death, he went to choose a burial site. Suddenly, a man appeared and said, "Do you want to be a feudal lord for a hundred generations? Or do you want to have emperors for four generations?" Sun Jian laughed and said, "I want to be an emperor." The man then pointed to a place, was delighted, and disappeared. Sun Jian found it strange and followed him. At that time, a sandbar suddenly emerged in Fuchun. When Sun Jian became a supervisor, his neighbors and relatives accompanied him up. The elders said to him, "This narrow and long sandbar will become Changsha in the future." Indeed, he raised an army in Changsha. [25]

    泰山高堂隆字升平，尝刻邺宫屋材，云后若干年当有天子居此宫。及晋惠帝幸邺宫，治屋者土剥更泥，始见刻字，计年正合。
    　　王敦既为逆，顿军姑孰，晋明帝躬往觇之。敦时昼寝，梦日环其城，乃卓然惊寤，曰：“营中有黄头鲜卑奴来，何不缚取？”帝所生母荀氏燕国人，故貌类焉。
    　　晋简文既废世子道生，次子郁又早卒，而未有息。濮阳令在帝面前祷，至三更，忽有黄气自西南来，逆室前。尔夜幸李太后而生孝皇帝。 [26]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [26]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 泰山高堂隆字升平 - Introduction of a person using traditional Chinese naming conventions.
    • 刻邺宫屋材 - Engraved on the timber of the Yecheng palace, implying a deliberate and possibly symbolic act.
    • 后若干年当有天子居此宫 - A prophecy or prediction that in several years, there will be an emperor residing in this palace.
    • 治屋者土剥更泥 - The person repairing the house peeled off the old mud and replaced it, indicating a repair process.
    • 始见刻字 - Only then did they see the engraved words, emphasizing the discovery.
    • 计年正合 - The calculation of years matched exactly, indicating a precise temporal alignment.
    • 顿军姑孰 - Stationed his troops at Gushu, suggesting a military presence.
    • 躬往觇之 - Personally went to spy on him, emphasizing the personal involvement.
    • 昼寝 - Took a daytime nap, a common expression for sleeping during the day.
    • 梦日环其城 - Dreamed of the sun encircling his city, a vivid and symbolic description.
    • 卓然惊寤 - Suddenly woke up in shock, conveying a sudden and intense reaction.
    • 营中有黄头鲜卑奴来 - There was a yellow-haired Xianbei slave in his camp, a specific description of a person.
    • 所生母荀氏燕国人 - His biological mother was a woman named Xun from Yan Province, clarifying the relationship.
    • 祷 - Prayed, indicating a religious or spiritual act.
    • 三更 - Three watches of the night, a traditional Chinese time division.
    • 黄气自西南来 - Yellow mist came from the southwest, a symbolic and possibly supernatural description.
    • 逆室前 - Approached the room, indicating movement.
    • 幸李太后而生孝皇帝 - Visited Li太后 and gave birth to Emperor Xiao, where '幸' is a euphemism for sexual intercourse in this context.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 泰山 - Refers to Mount Tai, one of the Five Great Mountains of China, known for its cultural and religious significance.
    • 高堂隆 - A historical figure, Gao Tang Long, who lived during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 邺宫 - The Yecheng palace, located in modern-day Hebei Province, was a significant imperial residence.
    • 晋惠帝 - Emperor Huai of Jin, the second emperor of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 王敦 - Wang Dun, a prominent military general and politician during the Jin dynasty.
    • 姑孰 - A place name, likely referring to a location in Anhui Province.
    • 晋明帝 - Emperor Ming of Jin, the third emperor of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 黄头鲜卑奴 - A reference to a Xianbei slave, indicating the presence of ethnic minorities in the Jin dynasty.
    • 燕国 - A historical state in northern China, now part of modern-day Hebei Province.
    • 李太后 - A historical figure, likely referring to a concubine or queen during the Jin dynasty.
    • 孝皇帝 - Emperor Xiao, likely referring to a specific emperor in the Jin dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 泰山 - Mount Tai.
    • 高堂隆 - Gao Tang Long.
    • 邺宫 - Yecheng palace.
    • 晋惠帝 - Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 王敦 - Wang Dun.
    • 姑孰 - A place name, likely referring to a location in Anhui Province.
    • 晋明帝 - Emperor Ming of Jin.
    • 李太后 - A historical figure, likely referring to a concubine or queen during the Jin dynasty.
    • 孝皇帝 - Emperor Xiao, likely referring to a specific emperor in the Jin dynasty.

    Gao Tang Long of Tai'an, courtesy name Shengping, once engraved on the timber of the Yecheng palace, predicting that in several years there would be an emperor residing in this palace. When Emperor Huai of Jin visited the Yecheng palace, the person repairing the house peeled off the old mud and replaced it, only then did they see the engraved words. The calculation of years matched exactly.

    Wang Dun, after rebelling, stationed his troops at Gushu. Emperor Ming of Jin personally went to spy on him. Wang Dun took a daytime nap and dreamed of the sun encircling his city. He suddenly woke up in shock and said, "There was a yellow-haired Xianbei slave in my camp. Why didn't I tie him up?" His biological mother was a woman named Xun from Yan Province, hence she resembled him.

    Emperor Ming of Jin, after deposing the crown prince Dao Sheng, had his second son Yu die early, and he had no heirs. The magistrate of Puyang prayed in front of him until three watches of the night. Suddenly, yellow mist came from the southwest and approached the room. That night, he visited Li太后 and gave birth to Emperor Xiao. [26]

    刘曜隐居管涔之山。夜中忽有一童子入跪曰：“管涔王使小臣奉谒赵皇帝，献剑一口。”置前再拜而去。以烛视之，剑长二尺，光泽非常，赤玉为饰，背有铭云：“神剑服御除众毒”。
    　　石勒为郭敬客时，襄国有谶曰：“力在左，革在右，让无言，或入口。”让去言为襄字，或入口乃国字也。勒后遂都襄国。
    　　石勒伐刘曜于洛阳，从大河南济。时河冻，将合军至而水自泮，舟楫无阂，遂生擒曜，谓是神灵之助，命曰灵昌津。
    　　晋时长安谣曰：“秦川城中血没，惟有凉州倚柱看。”及惠愍之间，关内歼破，浮血飘舟，张轨拥众一方，威恩共著。
    　　凉州张骏字公彦。九年天雨五谷于武威，炖煌，植之悉生，因名天麦。
    　　凉州张祚伪和平中有神见于玄武殿，自称玄冥，与人言语。祚日夜祈之，神言与之福利，祚甚信之。 [27]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [27]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 隐居管涔之山 - Refers to Liu Yao living in seclusion in the Guan Cen Mountains, a specific place in Shanxi Province.
    • 夜中忽有一童子入跪曰 - Describes a sudden appearance of a child at night, which is a common motif in Chinese folklore and legends.
    • 管涔王使小臣奉谒赵皇帝 - Indicates that the child is a messenger from the King of Guan Cen Mountain, paying respects to the Zhao Emperor.
    • 献剑一口 - Refers to the act of presenting a sword, a significant gesture in Chinese culture.
    • 置前再拜而去 - Describes the ritual of placing the sword before the emperor and bowing twice before leaving.
    • 以烛视之 - Means to examine the sword with a candle, emphasizing its importance and the careful inspection.
    • 剑长二尺，光泽非常，赤玉为饰，背有铭云 - Describes the sword's physical characteristics, including its length, shine, decoration, and inscription.
    • 神剑服御除众毒 - The inscription on the sword, meaning 'Divine Sword for Wearing and Removing All Poisons.'
    • 石勒为郭敬客时 - Refers to Shi Le being a guest of Guo Jing, a historical figure.
    • 襄国有谶曰 - Indicates a prophecy or omen in Xiangguo (modern-day Xiangfen, Shanxi).
    • 力在左，革在右，让无言，或入口 - The prophecy, which is cryptic and requires interpretation.
    • 让去言为襄字，或入口乃国字也 - Explanation of the prophecy, where '让' (let) minus '言' (speech) becomes '襄' (Xiang), and '或' (or) plus '口' (mouth) becomes '国' (guo, country).
    • 石勒后遂都襄国 - States that Shi Le later established his capital in Xiangguo.
    • 石勒伐刘曜于洛阳 - Describes Shi Le's military campaign against Liu Yao in Luoyang.
    • 从大河南济 - Refers to crossing the Yellow River.
    • 时河冻，将合军至而水自泮 - Describes the river freezing and then thawing just as the army arrives.
    • 舟楫无阂 - Means the boats had no obstacles, indicating smooth passage.
    • 遂生擒曜，谓是神灵之助 - States that Shi Le captured Liu Yao alive, attributing the success to divine assistance.
    • 命曰灵昌津 - Names the crossing point 'Lingchang Ferry.'
    • 晋时长安谣曰 - Refers to a rhyme or song in Chang'an during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 秦川城中血没 - Describes a scene of blood covering the streets, indicating a violent event.
    • 惟有凉州倚柱看 - Means only the people in Liangzhou (modern-day Gansu) were able to observe the events calmly.
    • 及惠愍之间 - Refers to the period of Emperor Huizong and Emperor Min of Jin, a time of chaos and war.
    • 关内歼破 - Describes the destruction and devastation within the passes.
