    • The sequence of events is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the narrative flow.
    • The sentence is clear but requires maintaining the emotional intensity.
    • The sentence is clear but requires understanding of the historical context.
    • The sentence is clear but requires understanding of the historical context.
    • The sentence is clear but requires maintaining the vivid imagery.
    • The sentence is clear but requires maintaining the emotional intensity.
    • The sentence is clear but requires understanding of the historical context.
    • The sentence is clear but requires maintaining the vivid imagery.
    • The sentence is clear but requires understanding of the historical context.
    • The sentence is clear but requires maintaining the emotional intensity.
    • The sentence is clear but requires understanding of the historical context.
    • The sentence is clear but requires maintaining the vivid imagery.
    • The sentence is clear but requires understanding of the historical context.
    • The sentence is clear but requires maintaining the emotional intensity.

    During the Jin Dynasty, in Danyang County, there was a temple dedicated to Yuan Shuang, who was the fourth son of Yuan Zhen. Yuan Zhen was executed by Huan Xuanwu, after which Yuan Shuang disappeared. During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu (Taiyuan period), Yuan Shuang's spirit appeared in Danyang, requesting the establishment of a temple. Before the temple was completed, there were frequent tiger attacks. The families affected would often dream of Yuan Shuang urging them to hasten the construction of the temple. The people then built a shrine, and thus the fierce tigers ceased their attacks. Nowadays, both monks and laypeople commonly hold a ceremony with drumming and dancing to pray at the temple on the last day of the second month. On that day, suddenly, wind and rain came. In the fifth year of the Yuanjia era, after the offering was set up, a villager named Qiu Du saw something at the back of the temple—a creature with a human face, a turtle body, a headscarf with seven holes, upright and with the smell of alcohol. It is unknown whether this was the spirit of Yuan Shuang or if it was possessed by this creature.

    The Xiao Gu Temple in Qingxi was said to be the temple of the third sister of Prince Jiang. There was a large tree with spreading branches where birds often nested. During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu (Taiyuan period), a man from Chen Prefecture named Xie Qing, holding a bow and riding a horse, killed several birds with a bowstring. He immediately felt a chill through his body. That night, he dreamed of a woman dressed beautifully, who angrily said, "These birds are mine, why do you harm them?" Several days later, Xie Qing died. Xie Qing's name was Huan, and he was the father of Xie Lingyun.

    In Yuhang County, there was a temple dedicated to King Chou, which had long been known for its many supernatural phenomena. During the early years of Emperor An's reign (Long'an period), a county official named Shu Bodao, who was granted leave to return home, sought a ride at Ruinan Bay. He saw a red boat with a noble person inside and asked to be carried along. After a short while, he fell asleep but still heard sounds like heavy rain. Soon, he arrived home and asked the boatman, who also said it was King Chou. Shu Bodao bowed in gratitude and returned. [42]

    隆安中吴兴有人年可二十，自号圣公，姓谢，死已百年。忽诣陈氏宅，言是已旧宅，可见还，不尔，烧汝。一夕火发荡尽，因有鸟毛插地，绕宅周匝数重，百姓乃起庙。
    　　晋义熙中虞道施乘车出行，忽有一人著鸟衣，迳来上车，云令寄载十里许耳。道施试视，此人头上有光，口目皆赤，面悉是毛，异于始时。既不敢遣。行十里中，如言而去。临别语道施曰：“我是驱除大将军，感汝相容。”因赠银铎一双而灭。
    　　晋时信安郑徽年少时登前桥，仿佛见一老翁以一囊与徽，云：“此是君命，慎勿令零落。若有破碎，便为凶兆。”言讫忽失所在。徽密开看，是一挺炭，意甚秘之，虽家人不之知也。后遭卢龙寇乱，恒保录之。至宋永初三年，徽年八十三，病笃，语子弟云：吾齿尽矣，可试启此囊。见炭悉碎折，于是遂绝。 [43]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [43]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 自号圣公 - Self-proclaimed Holy Lord, indicating a person claiming divine or supernatural status.
    • 驱除大将军 - General of Exorcism, indicating a supernatural role.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 隆安中 - A specific year in the reign of Emperor An of Jin (397-398 AD).
    • 晋义熙中 - A specific year in the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (405-418 AD).
    • 宋永初三年 - A specific year in the reign of Emperor Wu of Song (422 AD).
    • 卢龙寇乱 - Refers to the invasion by the Luolong, a group of nomadic tribes.
    • 信安 - A place name, likely referring to a region in present-day Zhejiang Province.

    Proper Nouns

    • 谢 - The surname of the self-proclaimed Holy Lord.
    • 陈氏 - The surname of the family whose house was visited.
    • 虞道施 - The name of the person traveling by carriage.
    • 郑徽 - The name of the person who encountered the old man.
    • 卢龙 - A group of nomadic tribes.

    During the reign of Emperor An of Jin, there was a man in Wu County who was about twenty years old, self-proclaimed as the Holy Lord, with the surname Xie, who had died a hundred years ago. He suddenly visited the Chen family's house and said that this was his old residence, and he could return, or else he would burn it down. One night, the fire broke out and completely destroyed it, and there were bird feathers stuck in the ground, surrounding the house several times. The people then built a temple.

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, Yu Daoshi was traveling by carriage. Suddenly, a person wearing bird clothing came directly onto the carriage and said he would ride along for about ten miles. Yu Daoshi looked carefully and saw that the person had a halo on his head, red eyes and mouth, and a face covered in feathers, which was different from before. Since he dared not send him away, after traveling ten miles, he left as he said. On parting, he said to Yu Daoshi, "I am the General of Exorcism, and I am grateful for your hospitality." He then gave two silver bells and disappeared.

    During the Jin dynasty, Zheng Hui of Xin'an climbed a bridge when he was young and saw an old man giving him a bag. The old man said, "This is your fate, be careful not to let it fall apart. If it breaks, it will be a bad omen." After saying this, he suddenly disappeared. Zheng Hui secretly opened it to look, and it was a piece of charcoal. He kept it very secret, and even his family did not know about it. Later, he encountered the invasion by the Luolong and always kept it safe. By the year of Yongchu in the reign of Emperor Wu of Song, Zheng Hui was eighty-three years old and seriously ill. He told his children, "I am old, you can try opening this bag." He saw the charcoal completely broken, and he died. [43]

    陈虞字君度，妇庐江杜氏，常事鬼子母，罗女乐以娱神。后一夕复会，弦管无声，歌者凄忾。杜氏尝梦鬼子母遑遽涕泗云：“凶人将来。”婢先与外人通，以梯布垣，登之入。神被服将剥夺，毕加取影象，焚М而后去。
    　　世有紫姑神，古来相传云：是人家妾，为大妇所嫉，每以秽事相次役。正月十五日，感激而死，故世人以其日作其形，夜于厕间或猪栏边迎之，祝曰：子胥不在，是其婿名也。曹姑亦归曹，即其大妇也。小姑可出戏，投者觉重，便是神来，奠设酒果，亦觉貌辉辉有色，即跳躞不住，能占众事，卜未来，蚕桑。又善射钩，好则大亻无，恶便仰眠。平昌孟氏恒不信，躬试往投，便自跃茅屋而去，永失所在也。 [44]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [44]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 字 - Courtesy name used by peers and subordinates, distinct from the personal name.
    • 事 - Worship or regular devotion/ritual.
    • 罗女乐 - Arranged female musicians, often associated with ritual or entertainment.
    • 复会 - Gathered again, implies a recurring ritual.
    • 弦管 - Stringed instruments and pipes, musical instruments.
    • 凄忾 - Sorrowful, conveys deep grief or melancholy.
    • 通 - Consorted, implies an illicit or improper relationship.
    • 布垣 - Against the wall, describes a physical action.
    • 登之入 - Climbed it inside, describes a physical action.
    • 被服 - Dressed, suggests a ritual or punitive act.
    • 剥夺 - Stripped, suggests a ritual or punitive act.
    • 毕加 - Took, describes a ritual act of destruction.
    • 焚之 - Burned it, describes a ritual act of destruction.
    • 感激 - Indignation, conveys a strong emotional reaction.
    • 作其形 - Make her image, suggests a ritual or commemorative act.
    • 厕间 - Latrine, specifies the location of the ritual.
    • 猪栏边 - Pigsty, specifies the location of the ritual.
    • 投者 - The one who throws, describes a physical action.
    • 觉重 - Feels it heavy, describes a physical sensation.
    • 神来 - Deity has come, indicates the presence of the deity.
    • 奠设 - Offer, describes a ritual offering.
    • 酒果 - Wine and fruit, describes a ritual offering.
    • 貌辉辉 - Radiant, describes the deity's appearance.
    • 有色 - With color, describes the deity's appearance.
    • 跳躞 - Dances, describes the deity's actions.
    • 不住 - Restlessly, describes the deity's actions.
    • 占众事 - Predict many things, suggests divination or prophecy.
    • 卜未来 - Foretell the future, a specific form of divination.
    • 蚕桑 - Silkworms and mulberry, refers to silk production.
    • 射钩 - Shooting hooks, suggests a specific skill or game.
    • 好则大亻无 - If she likes it, she stretches her arms, describes the deity's reactions.
    • 恶便仰眠 - If she dislikes it, she lies on her back, describes the deity's reactions.
    • 躬试 - Personally tried, describes a physical action.
    • 往投 - To throw, describes a physical action.
    • 跃茅屋 - Jumped over the thatched house, describes the deity's departure.
    • 而去 - And left, describes the deity's departure.
    • 永失所在 - Never seen again, indicates the deity's disappearance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陈虞 - Historical or fictional figure, likely from a specific context or story.
    • 鬼子母 - The Ghost Mother, a deity or spirit associated with protection or punishment.
    • 紫姑神 - The Purple Lady deity, a specific figure in folklore or religious tradition.
    • 正月十五日 - The Lantern Festival, a traditional Chinese holiday celebrated on the 15th day of the first lunar month.
    • 子胥 - Likely a reference to a specific person or deity, possibly related to the Purple Lady.
    • 曹姑 - Likely a reference to a specific person or deity, possibly related to the Purple Lady.
    • 小姑 - Likely a reference to a specific person or deity, possibly related to the Purple Lady.
    • 平昌孟氏 - The Meng family of Pingchang, likely a specific family or group with a particular stance on the Purple Lady.

    Chen Yu, courtesy name Jundu, was married to Du Shi of Lujiang. He often worshipped the Ghost Mother, arranging female musicians to entertain the deity. Later, on one night, they gathered again, but the stringed instruments and pipes made no sound, and the singers were sorrowful. Du Shi once dreamed that the Ghost Mother was in a state of panic, weeping and saying, "Evil people are coming." A maid had already consorted with an outsider and placed a ladder against the wall, climbing it inside. The deity was dressed and about to be stripped, and they took the deity's image, burned it, and then left.

    There is a Purple Lady deity, passed down since ancient times: she was a concubine in someone's household, envied by the senior wife, and often tasked with dirty work. On the fifteenth day of the first lunar month, she died out of indignation, so people on that day make her image, welcoming her at night at the latrine or pigsty. They pray, saying, "Mr. Zixu is not here," which is the name of her husband. "Mrs. Cao also returns to Cao," which is the senior wife. "The young mistress can come out and play," and the one who throws feels it heavy, meaning the deity has come. They offer wine and fruit, and also see her face radiant with color, as she immediately dances and moves restlessly, able to predict many things, foretell the future, and oversee silkworms and mulberry. She is also good at shooting hooks; if she likes it, she stretches her arms; if she dislikes it, she lies on her back. The Meng family of Pingchang always did not believe, and they personally tried to throw, but she immediately jumped over the thatched house and left, and was never seen again. [44]

    鸟伤陈氏有女未醮，著屐径上大枫树颠，了无危惧，顾曰：“我应为神，今便长去。惟左苍右黄当暂归耳。”家人悉出见之，举手辞诀，于是飘耸轻越，极睇乃没。人不了苍黄之意，每春辄以苍狗秋黄犬设祀于树下。
    　　剡县西乡有杨郎庙，县有人一先事之，后就祭酒。侯褚求入大道，遇谯郡楼无陇，诣褚共至神舍，烧神座器服，无陇乞将一扇。经岁，无陇闻有乘马人呼“楼无陇”数四声云：“汝故不还杨明府扇耶！”言毕回骑而去，陇遂得痿病死。
    　　晋武太始初，萧惠明为吴兴太守，郡界有卞山，山下有项羽庙，相传云：羽多居郡厅事，前后太守不敢上厅。惠明谓纲纪曰：孔季恭鲁为此郡，未闻有灾。遂命盛设筵榻接宾。未几，惠明忽见一人长丈余，张弓挟矢向之，既而不见，因发背，旬日而殒。 [45]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [45]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鳥傷陳氏有女未醮 - 鳥傷陳氏 is a proper name, and 有女未醮 means had a daughter who had not been married. 未醮 is an archaic term for an unmarried woman, specifically one who has not undergone the marriage ceremony.
    • 著屐徑上大枫树颠 - 著屐 means wearing clogs (traditional footwear), 穿徑上 means directly climbed, and 大枫树颠 means the top of a large paulownia tree. 穿徑上 is a colloquial expression indicating direct action without hesitation.
    • 了无危惧 - 了无 means completely without, and 危惧 means fear or danger. Together, it conveys a sense of complete fearlessness.
    • 顾曰 - 顾 means turned to look or glanced, and 曰 means said. It indicates a turn of speech.
    • 我应为神 - 应为 means should become or will become.
    • 今便长去 - 今便 means now then or at this moment, and 长去 means depart forever.
    • 惟左苍右黄当暂归耳 - 惟 means only or just, 左苍右黄 refers to a left blue and right yellow dog, 当 means should or must, 暂归 means temporarily return, and 耳 is a particle used to emphasize the statement.
    • 家人悉出见之 - 悉出 means all came out, and 见之 means to see her.
    • 举手辞诀 - 举手 means raised her hand, and 辞诀 means farewell or farewell gesture.
    • 于是飘耸轻越 - 于是 means then or thereupon, 飘耸 means flew up, and 轻越 means lightly crossed.
    • 极睇乃没 - 极睇 means looked far away, and 乃没 means then disappeared.
    • 苍黄之意 - 苍黄 can mean green and yellow or ambiguous, and 之意 means the meaning or the intention.
    • 每春辄以苍狗秋黄犬设祀于树下 - 每春辄 means every spring, 以 means with, 苍狗秋黄犬 refers to a blue dog and a yellow dog, 设祀 means to set up an offering, and 于树下 means under the tree.
    • 剡县西乡有杨郎庙 - 剡县 is a place name, 西乡 means western village, and 有杨郎庙 means has a temple dedicated to Yang Lang.
    • 县有人一先事之 - 县有人 means a person from the county, 一先 means firstly or initially, and 事之 means served it.
    • 后就祭酒 - 后 means later, 就 means attended, and 祭酒 means a ritual wine offering or a high-ranking official.
    • 侯褚求入大道 - 侯褚 is a proper name, 求 means sought, and 入大道 means entered the main road.
    • 遇谯郡楼无陇 - 遇 means encountered, 谯郡 is a place name, and 楼无陇 is a proper name.
    • 诣褚共至神舍 - 诣 means went to, 褚 is a proper name, 共 means together, and 至神舍 means to the shrine.
    • 烧神座器服 - 烧 means burned, 神座 means the seat of the deity, and 器服 means vessels and clothing.
    • 无陇乞将一扇 - 无陇 is a proper name, 乞 means asked for, and 将一扇 means to take one fan.
    • 经岁 - 经岁 means after a year.
    • 无陇闻有乘马人呼‘楼无陇’数四声 - 闻 means heard, 有乘马人 means a person riding a horse, 呼 means called out, 楼无陇 is a proper name, and 数四声 means four times.
    • 汝故不还杨明府扇耶 - 汝 means you, 故 means why, 不还 means not returned, 杨明府 is a proper name, and 扇耶 means the fan?
    • 言毕回骑而去 - 言毕 means after speaking, 回骑 means turned back on horse, and 而去 means and left.
    • 陇遂得痿病死 - 陇 is a proper name, 遂 means then, 得 means contracted, 痿病 means paralysis, and 死 means died.
    • 晋武太始初 - 晋武 refers to Emperor Wu of Jin, 太始 is his reign title, and 初 means beginning.
    • 萧惠明为吴兴太守 - 萧惠明 is a proper name, 为 means served as, and 吴兴太守 means the governor of Wu County.
    • 郡界有卞山 - 郡界 means within the county, 有 means has, and 卞山 is a place name.
    • 山下有项羽庙 - 山下 means at the foot of the mountain, 有 means has, and 项羽庙 means a temple dedicated to Xiang Yu.
    • 相传云 - 相传 means it is said, and 云 means that.
    • 羽多居郡厅事 - 羽 refers to Xiang Yu, 多居 means often stayed, and 郡厅事 means the county hall.
    • 前后太守不敢上厅 - 前后 means before and after, 太守 means governor, 不敢 means did not dare, and 上厅 means enter the hall.
    • 惠明谓纲纪曰 - 惠明 is a proper name, 谓 means told, 纲纪 means officials, and 曰 means said.
    • 孔季恭鲁为此郡 - 孔季恭 is a proper name, 鲁 means did not, and 为此郡 means for this county.
    • 未闻有灾 - 未闻 means had not heard, and 有灾 means of disaster.
    • 遂命盛设筵榻接宾 - 遂 means then, 命 means ordered, 盛设 means lavishly set up, 筵榻 means banquet tables, and 接宾 means to welcome guests.
    • 未几 - 未几 means not long after.
    • 惠明忽见一人长丈余 - 惠明 is a proper name, 忽见 means suddenly saw, 一人 means a person, and 长丈余 means more than ten feet tall.
    • 张弓挟矢向之 - 张弓 means drawn his bow, 挟矢 means held an arrow, and 向之 means towards him.
    • 既而不见 - 既而 means shortly afterward, and 不见 means did not see.
    • 因发背 - 因 means thereupon, and 发背 means developed a back ailment.
    • 旬日而殒 - 旬日 means ten days, and 而殒 means and died.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陈氏 - A family name, possibly referring to a specific family in ancient China.
    • 鸟伤 - A proper name, possibly referring to a person or a place.
    • 大枫树 - A large paulownia tree, which is significant in Chinese culture and often associated with longevity and nobility.
    • 杨郎庙 - A temple dedicated to Yang Lang, a historical figure or deity.
    • 剡县 - A county in ancient China, now part of modern-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 西乡 - The western village or area of a county.
    • 侯褚 - A proper name, possibly referring to a person of high rank.
    • 谯郡 - A county in ancient China, now part of modern-day Henan Province.
    • 楼无陇 - A proper name, possibly referring to a person.
    • 晋武 - Emperor Wu of Jin, a ruler during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 太始 - A reign title of Emperor Wu of Jin.
    • 萧惠明 - A proper name, possibly referring to a historical figure.
    • 吴兴太守 - The governor of Wu County.
    • 卞山 - A place name, possibly referring to a mountain in ancient China.
    • 项羽庙 - A temple dedicated to Xiang Yu, a historical figure.
    • 孔季恭 - A proper name, possibly referring to a historical figure.
    • 鲁 - Did not, indicating a negative action or state.
    • 纲纪 - Officials or administrators.

    A Woman with Two Heads and Four Arms

    During the reign of Emperor Ling, a woman from Luoyang was born with two heads and four arms.

    In ancient times, there was a woman from the Tianshan family named Niao Shang, who had a daughter who had not yet been married. One day, she wore clogs and climbed directly to the top of a large paulownia tree, showing no fear. She turned and said, "I should become a spirit now, and I will depart forever. Only the left blue and right yellow dogs should temporarily return." Her family all came out to see her, and she raised her hand in farewell. Then, she flew up lightly and crossed the air, looking far away before disappearing. People did not understand the meaning of "left blue and right yellow," so every spring, they would set up an offering with a blue dog and a yellow dog under the tree.

    In the western village of Shan County, there was a temple dedicated to Yang Lang. A person from the county first served the temple and later attended a ritual wine offering. Hou Chu sought to enter the main road and encountered Lou Wu Long from Qiao County. They went together to the shrine and burned the vessels and clothing of the deity. Lou Wu Long asked to take one fan. After a year, Lou Wu Long heard a person riding a horse call out "Lou Wu Long" four times, saying, "Why haven't you returned the fan of Yang Mingfu?" After speaking, the rider turned back on horse and left. Lou Wu Long then contracted paralysis and died.

    During the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, Xiao Huiming served as the governor of Wu County. Within the county, there was a mountain called Bian Mountain, at the foot of which was a temple dedicated to Xiang Yu. It was said that Xiang Yu often stayed in the county hall, and previous and subsequent governors did not dare to enter the hall. Xiao Huiming told his officials, "Kong Jigong did not dare to serve this county, and I have not heard of any disasters." He then ordered the lavish setting up of banquet tables to welcome guests. Not long after, Xiao Huiming suddenly saw a person more than ten feet tall, drawing his bow and holding an arrow towards him. Shortly afterward, he did not see the person again, and he developed a back ailment, dying after ten days. [45]

    元嘉九年二月二十四日，长山张舒奄见一人著朱衣，平上帻，手捉青柄马鞭，云如汝可教，便随我去。见素丝绳系长梯来下，舒上梯，乃造大城，绮堂洞室，地如黄金。有一人长大，不巾帻，独坐绛纱帐中，语舒曰：“主者误取汝赐汝秘术卜占，勿贪钱贿。”舒亦不觉，受之。
    　　元嘉四年五月三日，会稽余姚钱夜出屋，后为虎所取，十八日乃自还。说虎初取之时，至一宫府，入重门，见一人凭几而坐，形貌伟壮，左右侍者三十余人，谓曰：“吾欲使汝知术数之法，故令虎汝。汝无惧也。”留十五昼夜，语诸要术，尽教道之方。受法毕，便遣令还而不知道，即使人送出门，乃见归路。既得还家，大知卜占，无幽不验，经年乃卒。
    　　十二棋卜出自张文成，受法于黄石公，行师用兵，万不失一。逮至东方塑，审以占众事，自此以后，秘而不传。晋于康初，襄城寺法味道人忽遇一老公，著黄皮衣，竹筒盛此书以授法味，无何失所在，遂复传流于世云。 [46]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [46]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 奄见一人 - Literal expression meaning 'suddenly saw a person,' often used to describe unexpected or supernatural encounters.
    • 平上帻 - Refers to a flat-topped headgear, a common style in ancient China.
    • 青柄马鞭 - A whip with a green handle, likely symbolic or decorative.
    • 如汝可教 - Means 'if you can be taught,' implying a test or condition.
    • 素丝绳系长梯 - A white silk rope tied to a long ladder, likely a metaphorical or magical tool.
    • 绮堂洞室 - Describes a luxurious and spacious room, with '绮堂' meaning 'embroidered hall' and '洞室' meaning 'spacious room.'
    • 地如黄金 - A metaphorical description of the ground being as golden as gold, likely symbolizing wealth or divine presence.
    • 主者误取汝赐汝秘术卜占 - A complex phrase meaning 'the master mistakenly took you and bestowed upon you secret divination skills,' suggesting a divine or supernatural intervention.
    • 勿贪钱贿 - A warning against greed, specifically against coveting money or bribes.
    • 受之 - Means 'received it,' referring to the secret divination skills.
    • 夜出屋 - Literally 'went out of the house at night,' likely referring to a supernatural or dreamlike experience.
    • 为虎所取 - Means 'taken by a tiger,' likely a metaphorical or literal encounter with a tiger.
    • 至一宫府 - Means 'arrived at a palace or government office,' suggesting a place of authority or divinity.
    • 入重门 - Means 'entered through heavy doors,' emphasizing the grandeur or importance of the place.
    • 形貌伟壮 - Describes someone as having a great and strong appearance.
    • 左右侍者三十余人 - Means 'thirty or more attendants on both sides,' indicating a high status or divine presence.
    • 欲使汝知术数之法 - Means 'wants to make you know the methods of divination,' suggesting a divine or supernatural teaching.
    • 无惧也 - Means 'do not fear,' a reassurance given by the divine or supernatural figure.
    • 留十五昼夜 - Means 'stayed for fifteen days and nights,' indicating a prolonged period of teaching or experience.
    • 语诸要术 - Means 'taught various essential techniques,' referring to the skills or knowledge imparted.
    • 尽教道之方 - Means 'taught all the methods of teaching,' indicating a comprehensive education.
    • 受法毕 - Means 'completed the learning of the method,' indicating the end of the teaching period.
    • 不知道 - Means 'does not know the way,' suggesting a lack of understanding or direction.
    • 即使人送出门 - Means 'had someone escort them out,' indicating a formal or divine dismissal.
    • 乃见归路 - Means 'then saw the way back,' suggesting a return to normalcy or reality.
    • 大知卜占 - Means 'greatly understood divination,' indicating a significant improvement in divination skills.
    • 无幽不验 - Means 'nothing hidden is unverified,' suggesting that the divination is highly accurate.
    • 经年乃卒 - Means 'died after many years,' indicating a long life after the experience.
    • 十二棋卜 - Refers to a specific method of divination using twelve pieces.
    • 出自张文成 - Means 'originated from Zhang Wencheng,' indicating the source of the method.
    • 受法于黄石公 - Means 'received the method from Yellow Stone Elder,' indicating a legendary or historical source.
    • 行师用兵 - Means 'in military campaigns,' indicating the practical application of the divination method.
    • 万不失一 - Means 'never fails once,' suggesting the method's reliability.
    • 逮至东方朔 - Means 'up to Dongfang Shuo,' indicating a historical figure who used the method.
    • 审以占众事 - Means 'accurately predicted many things,' indicating the method's effectiveness.
    • 自此以后，秘而不传 - Means 'since then, it has been kept secret and not passed on,' indicating the method's rarity.
    • 晋于康初 - Means 'in the early period of Yu Kang of Jin,' indicating a specific historical period.
    • 襄城寺 - Refers to Xiangcheng Temple, a specific place.
    • 法味道人 - Refers to a monk named Fawei, indicating a specific person.
    • 著黄皮衣 - Means 'wearing yellow skin clothing,' likely a description of the monk's attire.
    • 竹筒盛此书 - Means 'a bamboo tube containing this book,' indicating a physical object.
    • 无何失所在 - Means 'soon lost his whereabouts,' indicating the mysterious disappearance of the figure.
    • 遂复传流于世云 - Means 'and so it was passed down to the world,' indicating the method's eventual dissemination.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉九年 - Refers to the ninth year of the Yuanchi era during the Liu Song dynasty (432 AD).
    • 长山 - Refers to Changshan, a specific place.
    • 张舒 - Refers to a person named Zhang Shu, indicating a specific individual.
    • 元嘉四年 - Refers to the fourth year of the Yuanchi era during the Liu Song dynasty (427 AD).
    • 会稽余姚 - Refers to Yuyao, a specific place in Kuaiji (modern-day Zhejiang Province).
    • 钱 - Refers to a person named Qian Jiao, indicating a specific individual.
    • 虎 - Refers to a tiger, often used metaphorically in Chinese literature to represent danger or supernatural power.
    • 宫府 - Refers to a palace or government office, indicating a place of authority.
    • 重门 - Refers to heavy doors, emphasizing the grandeur or importance of the place.
    • 形貌伟壮 - Describes someone as having a great and strong appearance, often used for deities or important figures.
    • 左右侍者三十余人 - Indicates a high status or divine presence, with thirty or more attendants.
    • 术数之法 - Refers to methods of divination or numerology, indicating a specific type of knowledge.
    • 秘术卜占 - Refers to secret divination techniques, indicating a rare or esoteric form of knowledge.
    • 钱贿 - Refers to money or bribes, often used in a moral context to warn against greed.
    • 夜出屋 - Refers to a supernatural or dreamlike experience, often used in Chinese literature to describe unusual events.
    • 为虎所取 - Refers to a metaphorical or literal encounter with a tiger, often used to represent danger or supernatural power.
    • 至一宫府 - Refers to a palace or government office, indicating a place of authority.
    • 入重门 - Refers to entering through heavy doors, emphasizing the grandeur or importance of the place.
    • 形貌伟壮 - Describes someone as having a great and strong appearance, often used for deities or important figures.
    • 左右侍者三十余人 - Indicates a high status or divine presence, with thirty or more attendants.
    • 欲使汝知术数之法 - Refers to the desire to teach divination methods, indicating a divine or supernatural teaching.
    • 无惧也 - Refers to a reassurance given by the divine or supernatural figure, indicating a lack of fear.
    • 留十五昼夜 - Refers to a prolonged period of teaching or experience, indicating the depth of the learning.
    • 语诸要术 - Refers to the teaching of various essential techniques, indicating the comprehensive nature of the education.
    • 尽教道之方 - Refers to the teaching of all the methods of teaching, indicating a complete education.
    • 受法毕 - Refers to the completion of the learning of the method, indicating the end of the teaching period.
    • 不知道 - Refers to a lack of understanding or direction, indicating a need for guidance.
    • 即使人送出门 - Refers to a formal or divine dismissal, indicating the end of the experience.
    • 乃见归路 - Refers to seeing the way back, indicating a return to normalcy or reality.
    • 大知卜占 - Refers to a significant improvement in divination skills, indicating the effectiveness of the teaching.
    • 无幽不验 - Refers to the high accuracy of the divination, indicating its reliability.
    • 经年乃卒 - Refers to dying after many years, indicating a long life after the experience.
    • 十二棋卜 - Refers to a specific method of divination using twelve pieces, indicating a unique form of divination.
    • 出自张文成 - Refers to the origin of the method from Zhang Wencheng, indicating a specific historical figure.
    • 受法于黄石公 - Refers to receiving the method from Yellow Stone Elder, indicating a legendary or historical source.
    • 行师用兵 - Refers to military campaigns, indicating the practical application of the divination method.
    • 万不失一 - Refers to the method's reliability.
    • 逮至东方朔 - Refers to a historical figure who used the method.
    • 审以占众事 - Refers to the method's effectiveness.
    • 自此以后，秘而不传 - Refers to the method's rarity.
    • 晋于康初 - Refers to a specific historical period.
    • 襄城寺 - Refers to Xiangcheng Temple, a specific place.
    • 法味道人 - Refers to a monk named Fawei, indicating a specific person.
    • 著黄皮衣 - Refers to the monk's attire.
    • 竹筒盛此书 - Refers to a physical object containing the book.
    • 无何失所在 - Refers to the mysterious disappearance of the figure.
    • 遂复传流于世云 - Refers to the method's eventual dissemination.

    In the ninth year of the Yuanchi era, on the twenty-fourth day of the second month, Zhang Shu of Changshan suddenly saw a person wearing a red robe, with a flat-topped headgear, and holding a whip with a green handle. The person said, "If you can be taught, follow me." Zhang Shu saw a white silk rope tied to a long ladder descending, climbed the ladder, and arrived at a grand city with luxurious halls and spacious rooms, the ground as golden as gold. There was a tall person without a headgear, sitting alone in a crimson silk tent, who said to Zhang Shu, "The master mistakenly took you and bestowed upon you secret divination skills; do not covet money or bribes." Zhang Shu did not realize it and received the skills.

    In the fourth year of the Yuanchi era, on the third day of the fifth month, Qian Jiao of Yuyao in Kuaiji went out of his house at night and was later taken by a tiger. On the eighteenth day, he returned on his own. He said that when the tiger first took him, he arrived at a palace or government office, entered through heavy doors, and saw a person leaning on a table, with a great and strong appearance, and thirty or more attendants on both sides. The person said, "I want to make you know the methods of divination, so I sent the tiger to take you. Do not fear." He stayed for fifteen days and nights, taught various essential techniques, and taught all the methods of teaching. Qian Jiao completed the learning of the method, was sent back but did not know the way, and was escorted out. He then saw the way back. After returning home, he greatly understood divination, with nothing hidden unverified, and died after many years.

    The Twelve-Piece Divination method originated from Zhang Wencheng, who received the method from Yellow Stone Elder. It is used in military campaigns and never fails once. Up to Dongfang Shuo, it was accurately used to predict many things, but since then, it has been kept secret and not passed on. In the early period of Yu Kang of Jin, a monk named Fawei at Xiangcheng Temple suddenly encountered an old man wearing yellow skin clothing, who gave him a bamboo tube containing this book. Soon, the old man lost his whereabouts, and so it was passed down to the world. [46]

    历阳石秀之，倏有一人著平巾褶，语之云：闻君巧侔班匠，刻几尤妙。太山府君相召。秀之自陈云：刘政能造。其人乃去。数旬而刘殒，石氏犹存。刘作几有名，遂以致毙。
    　　会稽石亭埭有大枫树，其中空朽，每雨水辄满溢。有估客载生至此，聊放一头于朽树中，以为狡狯。村民见之，以鱼非树中之物，咸谓是神，乃依树起屋，宰牲祭祀，未尝虚日，因遂名父庙。人有祈请及秽慢，则祸福立至。后估客返，见其如此，即取作霍，于是遂绝。
    　　吴猛还豫章，附载客船，一宿行千里。同行客视船下有两龙载之，船不着水。
    　　陶侃字士行，微时遭父艰，有人长九尺，端悦通刺，字不可识，心怪非常。出庭拜送，此人告侃曰：吾是王子晋，君有巨相，故来相看。于是脱衣合服仙羽，升鹄而腾。 [47]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [47]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 倏有一人著平巾褶 - Sudden appearance of a person wearing a flat cap and short cloak; describes an unexpected encounter with a supernatural being.
    • 闻君巧侔班匠 - Hearing that your craftsmanship matches that of the finest artisans; a compliment to the skill of the craftsman.
    • 刻几尤妙 - Especially skilled at carving tables; highlights the craftsman's particular talent.
    • 太山府君相召 - The Lord of Tai Shan has summoned you; a reference to a supernatural entity summoning the craftsman.
    • 自陈云 - Stated that; indicates the craftsman's response to the supernatural being.
    • 数旬而刘殒 - After several weeks, Liu died; marks the passage of time and the death of Liu.
    • 石氏犹存 - The Shi family still exists; contrasts the fate of Liu with that of the Shi family.
    • 有名 - Famous; indicates the renown of Liu's work.
    • 遂以致毙 - And thus led to his death; suggests that the fame of his work contributed to his demise.
    • 估客 - Merchant traveler; refers to a person who travels for trade.
    • 生 - Live fish; a reference to the type of fish being transported.
    • 狡狯 - Playful trick; suggests a lighthearted act.
    • 咸谓是神 - All considered it a god; indicates the villagers' belief in the supernatural.
    • 依树起屋 - Built a house around the tree; describes the villagers' actions.
    • 宰牲祭祀 - Sacrificed animals for worship; a religious practice.
    • 未尝虚日 - Never a day without sacrifice; emphasizes the frequency of the sacrifices.
    • 因遂名父庙 - Thus named the Fish Father Temple; is the name given to the temple.
    • 人有祈请及秽慢 - People who pray or show disrespect; refers to the behavior of visitors.
    • 祸福立至 - Misfortune or good fortune immediately follows; indicates the immediate consequences of behavior.
    • 附载客船 - Joined a passenger boat; describes the mode of travel.
    • 一宿行千里 - Traveled a thousand miles in one night; a hyperbolic description of speed.
    • 同行客视船下有两龙载之 - The fellow passengers saw two dragons beneath the boat carrying them; describes a supernatural event.
    • 船不着水 - The boat did not touch the water; indicates the boat's miraculous movement.
    • 陶侃字士行 - Tao Kan, styled Shixing; provides the name and style name of the historical figure.
    • 微时 - When he was poor; indicates the time of Tao Kan's poverty.
    • 遭父艰 - Suffered the loss of his father; describes a personal tragedy.
    • 长九尺 - Nine feet tall; describes the height of the supernatural being.
    • 端悦通刺 - Elegant and well-mannered, with a clear and smooth manner; describes the demeanor of the supernatural being.
    • 字不可识 - The name cannot be recognized; indicates the illegibility of the name.
    • 心怪非常 - Found it very strange; describes the craftsman's reaction.
    • 出庭拜送 - Went out to the courtyard to bid farewell; describes the craftsman's actions.
    • 吾是王子晋 - I am Prince Wang Zijun; is the supernatural being's self-identification.
    • 君有巨相 - You have a great appearance; a compliment to the craftsman.
    • 故来相看 - So I came to see you; explains the supernatural being's visit.
    • 脱衣合服仙羽 - Removed his clothes and put on celestial robes; describes the supernatural being's transformation.
    • 升鹄而腾 - Rode a crane and ascended into the clouds; describes the supernatural being's departure.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 历阳 - A place name in Anhui Province, China.
    • 石秀之 - A craftsman from the Shi family.
    • 刘政 - Another craftsman, whose name is used as a substitute.
    • 泰山府君 - The Lord of Tai Shan, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 王子晋 - Prince Wang Zijun, a legendary figure in Chinese mythology.
    • 陶侃 - A historical figure, known for his military and political achievements.
    • 吴猛 - Another historical figure, known for his supernatural experiences.

    Liyang's Shi Xiu zhi suddenly encountered a person wearing a flat cap and short cloak, who told him: "I have heard that your craftsmanship matches that of the finest artisans, especially skilled at carving tables. The Lord of Tai Shan has summoned you." Shi Xiu zhi replied, saying, "Liu Zheng can do it." The person then left. After several weeks, Liu Zheng died, while the Shi family still existed. Liu Zheng was famous for his work, and thus led to his death.

    In Kuaiji, there was a large paulownia tree at Shitingdai, which was hollow and rotten. Every time it rained, water would fill it up. A merchant traveler loaded live fish onto the boat and playfully placed one in the hollow tree. The villagers saw this and, thinking the fish was a god, built a house around the tree, sacrificed animals for worship, and never failed to offer sacrifices on any day. Thus, they named it the Fish Father Temple. People who prayed or showed disrespect would immediately experience misfortune or good fortune. Later, the merchant traveler returned and saw the situation, took the fish, and cooked it, and so the temple's activity ceased.

    Wu Meng returned to Yuzhang and joined a passenger boat, traveling a thousand miles in one night. His fellow passengers saw two dragons beneath the boat carrying them, and the boat did not touch the water.

    Tao Kan, styled Shixing, was poor when he suffered the loss of his father. One day, a person nine feet tall, elegant and well-mannered, with a clear and smooth manner, came to visit. His name could not be recognized, and Tao Kan found it very strange. He went out to the courtyard to bid farewell, and the person told Tao Kan, "I am Prince Wang Zijun. You have a great appearance, so I came to see you." Then, the person removed his clothes and put on celestial robes, rode a crane, and ascended into the clouds. [47]

    晋太元末，湘东姚祖为郡吏，经衡山，望岩下有数年少并执笔作书。祖谓是行侣休息，乃枉道过之。未至百许步，少年相与翻然飞，遗一纸书在坐处，前数句古时字，自后皆鸟迹。
    　　东阳徐公居在长山下，常登岭，见二人坐于山崖对饮。公索之，二人乃与一小杯。公饮之遂醉，后常不食，亦不饥。
    　　昔有人乘马山行，遥望岫里有二老翁相对樗指，遂下马造焉。以策注地而观之，自谓俄顷，视其马鞭，摧然已烂，顾瞻其马，鞍骸枯朽。既还至家，无复亲属，一恸而绝。
    　　陈思王曹植字子建，尝登鱼山，临东阿。忽闻岩岫里有诵经声，清通深亮，远谷流响，肃然有灵气，不觉敛衿祗敬，便有终焉之志。即效而则之。今之梵唱皆植依拟所造，一云：陈思王游山，忽闻空里诵经声，清远道亮，解音者则而写之，为神仙声。道士效之，作步虚声也。 [48]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [48]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 枉道 - To take a detour or make an unnecessary journey.
    • 遗一纸书 - To leave behind a piece of paper with writing.
    • 鸟迹 - A metaphorical term for writing that resembles bird tracks, possibly referring to a form of ancient script.
    • 对饮 - To drink together.
    • 索之 - To ask for something.
    • 摧然已烂 - To become rotten and decayed.
    • 顾瞻 - To look back or turn around to look.
    • 清通深亮 - Clear, profound, and bright.
    • 远谷流响 - The sound echoing in distant valleys.
    • 肃然有灵气 - Reverently feeling a divine presence.
    • 敛衿祗敬 - To dress formally and show deep respect.
    • 终焉之志 - A desire to end one's life or retire there.
    • 效而则之 - To imitate and follow.
    • 梵唱 - Buddhist chanting.
    • 神仙声 - Sounds of immortals.
    • 步虚声 - Taoist chanting.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • The Taiyuan era (376-396 AD) was a period of the Eastern Jin dynasty, marked by political instability and cultural development.
    • A region in Hunan province, historically significant for its cultural and political influence.
    • One of the Five Sacred Mountains in China, known for its scenic beauty and spiritual significance.
    • A place name, likely referring to a region in Zhejiang province.
    • A mountain name, possibly referring to a specific location in the region.
    • A prominent literary figure of the Three Kingdoms period, known for his poetry and literary contributions.
    • A mountain associated with Cao Zhi's literary activities.
    • A place name, possibly referring to a region in Shandong province.
    • A mountain associated with Cao Zhi's literary activities.
    • Buddhist chanting, a form of religious music and practice.
    • Taoist chanting, another form of religious music and practice.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋太元末 - The Taiyuan era of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 湘东 - A region in Hunan province.
    • 衡山 - A mountain in Hunan province.
    • 东阳 - A place name, likely in Zhejiang province.
    • 长山 - A mountain in the region.
    • 陈思王曹植 - A historical figure, Cao Zhi.
    • 鱼山 - A mountain in Shandong province.
    • 东阿 - A place name, likely in Shandong province.
    • 梵唱 - Buddhist chanting.
    • 步虚声 - Taoist chanting.

    At the end of the Taiyuan era of the Jin dynasty, Yao Zu from Xiangdong served as a local official and passed through Mount Heng. He saw several young men sitting under a rock, each holding a brush and writing. Zu thought they were resting travelers and took a detour to pass by. Before he reached within a hundred steps, the young men suddenly flew away, leaving behind a piece of paper with writing on it. The first few lines were in ancient script, while the rest resembled bird tracks.

    A man from Dongyang named Xu Gong lived at the foot of Chang Mountain. He often climbed a hill and saw two men sitting on a cliff, drinking together. Gong asked them for a drink, and they gave him a small cup. After drinking it, he became drunk and later never ate again, nor did he feel hungry.

    Once, a man was riding through the mountains when he saw two old men pointing at each other in a cave from afar. He dismounted and approached them. He placed his whip on the ground and watched them. He thought it was only a short while, but when he looked at his whip, it had already rotted. Looking back at his horse, he found its saddle and bones were dry and decayed. When he returned home, he had no more relatives, and he died of grief.

    Prince Cao Zhi of Chen, also known as Zijian, once climbed Fish Mountain and approached Dong'e. Suddenly, he heard the sound of reciting scriptures from within a cave. The sound was clear, profound, and bright, echoing through distant valleys. He felt a reverent presence and dressed formally in deep respect. He then developed a desire to end his life there. He imitated and followed the sound. Today's Buddhist chanting is all based on Cao Zhi's imitation. One account says that while traveling through the mountains, Cao Zhi suddenly heard the sound of reciting scriptures in the air, which was clear and distant. Those who understood the sound wrote it down, calling it the "sound of immortals." Taoists imitated it and created the "step-by-step empty sound." [48]

    沙门释慧远栖神庐岳，常有游龙翔其前。远公有奴，有石掷中，乃腾跃上升。有顷，风云飚□。公知是龙之所兴，登山烧香，会僧齐声唱偈，于是霹雳回向投龙之石，雨乃除。
    　　沙门竺慧炽，新野人，住江陵四层佛寺。永初二年卒，弟子为设七日会。其日将夕，烧香竟，沙门道贤因往视炽弟子。至房前，忽暖暧若人形，详视乃慧炽也。容貌衣服不异生时，谓贤曰：“君且食肉美否？”曰“美”。炽曰：“我生不能断肉，今落饿鬼地狱。”道贤惧，未及得答，炽复言：“汝若不信，试看我背后。”乃回背示贤，见三黄狗形，半似驴，眼甚赤光，照户内，状欲啮炽而复止，贤骇怖闷绝，良久乃苏。 [49]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [49]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 沙门释慧远栖神庐岳 - 沙门指佛教僧人，释慧远是僧人的名字，栖神意为居住在精神状态中，庐岳指庐山，中国著名山脉。
    • 常有游龙翔其前 - 游龙比喻龙在僧人面前飞舞或移动，象征着超自然或神圣的存在。
    • 远公有奴 - 远公是对僧人的尊称，有奴表示他有一个仆人。
    • 有石掷中，乃腾跃上升 - 有石掷中指石头被投掷并击中某物，乃腾跃上升描述了突然的跳跃或上升。
    • 有顷，风云飚□ - 有顷指一会儿之后，风云指风和云，飚□是一个不完整的词语，可能指突然的风暴或旋风。
    • 公知是龙之所兴 - 公是对僧人的尊称，知指知道，是龙之所兴指龙的活动或显现。
    • 登山烧香，会僧齐声唱偈 - 登山指爬山，烧香是烧香的仪式，会僧指聚集僧侣，齐声唱偈指一起唱诵佛教偈语。
    • 于是霹雳回向投龙之石 - 于是指因此，霹雳指雷电，回向指转向，投龙之石指投向龙的石头。
    • 云雨乃除 - 云雨指云和雨，乃除指它们被驱散或清除。
    • 沙门竺慧炽，新野人 - 沙门指佛教僧人，竺慧炽是僧人的名字，新野人表示他是来自新野的地方人。
    • 住江陵四层佛寺 - 住指居住，江陵是地名，四层佛寺指四层的佛教寺庙。
    • 永初二年卒 - 永初二年指永初时期的第二年（398-401年），卒指去世。
    • 弟子为设七日会 - 弟子指僧人的弟子，为设指安排或举行，七日会指七天的纪念会。
    • 其日将夕，烧香竟 - 其日将夕指当天傍晚，烧香指烧香的仪式，竟指完成。
    • 沙门道贤因往视炽弟子 - 沙门道贤是另一个僧人的名字，因往视指去拜访，炽弟子指已故僧人的弟子。
    • 至房前，忽暖暧若人形 - 至房前指到达房间前，忽暖暧描述突然的温暖或模糊的外观，若人形指像人形。
    • 详视乃慧炽也 - 详视指仔细看，乃慧炽也指确认是已故僧人。
    • 容貌衣服不异生时 - 容貌指外貌，衣服指衣物，不异生时指与生前无异。
    • 谓贤曰：“君且食肉美否？” - 谓指说，贤是对僧人的尊称，君且食肉美否指“你吃的肉好吃吗？”
    • 曰“美” - 曰指说，美指好吃或美味。
    • 炽曰：“我生不能断肉，今落饿鬼地狱。” - 炽曰指已故僧人说，我生不能断肉指“我生前无法戒除肉食”，今落饿鬼地狱指“现在我堕入饿鬼地狱”。
    • 道贤惧，未及得答 - 道贤惧指僧人感到害怕，未及得答指没有时间回答。
    • 炽复言：“汝若不信，试看我背后。” - 炽复言指已故僧人再次说，汝若不信指“如果你不相信”，试看我背后指“试着看看我的背后”。
    • 乃回背示贤 - 乃回背指转过身，示贤指给僧人看。
    • 见三黄狗形，半似驴，眼甚赤光，照户内 - 见三黄狗形指看到三个黄色的狗形，半似驴指半像驴，眼甚赤光指眼睛非常红光，照户内指照亮房间。
    • 状欲啮炽而复止，贤骇怖闷绝，良久乃苏 - 状欲啮炽而复止指它们似乎想咬僧人但又停止，贤骇怖闷绝指僧人感到极度恐惧并昏厥，良久乃苏指过了一会儿才苏醒。

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沙门释慧远 - 历史上的人物，中国著名的佛教僧人。
    • 庐岳 - 庐山，中国历史上和文化上重要的山脉，常与精神修养联系在一起。
    • 游龙 - 中国文化的隐喻，通常象征力量、神性或超自然现象。
    • 永初二年 - 永初时期的第二年（398-401年），中国历史上的一个时期。
    • 江陵四层佛寺 - 江陵的一座四层佛教寺庙，具有宗教意义。
    • 饿鬼地狱 - 佛教宇宙观中的一个概念，指那些生前过度放纵欲望，特别是贪婪的人死后所处的苦难领域。

    Buddhist Monk Huiyuan, known as Farong, dwelled in a spiritual state on Mount Lu. He often had a dragon flying in front of him. Farong had a servant, and when a stone was thrown and hit the dragon, it suddenly leaped upward. After a short while, there was a whirlwind of wind and clouds. Farong knew that this was the dragon's activity, so he climbed the mountain to burn incense, gathered monks, and they chanted Buddhist verses together. Then, thunder struck the stone that was thrown at the dragon, and the clouds and rain were dispelled.

    Buddhist Monk Zhi Hui Chi, from Xinye, resided in a four-story Buddhist temple in Jiangling. He passed away in the second year of the Yongchu era, and his disciples arranged a seven-day memorial service for him. As the day was ending and the incense was burned, the monk Dao Xian went to visit the disciples of Hui Chi. Upon arriving in front of the room, there was a sudden warm, hazy figure that appeared like a human form. Upon closer inspection, it was identified as Hui Chi. His appearance and clothing were no different from when he was alive. Hui Chi said to Dao Xian, "Are you enjoying the meat?" Dao Xian replied, "It's delicious." Hui Chi then said, "I couldn't abstain from meat in life, and now I've fallen into the realm of hungry ghosts." Dao Xian was terrified and didn't have time to reply. Hui Chi said again, "If you don't believe, try looking at my back." He turned his back to show Dao Xian, and he saw three yellow dog-like figures, half like donkeys, with very red eyes that illuminated the room. They seemed to want to bite Hui Chi but stopped. Dao Xian was terrified, fainted, and regained consciousness after a long time. [49]

    灵味寺在建康钟山蒋林里，永初三年沙门法意起造。晋末有高逸沙门，莫显名迹，岩栖谷隐，常在钟山之阿。一夜，忽闻怪石崩坠，声振林薄。明旦履，惟见清泉湛然，聚徒结宇，号曰灵味。
    　　武陵宗超之奉经好道，宋元嘉中亡。将葬，犹未阖棺，其从兄简之来会葬，启盖视之，但见双屐在棺中云。
    　　元嘉中丹阳多宝寺，画佛堂作金刚。寺主奴婢恶戏，以刀刮其目眼，辄见一人甚壮，五色彩衣，持小刀挑目睛，数夜眼烂，于今永盲。
    　　汲都卫士度，苦行居士也。其母尝诵经长斋，非道不行。家常饭僧，时日将中，母出斋堂，与诸尼僧逍遥眺望，忽见空中有一物下，正落母前，乃是天钵，中满香饣卞，举坐肃然，一时礼敬。母自分行斋人食之，皆七日不饥。此钵犹云尚存。士度以惠怀之际得道。 [50]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [50]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 靈味寺 - A Buddhist temple named 'Lingwei Temple,' where '靈味' likely means 'spiritual taste' or 'divine flavor.'
    • 永初三年 - The third year of the Yongchou era, a period during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 沙門法意 - A Buddhist monk named Faye, where '沙門' refers to a monk or ascetic.
    • 高逸沙門 - Refers to a highly reclusive and virtuous monk, '高逸' meaning 'noble and detached.'
    • 岩栖谷隐 - A compound phrase meaning 'living in caves and valleys,' describing a hermit lifestyle.
    • 钟山之阿 - Refers to the foothills of Mount Zhong, where '阿' means 'foot of a hill.'
    • 怪石崩坠 - Means 'strange rocks collapsing,' describing a sudden and dramatic event.
    • 声振林薄 - Means 'the sound shaking the forest and underbrush,' emphasizing the intensity of the event.
    • 清泉湛然 - Means 'clear spring water,' describing a serene and pure environment.
    • 聚徒结宇 - Means 'gathering disciples and building a dwelling,' indicating the establishment of a community.
    • 武陵宗超之 - A person named Zong Chaozhi from Wuling, where '武陵' is a historical region.
    • 奉经好道 - Means 'devoted to scriptures and the Dao,' indicating a life of religious practice.
    • 宋元嘉中亡 - Died during the Yuanjia period of the Song Dynasty.
    • 阖棺 - Means 'closing the coffin,' a ritual act.
    • 从兄简之 - A relative named Jianzhi, where '从兄' means 'cousin' or 'older brother in the same generation.'
    • 启盖视之 - Means 'opening the lid to look inside,' describing a specific action.
    • 但见双屐在棺中云 - Means 'only saw a pair of clogs in the coffin,' where '双屐' refers to a pair of wooden shoes.
    • 丹阳多宝寺 - A temple named 'Duobao Temple' in DanYang, where '多宝' means 'treasure-filled.'
    • 画佛堂作金刚 - Means 'painting the Buddha hall with images of Vajras,' where '金刚' refers to Vajras, a type of deity.
    • 寺主奴婢恶戏 - Means 'the temple's servants played a cruel trick,' where '恶戏' means 'cruel prank.'
    • 以刀刮其目眼 - Means 'scraping their eyes with a knife,' describing a violent act.
    • 辄见一人甚壮 - Means 'seeing a very strong person,' where '辄' means 'immediately' or 'always.'
    • 五色彩衣 - Means 'five-colored robe,' describing the appearance of the person.
    • 持小刀挑目睛 - Means 'holding a small knife and poking the eyeballs,' describing a specific action.
    • 数夜眼烂 - Means 'eyes became swollen for several nights,' indicating the duration and severity of the injury.
    • 于今永盲 - Means 'now blind forever,' indicating the permanent outcome.
    • 汲都卫士度 - A person named Jidu Weishi Du, where '汲都' is a place name.
    • 苦行居士 - Means 'ascetic layperson,' indicating a life of austerity and religious practice.
    • 其母尝诵经长斋 - Means 'his mother often recited scriptures and observed long fasts,' where '长斋' means 'long fast.'
    • 非道不行 - Means 'not doing anything that goes against the Dao,' indicating strict adherence to moral principles.
    • 家常饭僧 - Means 'regularly feeding monks,' indicating a habitual practice.
    • 时日将中 - Means 'as the day was approaching noon,' indicating a specific time.
    • 母出斋堂 - Means 'the mother went out of the斋堂,' where '斋堂' means 'refectory' or 'dining hall.'
    • 与诸尼僧逍遥眺望 - Means 'strolling and looking around with the nuns and monks,' indicating a leisurely activity.
    • 忽见空中有一物下 - Means 'suddenly saw something falling from the sky,' describing a miraculous event.
    • 正落母前 - Means 'falling directly in front of the mother,' indicating the precise location.
    • 乃是天钵 - Means 'it was a heavenly bowl,' where '天钵' refers to a divine or miraculous bowl.
    • 中满香饣卞 - Means 'filled with fragrant food,' where '香饣卞' refers to fragrant food.
    • 举坐肃然 - Means 'everyone became solemn,' indicating a respectful and reverent atmosphere.
    • 一时礼敬 - Means 'all paid homage at once,' indicating a collective act of reverence.
    • 母自分行斋人食之 - Means 'the mother distributed the food to the monks to eat,' indicating a specific action.
    • 皆七日不饥 - Means 'all remained full for seven days,' indicating the miraculous effect of the food.
    • 此钵犹云尚存 - Means 'this bowl is said to still exist,' indicating a historical claim.
    • 士度以惠怀之际得道 - Means 'Shidu attained enlightenment during the Huai period,' where '惠怀' refers to a specific historical period.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 灵味寺 - A Buddhist temple in Jiankang (modern-day Nanjing) during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 永初三年 - The third year of the Yongchou era (322 AD), a period during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 沙门法意 - A Buddhist monk active during the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 高逸沙门 - Refers to a highly reclusive and virtuous monk, a common ideal in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 岩栖谷隐 - Describes the lifestyle of hermits who live in caves and valleys, a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 钟山之阿 - Mount Zhong (modern-day Purple Mountain) in Nanjing, a significant geographical feature.
    • 怪石崩坠 - A natural phenomenon that often appears in Chinese literature as a sign of divine intervention.
    • 清泉湛然 - Describes a serene and pure environment, often associated with spiritual purity.
    • 聚徒结宇 - Refers to the establishment of a monastic community, a common practice in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 武陵宗超之 - A historical figure from Wuling, a region in Hunan.
    • 奉经好道 - Indicates a life dedicated to religious practice, common in Chinese Buddhism and Daoism.
    • 宋元嘉中亡 - The Yuanjia period (424-453 AD) of the Song Dynasty.
    • 阖棺 - A ritual act in Chinese funeral customs.
    • 从兄简之 - A familial relationship, indicating a cousin or older brother in the same generation.
    • 启盖视之 - A specific action in funeral customs, often associated with curiosity or superstition.
    • 但见双屐在棺中云 - A mysterious and unexplained event, often interpreted as a sign of supernatural intervention.
    • 丹阳多宝寺 - A temple in DanYang, a city in Jiangsu.
    • 画佛堂作金刚 - Refers to the practice of painting Buddhist deities in temples, a common form of religious art.
    • 寺主奴婢恶戏 - A historical incident involving temple servants, often used to illustrate the consequences of disrespecting sacred places.
    • 以刀刮其目眼 - A violent act that often appears in Chinese literature as a punishment or curse.
    • 辄见一人甚壮 - A supernatural encounter, often interpreted as a divine or demonic presence.
    • 五色彩衣 - Describes a deity or supernatural being, often associated with divine intervention.
    • 持小刀挑目睛 - A specific action in the supernatural encounter, indicating a violent or punitive act.
    • 数夜眼烂 - A prolonged and severe injury, often used to illustrate the consequences of disrespecting sacred places.
    • 于今永盲 - A permanent and irreversible consequence, often used to illustrate the severity of divine punishment.
    • 汲都卫士度 - A historical figure from Jidu, a place in Henan.
    • 苦行居士 - Refers to an ascetic layperson, a common ideal in Chinese Buddhism.
    • 其母尝诵经长斋 - Indicates a life dedicated to religious practice, common in Chinese Buddhism and Daoism.
    • 非道不行 - Indicates strict adherence to moral principles, a common theme in Chinese philosophy.
    • 家常饭僧 - A common practice in Chinese Buddhism, indicating a habit of generosity and hospitality.
    • 时日将中 - Indicates a specific time, often used to set the scene in Chinese literature.
    • 母出斋堂 - A specific location, often used to set the scene in Chinese literature.
    • 与诸尼僧逍遥眺望 - A leisurely activity, often used to illustrate a peaceful and serene environment.
    • 忽见空中有一物下 - A miraculous event, often interpreted as divine intervention.
    • 正落母前 - Indicates the precise location of the miraculous event.
    • 乃是天钵 - A divine or miraculous bowl, often associated with supernatural events.
    • 中满香饣卞 - Filled with fragrant food, often associated with divine blessings.
    • 举坐肃然 - Everyone became solemn, indicating a respectful and reverent atmosphere.
    • 一时礼敬 - All paid homage at once, indicating a collective act of reverence.
    • 母自分行斋人食之 - The mother distributed the food to the monks to eat, indicating a specific action.
    • 皆七日不饥 - All remained full for seven days, indicating the miraculous effect of the food.
    • 此钵犹云尚存 - This bowl is said to still exist, indicating a historical claim.
    • 士度以惠怀之际得道 - Shidu attained enlightenment during the Huai period, where '惠怀' refers to a specific historical period.

    The Lingwei Temple is located in Jianglin, near Zhongshan Mountain in Jiankang. In the third year of the Yongchou era, the monk Faye founded it. At the end of the Jin Dynasty, there was a highly reclusive and virtuous monk whose name and deeds were not recorded. He lived in caves and valleys, often found in the foothills of Zhongshan Mountain. One night, he suddenly heard strange rocks collapsing, with the sound shaking the forest and underbrush. The next morning, he found a clear spring, gathered disciples, and built a dwelling, naming it Lingwei.

    Zong Chaozhi of Wuling was devoted to scriptures and the Dao. He died during the Yuanjia period of the Song Dynasty. As he was being buried, the coffin had not yet been closed, and his cousin Jianzhi came to attend the funeral. He opened the lid to look inside and only saw a pair of clogs in the coffin.

    During the Yuanjia period, the Duobao Temple in DanYang painted the Buddha hall with images of Vajras. The temple's servants played a cruel prank, scraping the eyes of the Vajras with a knife. They immediately saw a very strong person in five-colored robes, holding a small knife and poking the eyeballs. Their eyes became swollen for several nights, and they remain blind to this day.

    Jidu Weishi Du was an ascetic layperson. His mother often recited scriptures and observed long fasts, not doing anything that went against the Dao. She regularly fed monks. One day, as the sun was approaching noon, she went out of the斋堂 and strolled and looked around with the nuns and monks. Suddenly, she saw something falling from the sky, landing directly in front of her. It was a heavenly bowl, filled with fragrant food. Everyone became solemn, and all paid homage at once. The mother distributed the food to the monks to eat, and they all remained full for seven days. This bowl is said to still exist. Shidu attained enlightenment during the Huai period. [50]

    太原王玄谟字彦德，始将见杀，梦人告曰：“诵《观世音》千遍则免。”玄谟梦中曰：“何可竟也？仍见授。”既觉诵之，且得千遍。明日将刑，诵之不辍，忽传唱停刑。
    　　释僧群清贫守节，蔬食持经，居罗江县之霍山，构立茅屋，孤在海中，上有石盂，水深六尺，常有清泉。古老相传是群仙所宅。群因绝粒，其庵舍去石盂隔一小涧，日夕往还，以木为梁，由之以汲水。年至一百三十，忽见一折翅鸭，舒翼当梁头就唼，群永不得过，欲举锡杖拨之，恐有转伤，因此回，遂绝水，经数日死。临死向人说年少时曾折一鸭翅，验此以为现报。

    　　●卷六
    　　晋宣帝诛王凌后寝疾，日见凌来逼，帝呼曰：“彦云缓我。”身上便有打处，贾逵亦为祟，少日遂薨。初，凌既被执，过贾逵庙呼曰：“贾梁道，王凌魏之忠臣，唯尔有神，知之！”故逵助焉。 [51]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [51]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.
    • 始将见杀 - Was about to be executed; was on the verge of being killed.
    • 梦人告曰 - A person in a dream told him.
    • 诵《观世音》千遍则免 - Recite the *Guanyin* sutra a thousand times and you will be spared.
    • 何可竟也 - How can I finish this? How can I possibly complete this?
    • 仍见授 - Still saw it taught; still received it.
    • 既觉诵之 - After waking up, he continued to recite it.
    • 诵之不辍 - Continued to recite without stopping.
    • 忽传唱停刑 - Suddenly heard the announcement that the execution was halted.
    • 释僧群清贫守节 - Monk Qun, who lived a life of poverty and maintained his principles.
    • 蔬食持经 - Ate simple food and held to the scriptures.
    • 居罗江县之霍山 - Lived in Huoshan, Luojiang County.
    • 构立茅屋 - Built a thatched house.
    • 孤在海中 - Isolated in the sea.
    • 上有石盂 - There was a stone basin above.
    • 水深六尺 - The water was six feet deep.
    • 常有清泉 - There was always clear spring water.
    • 古老相传是群仙所宅 - It was said by the ancients that this was the dwelling place of many immortals.
    • 群因绝粒 - Qun therefore abstained from food.
    • 其庵舍去石盂隔一小涧 - His hut was separated from the stone basin by a small stream.
    • 日夕往还 - He went back and forth every day.
    • 以木为梁 - Using a wooden beam.
    • 由之以汲水 - To draw water.
    • 年至一百三十 - He lived to be one hundred and thirty years old.
    • 忽见一折翅鸭 - Suddenly saw a duck with a broken wing.
    • 舒翼当梁头就唼 - Spread its wings and approached the beamhead to peck.
    • 群永不得过 - Qun could never pass.
    • 欲举锡杖拨之 - Wanted to lift his staff to move it.
    • 恐有转伤 - Feared it might cause further harm.
    • 因此回 - Because of this, he returned.
    • 遂绝水 - And then stopped drinking water.
    • 经数日死 - After several days, he died.
    • 临死向人说 - On his deathbed, he told people.
    • 年少时曾折一鸭翅 - When he was young, he once broke a duck's wing.
    • 验此以为现报 - This was verified as a present retribution.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 太原王玄谟 - Refers to Wang Xuanmo, a historical figure from Taiyuan.
    • 观世音 - Refers to the *Guanyin* sutra, a Buddhist text.
    • 释僧群 - Refers to Monk Qun, a historical figure.
    • 罗江县之霍山 - Refers to Huoshan in Luojiang County, a specific location.
    • 群仙 - Refers to immortals in Chinese mythology.
    • 锡杖 - Refers to a ritual staff used by Buddhist monks.

    Proper Nouns

    • 太原王玄谟 - Wang Xuanmo of Taiyuan.
    • 观世音 - *Guanyin* sutra.
    • 释僧群 - Monk Qun.
    • 罗江县之霍山 - Huoshan, Luojiang County.
    • 群仙 - Immortals.
    • 锡杖 - Ritual staff.

    Wang Xuanmo of Taiyuan, courtesy name Yan De, was about to be executed when he had a dream in which someone told him, "Recite the Guanyin sutra a thousand times and you will be spared." In his dream, Wang said, "How can I possibly complete this? Still, I received it." After waking up, he continued to recite the sutra and managed to recite it a thousand times. The next day, when he was about to be executed, he continued to recite without stopping, and suddenly heard the announcement that the execution was halted.

    Monk Qun lived a life of poverty and maintained his principles, eating simple food and holding to the scriptures. He lived in Huoshan, Luojiang County, where he built a thatched house. The house was isolated in the sea, with a stone basin above, the water six feet deep, and always clear spring water. It was said by the ancients that this was the dwelling place of many immortals. Because of this, Monk Qun abstained from food. His hut was separated from the stone basin by a small stream, and he went back and forth every day, using a wooden beam to draw water. He lived to be one hundred and thirty years old. One day, he suddenly saw a duck with a broken wing, which spread its wings and approached the beamhead to peck. Monk Qun could never pass, and he wanted to lift his staff to move it, fearing it might cause further harm. Because of this, he returned and then stopped drinking water. After several days, he died. On his deathbed, he told people that when he was young, he once broke a duck's wing, and this was verified as a present retribution.

    ● Chapter Six

    After the death of Wang Ling, the Duke of Jin, Emperor Wen of Jin fell ill. He saw Wang Ling coming to threaten him and called out, "Yan Yun, slow down!" He felt hits on his body, and even Jia Kui, the spirit of a famous general, appeared to torment him. After a few days, he died. Initially, when Wang Ling was captured, he passed by the temple of Jia Kui and called out, "Jia Liangdao, Wang Ling was a loyal minister of Wei. Only you have the power to know this!" Therefore, Jia Kui assisted him. [51]

    晋夏侯玄字太初，以当时才望，为司马景王所忌而杀之。宗族为之设祭，见玄来灵坐上，脱头置其傍，悉取果食鱼肉之属以内颈中，毕，还自安其头。既而言曰：“吾得诉于上帝矣。司马子元无嗣也。”寻有永嘉之乱。军还，世宗殂而无子。后有巫见帝，涕泗云：“国家倾覆，正由曹爽、夏侯玄诉怨得伸故也。”爽以势族致诛，玄以时望被戮。
    　　晋嵇中散常于夜中灯火下弹琴，有一人入室，初来时面甚小，斯须渐大，遂长丈余，颜色甚黑，单衣草带。嵇熟视良久，乃吹火灭，曰：“耻与魑魅争光。”
    　　晋邹湛南阳人。初，湛常见一人自称甄舒仲，余无所言，如此非一。久之乃悟，曰：“吾宅西有积上败瓦，其中必有死人甄舒仲者，子舍西土瓦中人也。”检之果然，乃厚加殡殓。毕，梦此人来谢。 [52]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [52]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 為之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋夏侯玄字太初 - Jin Xiahou Xuan, courtesy name Taichu.
    • 司马景王 - Sima Jingwang, a powerful official during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 宗族 - Refers to the family or clan of the deceased.
    • 灵坐 - The place where the spirit of the deceased is believed to reside during a memorial service.
    • 永嘉之乱 - The Yongjia Rebellion, a significant historical event during the Jin Dynasty.
    • 世宗 - Emperor Shizong, a ruler of the Jin Dynasty.
    • 巫 - A witch or shaman, often involved in spiritual or supernatural practices.
    • 曹爽 - A prominent figure during the Three Kingdoms period, known for his political power.
    • 嵇中散 - Ji Zhongshan, a famous musician and poet from the Jin Dynasty.
    • 邹湛 - Zou Zhan, a historical figure from the Jin Dynasty.
    • 南阳 - Nanyang, a city in Henan Province, China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋 - The Jin Dynasty.
    • 司马景王 - Sima Jingwang.
    • 宗族 - The family or clan.
    • 灵坐 - The place where the spirit of the deceased resides.
    • 永嘉之乱 - The Yongjia Rebellion.
    • 世宗 - Emperor Shizong.
    • 巫 - A witch or shaman.
    • 曹爽 - Cao Shuang.
    • 嵇中散 - Ji Zhongshan.
    • 邹湛 - Zou Zhan.
    • 南阳 - Nanyang.

    Jin Xiahou Xuan, courtesy name Taichu, was envied and killed by Sima Jingwang due to his reputation and talent at the time. The clan held a memorial service for him, during which they saw Xuan appear on the altar. He removed his head and placed it beside him, took all the fruits, food, fish, and meat, and placed them inside his neck. Afterward, he returned and placed his head back. Then he said, "I have been able to appeal to the Heavenly Emperor. Sima Ziyuan will have no heir." Soon after, there was the Yongjia Rebellion. When the army returned, Emperor Shizong died without an heir. Later, a witch saw the emperor, crying and saying, "The downfall of the country was due to Cao Shuang and Xuan being able to vent their grievances." Cao Shuang was killed because of his powerful family, and Xuan was killed because of his reputation and influence.

    Jin Ji Zhongshan often played the qin under a lamp at night. One night, a person entered the room. At first, the face was very small, but soon it grew larger and eventually reached over ten feet tall. The face was very dark, wearing a simple robe and a grass belt. Ji stared at him for a long time, then blew out the fire and said, "I am ashamed to compete with demons for light."

    Jin Zou Zhan was from Nanyang. Initially, Zhan often saw a person who called himself Zhen Shuzhong. He said nothing else, and this happened many times. After a long time, he realized, "There must be a dead person named Zhen Shuzhong in the accumulated broken tiles west of my house, he is the person from the west tile pit." When he checked, it was indeed true. He then gave him a proper burial. Afterward, he dreamed that this person came to thank him. [52]

    晋清河陆机初入洛，次河南之偃师。时久结阴，望道左，若有民居，因往投宿。见一年少，神姿端远，置易投壶，与机言论，妙得玄微。机心服其能，无以酹抗，乃提纬古今，总验名实，此年少不甚欣解。既晓便去，税骖逆旅，问逆旅妪，妪曰：“此东数十里无村落，止有山阳王家冢尔。”机乃怪怅，还睇昨路，空野霾云，拱木蔽日，方知昨所遇者信王弼也。一说陆云独行逗宿故人家，夜暗迷路，莫知所从。忽望草中有火光，云时饥之，因而诣前。至一家，墙院甚整。便寄宿。见一年少，可二十余，丰姿甚嘉，论叙平生，不异于人。寻共说老子，极有辞致。云出临别，语云：“我是山阳王辅嗣。”云出门，回望向处，止是一冢，云始谓俄顷已经三日，乃大怪怅。 [53]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [53]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 初入洛 - Means 'first entering Luoyang,' referring to the act of arriving in the city for the first time.
    • 次河南之偃师 - Means 'next to Yanshi in Henan,' indicating the traveler's journey through the region.
    • 久结阴 - Describes a prolonged period of overcast or rainy weather.
    • 若有民居 - Means 'as if there were a human settlement,' suggesting the appearance of a village or house.
    • 神姿端远 - Describes a person with a dignified and distant appearance, often associated with a divine or transcendent quality.
    • 置易投壶 - Refers to the game of *touhu* (投壶), a traditional Chinese ritual game involving throwing arrows into a pot.
    • 妙得玄微 - Means 'profoundly grasping the subtle and profound,' indicating a deep understanding of philosophical or metaphysical concepts.
    • 心服其能 - Means 'to be convinced of one's ability,' expressing admiration for someone's skills.
    • 无以酹抗 - Means 'unable to match or compete,' indicating a lack of suitable counterarguments or challenges.
    • 提纬古今 - Means 'to discuss past and present,' referring to a broad discussion of historical and contemporary matters.
    • 总验名实 - Means 'to verify names and realities,' indicating a thorough examination of the relationship between names and their actual meanings or functions.
    • 拱木蔽日 - Describes tall trees blocking the sun, creating a dense and shadowy environment.
    • 信王弼也 - Means 'indeed it was Wang Bi,' confirming the identity of the person encountered.
    • 逗宿 - Means 'to stay overnight,' referring to the act of lodging for the night.
    • 夜暗迷路 - Describes being lost in the dark, indicating a situation of disorientation.
    • 莫知所从 - Means 'not knowing which way to go,' expressing confusion and uncertainty.
    • 丰姿甚嘉 - Describes a person with a very handsome and graceful appearance.
    • 寻共说老子 - Means 'then they discussed *Laozi*,' referring to the *Tao Te Ching* (道德经), the foundational text of Taoism.
    • 我是山阳王辅嗣 - Means 'I am Wang Bi of Shanyang,' identifying the person as Wang Bi, a famous philosopher and commentator on *Laozi* and *Yijing*.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋清河陆机 - Refers to Lu Ji (陆机), a prominent scholar and poet from the Jin dynasty, known for his literary works and philosophical insights.
    • 洛阳 - Refers to Luoyang, an important city in Chinese history, especially during the Han and Jin dynasties.
    • 偃师 - Refers to Yanshi, a city in Henan province, known for its historical significance and cultural heritage.
    • 王弼 - Refers to Wang Bi (王弼), a renowned philosopher and commentator on *Laozi* and *Yijing* from the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 山阳王辅嗣 - Another reference to Wang Bi, emphasizing his identity and status.
    • 老子 - Refers to *Laozi* (老子), the *Tao Te Ching*, a foundational text of Taoism that explores the nature of the Tao (道) and the principles of wu wei (无为).

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋清河陆机 - Lu Ji (陆机), a prominent scholar and poet from the Jin dynasty.
    • 洛阳 - Luoyang, an important city in Chinese history.
    • 偃师 - Yanshi, a city in Henan province.
    • 王弼 - Wang Bi (王弼), a renowned philosopher and commentator on *Laozi* and *Yijing*.
    • 山阳王辅嗣 - Another reference to Wang Bi, emphasizing his identity and status.
    • 老子 - *Laozi* (老子), the *Tao Te Ching*.

    Lu Ji of Qinghe, Jin, first entered Luoyang and then passed through Yanshi in Henan. It had been raining heavily for a long time, and he saw what appeared to be a human settlement. He decided to seek shelter there. There, he met a young man with a dignified and distant appearance. They played touhu and engaged in a discussion, during which the young man profoundly grasped the subtle and profound aspects of philosophy. Lu Ji was convinced of the young man's abilities and found no way to challenge him. He then discussed past and present matters, verifying names and realities, but the young man showed little interest. After dawn, the young man left, and Lu Ji rented a room in a guesthouse. He asked an old woman about the place, and she said, "There are no villages for dozens of miles to the east; there is only the tomb of Wang Bi of Shanyang." Lu Ji was surprised and confused. He looked back at the path he had taken and saw a desolate wilderness with overcast skies and tall trees blocking the sun. He realized that the young man he had encountered was indeed Wang Bi.

    Another version of the story tells of Lu Yun traveling alone and seeking shelter at a friend's house. At night, he became lost in the dark and did not know which way to go. Suddenly, he saw a firelight in the grass and, being hungry, went towards it. Upon reaching a house, he saw that the walls and courtyard were neat and orderly. He decided to stay overnight. Inside, he met a young man in his twenties with a very handsome and graceful appearance. They talked about their lives, and the conversation was no different from any other. Then, they discussed Laozi, and the young man spoke eloquently. As Lu Yun was about to leave, the young man said to him, "I am Wang Bi of Shanyang." Lu Yun stepped out of the house and looked back at the place where he had stayed, and saw only a tomb. He realized that a short while had passed, but it felt like three days, and he was greatly surprised and saddened. [53]

    晋永嘉中，朱彦居永宁，披荒入舍，便闻管弦之声，及小儿啼呼之音。夜见一人身甚壮大，呼杀其犬。彦素胆勇，不以为惧，即不移居，亦无后患。
    　　晋永嘉中，李谦素善琵琶。元嘉初，往广州，夜集坐倦，悉寝，惟谦独挥弹未辍，便闻窗外有唱佳声，每至契会，无不击节。谦怪，语曰：“何不进耶？”对曰：“遗生已久，无宜于突。”始悟是鬼。
    　　刘聪建元三年，并州祭酒桓回于途遇一老父，问之云：“昔乐工成凭今居何职？我与其人有旧，为致清谈，得察孝廉。君若相见，令知消息。”回问姓字，曰：“我吴郡麻子轩也。”言毕而失。回见凭，具宣其意，凭叹曰：“昔有此人，计去世近五十年。”中郎荀彦舒闻之，为造祝，文令凭设酒饭祀于通衢之下。 [54]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [54]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 披荒入舍 - Literally 'to clear the wilderness and enter the dwelling,' describes the act of settling in a new place.
    • 博学求之 - Means to seek knowledge broadly from various sources.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋永嘉中 - Refers to the period during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin (279-313 AD).
    • 元嘉初 - Refers to the early years of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song (424-453 AD).
    • 刘聪建元三年 - Refers to the third year of the Jianyuan era of Liu Cong (304-318 AD).
    • 并州祭酒桓回 - Refers to Huan Hui, a scholar and official from the state of Bingzhou.
    • 吴郡麻子轩 - Refers to Ma Zixuan, an old man from Wu Prefecture.
    • 成凭 - Refers to Cheng Ping, a former musician.
    • 荀彦舒 - Refers to Xun Yanshu, a scholar who made a ritual offering.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋永嘉中 - The period during the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin.
    • 李谦 - A skilled pipa player.
    • 元嘉初 - The early years of the reign of Emperor Wu of Liu Song.
    • 广州 - A city in southern China.
    • 刘聪建元三年 - The third year of the Jianyuan era of Liu Cong.
    • 并州祭酒桓回 - Huan Hui, a scholar and official.
    • 吴郡麻子轩 - Ma Zixuan, an old man from Wu Prefecture.
    • 成凭 - Cheng Ping, a former musician.
    • 荀彦舒 - Xun Yanshu, a scholar who made a ritual offering.

    During the Yongjia period of the Jin Dynasty, Zhu Yan settled in Yongning. As he cleared the wilderness and entered the dwelling, he immediately heard the sound of musical instruments and the cries of a child. At night, he saw a man who was very strong and called for the killing of his dog. Zhu Yan, known for his courage, was not afraid and did not move his residence, and there were no further troubles.

    During the early Yuanjia period of the Liu Song Dynasty, Li Qian was skilled in playing the pipa. One night, while he was playing, all the guests fell asleep. Li Qian continued playing alone. Suddenly, he heard someone singing from outside the window. Whenever the singer reached a point of resonance, Li Qian would clap in approval. Surprised, Li Qian asked, "Why don't you come in?" The voice replied, "I have been dead for a long time and am not suitable to enter." Li Qian realized that it was a ghost.

    In the third year of the Jianyuan era of Liu Cong, Huan Hui, a scholar from Bingzhou, met an old man on the road. The old man asked about Cheng Ping, saying, "I have old ties with him. I can bring him a clear conversation and help him become a filial and upright official. If you see him, tell him about this." Huan Hui asked for the old man's name, and the old man replied, "I am Ma Zixuan from Wu Prefecture." The old man then disappeared. Huan Hui met Cheng Ping and told him everything. Cheng Ping said, "There was such a person, and he has been dead for nearly fifty years." Xun Yanshu, upon hearing this, prepared an offering and set up a sacrifice on a public road, asking Cheng Ping to set up food and drink for the sacrifice. [54]

    晋太元中，桓轨为巴东太守，留家江陵。妻乳母姓陈，儿道生随轨之郡，坠濑死。道生形见云：“今获在河伯左右，蒙假二十日，得踅还。”母哀至，辄有一黑乌以翅掩其口舌上，遂生一瘤，从此便不得复哭。
    　　晋颍川荀泽以太元中亡，恒形见。还与妇鲁国孔氏燕婉绸缪，遂有妊焉。十月而产，产悉是水，别房作酱，泽曰：汝知丧家不当作酱而故为之。今上官责我数豆致劬不复堪经，少时而绝。
    　　晋时会稽严猛妇出采薪，为虎所害。后一年，猛行至蒿中，忽见妇云：“君今日行必遭不善，我当相免也。”既而俱前，忽逢一虎，跳踉向猛，猛妇举手指挥，状如遮护。须臾有一胡人荷戟而过，妇因指之，虎即击胡，婿乃得免。 [55]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [55]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 形见云 - Spirit appears and says.
    • 遂有妊焉 - She became pregnant.
    • 十月而产，产悉是水 - She gave birth after ten months, but what was born was all water.
    • 别房作酱 - In a separate room, they made soy sauce.
    • 君今日行必遭不善，我当相免也 - You will surely encounter misfortune today, I will protect you.
    • 猛妇举手指挥，状如遮护 - Yanshan's wife raised her hand to command, as if to protect.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋太元 - The reign period of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (376-396 AD).
    • 桓轨 - A historical figure who served as the governor of Badong.
    • 巴东 - A region in modern-day Chongqing, China.
    • 江陵 - A city in modern-day Jingzhou, Hubei.
    • 河伯 - The Chinese water deity.
    • 颍川 - A region in modern-day Xuchang, Henan.
    • 荀泽 - A historical figure from Yingchuan.
    • 鲁国孔氏 - Refers to the Kong family from Luyi (modern-day Qufu, Shandong).
    • 会稽 - A region in modern-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang.
    • 严猛 - A historical figure from Kuaiji.
    • 蒿中 - A grassland area.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋太元 - The reign period of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (376-396 AD).
    • 桓轨 - A historical figure who served as the governor of Badong.
    • 巴东 - A region in modern-day Chongqing, China.
    • 江陵 - A city in modern-day Jingzhou, Hubei.
    • 河伯 - The Chinese water deity.
    • 颍川 - A region in modern-day Xuchang, Henan.
    • 荀泽 - A historical figure from Yingchuan.
    • 鲁国孔氏 - Refers to the Kong family from Luyi (modern-day Qufu, Shandong).
    • 会稽 - A region in modern-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang.
    • 严猛 - A historical figure from Kuaiji.
    • 蒿中 - A grassland area.

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, Huan Gui served as the governor of Badong and left his family in Jiangling. His wife's wet nurse was named Chen, and their son Dao Sheng followed Huan Gui to the prefecture, where he fell into a rapid and died. Dao Sheng appeared in the form of a spirit and said, "I am now in the service of the River Lord. I have been granted a leave of twenty days to return briefly." When the mother was grieving, there would always be a black bird covering her mouth and tongue with its wings, and she developed a tumor. From then on, she could no longer cry.

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, Xun Ze from Yingchuan died. He often appeared in spirit form and returned to his wife, Kong from Luyi, and they were intimate. She became pregnant and gave birth after ten months, but what was born was all water. In a separate room, they made soy sauce. Xun said, "You know that a mourning family should not make soy sauce, yet you did so. Now the officials are blaming me for counting beans and causing me trouble, and I cannot bear it anymore; I will die soon."

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, a woman from Yanshan in Kuaiji went out to gather firewood and was killed by a tiger. A year later, Yanshan went into the grassland and suddenly saw the woman, who said, "You will surely encounter misfortune today, I will protect you." Soon they moved forward together, and suddenly met a tiger, which leaped towards Yanshan. Yanshan's wife raised her hand to command, as if to protect him. Soon a foreigner carrying a halberd passed by. The woman pointed at him, and the tiger attacked the foreigner. Yanshan was saved. [55]

    晋新野庾绍之字道遐，与南阳宋协中表之亲，情好绸缪。桓玄时，庾为湘东太守，病亡。义熙中，忽见形诣协、一小儿通云：“庾湘东来。”须臾便至，两脚著械。既至，脱械置地而坐。协问：“何由得顾？”答云：“踅蒙假归，与卿亲好，故相过耳。”协问鬼神之事，绍辄漫略，不甚谐对，具问亲戚，因谈世事，末复求酒。协时时饵茱萸酒，因为设之。酒至，执杯还置云：“有茱萸气。”协曰：“卿恶之耶？”绍云：“上官皆畏之，非独我也。”绍为人语声高壮，此言论时不异恒日。有顷，协儿邃之来，绍闻屐声，极有惧色，乃谓协曰：“生气见陵，不得复住。与卿三年别耳。”因贯械而起，出户便灭。协后为正员郎，果三年而卒。 [56]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [56]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 情好绸缪 - A classical expression meaning 'close and intimate friendship,' often used to describe deep bonds between people.
    • 假归 - A term meaning 'to borrow and return,' but in this context, it likely refers to a ghost returning from the afterlife.
    • 顾 - A verb meaning 'to visit' or 'to pay a call,' often used in the context of ghosts or spirits visiting the living.
    • 恶之 - A phrase meaning 'to dislike' or 'to be averse to,' here referring to the ghost's reaction to the smell of wine.
    • 上官皆畏之 - A phrase meaning 'all superiors fear it,' implying that the ghost is feared by those in higher positions, possibly in the afterlife.
    • 执杯还置 - A phrase meaning 'to hold the cup and return it to the ground,' indicating the ghost's refusal to drink the wine due to its smell.
    • 生气见陵 - A phrase meaning 'the living are arrogant or disrespectful,' suggesting that the ghost feels disrespected by the living.
    • 贯械而起 - A phrase meaning 'to put on shackles and rise,' symbolizing the ghost's departure and return to the afterlife.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 庾绍之 - A historical figure, specifically a person named 庾绍之, who lived during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 宋协 - Another historical figure, specifically a person named 宋协, who is related to 庾绍之 by marriage.
    • 桓玄 - A historical figure, specifically a warlord and regent during the Eastern Jin dynasty, known for his rebellion against the Jin court.
    • 湘东太守 - A title meaning 'Governor of Xiangdong,' referring to the administrative position held by 庾绍之.
    • 义熙 - The era name of the Eastern Jin dynasty, used during the reign of Emperor An of Jin.
    • 中表之亲 - A term referring to relatives by marriage, specifically cousins or in-laws.
    • 正员郎 - A title meaning 'Regular Attendant,' a low-ranking official position in the Eastern Jin bureaucracy.
    • 义熙中 - A time period, specifically the middle of the义熙 era, which corresponds to the years 405-419 CE.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • 忽见形诣协、一小儿通云
    • 既至，脱械置地而坐
    • 因贯械而起，出户便灭
    • 有顷，协儿邃之来

    Proper Nouns

    • 庾绍之 - A historical figure, specifically a person named 庾绍之.
    • 宋协 - A historical figure, specifically a person named 宋协.
