    • 桓玄 - A historical figure, specifically a warlord and regent during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 湘东太守 - A title meaning 'Governor of Xiangdong.'
    • 义熙 - The era name of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 中表之亲 - A term referring to relatives by marriage.
    • 正员郎 - A title meaning 'Regular Attendant.'
    • 义熙中 - A time period, specifically the middle of the义熙 era.

    During the reign of Emperor An of Jin, there was a man from Xinye named Yu Shaozhi, whose courtesy name was Daoxia. He was closely related to Song Xie from Nanyang through marriage, and they had a close and intimate friendship. During the time of Huan Xuan, Yu served as the Governor of Xiangdong but later died of illness. In the middle of the Yixi era, Yu suddenly appeared in a spectral form to visit Song Xie, accompanied by a young boy who said, "Yu from Xiangdong has come." Shortly after, Yu arrived, with shackles on his feet. Upon arrival, he removed the shackles and sat down on the ground. Song Xie asked, "How did you manage to visit me?" Yu replied, "I was granted a temporary leave and came to see you because of our close relationship." Song Xie asked about matters concerning ghosts and spirits, but Yu answered vaguely and did not respond very coherently. They then discussed family members and talked about current affairs, and finally, Yu asked for some wine. Song Xie often drank wine with cornel berries, so he prepared some for Yu. When the wine was brought, Yu held the cup and returned it to the ground, saying, "There is a smell of cornel berries." Song Xie said, "Do you dislike it?" Yu replied, "All superiors are afraid of it, not just me." Yu had a loud and strong voice, and during this conversation, he did not differ much from his usual self. After a while, Song Xie's son Shuizhi came, and Yu heard the sound of his shoes and became extremely fearful. He then told Song Xie, "The living are arrogant and disrespectful, so I cannot stay here. I will be separated from you for three years." He then put on the shackles and stood up, and as he left the house, he disappeared. Song Xie later became a Regular Attendant and indeed died three years later. [56]

    晋义熙三年，山阴徐琦每出门见一女子，貌极艳丽，琦便解银铃赠之。女曰：“感君佳贶。”以青铜镜与琦，便结为伉俪。
    　　晋义熙中，乌伤葛辉夫，在女家宿。至三更，竟有两人把火至阶前，疑是凶人，往打之。欲下杖，悉变为蝴蝶，缤纷飞散。忽有一物冲辉夫腋下，便倒地，少时死。
    　　义熙中，高平檀茂祟丧亡，其母沛郡刘氏昼眠，梦见崇手执团扇，云：“崇年命未尽，横被灾厉，上永违离。今以此扇奉别。”母流涕惊觉，果于屏风问得扇，上皆如蜘蛛网络，抚执悲恸。
    　　义熙中，长山唐邦闻扣门声，出视，见两朱衣吏云：“官欲得汝。”遂将至县东岗殷安冢中。冢中有人语吏云：“本取唐福，何以滥取唐邦！”救鞭之，遣将出。唐福少时而死。 [57]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [57]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 義熙 - A reign title of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song, the second emperor of the Southern Dynasties period.
    • 解銀鈴贈之 - Literally 'unfastened a silver bell and gave it to her,' indicating a gesture of kindness or affection.
    • 感君佳贶 - Literally 'grateful for your generous gift,' a polite expression of gratitude.
    • 結為伉儷 - Literally 'became a couple,' indicating marriage or a committed relationship.
    • 三更 - Refers to the third watch of the night, typically from 11 PM to 1 AM.
    • 朱衣吏 - Literally 'red-robed officials,' likely referring to supernatural beings or spirits.
    • 官欲得汝 - Literally 'the official wants you,' suggesting a supernatural summons or punishment.
    • 永違離 - Literally 'eternally parted,' indicating death or separation.
    • 屏風問得扇 - Literally 'found the fan on the screen,' indicating a place where the fan was discovered.
    • 博學求之 - Literally 'broadly seek it,' suggesting a pursuit of knowledge or understanding.
    • 釣魚屠肉 - Literally 'fishing and butchering,' referring to manual occupations.
    • 飯牛牧羊 - Literally 'feeding cattle and herding sheep,' also referring to manual occupations.
    • 少時而死 - Literally 'died shortly after,' indicating a quick death.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 義熙三年 - The third year of the Yixi era, corresponding to 407 AD.
    • 山陰 - A county in present-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang, known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 烏傷 - A county in present-day Lanxi, Zhejiang, also known for its cultural and historical significance.
    • 高平檀茂祟 - A person named Tan Maosong from Gaoping (present-day Tai'an, Shandong), who is said to have died young.
    • 沛郡劉氏 - A woman from Pei (present-day Pei County, Jiangsu), likely the mother of Tan Maosong.
    • 殷安 - A historical figure or place name, possibly referring to a tomb or a person.
    • 靈帝 - A historical reference to Emperor Ling of Han, though this is not directly relevant to the text.
    • 洛陽 - A city in present-day Henan, historically significant as the capital of several Chinese dynasties.
    • 女子 - Refers to a woman or female, often used in classical texts to describe individuals.
    • 經史 - Refers collectively to the canonical classics and historical writings that preserve exemplary cases.
    • 朝廷憲章 - Refers to official decrees and regulations issued by the government.
    • 軍旅誓誥 - Refers to military proclamations and oaths.
    • 陶冶性靈 - A metaphor of smelting and shaping one's inner nature.
    • 從容諷諫 - Means to offer remonstrance in an indirect, measured, and graceful way.
    • 行有餘力，則可習之 - Means that one may study literary writing after fulfilling more basic duties.

    Proper Nouns

    • 義熙 - The reign title of Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song.
    • 山陰 - A county in present-day Shaoxing, Zhejiang.
    • 烏傷 - A county in present-day Lanxi, Zhejiang.
    • 高平 - A county in present-day Tai'an, Shandong.
    • 沛郡 - A county in present-day Pei County, Jiangsu.
    • 殷安 - A historical figure or place name, possibly referring to a tomb or a person.
    • 靈帝 - A historical reference to Emperor Ling of Han.
    • 洛陽 - A city in present-day Henan.
    • 女子 - Refers to a woman or female.
    • 經史 - Refers collectively to the canonical classics and historical writings.
    • 朝廷憲章 - Refers to official decrees and regulations issued by the government.
    • 軍旅誓誥 - Refers to military proclamations and oaths.
    • 陶冶性靈 - A metaphor of smelting and shaping one's inner nature.
    • 從容諷諫 - Means to offer remonstrance in an indirect, measured, and graceful way.
    • 行有餘力，則可習之 - Means that one may study literary writing after fulfilling more basic duties.

    In the third year of the Yixi era, Xu Qi of Shanyin often saw a woman when he went out, who was extremely beautiful. Qi then unfastened a silver bell and gave it to her. The woman said, "Thank you for your generous gift." She gave him a bronze mirror, and they became a couple.
    　　In the Yixi era, Ge Huifu of Wuzhang stayed at a woman's house. At the third watch, two people came with torches to the front steps. He suspected them to be evil spirits and went to beat them. As he was about to strike, they all turned into butterflies and flew away in a colorful display. Suddenly, something hit Huifu on the side, and he fell to the ground, dying shortly after.
    　　In the Yixi era, Tan Maosong of Gaoping died young. His mother, Liu of Pei, was sleeping during the day when she dreamed that Maosong held a fan and said, "My life has not yet ended, but I have been afflicted by disaster and must part forever. Here is this fan as a farewell." She woke up crying and found the fan on the screen, covered in spider webs. She held it and mourned deeply.
    　　In the Yixi era, Tang Bang of Changshan heard knocking at the door and went to see. He saw two red-robed officials who said, "The official wants you." They took him to the eastern hill of Yin'an's tomb. Inside the tomb, someone told the officials, "We were supposed to take Tang Fu, why did you take Tang Bang instead?" They beat him with a whip and sent him out. Tang Fu died shortly after. [57]

    晋孝武太元中，琅琊王轲之家有鬼歌子夜。殷允为章郡，侨人庾僧度家亦有鬼歌子夜。
    　　晋太元中，吴兴许寂之忽有鬼于空中语笑，或歌或哭，至夜偏盛。寂之有灵车，鬼共牵走，车为坏。寂之有长刀，乃以摄置瓮中，有大镜亦摄以纳器中。
    　　晋元兴中，东阳太守朱牙之忽有一老公从其妾董床下出，著黄裳衿帽，所出之舀甚滑泽有泉，遂与董交好。若有吉凶，遂以告。牙之儿疾疟，公曰：“此应得虎耶服之。”持戟向山，果得虎阴，尚余暖气，使儿灸啖，疟即断绝。公常使董梳头发如野猪毛，牙之后诣祭酒上章于是绝迹。乃作沸汤，试浇此舀，掘得数斛大蚁，不日村人捉大刀野行，逢一大夫，见刀，操黄金一饼求以易刀，及授刀，奄失其人所在。重察向金，乃是牛粪。计此，乃牙之家鬼。 [58]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [58]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鬼歌子夜 - Ghosts singing *Ziye*, where *Ziye* is an ancient Chinese folk song.
    • 若有吉凶，遂以告 - If there is good or bad news, it will be told.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋孝武太元中 - The Taiyuan era of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 子夜 - An ancient Chinese folk song, often associated with the night.
    • 侨人 - People who have migrated to a new place, often used to describe immigrants or settlers.
    • 灵车 - A supernatural vehicle, often associated with spirits or ghosts.
    • 晋元兴中 - The Yuanxing era during the Jin dynasty, another significant period in Chinese history.
    • 东阳太守 - The title of the governor of Dongyang, a region in ancient China.
    • 疟 - A disease, specifically malaria, which was common in ancient times.
    • 虎阴 - A tiger's skin, often used in traditional medicine.
    • 祭酒 - The title of the chief priest or a high-ranking official in religious or academic institutions.
    • 牛粪 - Cow dung, often used in agriculture and as fertilizer.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋孝武太元中 - The Taiyuan era of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 琅琊王轲之 - Wang Kezhi, a nobleman from Langya.
    • 殷允 - Yin Yun, a person from Zhangzhou.
    • 庾僧度 - Yu Shengdu, a person from Zhangzhou.
    • 晋元兴中 - The Yuanxing era during the Jin dynasty.
    • 东阳太守朱牙之 - Zhu Yazi, the governor of Dongyang.
    • 董 - Dong, Zhu Yazi's concubine.
    • 祭酒 - The title of the chief priest or a high-ranking official in religious or academic institutions.

    During the Taiyuan era of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, ghosts were singing Ziye at the home of Wang Kezhi, a nobleman from Langya. Yin Yun, a person from Zhangzhou, also reported that ghosts were singing Ziye at the home of Yu Shengdu, a person from Zhangzhou.

    During the Taiyuan era of the Jin dynasty, Xu Jizhi from Wuxing suddenly heard ghosts speaking and laughing in the air, sometimes singing and sometimes crying, with the activity peaking at night. Xu Jizhi had a divine carriage, which the ghosts pulled away, causing the carriage to break. Xu Jizhi had a long knife, which he captured and placed in a jar, along with a large mirror that was also captured and placed in a container.

    During the Yuanxing era of the Jin dynasty, Zhu Yazi, the governor of Dongyang, suddenly saw an old man emerge from under the bed of his concubine Dong, wearing yellow robes and a hat. The old man's appearance was very slippery and moist, like a spring. The old man then became friends with Dong and would inform her of good or bad news. Zhu Yazi's son had malaria, and the old man said, "This should be treated with a tiger's skin." Zhu Yazi held a spear and went to the mountain, and indeed found a tiger's skin. There was still warmth left, and he made his son roast and eat it, curing the malaria. The old man often made Dong comb her hair like a wild boar's bristles. Zhu Yazi then went to the chief priest and presented a petition, after which the ghost disappeared. He made boiling water and tried to pour it on the spring, digging up several shi of large ants. Soon villagers grabbed big knives and went into the wild, meeting a scholar who saw the knife and offered a piece of gold to exchange for it. When the knife was given, the scholar suddenly disappeared. Upon closer inspection, the gold turned out to be cow dung. Considering this, it was a ghost from Zhu Yazi's house. [58]

    沛郡人秦树者，家在曲阿小辛村。尝自京归，未至二十里许，天暗失道，遥望火光，往投之宿。见一女子，秉烛出，云：“女弱独居，不得宿客。”树曰：“欲进路，碍夜不可前去，乞寄外住。”女然之。树既进坐竟，以此女独居一室，虑其夫至，不敢安眠。女曰：“何以过嫌？保无虞，不相误也。”为树设食，食物悉是陈久，树曰：“承未出适，我亦未婚，欲结大义，能相顾否？”女笑曰：“自顾鄙薄，岂足伉俪。”遂与寝止。向晨，树去，乃俱起执别。女泣曰：“与君一睹，后面无期。”以指环一双赠之，结置衣带，相送出门。树低头急去数十步，顾其宿处，乃是冢墓。居数日，亡其指环，结带如故。 [59]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [59]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 沛郡人秦树者 - A formal introduction to the main character, meaning 'Qin Shu, a native of Pei Commandery.'
    • 尝自京归 - Means 'once returned from the capital,' indicating a journey.
    • 未至二十里许 - Means 'not quite twenty li away,' where '许' is a measure word indicating an approximate distance.
    • 天暗失道 - Means 'the sky grew dark and he lost his way,' describing the circumstances of the journey.
    • 遥望火光 - Means 'looked far and saw a light,' indicating the discovery of a place to stay.
    • 女弱独居，不得宿客 - Means 'a weak woman lives alone and cannot accommodate guests,' a polite refusal.
    • 欲进路，碍夜不可前去 - Means 'wants to continue the journey, but it is too dark to proceed,' explaining the need for shelter.
    • 乞寄外住 - Means 'asks to stay outside,' a request to be accommodated.
    • 女然之 - Means 'the woman agreed,' a concise expression of consent.
    • 以此女独居一室 - Means 'since the woman lives alone in a room,' providing context for the situation.
    • 虑其夫至 - Means 'fearing her husband might arrive,' a concern about potential interruptions.
    • 何以过嫌 - Means 'why be overly suspicious,' a rhetorical question to ease concerns.
    • 保无虞，不相误也 - Means 'I assure you there is no danger, and I will not mislead you,' a reassurance.
    • 为树设食 - Means 'prepared food for Qin Shu,' indicating hospitality.
    • 食物悉是陈久 - Means 'all the food was old and stale,' describing the quality of the meal.
    • 承未出适 - Means 'since you have not yet married,' a reference to the unmarried state.
    • 欲结大义 - Means 'wants to form a serious relationship,' a euphemism for marriage.
    • 能相顾否 - Means 'can you consider me?' a formal way of asking for a relationship.
    • 自顾鄙薄 - Means 'I consider myself unworthy,' a humble self-assessment.
    • 岂足伉俪 - Means 'how could I be worthy of a spouse?' a rhetorical question expressing humility.
    • 遂与寝止 - Means 'they then slept together,' a concise description of the intimate encounter.
    • 向晨 - Means 'early morning,' indicating the time of departure.
    • 俱起执别 - Means 'they both got up and took leave,' a formal farewell.
    • 与君一睹，后面无期 - Means 'this is our last meeting, we may never see each other again,' a poignant farewell.
    • 以指环一双赠之 - Means 'gave him a pair of finger rings,' a gesture of affection.
    • 结置衣带 - Means 'tied them to his belt,' a way of keeping a memento.
    • 顾其宿处，乃是冢墓 - Means 'looked back at where he stayed, and it turned out to be a tomb,' a shocking revelation.
    • 亡其指环 - Means 'lost the finger rings,' indicating the disappearance of the gift.
    • 结带如故 - Means 'the belt ties remained as before,' a detail that adds to the mystery.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 沛郡 - Refers to Pei Commandery, a historical region in present-day Anhui and Jiangsu provinces.
    • 曲阿小辛村 - Refers to Xiaoxin Village in Qu'a County, a specific location in the region.
    • 京 - Refers to the capital city, likely Chang'an or Luoyang, depending on the period.
    • 冢墓 - Refers to a tomb or grave, a place of burial.
    • 指环 - Refers to finger rings, a common symbol of affection in ancient China.
    • 衣带 - Refers to a belt, a common accessory for men in ancient China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 沛郡 - The name of the commandery.
    • 曲阿小辛村 - The name of the village.
    • 京 - The name of the capital city.
    • 冢墓 - The name of the tomb.
    • 指环 - The name of the gift.
    • 衣带 - The name of the accessory.

    Translation of the Source Text

    Translation:
    Qin Shu, a native of Pei Commandery, lived in Xiaoxin Village in Qu'a County. Once returning from the capital, he was not quite twenty li away when the sky grew dark and he lost his way. He saw a light in the distance and went to stay there for the night. He met a woman who came out with a candle and said, "I am a weak woman living alone and cannot accommodate guests." Qin Shu replied, "I wish to continue my journey, but it is too dark to proceed, so I ask to stay outside." The woman agreed. After Qin Shu entered and sat down, he realized that the woman lived alone in a room and feared her husband might arrive, so he dared not sleep soundly. The woman said, "Why be overly suspicious? I assure you there is no danger, and I will not mislead you." She prepared food for Qin Shu, but all the food was old and stale. Qin Shu said, "Since you have not yet married, I too am unmarried, and I wish to form a serious relationship, can you consider me?" The woman smiled and said, "I consider myself unworthy, how could I be worthy of a spouse?" They then slept together. In the early morning, Qin Shu left, and they both got up and took leave. The woman cried and said, "This is our last meeting, we may never see each other again." She gave him a pair of finger rings, tied them to his belt, and saw him off at the door. Qin Shu hurriedly walked away, looking back at where he stayed, and it turned out to be a tomb. A few days later, he lost the finger rings, but the belt ties remained as before. [59]

    南平国蛮兵在姑孰，便有鬼附之，声呦呦细长，或在檐宇之际，或在庭树上。每占吉凶，辄先索琵琶，随弹而言事，事有验。时郄倚为长史，问当迁官，云：“不久持节也。”寻为南蛮校尉。子为国郎中，亲领此土，荆州俗谚或云是老鼠所作，名曰灵侯。 [60]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [60]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 鬼附之 - Possessed by ghosts, implying a supernatural event.
    • 聲呦呦細長 - Describes a voice as soft, mournful, high-pitched, and drawn out.
    • 每占吉凶，輒先索琵琶，隨彈而言事 - A ritual where the possessed request a lute, play it, and then speak about the matter to predict good or bad fortune.
    • 事有驗 - The predictions come true, validating the ghost's ability.
    • 持節 - Refers to a high-ranking official position, often involving diplomatic or military duties.
    • 子為國郎中 - Indicates that the son became a Langzhong (an imperial official).
    • 親領此土 - Means to personally govern this land.
    • 靈侯 - A title or name, possibly referring to a deity or a legendary figure.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 南平國 - The Kingdom of Nanping, a historical region in southern China.
    • 蠻兵 - Barbarian soldiers, typically referring to non-Han Chinese groups.
    • 郄倚 - A historical figure, likely a high-ranking official during the time.
    • 南蠻校尉 - A military position responsible for overseeing the southern barbarian regions.
    • 荊州俗諺 - Refers to a local saying or proverb from the Jiangzhou region.
    • 老鼠所作 - Suggests that the phenomenon is attributed to rats, possibly a folk belief or superstition.

    Proper Nouns

    • 南平國 - The Kingdom of Nanping.
    • 姑孰 - A place name, likely a city or region.
    • 郄倚 - A historical figure.
    • 南蠻校尉 - A military position.
    • 荊州 - A historical region in southern China.
    • 靈侯 - A title or name, possibly a deity or legendary figure.

    In the Kingdom of Nanping, there were barbarian soldiers stationed at Gushu, and they were possessed by ghosts. The ghosts made a soft, mournful sound, high-pitched and drawn out. Sometimes they appeared under the eaves, and sometimes on the trees in the courtyard. Whenever they predicted good or bad fortune, they would first request a lute, play it, and then speak about the matter. Their predictions always came true. At the time, Xie Yi was the Longshi (a high-ranking official), and he asked the ghost if he would be promoted. The ghost said, "You will soon be appointed as a Nanman Xiaowei (a military position)." Soon after, Xie Yi was indeed appointed as the Nanman Xiaowei. His son became a Langzhong (an imperial official) and personally governed this land. The local saying in Jiangzhou sometimes attributes this phenomenon to rats, and they call it Linghou (a title or name, possibly a deity or legendary figure). [60]

    昔有老姥，雨夜纺绩，断失其钅奏所在。姥独骂云：“何物鬼担去！”户外即有应声言：“暂借避雨，实不偷钅奏。宜就觅之。”姥惊惧，窥外，略无所见，钅奏亦寻获。
    　　吴兴袁乞妻临终执乞手云：“我死，君再婚否？”乞言不忍也。既而服竟更娶。乞白日见其死妇语之云：“君先结誓，云何负言！”因以刀割其阳道，虽不致死，人性永废。
    　　临川聂包死数年，忽诣南丰相沈道袭作歌，其歌笑甚有伦次，每歌辄作“花上盈盈正闻行，当归不闻死复生。”事异辞怪。
    　　琅琊王凝之字叔平，妻左将军夫人谢氏奕之女也。尝频亡二男，悼惜甚过，哭泣累年。若居至艰，后忽见二儿俱还，皆若锁械，慰免其母，宜自宽割。儿并有罪，若垂哀怜，可为作福。于是哀痛稍止而勤功德。 [61]

    琅琊王骋之妻陈郡谢氏生一男，小字奴子。经年后王以妇婢招利为妾。谢元嘉八年病终，王之墓在会稽，假瘗建康东冈。既窆反虞，舆灵入屋，凭儿忽于空中掷地，便有嗔声曰：“何不作挽歌，今我寂寂上道耶！”骋之云：“非为永葬，故不具仪耳。”
    　　沙门有支法存者，本自胡人，生长广州，妙善医术，遂成巨富。有八尺<翕毛>，光彩耀目，作百种形象。又有沈香八尺板床，居常香馥。太原王琰为广州刺吏，大儿邵之屡求二物，法存不与，王因状法存豪纵，乃杀而籍没家财焉。法存死后，形见于府内，辄打阁下鼓，似若称冤，如此经日，王寻得病，恒见法存守之，少时遂亡。邵之比至扬都，亦丧。
    　　河内司马惟之奴天雄死后还，其妇来喜闻体有鞭痕而脚著锁，问云：“有何过，至如此？”曰：“曾因醉窃骂大家，今受此罪。” [62]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [62]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 琅琊王骋之妻陈郡谢氏生一男，小字奴子 - Describes the birth of a son to Lady Xie of Chenjun, wife of Wang Cheng of Langya, with the child given the nickname 'Nusi'.
    • 经年后王以妇婢招利为妾 - Indicates that after a year, Wang Cheng took a female servant named Zhaoli as his concubine.
    • 谢元嘉八年病终 - States that Lady Xie died in the eighth year of the Yuchang era (431 AD) during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 假瘗建康东冈 - Means that she was temporarily buried in the east of Jiankang Hill.
    • 既窆反虞 - Refers to the ritual of returning to the house after the burial.
    • 舆灵入屋 - Describes the carriage carrying the coffin entering the house.
    • 凭儿忽于空中掷地 - Describes a sudden event where a spirit or ghost appears to throw something to the ground.
    • 便有嗔声曰：“何不作挽歌，今我寂寂上道耶！” - Describes the angry voice saying, 'Why don't you sing a lament, now I am silently going on the road!'
    • 骋之云：“非为永葬，故不具仪耳。” - Means that Wang Cheng said, 'It's not for a permanent burial, so the full ceremony is not held.'
    • 沙门有支法存者，本自胡人，生长广州，妙善医术，遂成巨富 - Introduces a Buddhist monk named Zhi Fachu, originally from the Western Regions, who grew up in Guangzhou and became wealthy through his medical skills.
    • 有八尺<翕毛>，光彩耀目，作百种形象 - Describes a magical item, possibly a talisman or amulet, that is eight chi long and emits dazzling light, capable of taking on various forms.
    • 又有沈香八尺板床，居常香馥 - Describes an eight chi long board bed made of agarwood, which always emits a fragrant aroma.
    • 太原王琰为广州刺吏，大儿邵之屡求二物，法存不与 - States that Wang Yan, governor of Guangzhou, and his eldest son, Shaozhi, repeatedly asked for these two items, but Zhi Fachu refused to give them.
    • 王因状法存豪纵，乃杀而籍没家财焉 - Means that Wang Yan accused Zhi Fachu of being arrogant and extravagant, and had him killed, confiscating his property.
    • 法存死后，形见于府内，辄打阁下鼓，似若称冤 - Describes how after Zhi Fachu's death, his spirit appeared in the government office, hitting the drum in the hall, as if complaining about his wrongful death.
    • 如此经日，王寻得病，恒见法存守之，少时遂亡 - Means that for several days, Wang Yan fell ill, constantly seeing Zhi Fachu's spirit guarding him, and eventually died.
    • 邵之比至扬都，亦丧 - States that Shaozhi, upon arriving in Yangdu, also died.
    • 河内司马惟之奴天雄死后还，其妇来喜闻体有鞭痕而脚著锁 - Describes how after Tianxiong, a servant of Sima Wei of Hedong, died, his wife, Laixi, heard that his body had marks of beating and was chained.
    • 问云：“有何过，至如此？”曰：“曾因醉窃骂大家，今受此罪。” - Describes Laixi asking what crime Tianxiong committed to deserve such punishment, and he replied that he had insulted the master while drunk.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 琅琊王骋 - Refers to Wang Cheng, a nobleman from Langya.
    • 陈郡谢氏 - Refers to Lady Xie of Chenjun, indicating her noble status.
    • 元嘉八年 - Refers to the eighth year of the Yuchang era (431 AD) during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 建康 - Refers to Jiankang, the capital of the Eastern Jin and Southern Dynasties, now known as Nanjing.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks.
    • 支法存 - Refers to a specific Buddhist monk.
    • 胡人 - Refers to people from the Western Regions, often associated with Central Asia.
    • 广州 - Refers to Guangzhou, a major city in southern China.
    • 太原王琰 - Refers to Wang Yan, a governor of Guangzhou.
    • 邵之 - Refers to Shaozhi, the eldest son of Wang Yan.
    • 扬都 - Refers to Yangdu, another name for Jiankang.
    • 河内司马惟之 - Refers to Sima Wei of Hedong, a nobleman.
    • 天雄 - Refers to Tianxiong, a servant of Sima Wei.
    • 来喜 - Refers to Laixi, the wife of Tianxiong.
    • 元嘉 - Refers to the reign title of Emperor Wu of Liu Song.
    • 沈香 - Refers to agarwood, a valuable fragrant wood.
    • <翕毛> - Refers to a type of magical item or talisman.
    • 百种形象 - Refers to the ability to take on various forms.
    • 豪纵 - Refers to arrogance and extravagance.
    • 籍没 - Refers to the confiscation of property.
    • 阁下鼓 - Refers to the drum in the hall of a government office.
    • 称冤 - Refers to complaining about a wrongful death.
    • 鞭痕 - Refers to marks of beating.
    • 锁 - Refers to chains.
    • 大家 - Refers to the master or owner.

    Proper Nouns

    • 琅琊王骋 - Wang Cheng of Langya.
    • 陈郡谢氏 - Lady Xie of Chenjun.
    • 元嘉八年 - Eighth year of the Yuchang era (431 AD) during the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 建康 - Jiankang, the capital of the Eastern Jin and Southern Dynasties, now known as Nanjing.
    • 沙门 - Buddhist monks.
    • 支法存 - Buddhist monk Zhi Fachu.
    • 胡人 - People from the Western Regions, often associated with Central Asia.
    • 广州 - Guangzhou, a major city in southern China.
    • 太原王琰 - Wang Yan, a governor of Guangzhou.
    • 邵之 - Shaozhi, the eldest son of Wang Yan.
    • 扬都 - Yangdu, another name for Jiankang.
    • 河内司马惟之 - Sima Wei of Hedong, a nobleman.
    • 天雄 - Tianxiong, a servant of Sima Wei.
    • 来喜 - Laixi, the wife of Tianxiong.
    • 元嘉 - Reign title of Emperor Wu of Liu Song.
    • 沈香 - Agarwood, a valuable fragrant wood.
    • <翕毛> - A type of magical item or talisman.
    • 百种形象 - The ability to take on various forms.
    • 豪纵 - Arrogance and extravagance.
    • 籍没 - Confiscation of property.
    • 阁下鼓 - The drum in the hall of a government office.
    • 称冤 - Complaining about a wrongful death.
    • 鞭痕 - Marks of beating.
    • 锁 - Chains.
    • 大家 - The master or owner.

    A Son Born to Lady Xie of Chenjun and Wang Cheng of Langya

    Lady Xie of Chenjun, the wife of Wang Cheng of Langya, gave birth to a son, whom she nicknamed "Nusi." After a year, Wang Cheng took a female servant named Zhaoli as his concubine. Lady Xie died in the eighth year of the Yuchang era (431 AD) during the Liu Song dynasty. Her tomb was located in Kuaiji, but she was temporarily buried in the east of Jiankang Hill. After the burial, when they returned to the house, the carriage carrying the coffin entered the house. Suddenly, a spirit appeared in the air and threw something to the ground, uttering an angry voice: "Why don't you sing a lament, now I am silently going on the road!" Wang Cheng replied, "It's not for a permanent burial, so the full ceremony is not held."

    There was a Buddhist monk named Zhi Fachu, originally from the Western Regions, who grew up in Guangzhou and became wealthy through his medical skills. He possessed a magical item, possibly a talisman or amulet, that was eight chi long and emitted dazzling light, capable of taking on various forms. He also had an eight chi long board bed made of agarwood, which always emitted a fragrant aroma. Wang Yan, governor of Guangzhou, and his eldest son, Shaozhi, repeatedly asked for these two items, but Zhi Fachu refused to give them. Wang Yan accused Zhi Fachu of being arrogant and extravagant and had him killed, confiscating his property. After Zhi Fachu's death, his spirit appeared in the government office, hitting the drum in the hall, as if complaining about his wrongful death. For several days, Wang Yan fell ill, constantly seeing Zhi Fachu's spirit guarding him, and eventually died. Shaozhi, upon arriving in Yangdu, also died.

    After Tianxiong, a servant of Sima Wei of Hedong, died, his wife, Laixi, heard that his body had marks of beating and was chained. She asked, "What crime did you commit to deserve such punishment?" Tianxiong replied that he had insulted the master while drunk and was now suffering this punishment. [62]

    陈郡颜延之字延年有爱妾，死，延之痛惜甚。至以冬日临哭，忽见妾排屏风以压延之，延之惧，坠地，因病卒。
    　　永初中，张骥于都丧亡。司马茂之往哭，见骥凭几而坐，以箸刺ХЦ食之。
    　　元嘉二十六年，豫章胡庇之尝为武昌郡。入厩中，便有鬼怪，中宵笼月，户牖少开，有人倚立户外，状似小儿。户闭便闻人行，如著木屐声，看则无所见。如此甚数。二十八年三月，举家悉得时病，既而渐差。
    　　刘元字幼祖，少与武帝善，而轻何无忌，遂不相得。乃去游吴郡虎邱山，必欲留焉。夜临风长啸，对月鼓琴，于剑池上忽闻环音，一女子衣紫罗之衣，垂钿带，谓元曰：“吴王爱女，愿来相访。”元曰：“吴王爱女，岂非韩重妻紫玉耶？”遂与元偕行，谓元曰：“闻君与刘裕相得，裕是王者，然与何无忌不美。此人恐为君患。若北还仕魏朝官，亦不减牧伯。”言旋忽不见，乃在一大陵松树下约去虎邱三里许，元乃北去仕魏，累官青州刺史。 [63]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [63]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 以冬日临哭 - Literal phrase meaning 'to cry at the winter day,' likely referring to a ritual or emotional act performed during a specific season.
    • 排屏风以压延之 - Vivid description of a supernatural event where a ghost pushes a screen to press down on Yan Zhi.
    • 临风长啸 - Poetic expression meaning 'to howl at the wind,' often associated with deep emotions or a sense of isolation.
    • 对月鼓琴 - Literary phrase meaning 'to play the qin (a traditional Chinese zither) under the moon,' symbolizing solitude and reflection.
    • 环佩音 - Term describing the sound of jade pendants, often used to indicate the presence of a ghost or spirit.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 陈郡颜延之 - Yan Zhi, a prominent figure in the Southern Song dynasty, known for his literary works and political career.
    • 张骥 - Historical figure whose death is mentioned in the text.
    • 司马茂之 - Contemporary of Yan Zhi, known for his involvement in various historical events.
    • 元嘉二十六年 - The 26th year of the Yuangang era of the Southern Song dynasty (449 AD).
    • 豫章胡庇之 - Historical figure from the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 武昌郡 - Region in present-day Hubei Province, known for its historical significance.
    • 虎邱山 - Mountain in Jiangsu Province, famous for its historical and cultural sites.
    • 剑池 - Famous scenic spot in Suzhou, known for its historical and mythological significance.
    • 刘元 - Historical figure who had a close relationship with Emperor Wu of Song.
    • 何无忌 - High-ranking official and military leader during the Southern Song dynasty, known for his loyalty and bravery.
    • 魏朝官 - Officials in the Northern Wei dynasty, a powerful state in northern China during the Northern and Southern Dynasties period.
    • 青州刺史 - Position of Governor of Qingzhou, a province in northern China.

    Proper Nouns

    • 陈郡颜延之 - Yan Zhi, a prominent literary figure and politician.
    • 张骥 - Historical figure.
    • 司马茂之 - Contemporary of Yan Zhi.
    • 胡庇之 - Historical figure from the Southern Song dynasty.
    • 刘元 - Historical figure with a close relationship to Emperor Wu of Song.
    • 何无忌 - High-ranking official and military leader.
    • 魏朝官 - Officials in the Northern Wei dynasty.
    • 青州刺史 - Position of Governor of Qingzhou.

    Tancheng Yan Yanzhi, whose courtesy name was Yannian, had a beloved concubine who died, and Yanzhi was deeply grieved. He even cried at the winter day, and suddenly saw his concubine push a screen to press down on him. Yanzhi was terrified, fell to the ground, and subsequently fell ill and died.

    During the Yongchun period, Zhang Ji died in the capital. Sima Maowei went to mourn him and saw Zhang Ji sitting by a table, using chopsticks to pierce and eat food.

    In the 26th year of the Yuangang era, Hu Bi from Yuzhang had once been the governor of Wuchang. Upon entering the stable, he encountered ghostly phenomena. At midnight, with the moon shining through the windows, a person stood leaning against the door, looking like a child. When the door closed, he heard footsteps, as if someone was wearing wooden clogs, but when he looked, there was nothing to be seen. This happened repeatedly. In the 28th year of the Yuangang era, in March, the entire family contracted a seasonal illness, but gradually recovered.

    Liu Yuan, whose courtesy name was Youzu, was close to Emperor Wu in his youth but looked down on He Wuj [63]

    元嘉二十年，王怀之丁母忧。墓毕，忽见树上有妪，头戴大，身披白罗裙，足不践柯亭然虚立。还家叙，述其女遂得暴疾，面乃变作向树杪鬼状，乃与麝香服之，寻复如常。世云麝香辟恶，此其验也。
    　　元嘉中魏郡张承吉息元庆年十二，见一鬼长三尺，一足而鸟爪，背有鳞甲，来招元庆。恍惚如狂，游走非所。父母挞之，俄闻空中云：“是我所教，幸勿与罚。”张有二卷羊中敬书，忽失所在，鬼于梁上掷还一卷，少裂坏，乃为补治。王家嫁女就张借，（疑有缺）鬼求纸笔代答。张素工巧，尝造一弹弓，鬼借之，明日送还而皆折坏。 [64]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [64]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元嘉二十年 - Indicates the year 443 CE during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 丁母憂 - A euphemism for experiencing the death of one's mother, followed by a period of mourning.
    • 墓畢 - Means 'after the burial' or 'once the tomb was completed.'
    • 忽見樹上有妪 - Suddenly saw an old woman on a tree.
    • 頭戴大 - Describes the old woman wearing a large hairpin or ornament on her head. The character '' is likely a variant of '髻' (a bun or hairstyle).
    • 身披白羅裙 - Means 'wearing a white silk skirt.'
    • 足不践柯亭然虚立 - Her feet did not touch the tree, and she stood there as if suspended in mid-air. This describes the supernatural nature of the old woman.
    • 還家叙 - 'When he returned home and told the story.'
    • 述其女遂得暴疾 - 'His daughter suddenly fell ill with a severe disease.'
    • 面乃变作向树杪鬼状 - 'Her face transformed into the appearance of a ghost from the tree-top.'
    • 乃与麝香服之 - 'Then he gave her musk to take.'
    • 寻复如常 - 'Soon she recovered and returned to normal.'
    • 世云麝香辟恶 - 'People say that musk repels evil.'
    • 此其验也 - 'This is the proof of it.'
    • 元嘉中魏郡张承吉息元庆年十二 - 'During the Yuanjia era, in the Wei commandery, Zhang Chengji's son Yuanqing was twelve years old.'
    • 见一鬼长三尺 - 'He saw a ghost three feet tall.'
    • 一足而鸟爪 - 'With one foot and bird-like claws.'
    • 背有鳞甲 - 'With scales on its back.'
    • 来招元庆 - 'It came to beckon Yuanqing.'
    • 恍惚如狂 - 'He became dazed and acted like a madman.'
    • 游走非所 - 'He wandered aimlessly.'
    • 父母挞之 - 'His parents beat him.'
    • 俄闻空中云 - 'Soon, a voice from the air said.'
    • 是我所教，幸勿与罚 - 'This was what I taught him; please do not punish him.'
    • 张有二卷羊中敬书 - 'Zhang had two scrolls of writings by Yang Zhongjing.'
    • 忽失所在 - 'They suddenly disappeared.'
    • 鬼于梁上掷还一卷 - 'The ghost threw one scroll back from the beam.'
    • 少裂坏 - 'It was slightly torn and damaged.'
    • 乃为补治 - 'He then repaired it.'
    • 王家嫁女就张借 - 'The Wang family, who were marrying off a daughter, borrowed from Zhang.'
    • 鬼求纸笔代答 - 'The ghost asked for paper and ink to write the reply.'
    • 张素工巧 - 'Zhang was skilled and ingenious.'
    • 尝造一弹弓 - 'He once made a slingshot.'
    • 鬼借之 - 'The ghost borrowed it.'
    • 明日送还而皆折坏 - 'The next day, it returned it broken.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉 - The era name of Emperor Wen of Song, referring to the years 424-453 CE.
    • 魏郡 - An administrative region in the Central Plains during the Southern and Northern Dynasties.
    • 张承吉 - A fictional or historical figure in this context.
    • 元庆 - The name of Zhang Chengji's son.
    • 羊中敬 - A historical or literary figure whose writings are referenced.
    • 王家 - A family name, likely referring to a noble or wealthy family.
    • 麝香 - Musk, a traditional Chinese medicine and incense ingredient believed to have various properties, including repelling evil spirits.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元嘉 - The era name of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 魏郡 - An administrative region in the Central Plains.
    • 张承吉 - A historical or fictional figure.
    • 元庆 - The name of Zhang Chengji's son.
    • 羊中敬 - A historical or literary figure.
    • 王家 - A family name, likely referring to a noble or wealthy family.

    During the twentieth year of the Yuanjia era, Wang Huizhi experienced the death of his mother and observed the customary period of mourning. After the burial was completed, he suddenly saw an old woman on a tree, wearing a large hairpin and a white silk skirt, standing there as if suspended in mid-air with her feet not touching the tree. When he returned home and told the story, he described how his daughter suddenly fell ill with a severe disease, and her face transformed into the appearance of a ghost from the tree-top. He then gave her musk to take, and soon she recovered and returned to normal. People say that musk repels evil, and this is the proof of it.

    During the Yuanjia era, in the Wei commandery, Zhang Chengji's son Yuanqing, who was twelve years old, saw a ghost three feet tall, with one foot and bird-like claws, and scales on its back. The ghost came to beckon Yuanqing, who became dazed and acted like a madman, wandering aimlessly. His parents beat him, and soon a voice from the air said, "This was what I taught him; please do not punish him." Zhang had two scrolls of writings by Yang Zhongjing, which suddenly disappeared. The ghost threw one scroll back from the beam, which was slightly torn and damaged, and he then repaired it. The Wang family, who were marrying off a daughter, borrowed from Zhang, and the ghost asked for paper and ink to write the reply. Zhang was skilled and ingenious, and he once made a slingshot. The ghost borrowed it, and the next day, it returned it broken. [64]

    元嘉中，颍川宋寂昼忽有一足鬼长三尺，遂为寂驱使，欲与邻人ヅυ而无五木，鬼乃取刀斫庭中杨枝于户间，作之即烧灼，黑白虽分明但朴耳。
    　　元嘉十二年，长山郭悖病亡。后儿孙见悖著帻布裙，在灵床上呼孙，与语云：“今得七日假，假满将去。二小鬼捉幞在门，可就取也。”孙求幞即得。人云：“汝叔从都还，得犁钅辟，可试取看。”便以呈之，仍以两铁钳加苍苍作声，语孙曰：“我无复归缘。”从此而绝。
    　　黄州治下有黄父鬼，出则为祟，所著衣袷皆黄。至人家张口而笑，必得疫疠，长短无定，随篱高下，自不出。已十余年，土俗畏怖，惶恐不绝。 [65]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [65]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元嘉中 - Refers to the middle period of the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song (424-453 AD).
    • 驅使 - Literally 'to drive and use,' here meaning to control or command.
    • 五木 - Refers to the game pieces used in the ancient Chinese game of *weiqi* (Go).
    • 燒灼 - A technique used in *weiqi* to mark the game board.
    • 黑白雖分明但朴耳 - A description of the game board, indicating that while the black and white pieces are clearly distinguishable, they are simple and unadorned.
    • 得七日假 - Means 'to get a seven-day leave,' a common expression in classical Chinese for a temporary departure from the usual state.
    • 假滿將去 - Means 'when the leave is over, I will leave.'
    • 二小鬼捉幞在門 - Means 'two small ghosts are holding a *fu* (a type of headscarf) at the door.'
    • 可就取也 - Means 'you can go and take it.'
    • 從都還 - Means 'coming back from the capital.'
    • 加苍苍作声 - Means 'make a creaking sound,' suggesting the use of a tool or object.
    • 無復歸緣 - Means 'no longer have a reason to return.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Liu Song (424-453 AD).
    • 颍川 - A historical region in Henan Province, known for its cultural significance.
    • 长山 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location in the region.
    • 黄州 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in the region.
    • 黄父鬼 - Refers to a type of ghost or spirit, possibly a local legend or folk belief.
    • 苍苍 - A term often used to describe a creaking or groaning sound.
    • 土俗 - Refers to local customs and traditions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元嘉 - The reign title of Emperor Wen of Liu Song.
    • 颍川 - A historical region in Henan Province.
    • 长山 - A place name, possibly referring to a specific location in the region.
    • 黄州 - A place name, likely referring to a specific location in the region.
    • 郭悖 - The name of a person who appears as a ghost.
    • 黄父鬼 - A type of ghost or spirit.

    During the reign of Emperor Wen (Yuanjia period), in Yen-chuan, Cao Ji suddenly had a one-legged ghost appear, three feet tall, who was then controlled by Cao Ji. The ghost wanted to play a game with a neighbor but lacked the necessary game pieces. It then took a knife and cut branches from a willow tree in the courtyard, marking the game board with them. The black and white pieces were clearly distinguishable but were simple and unadorned.

    In the twelfth year of the Yuanjia reign, in Chang-shan, Guo Bo died. After his death, his descendants saw him appear, wearing a headscarf and a cloth skirt, on the spirit table. He called out to his grandson and said, "I have been given a seven-day leave, and when it ends, I will leave. Two small ghosts are holding a headscarf at the door; you can go and take it." The grandson went to get the headscarf and succeeded. Someone said, "Your uncle came back from the capital and brought a tool or weapon; you can try it." The grandson then showed it to the ghost, who used two iron clamps to make a creaking sound and said, "I no longer have a reason to return." From then on, the ghost disappeared.

    In Huangzhou, there was a yellow-clad ghost that caused harm when it appeared. The ghost wore yellow clothing and would appear at people's homes, opening its mouth and laughing, which would inevitably bring illness. The ghost's height varied, and it would appear according to the height of the fences, never staying long. This had been going on for over ten years, and the local customs were filled with fear and constant anxiety. [65]

    庐陵人郭庆之有家生婢，名采薇，年少有美色。宋孝建年中忽有一人自称山灵，如人裸身形，长丈余，胸臂皆有黄色，肤貌端洁，言音周正，呼为黄父鬼，来通此婢。婢云：意事如人。鬼遂数来，常隐其身。时或露形，形变无常，乍大乍小，或似烟气，或为石，或为小鬼，或为妇人，或如鸟兽足迹，或如人长二尺许，或似鹅迹，掌大如盘，开户闭牖，其入如神，与婢戏笑如人也。
    　　元嘉十四年，徐道饶忽见一鬼自言是其先人。于时冬日，天气清朗，先积稻屋下，云汝明日可曝谷。天方大雨，未有晴时，饶从其教，鬼亦助辇，后果霖雨。时有见者，形如猕猴。饶就道士请符，悬著窗中，见便大笑云：“欲以此断我，我自能从狗窦中入。”虽则此语而不复进。经数日，叹云：“徐叔宝来，吾不宜见之。”后日果至，于是遂绝。 [66]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [66]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 忽有一人自称山灵 - Sudden appearance of a person claiming to be a mountain spirit.
    • 如人裸身形 - Describes the appearance as a naked human form.
    • 胸臂皆有黄色 - Indicates the ghost's chest and arms were yellow, suggesting divine or supernatural nature.
    • 肤貌端洁 - Means the skin and appearance were clean and pure, suggesting a refined or noble appearance.
    • 言音周正 - Means the speech was correct and proper, indicating clarity and correctness.
    • 呼为黄父鬼 - Refers to the ghost being called 'Yellow Father Ghost,' a specific title or name given to the spirit.
    • 意事如人 - Means the matter was like a human, suggesting the ghost's interactions were similar to those of a human.
    • 形变无常 - Means the shape changes without constancy, indicating the ghost could change its form frequently.
    • 乍大乍小 - Means sometimes big, sometimes small, describing the ghost's size changes.
    • 或似烟气 - Means sometimes like smoke, indicating the ghost could appear as smoke.
    • 或为石 - Means sometimes like a stone, indicating the ghost could appear as a stone.
    • 或为小鬼 - Means sometimes like a small ghost, indicating the ghost could appear as a small ghost.
    • 或为妇人 - Means sometimes like a woman, indicating the ghost could appear as a woman.
    • 或如鸟兽足迹 - Means sometimes like bird or animal footprints, indicating the ghost could leave traces resembling those of animals.
    • 或如人长二尺许 - Means sometimes like a person about two feet tall, indicating the ghost could appear as a small human.
    • 或似鹅迹 - Means sometimes like a goose's footprint, indicating the ghost could leave traces resembling those of a goose.
    • 掌大如盘 - Means palms as large as a plate, describing the ghost's large palms.
    • 开户闭牖 - Means open doors and windows, indicating the ghost could manipulate doors and windows.
    • 其入如神 - Means its entry was like a god, suggesting the ghost's movements were supernatural.
    • 与婢戏笑如人也 - Means played and laughed with the maid like a human, indicating the ghost interacted with the maid in a human-like manner.
    • 元嘉十四年 - Refers to the year 437 AD during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 徐道饶忽见一鬼自言是其先人 - Means Meng Dao Rao suddenly saw a ghost who claimed to be his ancestor.
    • 于时冬日，天气清朗 - Means at that time, it was winter, and the weather was clear and bright.
    • 先积稻屋下 - Means firstly, rice was stored under the house.
    • 云汝明日可曝谷 - Means he said you can dry the rice tomorrow.
    • 天方大雨，未有晴时 - Means it was raining heavily, and there was no sign of clearing up.
    • 饶从其教 - Means Meng Dao Rao followed his advice.
    • 鬼亦助辇 - Means the ghost also helped carry the rice.
    • 后果霖雨 - Means later, it rained heavily.
    • 时有见者，形如猕猴 - Means sometimes those who saw it had a shape like a monkey.
    • 饶就道士请符 - Means Meng Dao Rao went to a Taoist priest to ask for a talisman.
    • 悬著窗中 - Means hung it in the window.
    • 见便大笑云 - Means when he saw it, he laughed and said.
    • 欲以此断我 - Means you want to stop me with this.
    • 我自能从狗窦中入 - Means I can enter through the dog hole myself.
    • 虽则此语而不复进 - Means although he said this, he did not enter again.
    • 经数日 - Means after several days.
    • 叹云 - Means he sighed and said.
    • 徐叔宝来，吾不宜见之 - Means when Xu Shubao comes, I should not see him.
    • 后日果至 - Means the next day, he indeed came.
    • 于是遂绝 - Means and thus it ceased.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 庐陵人郭庆之 - Refers to a person named Guo Qingzhi from Luxi (庐陵), an ancient region in Jiangxi Province.
    • 宋孝建年中 - Refers to the year 454 AD during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 徐道饶 - Refers to a person named Meng Dao Rao.
    • 元嘉十四年 - Refers to the year 437 AD during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 徐叔宝 - Refers to a person named Xu Shubao, likely a relative or acquaintance of Meng Dao Rao.

    Proper Nouns

    • 庐陵 - An ancient region in Jiangxi Province.
    • 郭庆之 - A person named Guo Qingzhi.
    • 采薇 - A household maid named Caiwei.
    • 宋孝建 - The year 454 AD during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 徐道饶 - A person named Meng Dao Rao.
    • 元嘉十四年 - The year 437 AD during the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 徐叔宝 - A person named Xu Shubao.

    A man from Luxi named Guo Qingzhi had a household maid named Caiwei, who was young and beautiful. During the year 454 AD, a person suddenly appeared who called himself a mountain spirit. He had a naked human form, was over ten feet tall, had yellow chests and arms, and had a clean and pure appearance. His speech was correct and proper, and he was called the "Yellow Father Ghost." He came to communicate with the maid. The maid said that the matter was like a human. The ghost frequently visited but often remained hidden. Sometimes he revealed his form, which changed without constancy, sometimes appearing large, sometimes small, sometimes like smoke, sometimes like a stone, sometimes like a small ghost, sometimes like a woman, sometimes like bird or animal footprints, sometimes like a person about two feet tall, sometimes like a goose's footprint, with palms as large as a plate. He could open doors and windows, and his entry was like a god, playing and laughing with the maid like a human.

    In the year 437 AD, Meng Dao Rao suddenly saw a ghost who claimed to be his ancestor. At that time, it was winter, and the weather was clear and bright. The ghost first advised him to store rice under the house, saying he could dry the rice the next day. It was raining heavily, and there was no sign of clearing up. Meng Dao Rao followed his advice, and the ghost also helped carry the rice. Later, it rained heavily. Sometimes those who saw it had a shape like a monkey. Meng Dao Rao went to a Taoist priest to ask for a talisman, which he hung in the window. When he saw it, he laughed and said, "You want to stop me with this, I can enter through the dog hole myself." Although he said this, he did not enter again. After several days, he sighed and said, "When Xu Shubao comes, I should not see him." The next day, he indeed came, and thus it ceased. [66]

    安定梁清字道修，居扬州右尚方间桓徐州故宅。元嘉十四年二月，数有异光，仍闻擘萝声。令婢子松罗往看，见一人问，云：“姓华名芙蓉，为六甲至尊所使，从太微紫宫下来过旧居。”乃留不去。或鸟头人身，举面是毛，掷洒粪秽，引弓射之，应弦而灭，并有绛汁染箭。又睹一物形如猴，悬在树标，令人刺中，其体堕地淹没，经日反从屋上跛行，就婢乞食，团饣卞授之，顿造二升。经日众鬼群至，丑恶不可称论。松罗床帐尘石飞扬，累晨不息。婢采菊，路逢一鬼，著衣帻乘马，卫从数十，谓采菊曰：“我是天上仙人，勿名作鬼。 [67]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [67]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 安定梁清字道修 - Formal introduction of a person, including their place of origin, surname, given name, and courtesy name.
    • 居扬州右尚方间桓徐州故宅 - Describes the location of the residence using specific historical and geographical references.
    • 数有异光，仍闻擘萝声 - Describes a series of strange phenomena, with '擘萝' being a metaphor for the sound of silk being torn.
    • 令婢子松罗往看 - Indicates the action of sending someone to investigate the strange phenomena.
    • 云：“姓华华芙蓉，为六甲至尊所使，从太微紫宫下来过旧居。” - Supernatural explanation for the phenomena, involving a celestial being.
    • 乃留不去 - Indicates the persistence of the supernatural presence.
    • 或鸟头人身，举面是毛 - Describes the appearance of the supernatural being.
    • 掷洒粪秽 - Describes a disturbing action by the supernatural being.
    • 引弓射之，应弦而灭 - Describes an attempt to drive away the supernatural being.
    • 并有绛汁染箭 - Describes the aftermath of the shooting.
    • 又睹一物形如猴，悬在树标 - Describes another supernatural entity.
    • 令人刺中，其体堕地淹没 - Describes another attempt to drive away the supernatural entity.
    • 经日众鬼群至，丑恶不可称论 - Describes the culmination of the supernatural events.
    • 松罗床帐尘石飞扬，累晨不息 - Describes the physical effects of the supernatural events.
    • 婢采菊，路逢一鬼，著衣帻乘马，卫从数十 - Describes another encounter with a supernatural being.
    • 谓采菊曰：“我是天上仙人，勿名作鬼。” - Declaration by the supernatural being to clarify its identity.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 安定 - A place name, possibly referring to a region in ancient China.
    • 扬州右尚方间桓徐州故宅 - Refers to a specific location in Yangzhou, using historical and geographical references.
    • 六甲至尊 - Refers to a supreme being associated with the Six Jia, a concept in Chinese cosmology.
    • 太微紫宫 - Refers to the Purple Palace in the Great Microcosm, a celestial realm in Chinese mythology.
    • 六甲 - Refers to the Six Jia, a concept in Chinese cosmology and astrology.
    • 太微 - Refers to the Great Microcosm, a celestial realm in Chinese mythology.
    • 紫宫 - Refers to the Purple Palace, a celestial realm in Chinese mythology.
    • 松罗 - The name of the maid, possibly a common name in the period.
    • 华芙蓉 - The name of the supernatural being, possibly a symbolic or literary name.
    • 采菊 - The act of picking chrysanthemums, often associated with leisure and contemplation in Chinese culture.
    • 天上仙人 - Refers to a celestial immortal, a common figure in Chinese mythology.

    Proper Nouns

    • 安定梁清字道修 - The full name of the person, including place of origin, surname, given name, and courtesy name.
    • 扬州右尚方间桓徐州故宅 - The specific location of the residence, using historical and geographical references.
    • 华芙蓉 - The name of the supernatural being.
    • 松罗 - The name of the maid.
    • 采菊 - The act of picking chrysanthemums.
    • 天上仙人 - The name of the celestial immortal.

    An Ding Liang Qing, courtesy name Dao Xiu, lived in the old mansion of Huán Xu Zhou in the Right Imperial Arsenal area of Yangzhou. In the 14th year of the Yuanjia period (AD 437), he frequently saw unusual light and heard the sound of breaking silk. He sent the maid Song Luo to look, and she saw a being who said, "My surname is Hua, my given name is Fu Rong. I am sent by the Supreme Being of the Six Jia to visit your old residence from the Purple Palace in the Great Microcosm." So he stayed and did not leave. Sometimes it had a bird's head and human body, with feathers covering its face. It threw and scattered feces, and people drew bows to shoot it, and it disappeared with a twang, with red dye on the arrow. They also saw a creature shaped like a monkey, hanging from a tree branch. People tried to stab it, and its body fell to the ground and disappeared. After a day, many ghosts gathered, their ugliness beyond description. Song Luo's bed and curtains were covered in dust and stones, flying for several mornings without stopping. The maid who picked chrysanthemums met a ghost on the road, dressed in a cap and riding a horse, with dozens of attendants. He said to the chrysanthemum picker, "I am a celestial immortal, do not call me a ghost." [67]

    ”问：“何以恒掷秽污？”答曰：“粪污者，钱财之象也。投掷者，速迁之征也。”顷之，清果为扬武将军、北鲁郡太守。清厌毒既久，乃呼外国道人波罗ふ诵咒文，见诸鬼怖惧，逾垣穴壁而走，皆作鸟声，于此都绝。在郡少时，夜中松罗复见威仪器械人众数十一人，戴帻送书，粗纸有七十许字，笔迹婉媚，远拟羲、献。又歌云：坐侬孔雀楼，遥闻凤凰鼓。下我邹山头，仿佛见梁鲁。鬼有叔操丧，哭泣答吊，不异世人。鬼传教，曾乞松罗一函书，题云：故孔修之死罪白笺，以吊其叔丧，叙致哀情，甚有铨次。复云近往西方见一沙门，自名大摩刹，问君消息，寄五丸香以相与之。清先奉使敦煌，忆见此僧。清有婢产，于此遂绝。 [68]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [68]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 何以恒掷秽污 - A question asking why the person constantly throws filth. '何以' means 'why' or 'how,' and '恒' means 'always' or 'constantly.'
    • 粪污者，钱财之象也 - '粪污' refers to filth or waste, and '象' means 'symbol.' This phrase suggests that throwing filth symbolizes money.
    • 速迁之征也 - '速迁' means 'quickly moving' or 'rapidly changing,' and '征' means 'sign' or 'omen.' This phrase implies that throwing filth is a sign of rapid change.
    • 清果为扬武将军、北鲁郡太守 - '清' refers to a person named Qing, who becomes a military general and a governor.
    • 清厌毒既久 - '厌毒' means 'disgusted with poison' or 'repulsed by evil,' and '既久' means 'for a long time.' This phrase indicates that Qing has been repulsed by evil for a long time.
    • 呼外国道人波羅ふ诵咒文 - '呼' means 'call' or 'summon,' '外国道人' means 'foreign monk,' and '诵咒文' means 'recite incantations.' This phrase describes Qing summoning a foreign monk to recite incantations.
    • 见诸鬼怖惧，逾垣穴壁而走 - '见诸鬼怖惧' means 'seeing the ghosts frightened,' and '逾垣穴壁而走' means 'jumping over walls and through holes, running away.' This phrase describes the ghosts fleeing in fear.
    • 皆作鸟声 - '皆' means 'all,' and '作鸟声' means 'making bird sounds.' This phrase indicates that the ghosts are making bird-like noises.
    • 在郡少时 - '在郡' means 'in the prefecture,' and '少时' means 'for a short time.' This phrase indicates that Qing was in the prefecture for a short period.
    • 夜中松罗复见威仪器械人众十一人 - '夜中' means 'at night,' '松罗' is a place name, '复见' means 'again saw,' '威仪' means 'impressive appearance,' '器械' means 'equipment,' and '人众' means 'people.' This phrase describes seeing a group of people with impressive appearances and equipment at night in a place called Song Luo.
    • 戴帻送书 - '戴帻' means 'wearing a headband,' and '送书' means 'delivering a letter.' This phrase describes the people wearing headbands and delivering a letter.
    • 粗纸有七十许字 - '粗纸' means 'rough paper,' and '七十许字' means 'about seventy characters.' This phrase indicates that the letter was written on rough paper with about seventy characters.
    • 笔迹婉媚，远拟羲、献 - '笔迹' means 'handwriting,' '婉媚' means 'graceful and charming,' and '远拟羲、献' means 'resembling the style of Wang Xizhi and Wang Xianzhi.' This phrase describes the handwriting as graceful and resembling the styles of the famous calligraphers Wang Xizhi and Wang Xianzhi.
    • 又歌云 - '又歌云' means 'and sang, saying.' This phrase introduces a song.
    • 坐侬孔雀楼，遥闻凤凰鼓 - '坐侬' means 'sitting in,' '孔雀楼' means 'Peacock Tower,' '遥闻' means 'heard from afar,' and '凤凰鼓' means 'Phoenix drum.' This phrase describes sitting in Peacock Tower and hearing the sound of the Phoenix drum from afar.
    • 下我邹山头，仿佛见梁鲁 - '下我' means 'take me down,' '邹山头' means 'the top of Mount Zou,' and '仿佛见梁鲁' means 'vaguely see Liang and Lu.' This phrase describes being taken down to the top of Mount Zou and vaguely seeing the regions of Liang and Lu.
    • 鬼有叔操丧，哭泣答吊 - '鬼有叔操丧' means 'the ghost of Uncle Cao's funeral,' and '哭泣答吊' means 'crying and responding to condolences.' This phrase describes the ghost of Uncle Cao's funeral crying and responding to condolences.
    • 不异世人 - '不异' means 'no different from,' and '世人' means 'ordinary people.' This phrase indicates that the ghost's behavior is no different from that of ordinary people.
    • 鬼传教 - '鬼传教' means 'the ghost teaching.' This phrase describes the ghost teaching.
    • 曾乞松罗一函书 - '曾' means 'once,' '乞' means 'asked for,' '松罗' is a place name, and '一函书' means 'a letter.' This phrase indicates that the ghost once asked for a letter from Song Luo.
    • 题云：故孔修之死罪白笺 - '题云' means 'titled as,' '故孔修之死罪' means 'the death penalty of the late Kong Xiu,' and '白笺' means 'a white note.' This phrase describes the letter being titled as 'a white note for the death penalty of the late Kong Xiu.'
    • 以吊其叔丧 - '以' means 'to,' '吊' means 'condole,' and '其叔丧' means 'his uncle's funeral.' This phrase indicates that the letter is to condole with his uncle's funeral.
    • 叙致哀情，甚有铨次 - '叙致' means 'expressing,' '哀情' means 'grief,' and '甚有铨次' means 'very well organized.' This phrase describes the letter expressing grief very well organized.
    • 复云近往西方见一沙门 - '复云' means 'and said,' '近往' means 'recently went,' '西方' means 'the West,' and '见一沙门' means 'saw a monk.' This phrase describes the ghost saying that he recently went to the West and saw a monk.
    • 自名大摩刹 - '自名' means 'called himself,' and '大摩刹' means 'Great Moshaka.' This phrase indicates that the monk called himself Great Moshaka.
    • 问君消息，寄五丸香以相与之 - '问君消息' means 'asking about your news,' '寄' means 'sent,' '五丸香' means 'five incense balls,' and '以相与之' means 'to give to you.' This phrase describes the monk sending five incense balls to give to Qing.
    • 清先奉使敦煌 - '清' refers to Qing, '先' means 'previously,' '奉使' means 'sent as an envoy,' and '敦煌' means 'Dunhuang.' This phrase indicates that Qing was previously sent as an envoy to Dunhuang.
    • 忆见此僧 - '忆见' means 'remember seeing,' and '此僧' means 'this monk.' This phrase indicates that Qing remembers seeing this monk.
    • 清有婢产 - '清' refers to Qing, '有' means 'had,' '婢' means 'maid,' and '产' means 'gave birth.' This phrase indicates that Qing's maid gave birth.
    • 于此遂绝 - '于此' means 'here,' and '遂绝' means 'ended.' This phrase indicates that the events ended here.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 扬武将军、北鲁郡太守 - These are titles of military and administrative positions in ancient China.
    • 波羅ふ - This is likely a transliteration of a foreign name, possibly referring to a monk or sage from a foreign land.
    • 王羲之、王献之 - These are famous calligraphers from the Eastern Jin Dynasty, known for their elegant and influential styles.
    • 敦煌 - A city in Gansu Province, historically an important center of trade and culture along the Silk Road.
    • 孔雀楼、凤凰鼓、邹山、梁鲁 - These are place names or symbolic locations, possibly referring to specific sites or regions in ancient China.
    • 大摩刹 - This is likely a transliteration of a foreign name, possibly referring to a monk or sage from a foreign land.
    • 五丸香 - This could refer to incense balls, which were used in religious or ceremonial contexts.

    A Questioner asked, "Why do you constantly throw filth?" The respondent replied, "Throwing filth is a symbol of wealth. The act of throwing it is a sign of rapid change." Soon after, Qing indeed became a military general and the governor of Beilu Prefecture. Having been repulsed by evil for a long time, Qing summoned a foreign monk named Bolo Fu to recite incantations. The ghosts, seeing this, were filled with fear, jumping over walls and through holes, running away, and all making bird-like noises, thus ending their presence in the area. While serving as governor for a short time, one night in Song Luo, Qing again saw a group of eleven people with impressive appearances and equipment. They wore headbands and delivered a letter, written on rough paper with about seventy characters, whose handwriting was graceful and resembled the styles of Wang Xizhi and Wang Xianzhi. They also sang, saying: "Sitting in the Peacock Tower, I heard the sound of the Phoenix drum from afar. Take me down to the top of Mount Zou, and I vaguely see Liang and Lu." The ghost of Uncle Cao's funeral was there, crying and responding to condolences, no different from ordinary people. The ghost taught, once asking for a letter from Song Luo, titled as "A white note for the death penalty of the late Kong Xiu," to condole with his uncle's funeral, expressing grief very well organized. The ghost also said that he recently went to the West and saw a monk who called himself Great Moshaka, asking about Qing's news and sending five incense balls to give to him. Qing, who was previously sent as an envoy to Dunhuang, remembered seeing this monk. Qing's maid gave birth, and the events ended here. [68]

    句章人吴平，州门前忽生一株青桐树，上有谣歌之声。平恶而斫杀。平随军北征，首尾三载，死桐自还立于故根之上。又闻树巅空中歌曰：死桐今更青，吴平寻当归。适闻杀此树已复有光辉，平寻复归如见。

    　　●卷七
    　　汉武帝冢里先有玉箱、瑶杖各一，是西胡康渠王所献，帝平素常玩之，故入梓宫中。其后四年，有人于扶风市买得此二物，帝左右识而认之，说卖者形状，乃帝也。 [69]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [69]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有謠歌之聲 - The tree emits a sound like a song or rhyme.
    • 死桐今更青，吳平尋當歸 - The song predicts that the dead tree will turn green again and Wu Ping will return.
    • 適聞殺此樹已復有輝光 - The song mentions that after Wu Ping killed the tree, it regained its luster.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 句章 - A place name, likely referring to a town or region in ancient China.
    • 吳平 - A personal name, possibly a commoner or a soldier.
    • 漢武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han, a prominent ruler of the Han Dynasty.
    • 玉箱、瑶杖 - Precious items, likely symbolic of high status or power.
    • 西胡康渠王 - King Kangqu of the Western Barbarians, a foreign ruler.
    • 扶风市 - A market in Fufeng, a place name.
    • 梓宮 - A coffin made of cypress wood, often used for emperors.

    Proper Nouns

    • 句章 - A place name.
    • 吳平 - A personal name.
    • 漢武帝 - Emperor Wu of Han.
    • 玉箱、瑶杖 - Precious items.
    • 西胡康渠王 - King Kangqu of the Western Barbarians.
    • 扶风市 - A market in Fufeng.
    • 梓宮 - A coffin made of cypress wood.

    A man from Juzhang named Wu Ping was walking in front of the city gate when a green Chinese parasol tree suddenly sprouted, emitting a sound like a song. Fearing the sound, Wu Ping cut down the tree. He then joined the army and went on a northern campaign that lasted three years. After his death, the tree mysteriously reappeared on its old roots. The tree sang a song from its top, as if from the air: "The dead tree is now greener, Wu Ping will soon return." The song mentioned that after Wu Ping killed the tree, it regained its luster, and indeed, Wu Ping returned as predicted.

    In the tomb of Emperor Wu of Han, there were already a jade box and a jade staff, gifts from King Kangqu of the Western Barbarians. Emperor Wu often played with these items, so they were placed in his coffin. Four years later, someone bought these two items in the market of Fufeng. The emperor's attendants recognized them and described the seller, who turned out to be the emperor. [69]

    魏武北征蹋顷，升岭眺，瞩见一山冈，不生草木，王粲曰：“必是古冢。此人在世服生矾石，死而石气蒸出外，故卉木焦灭。”即令凿看，果得大墓，有矾石满茔。仲宣博识强记，皆此类也。一说粲在荆州，从刘表登障山而见此异。魏武之平乌桓，粲犹在江南，此言为谲。
    　　苍梧王士燮，汉末死于交趾，遂葬南境，而墓常蒙雾，灵异不恒。屡经离乱，不复发掘。晋兴宁中，太原温放之为刺史，躬乘骑往开之，还即坠马而卒。
    　　剡县陈务妻少与二子寡居，好饮茶茗。宅中先有古冢，每日作茗，饮先辄祀之，二子患之，曰：“古冢何知，徒以劳祀！”欲掘去之。母苦禁而止。及夜，母梦一人曰：“吾止此冢二百余年，谬蒙惠泽。卿二子恒欲见毁，赖相保护。人飨吾佳茗，虽泉坏朽骨，岂忘翳桑之报。”遂觉。明日晨兴，乃于庭内获钱十万，似久埋者，而贯皆新提。还告其儿，儿并有惭色，从是祷酹愈至。 [70]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [70]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 魏武北征蹋頡 - Refers to Cao Cao's northern campaign against the Ta Xie tribe.
    • 升岭眺 - Climbing the hill to look out, describing Cao Cao's action.
    • 必是古冢 - It must be an ancient tomb, a logical deduction.
    • 服生矾石 - Wearing live alum stone, a practice involving minerals.
    • 死而石气蒸出外 - After death, the stone vapor rises to the outside, a superstitious belief.
    • 卉木焦灭 - Plants and trees wither and die, describing the effect of the stone vapor.
    • 即令凿看 - Immediately ordered to dig and inspect, indicating a command to investigate.
    • 果得大墓 - Indeed found a large tomb, confirming the discovery.
    • 有矾石满茔 - Filled with alum stone, describing the contents of the tomb.
    • 仲宣博识强记 - Zhong Xuan has extensive knowledge and strong memory, referring to Wang Can.
    • 皆此类也 - All such cases, indicating similar occurrences.
    • 一说粲在荆州 - Another says that Cao Cao was in Jingzhou, providing an alternative account.
    • 苍梧王士燮 - King Shi Xie of Cangwu, a prominent figure in the Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 遂葬南境 - Finally buried in the southern border, indicating the location of the burial.
    • 墓常蒙雾 - The tomb is often covered in mist, describing a recurring phenomenon.
    • 灵异不恒 - Supernatural occurrences are not constant, suggesting intermittent phenomena.
    • 屡经离乱 - Many times during turmoil, indicating significant unrest.
    • 不复发掘 - No longer excavated, suggesting the tomb has not been disturbed.
    • 晋兴宁中 - During the Xingning period of the Jin dynasty, specifying the time frame.
    • 太原温放之为刺史 - Taiyuan's Wen Fangzhi became the governor, introducing a historical figure.
    • 躬乘骑往开之 - He personally rode to open it, describing Wen Fangzhi's actions.
    • 还即坠马而卒 - Upon returning, he fell off his horse and died, describing the tragic outcome.
    • 剡县陈务妻少与二子寡居 - The wife of Chen Wu from Yan County, who was young and widowed with two sons, introducing the main characters.
    • 好饮茶茗 - Likes to drink tea, describing the wife's habit.
    • 宅中先有古冢 - There was an ancient tomb in the house, indicating the presence of a tomb on the property.
    • 每日作茗，饮先辄祀之 - Every day when making tea, she would first offer it to the tomb, describing a ritual practice.
    • 二子患之 - Her two sons were troubled by this, indicating their dissatisfaction.
    • 母苦禁而止 - Her mother strongly forbade and stopped them, describing the mother's intervention.
    • 及夜，母梦一人 - At night, her mother dreamed of a person, introducing a supernatural element.
    • 吾止此冢二百余年 - I have been in this tomb for over two hundred years, the dreamer's statement.
    • 谬蒙惠泽 - Graciously received your kindness, expressing gratitude.
    • 卿二子恒欲见毁 - Your two sons always wanted to destroy it, indicating the sons' intentions.
    • 赖相保护 - Thanks to your protection, expressing gratitude for the mother's actions.
    • 人飨吾佳茗 - People offered me good tea, describing the offerings.
    • 虽泉坏朽骨 - Even though the water damaged the bones, indicating the negative effects of the offerings.
    • 岂忘翳桑之报 - How could I forget the shade of the mulberry tree, a metaphorical expression of gratitude.
    • 遂觉 - Then woke up, describing the end of the dream.
    • 明日晨兴 - The next morning, she woke up, indicating the continuation of the story.
    • 乃于庭内获钱十万 - She found ten thousand coins in the courtyard, describing the unexpected discovery.
    • 似久埋者 - Seemed to have been buried for a long time, indicating the age of the coins.
    • 而贯皆新提 - But all the strings were new, suggesting that the coins were recently gathered.
    • 还告其儿 - She told her sons, describing her actions.
    • 儿并有惭色 - Her sons all had a look of shame, indicating their reaction.
    • 从是祷酹愈至 - From then on, the offerings became more frequent, describing the change in behavior.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 魏武 - Refers to Cao Cao (曹操), a prominent warlord and statesman during the late Eastern Han dynasty.
    • 蹋頡 - A nomadic tribe that Cao Cao fought against during his northern campaigns.
    • 仲宣 - Refers to Wang Can (王粲), a scholar and poet of the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 苍梧王士燮 - Shi Xie (士燮), a prominent figure in the Eastern Han dynasty, known for his rule over Cangwu (modern-day Guangxi).
    • 晋兴宁中 - The Xingning period of the Jin dynasty, a specific time frame in Chinese history.
    • 太原温放之 - Wen Fangzhi (温放之), a person from Taiyuan who held the position of governor.
    • 剡县 - Yan County, a place in Zhejiang province.
    • 陈务妻 - The wife of Chen Wu, a character in the story.
    • 古冢 - Ancient tomb, often associated with supernatural phenomena in Chinese folklore.
    • 茶茗 - Tea, a significant part of Chinese culture and daily life.
    • 梦 - Dream, often used in Chinese literature to convey supernatural or divine messages.

    Cao Cao, during his northern campaign against Ta Xie, climbed a hill to look out and saw a mountain ridge where no grass or trees grew. Wang Can remarked, "It must be an ancient tomb. This person wore live alum stone while alive, and after death, the stone vapor rose to the outside, causing the plants and trees to wither and die." Cao Cao immediately ordered the tomb to be dug up, and indeed found a large tomb filled with alum stone. Wang Can, known for his extensive knowledge and strong memory, made such deductions. Another account says that Wang Can was in Jingzhou at the time, accompanying Liu Biao up a mountain and witnessing this strange phenomenon. By the time Cao Cao had pacified the Wuhuan, Wang Can was still in the south, so this statement is considered a fabrication.

    King Shi Xie of Cangwu died in Jiaozhi during the late Eastern Han period and was finally buried in the southern border. His tomb was often covered in mist, and supernatural occurrences were not constant. Despite many times of turmoil, the tomb was no longer excavated. During the Xingning period of the Jin dynasty, Wen Fangzhi of Taiyuan became the governor and personally rode to open the tomb, but upon returning, he fell off his horse and died.

    The wife of Chen Wu from Yan County, who was young and widowed with two sons, liked to drink tea. There was an ancient tomb in her house, and every day when she made tea, she would first offer it to the tomb. Her two sons were troubled by this and said, "What does the ancient tomb know? It's just a waste of offerings!" They wanted to dig it up and destroy it. The mother strongly forbade them and stopped them. At night, the mother dreamed of a person who said, "I have been in this tomb for over two hundred years and graciously received your kindness. Your two sons always wanted to destroy it, thanks to your protection. People offered me good tea, even though the water damaged the bones. How could I forget the shade of the mulberry tree?" She then woke up. The next morning, she woke up and found ten thousand coins in the courtyard, which seemed to have been buried for a long time, but all the strings were new. She told her sons, and they all had a look of shame. From then on, the offerings became more frequent. [70]

    晋隆安中，颜从尝起新屋，夜梦人语云：“君何坏吾冢？”明日床前亟掘之，遂见一棺，从便为设祭，云：“今当移好处，别作小冢。”明朝一人诣门求通，姓朱名护，列坐乃言：“我居四十年，昨蒙厚贶，相感何如！今是吉日，便可出棺矣。”仆巾箱中有金镜，愿以相助。”遂于棺头巾箱中取金镜三枚赠从，忽然不见。
    　　晋司空郄方回葬妇于骊山，使会稽郡吏史泽治墓，多平夷古坟。后坏一冢，构制甚伟，器物殊盛。冢发，闻鼓角声。
    　　颍川诸葛闾字道明，墓在扬州庄蒋山之西。每至阴雨，冢中辄有弦歌之声。
    　　朱文绣与罗子锺为友，俱仕于梁。绣既死，子锺哭之，其夜亦亡。梁南七里有鸡山，绣葬于其中。北九里有雉涧，埋锺于其内。绣神灵变为鸡，锺魂魄化为雉，清鸣哀响，往来不绝，故诗曰：鸡山别飞响，雉涧和清音。 [71]

    During the Long'an period of the Eastern Jin dynasty, Yan Cong once built a new house. One night, he dreamed of someone speaking to him: "Why did you destroy my tomb?" The next morning, Yan Cong dug up the spot where he had seen the dream and found a coffin. He set up an offering and said, "Now I will move you to a better place, and build a smaller tomb." The next morning, a man came to the door to seek an audience. His name was Zhu Hu. After sitting down, he said, "I lived here for forty years. Yesterday, I received your generous gift. How grateful I am to you! Today is a good day, so you can take the coffin out now." He had gold mirrors in his box and wished to give them to Yan Cong as a gift. Yan Cong then took three gold mirrors from the box at the head of the coffin and gave them to him. He suddenly disappeared.

    During the Jin dynasty, the Grand Tutor Xie Fanghui buried his wife on Li Mountain. He sent the county official Shi Ze to manage the tomb and leveled many ancient tombs. When one tomb was opened, the sound of drums and horns was heard.

    Zhang Zhi from Yingchuan, whose courtesy name was Daoming, had his tomb on the west side of Jiangshan in Yangzhou. Every time it rained, there were sounds of stringed instruments and singing in the tomb.

    Zhu Wenxiu and Luo Zizhong were friends and both served in the state of Liang. After Zhu Wenxiu died, Luo Zizhong mourned him. That night, Luo Zizhong also died. Seven miles south of Liang, there is a hill called Jishan. Zhu Wenxiu was buried there. Nine miles north, there is a ravine called Zijian. Luo Zizhong was buried there. The spirit of Zhu Wenxiu transformed into a chicken, and the spirit of Luo Zizhong transformed into a pheasant. They cried out clearly and mournfully, never ceasing. Therefore, the poem says: "The chicken hill emits a separate sound, the pheasant ravine harmonizes with clear tones." [71]

    武昌戴熙家道贫陋，墓在樊山间。占者云有王气。宣武仗钺西下，停武昌，令凿之，得一物，大如水牛，青色无头脚，时亦动摇，斫刺不陷，乃纵著江中，得水便有声，如雷向发长川。熙后嗣沦胥殆尽。
    　　元嘉中豫章胡家奴开昌邑王冢，青州人开齐襄公冢，并得金钩，而尸骸露在岩中俨然。兹亦未必有凭而然也。京房尸至义熙中犹完具，僵尸人肉，堪为药军士分割之。
    　　海陵如皋县东城村边海岸崩坏，见一古墓，有方头漆棺，以朱题上云：“七百年堕水，元嘉二十载三月坠于悬。”和盖从潮漂沈，辄溯流还依本处。村人朱护等异而启之，见一老姥，年可七十许，皤头著桂，鬓皓白，不如生人，钗髻衣服，粲然若新，送葬器物，枕履悉存。护乃赍酒脯施于柩侧。尔夜护归梦见姥云：“向获名贶感至无已。但我墙屋毁发，形骸飘露。今以值一千乞为治护也。”置钱便去。明觉，果得，即用改殓移于高阜。 [72]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [72]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 仗钺西下 - Military idiom meaning 'to march westward with the axe (symbol of authority), indicating a military campaign.
    • 得一物，大如水牛，青色无头脚 - Unusual descriptive phrase, may require explanation or a note on its rarity or strangeness.
    • 时亦动摇，斫刺不陷 - Describes the object's movement and resistance to cutting or piercing, indicating its unusual nature.
    • 乃纵著江中，得水便有声，如雷向发长川 - Vivid description of the object's reaction when placed in water, likening its sound to thunder.
    • 熙后嗣沦胥殆尽 - Phrase suggesting the decline of the family lineage, with '沦胥殆尽' meaning 'almost entirely perished.'
    • 青州人开齐襄公冢 - Refers to the opening of the tomb of Duke Xiang of Qi by a person from Qingzhou, a historical reference.
    • 金钩 - Term for a golden hook, which may have symbolic or practical significance.
    • 尸骸露在岩中俨然 - Describes the remains as being exposed in the rock but still appearing dignified or intact.
    • 京房尸至义熙中犹完具 - Refers to the body of Jing Fang remaining intact during the Yixi period, a historical reference.
    • 僵尸人肉，堪为药军士分割之 - Describes the use of the body parts as medicinal substances, indicating a historical or cultural practice.
    • 海陵如皋县东城村边海岸崩坏 - Describes a natural event leading to the discovery of a tomb, with '崩坏' meaning 'collapse' or 'collapse and destruction.'
    • 方头漆棺，以朱题上云 - Describes the appearance of the coffin, with '方头漆棺' meaning 'square-headed lacquered coffin' and '以朱题上云' meaning 'with red inscriptions on top saying.'
    • 七百年堕水，元嘉二十载三月坠于悬 - Specific inscription on the coffin indicating its age and the date of its fall, with '元嘉二十载三月' meaning 'March of the 20th year of the Yuanjia era.'
    • 和盖从潮漂沈，辄溯流还依本处 - Describes the coffin's movement with the tide, returning to its original location.
    • 村人朱护等异而启之 - Describes the villagers' reaction and their opening of the coffin, with '异而启之' meaning 'finding it strange and opening it.'
    • 皤头著桂，鬓皓白 - Describes the appearance of the old woman, with '皤头著桂' meaning 'white-haired and wearing a wreath of osmanthus' and '鬓皓白' meaning 'white hair and beard.'
    • 不如生人，钗髻衣服，粲然若新 - Describes the old woman's appearance as lifeless but well-preserved, with '不如生人' meaning 'not like a living person' and '粲然若新' meaning 'brightly shining as if new.'
    • 送葬器物，枕履悉存 - Describes the presence of burial goods, pillows, and shoes, indicating a well-preserved tomb.
    • 护乃赍酒脯施于柩侧 - Describes the villagers offering wine and meat to the coffin, a ritual act.
    • 明觉，果得，即用改殓移于高阜 - Describes the outcome of the ritual, with '明觉，果得' meaning 'upon waking, indeed they found' and '即用改殓移于高阜' meaning 'and immediately used the money to re-inter the body on a high ground.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 武昌 - Refers to Wuchang, an ancient city in Hubei Province.
    • 樊山 - Refers to Fan Mountain, a mountain in Wuchang.
    • 宣武 - Refers to Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song, a ruler of the Liu Song dynasty.
    • 戴熙 - A historical figure whose family story is being recounted.
    • 豫章 - Refers to Yuzhang, an ancient name for Nanchang, the capital of Jiangxi Province.
    • 昌邑王 - Refers to the King of Changyi, a historical figure.
    • 青州 - Refers to Qingzhou, an ancient administrative region in Shandong Province.
    • 齐襄公 - Refers to Duke Xiang of Qi, a historical ruler of the State of Qi.
    • 元嘉 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor Wen of Liu Song.
    • 义熙 - Refers to the reign period of Emperor An of Liu Song.
    • 海陵 - Refers to Haiting, an ancient name for Yangzhou, Jiangsu Province.
    • 如皋县 - Refers to Rugao County, a county in Jiangsu Province.
    • 东城村 - Refers to Dongcheng Village, a village in Rugao County.

    Proper Nouns

    • 武昌 - Wuchang, an ancient city in Hubei Province.
    • 樊山 - Fan Mountain, a mountain in Wuchang.
    • 宣武 - Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song.
    • 戴熙 - A historical figure.
    • 豫章 - Yuzhang, an ancient name for Nanchang, the capital of Jiangxi Province.
    • 昌邑王 - The King of Changyi.
    • 青州 - Qingzhou, an ancient administrative region in Shandong Province.
    • 齐襄公 - Duke Xiang of Qi.
    • 元嘉 - The reign period of Emperor Wen of Liu Song.
    • 义熙 - The reign period of Emperor An of Liu Song.
    • 海陵 - Haiting, an ancient name for Yangzhou, Jiangsu Province.
    • 如皋县 - Rugao County, a county in Jiangsu Province.
    • 东城村 - Dongcheng Village, a village in Rugao County.

    The Poor Family of Dai Xi in Wuchang

    In Wuchang, the family of Dai Xi lived in poverty, and their tomb was located between Fan Mountain. It was said that there was a "kingly aura" emanating from the area. Emperor Xiaowu of Liu Song, wielding the axe of authority, marched westward and halted in Wuchang, ordering the tomb to be excavated. Inside, they found an object as large as a water buffalo, blue in color with no head or feet. It occasionally moved, and when struck with a knife or spear, it would not be pierced. They then released it into the river, where it made a loud sound upon contact with the water, like thunder echoing across the river. After this event, the descendants of Dai Xi almost entirely perished.

    During the Yuanjia period, a slave from Yuzhang opened the tomb of the King of Changyi, and a person from Qingzhou opened the tomb of Duke Xiang of Qi. Both found golden hooks, but the remains were exposed in the rock, still appearing dignified. This may not be entirely credible, but it is recorded nonetheless. The body of Jing Fang remained intact during the Yixi period, and the flesh was used as medicine, with soldiers dividing it up.

    In the vicinity of Dongcheng Village in Rugao County, near the coast of Haiting, a natural collapse revealed an ancient tomb. Inside was a square-headed lacquered coffin, with red inscriptions on top that read: "Seven hundred years of falling into the water, in March of the 20th year of the Yuanjia era, it fell into the suspended stream." The coffin floated with the tide and returned to its original location. The villagers, including Zhu Hu, found it strange and opened the coffin. Inside was an elderly woman, around seventy years old, with white hair and a wreath of osmanthus, and white hair and beard. She did not look like a living person, but her hair, headdress, and clothes were bright and new. The burial goods, pillows, and shoes were all intact. Zhu Hu offered wine and meat to the coffin. That night, he dreamed that the woman said, "I was deeply grateful for your offering. But my walls and house have been destroyed, and my body is exposed. I now ask for one thousand coins to be used to properly re-inter me." After placing the money, she left. Upon waking, Zhu Hu indeed found the money, and they immediately used it to re-inter the body on a high ground. [72]

    广陵郡东界有黄公冢高坟二所，前有一井，面广数尺，每旱不竭。有人于其中得铜釜及罐各一。又云江都郡东界有黄公坟三所，阴天恒闻有な角之声。
    　　即墨有古冢，或发之，有金牛塞埏门，不可移动，犯之则大祸。
    　　嵇康字叔夜，谯国人也。少尝昼寝，梦人身长丈余，自称黄帝，伶人骸骨在公舍东三里林中，为人发露，乞为葬埋，当厚相报。康至其处，果有白骨，胫长三尺，遂收葬之。其夜复梦长人来，授以广陵散曲。及觉，抚琴而作，其声正妙，都不遗忘。高贵乡公时康为中散大夫，后为锺会所谗，司马文王诛之。 [73]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [73]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 廣陵郡 - Administrative region in ancient China.
    • 江都郡 - Administrative region in ancient China.
    • 黄公冢 - Tomb of a legendary figure named Huang Gong, associated with alchemy and immortality.
    • な角之声 - Sound of an ancient Chinese musical instrument, possibly a 'na' horn.
    • 即墨 - Ancient city in Shandong Province.
    • 古冢 - Ancient tomb.
    • 金牛 - Golden ox, associated with wealth and good fortune in Chinese mythology.
    • 埏门 - Entrance to a tomb.
    • 嵇康 - Famous scholar, musician, and philosopher of the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 叔夜 - Courtesy name of Ji Kang.
    • 谯国人 - Someone from Qiao, an ancient city in Henan Province.
    • 昼寝 - Sleeping during the day.
    • 黄帝 - Legendary Yellow Emperor, a central figure in Chinese mythology and history.
    • 伶人 - Musician or performer, often associated with court entertainers.
    • 骸骨 - Bones or remains.
    • 公舍 - Public building or office.
