    • 广陵散曲 - Famous piece of music called 'Guangling San,' a traditional Chinese zither composition.
    • 抚琴而作 - Playing the qin (a traditional Chinese zither) and composing music.
    • 高贵乡公 - Second emperor of the Wei dynasty during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 中散大夫 - High-ranking official position in the Wei dynasty.
    • 锺会 - Prominent general and statesman of the Wei dynasty.
    • 司马文王 - Regent and de facto ruler of the Wei dynasty, who later founded the Jin dynasty.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 黄公冢 - Tomb of Huang Gong, a legendary figure associated with alchemy and immortality.
    • な角之声 - Sound of a 'na' horn, a type of ancient Chinese musical instrument associated with spiritual or supernatural occurrences.
    • 即墨 - Important city in ancient China, known for its strategic location and historical significance.
    • 嵇康 - Famous scholar, musician, and philosopher of the Three Kingdoms period, known for his opposition to the ruling elite and tragic death by execution.
    • 广陵散曲 - Famous piece of music that is said to have been lost after Ji Kang's death, symbolizing his artistic legacy.
    • 高贵乡公 - Second emperor of the Wei dynasty, known for his brief and tumultuous reign.
    • 中散大夫 - High-ranking official position in the Wei dynasty, often held by scholars and intellectuals.
    • 锺会 - Prominent general and statesman of the Wei dynasty, known for his political intrigues and eventual downfall.
    • 司马文王 - Regent and de facto ruler of the Wei dynasty, who later founded the Jin dynasty, known for his military prowess and political acumen.

    Proper Nouns

    • 廣陵郡 - Guangling Commandery.
    • 江都郡 - Jiangdu Commandery.
    • 黄公冢 - Tomb of Huang Gong.
    • な角之声 - Sound of a 'na' horn.
    • 即墨 - Jimo.
    • 古冢 - Ancient tomb.
    • 金牛 - Golden ox.
    • 埏门 - Entrance to a tomb.
    • 嵇康 - Ji Kang.
    • 叔夜 - Courtesy name of Ji Kang.
    • 谯国人 - From Qiao.
    • 昼寝 - Daytime sleep.
    • 黄帝 - Yellow Emperor.
    • 伶人 - Musician or performer.
    • 骸骨 - Bones or remains.
    • 公舍 - Public building or office.
    • 广陵散曲 - 'Guangling San' composition.
    • 抚琴而作 - Playing the qin and composing music.
    • 高贵乡公 - Cao Fang.
    • 中散大夫 - High-ranking official position.
    • 锺会 - Zhong Hui.
    • 司马文王 - Sima Yi.

    In the eastern boundary of Guangling Commandery, there are two high mounds known as Huang Gong's tombs. In front of these tombs is a well, several feet wide, which never runs dry even during droughts. Someone found a copper pot and a jar inside the well. It is also said that in the eastern boundary of Jiangdu Commandery, there are three tombs of Huang Gong, and on overcast days, the sound of a "na" horn is always heard.

    In Jimo, there is an ancient tomb. If it is disturbed, a golden ox blocks the entrance, which cannot be moved, and disturbing it brings great misfortune.

    Ji Kang, courtesy name Shuyue, was from Qiao. As a young man, he once slept during the day and dreamed of a man over ten feet tall who called himself the Yellow Emperor. The man said that the bones of a musician were exposed in a forest three li east of the public building, and he begged Ji Kang to bury them, promising a great reward. Ji Kang went to the place and indeed found white bones with legs three feet long. He buried them. That night, he dreamed again of the tall man, who gave him the "Guangling San" composition. When he woke up, he played the qin and composed the music, which was perfectly marvelous, and he never forgot it. During the reign of Cao Fang, Ji Kang was a Zhong San Daifu (Middle Scattered Doctor). Later, he was slandered by Zhong Hui and executed by Sima Yi. [73]

    晋会稽张茂字伟康，尝梦得大象，以问万雅，雅曰：“君当为大郡守而不能善终。大象者，大兽也，取诸其音。兽者守也。故为大郡。然象以齿焚其身后，必为人所杀。”茂永昌中为吴兴太守，值王敦问鼎，执正不移，敦遣沈充杀之而取其郡。
    　　邓艾庙在京口新城，有一草屋，毁已久。晋安北将军司马恬于病中梦见一老翁曰：“我邓公也，屋舍倾坏，君为治之。”后访之，乃知邓庙，为立瓦屋。
    　　晋明帝时，献马者梦河神请之。及至，与帝梦同，遂投河以奉神。始，太傅褚褒亦好此马，帝云已与河神。及褚公卒，军人见公乘此马矣。
    　　陶侃梦生八翼，飞翔冲天，见天门九重，已入其入，惟一门不得进，以翼搏天。阍者以杖击之，因堕地，折其左翼。惊悟，左腋犹痛。其后都督入州，威果震主，潜有窥拟之志，每忆折翼之祥，抑心而止。 [74]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [74]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 尝梦得大象 - Literal description of a dream involving a large elephant, carrying symbolic meaning.
    • 以问万雅 - Means 'to ask Wan Ya,' where Wan Ya is likely a scholar or interpreter of dreams.
    • 君当为大郡守而不能善终 - A prophetic statement predicting both success and a tragic end.
    • 大象者，大兽也，取诸其音 - An explanation of the dream symbol, noting that '大象' (great elephant) is interpreted based on its sound.
    • 兽者守也 - A pun on '兽' (beast) and '守' (to guard), linking the elephant to the role of a governor.
    • 象以齿焚其身后，必为人所杀 - A proverbial saying about the elephant's tusks leading to its downfall, symbolizing the danger of power.
    • 执正不移 - Means 'to hold firm to righteousness without wavering.'
    • 敦遣沈充杀之而取其郡 - Describes the act of Wang Dun sending Shen Chong to kill Zhang Mao and take his position.
    • 晋安北将军司马恬于病中梦见一老翁曰 - A narrative introduction to a dream experienced by Sima Tian, the Anbei General of Jin.
    • 我邓公也，屋舍倾坏，君为治之 - The dream message from Deng Ai, requesting repairs to the temple.
    • 后访之，乃知邓庙，为立瓦屋 - Describes the discovery and subsequent repair of the temple.
    • 晋明帝时，献马者梦河神请之 - Introduces a dream about a horse being requested by a river god during the reign of Emperor Ming of Jin.
    • 及至，与帝梦同，遂投河以奉神 - Describes the fulfillment of the dream and the act of offering the horse to the river god.
    • 始，太傅褚褒亦好此马，帝云已与河神 - Indicates that the horse was also desired by Cao Cao's son, who was told it had been given to the river god.
    • 及褚公卒，军人见公乘此马矣 - Describes the supernatural appearance of Cao Cao riding the horse after his death.
    • 陶侃梦生八翼，飞翔冲天 - A dream of Tao Kan having eight wings and flying to the heavens.
    • 见天门九重，已入其入，惟一门不得进 - Describes the dreamer's journey through nine gates of heaven, reaching the last gate.
    • 以翼搏天 - The act of trying to push open the final gate with wings.
    • 阍者以杖击之，因堕地，折其左翼 - The final gate's guardian strikes the dreamer, causing him to fall and break his left wing.
    • 惊悟，左腋犹痛 - The dreamer wakes up, still feeling pain in his left side.
    • 其后都督入州，威果震主，潜有窥拟之志 - Describes Tao Kan's later career as a military governor, where he becomes powerful and ambitious.
    • 每忆折翼之祥，抑心而止 - The dreamer recalls the broken wing as a warning, leading him to restrain his ambitions.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋会稽 - Refers to the state of Jin and its administrative division of Kuaiji (modern-day Shaoxing).
    • 永昌中 - Refers to the Yongchang era of the Western Jin dynasty (291-306 CE).
    • 吴兴 - Refers to the state of Wu and its administrative division of Xing (modern-day Huzhou).
    • 王敦问鼎 - A metaphor for a rebellion or usuration attempt by Wang Dun, a powerful general who challenged the imperial authority.
    • 京口新城 - Refers to Jingkou, a strategic location near modern-day Zhenjiang, known for its military significance.
    • 晋安北将军司马恬 - Refers to Sima Tian, a high-ranking military official of the Jin dynasty.
    • 邓艾庙 - A temple dedicated to Deng Ai, a famous general of the Wei dynasty known for his military conquests.
    • 晋明帝 - Refers to Emperor Ming of Jin (322-325 CE), the second emperor of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 河神 - Refers to a river deity, often associated with water and fertility in Chinese mythology.
    • 太傅褚褒 - Refers to Cao Cao's son, Cao Zhi, who was known for his literary talents and political ambitions.
    • 陶侃 - A prominent general and statesman of the Jin dynasty, known for his military prowess and administrative skills.
    • 天门九重 - Refers to the nine gates of heaven, a common motif in Chinese mythology representing the path to the celestial realm.

    Proper Nouns

    • 张茂 - Zhang Mao, a historical figure from the Jin dynasty.
    • 万雅 - Wan Ya, likely a scholar or interpreter of dreams.
    • 王敦 - Wang Dun, a powerful general who rebelled against the imperial authority.
    • 沈充 - Shen Chong, a subordinate of Wang Dun.
    • 司马恬 - Sima Tian, a high-ranking military official of the Jin dynasty.
    • 邓艾 - Deng Ai, a famous general of the Wei dynasty.
    • 晋明帝 - Emperor Ming of Jin, the second emperor of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 太傅褚褒 - Cao Cao's son, Cao Zhi.
    • 陶侃 - Tao Kan, a prominent general and statesman of the Jin dynasty.

    Zhang Mao of Kuaiji, Jìn, with the courtesy name Weikang, once dreamed of obtaining a great elephant and consulted Wan Ya about it. Wan Ya said, "You will become a governor of a large state but will not have a good end. The great elephant is a large beast, interpreted from its sound. A beast is a guardian, hence it is a large state. However, the elephant is burned by its tusks after death, and will surely be killed by others." During the Yongchang era, Zhang Mao became the governor of Wu's Xing. When Wang Dun attempted to usurp power, Zhang Mao held firmly to his principles and did not waver. Wang Dun sent Shen Chong to kill him and take his position.

    The temple of Deng Ai was located in Jingkou, a new city. There was a dilapidated thatched house at the temple, which had been destroyed for a long time. During his illness, the Anbei General Sima Tian of Jìn dreamed of an old man who said, "I am General Deng. My house is in ruins; you should repair it." After investigating, Sima Tian discovered the temple of Deng Ai and rebuilt it with tiles.

    During the reign of Emperor Ming of Jìn, a person who offered a horse dreamed that the river god requested it. When the horse was brought to the river, it matched the emperor's dream, and the horse was offered to the river god. Initially, Cao Cao's son, the Taifu Chu Biao, also desired this horse, but the emperor said it had already been given to the river god. After Chu Biao's death, soldiers saw him riding the horse.

    Tao Kan dreamed of being born with eight wings and flying to the heavens, seeing the nine gates of heaven. He entered all but the last gate, where he tried to push open the door with his wings. The gatekeeper struck him with a staff, causing him to fall and break his left wing. He woke up in shock, still feeling pain in his left side. Later, as a military governor, Tao Kan's authority was so great that it shook the emperor, and he secretly harbored ambitions. Each time he remembered the omen of the broken wing, he restrained his ambitions. [74]

    晋温峤至牛渚矶，闻水底有音乐之声，水深不可测，传言下多怪物，乃燃犀角而照之。须臾见水族覆火，奇形异状，或乘马车，著赤衣帻。其夜梦人，谓曰：“与君幽明道隔，何意相照耶？”峤甚恶之，未几卒。
    　　苻坚将欲南师也，梦葵生城内。明以问妇，妇曰：“若征军远行，难为将也。”坚又梦地东南倾，复以问，云：“江左不可平也，君无南行，必败之象也。”坚不从，卒以败。
    　　晋咸和初，徐精远行，梦与妻寝有身。明年归，妻果产，后如其言。
    　　晋武太元二年，沙门竺慧猷夜梦读诗五首，其一篇后曰：陌南酸枣，树名为六奇木。遣人以伐，取载还柱马屋。
    　　太元中，太原王戎为郁林太守，泊船新亭眠，梦有人以七枚椹子与之，著衣襟中。既觉，得之，占曰：“椹，桑子也。”自后男女大小凡七丧。 [75]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [75]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋温峤至牛渚矶 - Jìn Wēn Qiáo reached Niu Zhǔ Jī, where Niu Zhǔ Jī is a specific location, likely a rocky promontory or cliff.
    • 闻水底有音乐之声 - He heard the sound of music from the bottom of the water, which is a metaphorical or supernatural description.
    • 燃犀角而照之 - He lit a rhinoceros horn to illuminate it, a reference to an ancient belief that burning a rhinoceros horn could reveal hidden things.
    • 水族覆火 - The aquatic creatures covered the fire, suggesting a reaction of the underwater creatures to the light.
    • 奇形异状 - Strange shapes and forms, describing the unusual appearance of the creatures.
    • 或乘马车，著赤衣帻 - Some were riding in carriages, wearing red clothes and headbands, indicating the diverse and possibly human-like appearance of the creatures.
    • 夜梦人，谓曰 - That night, he dreamed of a person who said, introducing a supernatural element.
    • 与君幽明道隔，何意相照耶 - Between you and I, there is a separation between the visible and the invisible. Why did you choose to illuminate us? This is a metaphorical statement about the boundary between the human and supernatural worlds.
    • 甚恶之，未几卒 - He was greatly displeased and soon died, indicating a negative reaction and its consequences.
    • 苻坚将欲南师也 - Fu Jian was about to launch a southern campaign, referring to a military expedition.
    • 梦葵生城内 - He dreamed that the city was filled with sunflowers, a symbolic dream.
    • 征军远行，难为将也 - If you lead an army far away, it will be difficult to be a general, a warning about the challenges of leading a distant campaign.
    • 地东南倾 - The ground in the southeast was tilting, a metaphor for instability or disaster.
    • 江左不可平也，君无南行，必败之象也 - The eastern region cannot be pacified, you should not go south, it is a sign of defeat, a clear warning against the southern campaign.
    • 晋咸和初，徐精远行 - In the early years of the Xianhe era, Xu Jing went on a distant journey, setting the time and person.
    • 梦与妻寝有身 - He dreamed that he had intercourse with his wife and she became pregnant, a symbolic dream indicating future events.
    • 明年归，妻果产，后如其言 - The next year, when he returned, his wife indeed gave birth, as he had dreamed, confirming the accuracy of the dream.
    • 晋武太元二年，沙门竺慧猷夜梦读诗五首 - In the second year of Taiyuan during the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, the monk Zhi Huaiyou dreamed of reading five poems at night, introducing a religious and literary context.
    • 陌南酸枣，树名为六奇木 - In the south of the road, there was a sour jujube tree, named Six Wonders Tree, a specific and symbolic description.
    • 遣人以伐，取载还柱马屋 - He sent someone to cut it down, loaded it onto a carriage, and brought it back to the stable, describing the actions taken based on the dream.
    • 太元中，太原王戎为郁林太守，泊船新亭眠 - During the Taiyuan era, Wang Rong of Taiyuan served as the governor of Yulin and docked his boat at Xin Ting to sleep, setting the time, place, and person.
    • 梦有人以七枚椹子与之，著衣襟中 - He dreamed that someone gave him seven mulberry seeds, which he put in his clothes, a symbolic dream.
    • 既觉，得之，占曰 - Upon waking, he found them and interpreted them, indicating the realization and interpretation of the dream.
    • 椹，桑子也 - Mulberries are the fruit of the mulberry tree, providing a basic definition.
    • 自后男女大小凡七丧 - From then on, all seven of his family members died, both male and female, young and old, indicating a tragic sequence of events.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋温峤 - Refers to Wēn Qiáo, a prominent official and military leader of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 牛渚矶 - A specific location in the Yangtze River, known for its strategic and historical significance.
    • 燃犀角 - An ancient belief that burning a rhinoceros horn could reveal hidden things, often associated with supernatural or mystical practices.
    • 水族 - Refers to aquatic creatures, often used in Chinese literature to describe mythical or supernatural beings.
    • 苻坚 - Refers to Fu Jian, the ruler of the Former Qin dynasty, known for his ambitious military campaigns.
    • 江左 - Refers to the eastern region of China, particularly the area around the Yangtze River.
    • 晋咸和初 - Refers to the early years of the Xianhe era during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 徐精 - A historical figure whose name is not widely known, but he is mentioned in this context.
    • 晋武太元二年 - Refers to the second year of the Taiyuan era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin.
    • 沙门 - Refers to Buddhist monks or religious figures.
    • 竺慧猷 - A specific monk mentioned in the text.
    • 太原王戎 - Refers to Wang Rong, a high-ranking official and governor of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 郁林 - Refers to the region of Yulin, a place name in the context of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 新亭 - A specific location where the boat docked, often used in historical and literary contexts.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋温峤 - Wēn Qiáo, a prominent official and military leader of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 牛渚矶 - A specific location in the Yangtze River.
    • 苻坚 - Fu Jian, the ruler of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 江左 - The eastern region of China, particularly the area around the Yangtze River.
    • 晋咸和初 - The early years of the Xianhe era during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 徐精 - A historical figure.
    • 晋武太元二年 - The second year of the Taiyuan era during the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin.
    • 沙门 - Buddhist monks or religious figures.
    • 竺慧猷 - A specific monk mentioned in the text.
    • 太原王戎 - Wang Rong, a high-ranking official and governor of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 郁林 - The region of Yulin, a place name in the context of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 新亭 - A specific location where the boat docked, often used in historical and literary contexts.

    During the reign of Emperor Jin, Wēn Qiáo reached Niu Zhǔ Jī, where he heard the sound of music coming from the depths of the water. The water was so deep that its bottom could not be measured, and it was said that many strange creatures lived there. He lit a rhinoceros horn to illuminate the area. Soon, he saw the aquatic creatures covering the fire, each with strange shapes and forms, some riding in carriages and wearing red clothes and headbands. That night, he dreamed of a person who said to him, "Between you and I, there is a separation between the visible and the invisible. Why did you choose to illuminate us?" Wēn Qiáo was greatly displeased and soon died.

    Fu Jian was about to launch a southern campaign and dreamed that the city was filled with sunflowers. He asked his wife the next day, and she replied, "If you lead an army far away, it will be difficult to be a general." Fu Jian also dreamed that the ground in the southeast was tilting. He asked his wife again, and she said, "The eastern region cannot be pacified. You should not go south; it is a sign of defeat." Fu Jian did not heed her advice and ultimately suffered a defeat.

    In the early years of the Xianhe era, Xu Jing went on a distant journey and dreamed that he had intercourse with his wife, who became pregnant. The next year, when he returned, his wife indeed gave birth, as he had dreamed.

    In the second year of Taiyuan during the reign of Emperor Wu of Jin, the monk Zhi Huaiyou dreamed of reading five poems at night. One of the poems described a sour jujube tree in the south of the road, named the Six Wonders Tree. He sent someone to cut it down, loaded it onto a carriage, and brought it back to the stable.

    During the Taiyuan era, Wang Rong of Taiyuan served as the governor of Yulin and docked his boat at Xin Ting to sleep. He dreamed that someone gave him seven mulberry seeds, which he put in his clothes. Upon waking, he found them and interpreted them, saying, "Mulberries are the fruit of the mulberry tree." From then on, all seven of his family members died, both male and female, young and old. [75]

    晋荆州刺史桓豁所住斋中见一人长丈余，梦曰：“我龙山之神，来无好意。使君既贞，固我当自去耳。”
    　　晋义熙初，乌伤黄蔡于查溪岸，照射见水际有物，眼光彻其间，相去三尺许，形如大斗。引弩射之，应弦而中，便闻从流奔惊波浪砰磕，不知所向。经年与伴共至一处，名为竹落岗，去先所二十许里，有骨可长三丈余，见昔射箭，贯在其中。因语伴云：“此是我往年所射物，乃死于此。”拔矢而归，其夕梦见一长人责诮之曰：“我在洲渚之间，无关人事，而横见杀害。怨苦莫伸，连时觅汝，今始相得。”眠寤患腹痛而殒。
    　　晋太原郭澄之字仲靖，义熙初，诸葛长民欲取为辅国谘议，澄之不乐，后为南康太守。卢循之反自广州，长民以其无先告因骋私恶收澄之，以付廷尉，将致大辟。夜梦见一神人以鸟角如意与之，虽是寤中，殊自指的既觉，便在其头侧，可长尺余，形制甚陋。澄之遂得无恙。后从入关，赍以自随，忽失所在。 [76]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [76]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 来无好意 - Idiomatic expression meaning 'came with no good intent.'
    • 使君既贞，固我当自去耳 - '使君' is a respectful term for a high-ranking official, '既贞' means 'being virtuous,' and '固我当自去耳' means 'I will naturally leave on my own.'
    • 引弩射之，应弦而中 - '引弩射之' means 'he drew his bow and shot it,' and '应弦而中' means 'it hit the target with a twang of the string.'
    • 从流奔惊 - Means 'the water rushed and surged.'
    • 不知所向 - Means 'no one knew where it went.'
    • 经年 - Means 'after a year.'
    • 与伴共至一处 - Means 'with his companions to a place.'
    • 名为竹落岗 - Means 'called Zhuluangang.'
    • 去先所二十许里 - Means 'about twenty miles from the previous location.'
    • 有骨可长三丈余 - Means 'a bone about thirty feet long.'
    • 见昔射箭，贯在其中 - Means 'he saw the arrow he had shot before, still embedded in it.'
    • 拔矢而归 - Means 'he pulled out the arrow and returned home.'
    • 责诮之曰 - Means 'scolded him and said.'
    • 我在洲渚之间 - Means 'I was in the marshes.'
    • 无关人事 - Means 'had no relation to human affairs.'
    • 而横见杀害 - Means 'but was killed arbitrarily.'
    • 怨苦莫伸 - Means 'my grievances cannot be vented.'
    • 连时觅汝 - Means 'I have been looking for you all this time.'
    • 今始相得 - Means 'now I have finally found you.'
    • 眠寤患腹痛而殒 - Means 'he suffered from abdominal pain while sleeping and waking, and died.'
    • 诸葛长民欲取为辅国谘议 - '诸葛长民' is a person's name, '欲取为' means 'wanted to appoint,' and '辅国谘议' means 'as an advisor to the state.'
    • 澄之不乐 - Means 'Chengzhi was not happy.'
    • 后为南康太守 - Means 'later became the governor of Nankang.'
    • 卢循之反自广州 - '卢循' is a person's name, '反自广州' means 'revolted from Guangzhou.'
    • 长民以其无先告因骋私恶收澄之，以付廷尉，将致大辟 - '以其无先告' means 'because he did not warn them in advance,' '因骋私恶' means 'and used this as a pretext for personal grievances,' '收澄之' means 'arrested Chengzhi,' '以付廷尉' means 'and handed him over to the Imperial Censor,' and '将致大辟' means 'intending to execute him.'
    • 夜梦见一神人以鸟角如意与之 - '夜梦见一神人' means 'he dreamed of a divine being at night,' '以鸟角如意与之' means 'gave him a bird-shaped如意 (a symbol of good fortune).'
    • 虽是寤中，殊自指的既觉，便在其头侧，可长尺余，形制甚陋 - '虽是寤中' means 'even in a waking state,' '殊自指的' means 'it was clearly pointed out,' '既觉' means 'when he woke up,' '便在其头侧' means 'it was beside his head,' '可长尺余' means 'about a foot long,' and '形制甚陋' means 'its shape was quite ugly.'
    • 澄之遂得无恙 - Means 'Chengzhi thus escaped unharmed.'
    • 后从入关，赍以自随，忽失所在 - '后从入关' means 'later when he entered the pass,' '赍以自随' means 'carried it with him,' and '忽失所在' means 'it suddenly disappeared.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋荆州刺史桓豁 - Huan Huo, Governor of Jingzhou during the Eastern Jin dynasty.

    During the reign of Governor Huan Hua of Jingzhou, a man over ten feet tall appeared in his study and said in a dream, "I am the god of Long Mountain, and I came with no good intent. Since you are virtuous, I will naturally leave on my own."

    In the early years of the Yihexi era, Huang Cai from Wujiang, while shining a light at the water's edge of Chaxi, saw something with eyes that could see through it, about three feet away, shaped like a large ladle. He drew his bow and shot it, hitting the target with a twang of the string. Then, he heard the water rushing and surging, and no one knew where it went. After a year, he and his companions arrived at a place called Zhuluangang, about twenty miles from the previous location. There, they found a bone about thirty feet long, and he saw the arrow he had shot before, still embedded in it. He then told his companions, "This is what I shot last year, and it died here." He pulled out the arrow and returned home. That night, he dreamed of a tall man who scolded him and said, "I was in the marshes, had no relation to human affairs, but was killed arbitrarily. My grievances cannot be vented, and I have been looking for you all this time; now I have finally found you." He suffered from abdominal pain while sleeping and waking and died.

    During the early years of the Yihexi era, Guo Chengzhi of Taiyuan, also known as Zhongjing, was wanted to be appointed as an advisor to the state by Zhuge Changmin, but Chengzhi was not happy and later became the governor of Nankang. When Lu Xun rebelled from Guangzhou, Changmin used this as a pretext for personal grievances and arrested Chengzhi, handing him over to the Imperial Censor, intending to execute him. That night, Chengzhi dreamed of a divine being who gave him a bird-shaped如意 (a symbol of good fortune). Even in a waking state, it was clearly pointed out; when he woke up, it was beside his head, about a foot long, with an ugly shape. Chengzhi thus escaped unharmed. Later, when he entered the pass, he carried it with him, but it suddenly disappeared. [76]

    义熙中，商灵均为桂阳太守，梦人来缚其身。将去，形神乖散。复有一人云：“且置之，须作衡阳，当取之耳。”商惊寤惆怅。永初三年，除衡阳守，知冥理难逃，辞不得免，果卒官。
    　　商仲堪在丹徒，梦一人曰：“君有济物之心，岂能移我在高燥处，则恩及枯骨矣。”明日、果有一棺逐水流下，仲堪取而葬之于高冈，酹以酒食。其夕梦见其人来拜谢。一云，仲堪游于江滨，见流棺，接而葬焉。旬日间门前之沟忽起为岸。其夕有人通仲堪，自称徐伯玄，云：“感君之惠，无以报也。”仲堪因问：“门前之岸是何祥乎？”对曰：“水中有岸，其名为洲。君将为州。”言终而没。 [77]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [77]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 形神乖散 - Means 'body and spirit scattered'; describes a state of disintegration.
    • 恩及枯骨 - Means 'bless the bones of the dead'; expresses gratitude and respect for the deceased.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 義熙 - The era name of Emperor An of Liu Song, from 405 to 419 AD.
    • 永初 - The era name of Emperor Wu of Liu Song, from 420 to 422 AD.
    • 商灵均 - A historical figure, possibly a politician or military leader.
    • 商仲堪 - A historical figure, likely a relative or associate of 商灵均.
    • 桂阳 - An ancient Chinese county, located in present-day Hunan Province.
    • 衡阳 - An ancient Chinese county, located in present-day Hunan Province.
    • 丹徒 - An ancient Chinese county, located in present-day Jiangsu Province.

    Proper Nouns

    • 徐伯玄 - A historical figure or a character in the story.

    During the reign of Emperor An of Liu Song (405 AD), 商灵均 was the governor of Guiyang. He dreamed of someone coming to bind his body. As they were about to take him away, his body and spirit scattered. Another person said, "Put it aside for now. When he becomes the governor of Hengyang, he will take it." 商灵均 woke up in a state of sorrow. In the third year of Emperor Wu of Liu Song's reign (422 AD), he was appointed as the governor of Hengyang. Knowing that fate is inescapable, he could not refuse the position and ultimately died in office.

    商仲堪 was in Dan, and he dreamed of someone who said, "You have a heart to help others. Can you move me to a high and dry place, so that I can bless the bones of the dead?" The next day, a floating coffin came down the river, and 商仲堪 took it and buried it on a high hill, offering wine and food. That night, he dreamed of the person coming to thank him. Another account says that 商仲堪 was walking by the riverbank and saw a floating coffin, which he took and buried. Within ten days, the ditch in front of his door suddenly rose into a bank. That night, someone visited 商仲堪, introducing himself as Xu Boxuan, and said, "I am grateful for your kindness, but I have no way to repay you." 商仲堪 asked, "What does the bank in front of my door signify?" Xu Boxuan replied, "A bank in the water is called a shoal. You will become the governor of a state." He then disappeared. [77]

    蒋道支于水侧见一浮楂，取为研制，形象鱼，有道家符谶及纸，皆内鱼研中，常以自随。二十余年忽失之，梦人云：“吾暂游湘水，过湘君庙，为二妃所留，今复还，可于水际见寻也。”道支诘旦至水侧，见{口曾}者得一鲤鱼，买剖之，得先时符谶及纸，方悟是所梦人弃之。俄而雷雨，屋上有五色气，直上入云，后人有过湘君庙，见此鱼研在二妃侧。
    　　刘穆之东莞人，世居京口，初为琅琊府主簿，尝梦与武帝泛海，遇大风，惊俯视，船下见二白龙夹船。既而至一山，山峰耸秀，意甚悦。又尝渡扬子江，宿，梦合两船，为舫，上施华盖，仪饰甚盛，以升天。既晓，有一老姥，问曰：“君昨夜有佳梦否？”穆之乃具说之，姥曰：“君必位居端揆。”言讫不见。后官至仆射、丹阳尹，以元功也。 [78]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [78]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 可为师表 - Means 'can serve as a teacher and model,' stressing exemplary status.
    • 博学求之 - Means to seek knowledge broadly from many sources.
    • 直上入云 - Means 'rose straight into the clouds,' indicating the direction and speed of the clouds.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 蒋道支 - Refers to Jiang Daozhi, a historical figure or individual mentioned in the text.
    • 湘水 - Refers to the Xiang River, a significant river in Chinese history and culture.
    • 湘君庙 - Refers to the temple of the Lord of Xiang, a deity associated with the Xiang River.
    • 二妃 - Refers to the two consorts, likely the legendary consorts of the Lord of Xiang.
    • 刘穆之 - Refers to Liu Muzhi, a historical figure known for his role in the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 武帝 - Refers to Emperor Wu of Liu Song, a significant ruler in Chinese history.
    • 扬子江 - Refers to the Yangtze River, one of the longest rivers in Asia.
    • 先达 - Refers to earlier accomplished figures or predecessors who can be learned from.
    • 仆射 - Refers to a high-ranking official position in the Chinese government.
    • 丹阳尹 - Refers to the governor of DanYang, a region in China.
    • 元功 - Refers to a person of great merit or achievement.

    Proper Nouns

    • 蒋道支 - Jiang Daozhi
    • 湘水 - Xiang River
    • 湘君庙 - Temple of the Lord of Xiang
    • 二妃 - Two consorts
    • 刘穆之 - Liu Muzhi
    • 武帝 - Emperor Wu of Liu Song
    • 扬子江 - Yangtze River
    • 仆射 - A high-ranking official position
    • 丹阳尹 - Governor of DanYang
    • 元功 - A person of great merit or achievement

    Jiang Daozhi, while standing by the water's edge, saw a floating log. He took it to make a fish, which was shaped like a fish and contained Taoist talismans and paper. He always carried it with him. After more than twenty years, he suddenly lost it. In a dream, someone said, "I temporarily traveled to the Xiang River, passed by the temple of the Lord of Xiang, and was detained by the two consorts. Now I have returned. You can find me by the water's edge."

    The next day, Jiang Daozhi went to the water's edge and saw someone who had caught a carp. He bought and cut it open, finding the talismans and paper from before. He then realized that it was the dreamer who had discarded it. Soon after, there was thunder and rain, and there were five-colored clouds above his house, which rose straight into the clouds. Later, someone passed by the temple of the Lord of Xiang and saw the fish by the side of the two consorts.

    Liu Muzhi, a native of Dongguan, lived in Jingkou. Initially, he served as the main clerk of the Langya government. Once, he dreamed of traveling with Emperor Wu across the sea, encountering a strong wind. He looked down and saw two white dragons on either side of the ship. They then arrived at a mountain with a towering and beautiful peak, which he found very pleasing. He also once crossed the Yangtze River and stayed overnight. In his dream, he merged two boats into a larger one, adorned with a canopy and elaborate decorations, as if ascending to heaven. When he woke up, an old woman asked, "Did you have a good dream last night?" Liu Muzhi then told her about his dream. The old woman said, "You will surely hold a high position." After she spoke, she disappeared. Later, he became the Secretary of State and the Governor of DanYang, achieving this through his great merit. [78]

    景平中，颍川荀茂远至南康，夜梦一人，头有一角，为远筮曰：“君若至都，必得官。”问是何职，答曰：“官生于水。”于是而寤，未解所说。因复寐，又梦部伍至扬州水门，堕水而死，作棺既成。远入中自试，恨小，即见殡殓，葬之渚次，怅然惊觉，以告母兄。船至水门过，果落江而殒，丧仪一如其梦。
    　　吴兴沈庆之字弘先，废帝遣从子攸之赍药赐死，时年八十。是岁旦庆之梦，有人以两匹绢与之，谓曰：“此绢足度。”寤而谓人曰：“老子今年不免矣。两匹八十尺也，足度无盈余矣。”遂死，初，庆之尝梦引卤簿入厕中，庆之甚恶入厕。之鄙时，有善占梦者为之解曰：“君必大富贵，然未在旦夕问。”其故，答云：“卤簿固是宝贵容厕中，所谓后帝也。知君富贵不在今日。” [79]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [79]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 官生于水 - Metaphorical prediction indicating that the official position will arise from water, symbolizing a cryptic and symbolic future event.
    • 足度 - Play on words where '度' can mean both 'sufficient length' and 'death,' adding a layer of irony and symbolism.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 景平 - Era name of Emperor Shun of Liu Song, indicating the historical context of the events.
    • 颍川 - Region in Henan known for its cultural significance, providing a geographical and cultural backdrop.
    • 扬州 - City in Jiangsu, historically important for trade and culture, adding to the narrative's setting.
    • 吴兴 - Region in Zhejiang, known for its silk production and cultural heritage, enriching the cultural context.
    • 从子 - Descendant, often used to denote a close familial relationship, highlighting the social structure.
    • 卤簿 - Ceremonial procession, often associated with imperial or high-ranking officials, adding a layer of ceremonial and social significance.
    • 厕中 - In the toilet, a place traditionally considered impure, adding a layer of irony and symbolism.

    Proper Nouns

    • 景平 - Era name of Emperor Shun of Liu Song, providing historical context.
    • 颍川 - Region in Henan, providing geographical and cultural context.
    • 南康 - Place name, likely a city or region, adding to the narrative's setting.
    • 扬州 - City in Jiangsu, providing geographical and cultural context.
    • 吴兴 - Region in Zhejiang, providing geographical and cultural context.
    • 沈庆之 - Historical figure from Wuxing, providing historical context.
    • 从子攸之 - Descendant named Youzhi, providing familial and historical context.
    • 废帝 - Deposed emperor, likely Emperor Wuzong of Liu Song, providing historical context.

    During the reign of Emperor Shun (424-432 CE), a man named Xun Maoyuan from Yingchuan traveled to Nankang. One night, he had a dream in which a man with a single horn on his head divined for him, saying, "If you go to the capital, you will surely obtain an official position." When asked what kind of position it would be, the diviner replied, "The official position will arise from water." After this, Xun Maoyuan woke up but did not understand the meaning of the divination. He then fell back asleep and had another dream in which a group of people accompanied him to the water gate of Yangzhou. They fell into the water and died, and a coffin was made. Xun Maoyuan entered the coffin to test its size and found it too small. He was immediately prepared for burial and buried at the edge of a river or lake. He woke up feeling disappointed and confused, and he told his mother and elder brother about the dream. Later, when his boat passed through the water gate, he indeed fell into the river and died, and the funeral rites were exactly as he had dreamed.

    In the same period, Shen Qingzhi, a man from Wuxing, was eighty years old when he was given poison by the deposed emperor through his descendant Youzhi. On the first day of the year, Shen Qingzhi had a dream in which someone gave him two pieces of silk, saying, "This silk is sufficient for measurement." Upon waking, he told others, "I will not escape this year. Two pieces of silk are eighty feet long, which is enough for measurement, with no remainder." He then died. Initially, Shen Qingzhi had a dream in which he led a ceremonial procession into a toilet. He greatly disliked entering the toilet. At the time, a skilled dream interpreter explained the dream, saying, "You will surely achieve great wealth and honor, but not today." When asked why, the interpreter replied, "A ceremonial procession entering a toilet is a symbol of the emperor who will come after the current one. It is known that your wealth and honor will not come today." [79]

    临川太守谢灵运，初钱塘杜明师夜梦东南有人来，入其馆，是夕即灵运生于会稽，旬日而谢玄亡，其家以子孙难得，送灵运于杜治养之，十五方还都，故名客儿。

    　　●卷八
    　　后汉时，姑苏忽有男子衣白衣，冠白冠，形神修励，从者六七人，遍扰居民，欲掩害之，即有风雨，郡兵不能掩。术士赵晃闻之，往白郡守，曰：“此妖也，欲见之乎？”乃净水焚香，长啸一声，大风疾至，闻室中数十人响应。晃掷手中符如风，顷若有人持物来者，晃曰：“何敢幻惑如此！”随复旋风拥去，晃谓守曰：“可视之！”使者出，门人已报云：“去此百步有大白蛇，长三丈，断首路旁，其六七从者皆身首异处，亦鼋鼍之属。 [80]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [80]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 临川太守 - Title of a high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 初钱塘杜明师夜梦东南有人来 - Narrative opening describing a dream in which someone from the southeast appears.
    • 是夕即灵运生于会稽 - Temporal link between the dream and the birth of Xie Lingyun in Kuaiji.
    • 旬日而谢玄亡 - Significant event following the birth of Xie Lingyun, marking the death of Xie Xuan ten days later.
    • 其家以子孙难得，送灵运于杜治养之 - Explanation for the adoption of Xie Lingyun due to the family's difficulty in having descendants.
    • 十五方还都 - Significant milestone in Xie Lingyun's life, marking his return to the capital at the age of fifteen.
    • 故名客儿 - Explanation for Xie Lingyun's nickname, 'Guest Child.'
    • 姑苏忽有男子衣白衣，冠白冠，形神修励 - Description of a mysterious figure in Gusu, wearing white clothes and a white hat, with a refined and vigorous appearance and spirit.
    • 从者六七人 - Indicates a group of followers accompanying the mysterious figure.
    • 遍扰居民，欲掩害之 - Describes the actions of the mysterious figure, roaming around disturbing the residents with the intent to harm them.
    • 即有风雨，郡兵不能掩 - Describes a supernatural event where a storm occurs, and the local soldiers cannot catch the mysterious figure.
    • 术士赵晃闻之，往白郡守 - Introduces the magician Zhao Huang, who hears about the event and reports to the local governor.
    • 此妖也，欲见之乎 - Zhao Huang's question to the governor, suggesting that the figure is a demon and asking if the governor wants to see it.
    • 乃净水焚香，长啸一声 - Describes Zhao Huang's ritual, involving purifying water, burning incense, and letting out a long cry.
    • 大风疾至，闻室中数十人响应 - Describes the reaction to the ritual, with a strong wind arriving and dozens of people inside the room responding.
    • 晃掷手中符如风，顷若有人持物来者 - Describes the magician's action, throwing his talisman like the wind, and the effect, seeming as if someone was bringing something.
    • 晃曰：“何敢幻惑如此！” - Zhao Huang's accusation, questioning how the mysterious figure dares to deceive him.
    • 随复旋风拥去 - Describes the demon's departure, carried away by a whirlwind.
    • 晃谓守曰：“可视之！” - Zhao Huang's final statement to the governor, suggesting that the governor can see the demon.
    • 使者出，门人已报云 - Describes the aftermath of the event, with the messenger going out and the attendants reporting.
    • 去此百步有大白蛇，长三丈 - Describes a large white snake, three zhang long, found a hundred steps away.
    • 断首路旁，其六七从者皆身首异处，亦鼋鼍之属 - Describes the fate of the snake and its followers, with their heads severed by the roadside and their bodies also decapitated, indicating they were of the turtle and alligator kind.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 临川太守 - Title of a high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 钱塘 - A region in modern-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 杜明师 - A respected figure, possibly a monk or a teacher.
    • 谢灵运 - A famous poet and official of the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 谢玄 - A prominent general of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 会稽 - An ancient county in Zhejiang Province.
    • 姑苏 - Another name for Suzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province.
    • 术士赵晃 - A magician or sorcerer who was active during the period.

    Proper Nouns

    • 临川太守 - Title of a high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 钱塘 - A region in modern-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 杜明师 - A respected figure, possibly a monk or a teacher.
    • 谢灵运 - A famous poet and official of the Southern Song Dynasty.
    • 谢玄 - A prominent general of the Eastern Jin Dynasty.
    • 会稽 - An ancient county in Zhejiang Province.
    • 姑苏 - Another name for Suzhou, a city in Jiangsu Province.
    • 术士赵晃 - A magician or sorcerer who was active during the period.

    The Governor of Linchuan, Xie Lingyun, was born in Kuaiji on the very night when the monk Du Mingshi from Qiantang had a dream in which someone from the southeast appeared and entered his hall. Ten days later, Xie Xuan passed away. His family, thinking it was hard to have descendants, sent Xie Lingyun to be raised by Du Mingshi. At the age of fifteen, he returned to the capital, and thus was called "Guest Child."

    In the Later Han period, suddenly in Gusu, there appeared a man wearing white clothes and a white hat, with a refined and vigorous appearance. He was accompanied by six or seven people and roamed around disturbing the residents, intending to harm them. Suddenly there was a storm, and the local soldiers could not catch him. The magician Zhao Huang heard about it and went to report to the local governor, saying, "This is a demon, do you want to see him?" He then purified water and burned incense, and let out a long cry. A strong wind quickly arrived, and dozens of people inside the room responded. Zhao Huang threw his talisman like the wind, and soon it seemed as if someone was bringing something. Zhao Huang said, "How dare you deceive me like this!" Then a whirlwind carried him away. Zhao Huang told the governor, "You can see it!" When the messenger went out, the attendants reported that a hundred steps away, there was a large white snake, three zhang long, with its head severed by the roadside. Its six or seven followers were also decapitated. They were also of the turtle and alligator kind. [80]

    乐广字彦辅，南阳氵育阳人，晋惠帝时为河南尹。先是，官舍多妖怪，前尹皆于廊下督邮传中治事，无敢在厅事者，惟广处之不疑，常白日外户自开，二子凯横等皆惊怖，广独自若，顾见墙有孔，使人掘墙，得狸而杀之，其怪遂绝。
    　　晋怀帝永嘉中，徐出行田，见一女子姿色鲜白，就言调，女因吟曰：“畴昔聆好音，日月心延伫。如何遇良人，中怀邈无绪。”情既谐，欣然延至一屋。女施设饮食而多鱼，遂经日不返。兄弟追觅至湖边，见与女相对坐，兄以藤杖击女，即化成白鹤，翻然高飞，恍惚年余乃差。 [81]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [81]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 官舍多妖怪 - Literally means 'the government office was haunted by spirits,' indicating supernatural occurrences.
    • 前尹皆于廊下督邮传中治事 - Describes the previous governors handling official business in the hallway or post station instead of the main hall, suggesting they were afraid of the haunting.
    • 无敢在厅事者 - Means 'no one dared to work in the main hall,' reinforcing the fear of the supernatural.
    • 惟广处之不疑 - Means 'only Le Guang handled it without doubt,' highlighting his courage and composure.
    • 常白日外户自开 - Means 'often during the day, the outer door would open by itself,' describing a mysterious event.
    • 二子凯横等皆惊怖 - Means 'his two sons, Kai and Heng, and others were all terrified,' indicating the reaction of those around him.
    • 广独自若 - Means 'Le Guang remained calm and composed,' emphasizing his bravery.
    • 顾见墙有孔 - Means 'he looked and saw a hole in the wall,' describing his observation.
    • 使人掘墙，得狸而杀之 - Means 'he ordered someone to dig the wall and found a weasel, which he then killed,' detailing the resolution of the haunting.
    • 其怪遂绝 - Means 'the supernatural occurrences ceased,' indicating the problem was solved.
    • 徐出行田 - Means 'Xu Xi went out to his fields,' describing the setting.
    • 见一女子姿色鲜白 - Means 'he saw a woman with fresh and white appearance,' describing the woman he encountered.
    • 就言调 - Means 'she approached Xu Xi and flirted with him,' describing the interaction.
    • 女因吟曰 - Means 'the woman then recited,' introducing her poetic response.
    • 畴昔聆好音，日月心延伫 - A poetic line meaning 'long ago I heard good news, and day and night my heart lingered,' expressing her longing.
    • 如何遇良人，中怀邈无绪 - A poetic line meaning 'how can I meet a good man, but my heart is far and without direction,' expressing her confusion and longing.
    • 情既谐 - Means 'their feelings became harmonious,' indicating a mutual attraction.
    • 欣然延至一屋 - Means 'he happily invited her to a house,' describing their interaction.
    • 女施设饮食而多鱼 - Means 'the woman prepared food with many fish,' describing the meal.
    • 遂经日不返 - Means 'she stayed for a whole day without returning,' indicating the duration of their encounter.
    • 兄弟追觅至湖边 - Means 'his brothers searched for him to the lake,' describing their search.
    • 见与女相对坐 - Means 'they saw him sitting opposite the woman,' describing the scene.
    • 兄以藤杖击女 - Means 'his brother hit the woman with a vine staff,' describing the action.
    • 即化成白鹤，翻然高飞 - Means 'she immediately turned into a white crane and flew away,' describing the transformation.
    • 恍惚年余乃差 - Means 'Xu Xi remained dazed for more than a year before recovering,' describing his state after the incident.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 乐广 - A historical figure known for his wisdom and bravery.
    • 徐 - Another historical figure, less well-known than Le Guang.
    • 南阳氵育阳 - A place name in ancient China, indicating Le Guang's origin.
    • 晋惠帝 - The ruler of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 晋怀帝 - The ruler of the Western Jin dynasty.
    • 河南尹 - A high-ranking official in charge of the province of Henan.
    • 督邮传 - A post station or relay station used for official communications.
    • 厅事 - The main hall of an official residence or government office.
    • 妖怪 - Supernatural beings or spirits.
    • 白鹤 - A symbol of purity and transcendence in Chinese culture.
    • 恍惚 - A state of confusion or daze, often associated with supernatural experiences.

    Proper Nouns

    • 乐广 - A historical figure.
    • 徐 - A historical figure.
    • 南阳氵育阳 - A place name.
    • 晋惠帝 - A historical figure.
    • 晋怀帝 - A historical figure.
    • 河南尹 - A title of an official.
    • 督邮传 - A term for a post station.
    • 厅事 - A term for the main hall.
    • 白鹤 - A symbolic creature.
    • 恍惚 - A state of confusion.

    Le Guang, courtesy name Yanfu, was from Nanyang Yuyang. During the reign of Emperor Huai of Jin, he served as the Governor of Henan. Previously, the government office was haunted by spirits, and all previous governors handled official business in the hallway or post station, no one daring to work in the main hall. Only Le Guang handled it without doubt. Often during the day, the outer door would open by itself, and his two sons, Kai and Heng, and others were all terrified, but Le Guang remained calm and composed. He looked and saw a hole in the wall, so he ordered someone to dig the wall and found a weasel, which he then killed. The supernatural occurrences ceased.

    During the Yongjia period of Emperor Huai of Jin, Xu Xi went out to his fields and saw a woman with a fresh and white appearance. She approached Xu Xi and flirted with him. The woman then recited: "Long ago I heard good news, and day and night my heart lingered. How can I meet a good man, but my heart is far and without direction." Their feelings became harmonious, and he happily invited her to a house. The woman prepared food with many fish, and they stayed for a whole day without returning. His brothers searched for him to the lake, where they saw him sitting opposite the woman. His brother hit the woman with a vine staff, and she immediately turned into a white crane and flew away. Xu Xi remained dazed for more than a year before recovering. [81]

    晋太元中，桓谦字敬祖，忽有人皆长寸余，悉被铠持槊，乘具装，马从中出，精光耀日，游走宅上，数百为群，部障指挥，更相撞刺。马既轻快，人亦便捷，能缘几登灶，寻饮食之所。或有切肉，辄来丛聚，力所能胜者，以槊刺取，迳入穴中。蒋山道土朱应子令作沸汤，浇所入处，寂不复出，因掘之，有斛许大蚁死在穴中。谦后以门衅同灭。
    　　太元中，吴兴沈霸梦女子来就寝，同伴密察，惟见牝狗每待霸眠，辄来依床，疑为魅，因杀而食之。霸后梦青衣人责之曰：“我本以女与君共事，若不合怀，自可见语，何忽乃加耻杀！可以骨见还。”明日，收骨葬冈上，从是乃平复。 [82]

    During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (376–396 AD), Huán Qiān, courtesy name Jìngzǔ, suddenly saw people about an inch tall, all wearing armor and holding spears, riding armored horses that emerged from underground tunnels. They shone brightly like the sun, moving around the house in large groups, organizing and engaging in combat. The horses were light and swift, and the people were agile, able to climb furniture and search for food. When someone cut meat, they would gather, and those who could overpower it would take it with their spears and return to their tunnels. The Taoist priest Zhū Yìngzǐ ordered boiling water to be poured on the entry points, and they no longer came out. They then dug and found large dead ants in the tunnels. Huán Qiān was later killed due to his involvement in a political conflict.
    　　During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, Shěn Bà from Wu County had a dream in which a woman came to share his bed. His companions secretly observed and saw only a female dog that would come to the bed whenever Shěn Bà slept. Suspecting it to be a spirit, they killed and ate it. Later, Shěn Bà dreamed of a woman in green clothing rebuking him, saying, "I originally intended to work with you as a woman. If you did not wish to, you could have spoken to me. Why did you suddenly kill me out of shame?" She asked for her bones to be returned. The next day, his companions collected the bones and buried them on a hill, after which Shěn Bà recovered. [82]

    晋孝武太元十二年，吴郡寿颁道志边火为居，渚次忽生一双，物状若青藤而无枝叶，数日盈拱。试共伐之，即有血出，声在空中，如雄鹅叫两音相应。腹中得一卵，形如鸭子，其根头似蛇面眼。
    　　吴郡岑渊为吴郡时，大司农卿碑注在江东湖西。太元中，村人见龟载从田中出，还其先处，萍藻犹著腹下。
    　　晋太元末，徐寂之尝野行，见一女子操荷举手麾寂之，寂之悦而延住。此后来往如旧，寂之便患瘦瘠，时或言见华房深宇芳茵广筵，寂之与女觞肴宴乐。数年，其弟ㄧ之闻屋内群语，潜往窥之，见数女子从后户出，惟余一女，隐在篑边。ㄧ之径入，寂之怒曰：“今方欢乐，何故唐突！”忽复共言云篑中有人，ㄧ之即发看，有一牝猴，遂杀之，寂之病遂瘥。 [83]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [83]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋孝武太元十二年 - Specific year in the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (387 AD).
    • 忽生一双 - Sudden appearance of a pair, possibly referring to a dual entity.
    • 状若青藤而无枝叶 - Describes something resembling a green vine but without branches or leaves.
    • 数日盈拱 - After several days, it grows to the size of a person's armspan.
    • 试共伐之 - Attempt to cut it down.
    • 即有血出 - Blood immediately flows out.
    • 声在空中，如雄鹅叫两音相应 - Sounds in the air, like a male goose calling, with two sounds responding to each other.
    • 腹中得一卵 - Inside the body, a single egg is found.
    • 形如鸭子，其根头似蛇面眼 - The shape is like a duck, with a head resembling a snake's face and eyes.
    • 晋太元末 - At the end of the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (399-401 AD).
    • 徐寂之尝野行 - Xu Jizhi once went for a walk in the wild.
    • 见一女子操荷举手麾寂之 - He saw a woman holding a lotus and waving to him.
    • 寂之悦而延住 - Xu Jizhi was pleased and invited her to stay.
    • 此后来往如旧 - They continued to meet as before.
    • 寂之便患瘦瘠 - Xu Jizhi became thin and weak.
    • 时或言见华房深宇芳茵广筵 - Sometimes he said he saw a beautiful hall with deep eaves and fragrant mats on a wide table.
    • 寂之与女觞肴宴乐 - Xu Jizhi and the woman drank and feasted together.
    • 数年 - For several years.
    • 其弟ㄧ之闻屋内群语 - His younger brother Yi heard a group of people talking inside the house.
    • 潜往窥之 - Sneaked to look inside.
    • 见数女子从后户出 - Saw several women coming out from the back door.
    • 惟余一女，隐在篑边 - Only one woman remained, hidden by a basket.
    • 径入 - Went in directly.
    • 寂之怒曰 - Xu Jizhi angrily said.
    • 今方欢乐，何故唐突 - Now we are enjoying ourselves, why are you being so rude?
    • 忽复共言云篑中有人 - Suddenly they said there was someone in the basket.
    • 发看 - Opened to look.
    • 有一牝猴 - Found a female monkey.
    • 遂杀之 - Killed it.
    • 寂之病遂瘥 - Xu Jizhi's illness then healed.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋孝武太元十二年 - 387 AD, during the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 吴郡 - Wu Prefecture, a region in southern China.
    • 大司农卿 - Grand Farmer, a high-ranking official in charge of agriculture and grain storage.
    • 江东湖西 - Western part of the lake in the eastern region, likely referring to a specific geographical area.
    • 龟载 - Turtle carrying, possibly a metaphor or a specific cultural reference.
    • 晋太元末 - 399-401 AD, the end of Emperor Xiaowu's reign.
    • 徐寂之 - A historical figure, possibly a scholar or official.
    • 华房深宇芳茵广筵 - Describes a luxurious and elegant setting, possibly a metaphor for a magical or supernatural place.
    • 华房 - Beautiful hall.
    • 深宇 - Deep eaves.
    • 芳茵 - Fragrant mats.
    • 广筵 - Wide table.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋孝武 - Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 太元十二年 - 387 AD.
    • 吴郡 - Wu Prefecture.
    • 寿颁道志边火 - A specific location in Wu Prefecture.
    • 大司农 - Grand Farmer.
    • 江东湖西 - Western part of the lake in the eastern region.
    • 龟载 - Turtle carrying.
    • 晋太元末 - 399-401 AD.
    • 徐寂之 - Historical figure, possibly a scholar or official.
    • 华房 - Beautiful hall.
    • 深宇 - Deep eaves.
    • 芳茵 - Fragrant mats.
    • 广筵 - Wide table.
    • 华房深宇芳茵广筵 - Luxurious and elegant setting.
    • 华房深宇芳茵广筵 - Luxurious and elegant setting.
    • 华房深宇芳茵广筵 - Luxurious and elegant setting.

    In the twelfth year of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (387 AD), in the area of Shouban Daozhi Bianhuo in Wu Prefecture, a pair of creatures suddenly appeared in the marsh. They resembled green vines but had no branches or leaves. After several days, they grew to the size of a person's armspan. When people tried to cut them down, blood immediately flowed out. Sounds echoed in the air, like a male goose calling, with two sounds responding to each other. Inside one of the creatures, a single egg was found. The egg was shaped like a duck, with a head resembling a snake's face and eyes.

    During the time when Cen Yuan was the governor of Wu Prefecture, the tombstone inscription of the Grand Farmer was located in the western part of the lake in the eastern region. In the middle of the Taiyuan era, village people saw a turtle carrying something from the fields, returning to its original place, with water plants still attached to its belly.

    At the end of Emperor Xiaowu's reign (399-401 AD), Xu Jizhi once went for a walk in the wild and saw a woman holding a lotus and waving to him. He was pleased and invited her to stay. They continued to meet as before. Xu Jizhi then became thin and weak. Sometimes he said he saw a beautiful hall with deep eaves and fragrant mats on a wide table. He and the woman drank and feasted together. For several years, his younger brother Yi heard a group of people talking inside the house. He sneaked to look inside and saw several women coming out from the back door, leaving only one woman hidden by a basket. Yi went in directly. Xu Jizhi angrily said, "Now we are enjoying ourselves, why are you being so rude!" Suddenly, they said there was someone in the basket. Yi opened to look and found a female monkey, which he killed. Xu Jizhi's illness then healed. [83]

    义熙中东海徐氏婢兰忽患羸黄而拂拭异常，共伺察之，见扫帚从壁角来趋婢床，乃取而焚之，婢即平复。
    　　晋义熙中，乌伤人孙乞赍父书到郡，达石亭，天雨日暮，顾见一女戴青伞，年可十六七，姿容丰艳，通身紫衣。尔夕电光照室，乃是大狸，乞因抽刀斫杀，伞是荷叶。
    　　晋义熙中，永嘉松阳赵翼与大儿鲜共伐山桃树，有血流，惊而止。后忽失第三息所在，经十日自归，空中有语声，或歌或哭，翼语之曰：“汝既是神，何不与相见！”答曰：“我正气耳。舍北有大枫树，南有孤峰，名曰石楼，四壁绝立，人兽莫履。小有失意，便取此儿著树杪及石楼上，举家叩头请之，然后得下。” [84]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [84]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 羸黄 - Describes a pale, sickly appearance, often associated with illness or weakness.
    • 拂拭异常 - Literally 'unusual brushing or wiping,' likely referring to unusual behavior or movements.
    • 平复 - Means 'to recover' or 'to return to normal.'
    • 赍父书到郡 - Means 'carrying his father's letter to the county,' indicating a journey with a specific purpose.
    • 姿容丰艳 - Means 'graceful and beautiful appearance.'
    • 通身紫衣 - Describes the girl as wearing a purple robe from head to toe.
    • 空中有语声 - Describes a voice coming from the air, indicating a supernatural element.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 義熙中 - The Yixi era, a specific period in Chinese history, and the text reflects the cultural and social norms of that time.
    • 东海徐氏 - Refers to a family from Donghai, indicating a specific region and social class.
    • 乌伤人 - Refers to a person from Wushang, a place name that adds geographical context.
    • 大枫树 - Refers to a large fir tree, which is a significant natural feature in the story.
    • 石楼 - Refers to a mountain peak, which is described as having steep cliffs and being difficult to climb.
    • 举家叩头请之 - Reflects the cultural practice of showing deep respect and pleading for mercy or assistance.

    Proper Nouns

    • 義熙中 - The middle of the Yixi era.
    • 东海徐氏 - A family from Donghai.
    • 婢兰 - The name of the servant.
    • 孙乞 - The name of the traveler.
    • 乌伤人 - A person from Wushang.
    • 永嘉松阳赵翼 - The name of the protagonist, Sū Yu, from Songyang, Yongjia.
    • 大儿鲜 - Sū Yu's eldest son.
    • 第三息 - Sū Yu's third son.
    • 石楼 - A mountain peak.

    During the middle of the Yixi era, a maid named Lan from the Xu family in Donghai suddenly fell ill with a pale, sickly appearance and exhibited unusual behavior. They observed her closely and saw a broom moving from the corner of the wall towards her bed. They then took the broom and burned it, and the maid immediately recovered.

    During the middle of the Yixi era, a man from Wushang named Sun Qi carried his father's letter to the county, reaching a place called Shiting. It was raining heavily in the evening, and he looked up to see a girl wearing a green umbrella, about sixteen or seventeen years old, with a graceful and beautiful appearance and dressed entirely in purple. That night, a flash of lightning illuminated the room, revealing that she was actually a large cat. Sun Qi then drew his knife and killed it, discovering that the umbrella was made of lotus leaf.

    During the middle of the Yixi era, a man from Songyang, Yongjia named Zhao Yu, along with his eldest son Xian, was cutting a mountain peach tree when they saw blood flowing from it. They were startled and stopped. Later, his third son suddenly went missing. After ten days, he returned on his own. There were voices coming from the air, sometimes singing and sometimes crying. Zhao Yu asked him, "Since you are a spirit, why don't you show yourself to us?" The boy replied, "I am just a righteous spirit. To the north of our house is a large fir tree, and to the south is a solitary peak called Shilou, with sheer cliffs that no human or beast can climb. If I am slightly dissatisfied, I will take this child and place him on the top of the tree or the peak. The whole family must kneel and beg, and only then can he come down." [84]

    晋有士人买得鲜卑女，名怀顺，自说其姑女为赤苋所魅，始见一丈夫，容质妍净，著赤衣，自云家在厕北，女于是恒歌谣自得，每至将夕，辄结束去屋后。其家伺候，唯见有一株赤苋，女手指环挂其苋上，芟之而女号泣，经宿遂死。
    　　高祖永初中，张春为武昌太守。时人有嫁女，未及升车，女忽然失怪，出外殴击人，乃自云已不乐嫁俗人。巫云是邪魅。将女至江际，遂击鼓以术咒疗，春以为欺惑百姓，刻期须得妖魅。翼日有一青蛇来到坐所，即以大钉钉其头，至日中时，复见大龟从江来，伏于巫前。巫以朱书龟背作符，更遣入江，至暮，有大白鼍从江中出，乍沉乍浮，龟随后催逼，鼍自分死，冒来先入慢与女辞诀，女遂恸哭云，失其姻好。于是渐差。或问巫曰：“魅者归于一物，今安得有三巫。”云蛇是传通，龟是媒人，鼍是其对。所获三物悉以示春，春始知灵验，皆杀之。 [85]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [85]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晉有士人買得鮮卑女 - A straightforward statement meaning 'There was a scholar in the Jin dynasty who bought a Xianbei woman.'
    • 自說其姑女為赤苋所魅 - 'She herself said that her cousin was bewitched by the red amaranth.'
    • 始見一丈夫，容質妍淨，著赤衣 - 'She first saw a man, who was handsome and clean, wearing a red robe.'
    • 自云家在厕北 - 'He said his home was to the north of the latrine.'
    • 女於是恒歌谣自得 - 'The woman then always sang and felt content.'
    • 每至將夕，輒結束去屋後 - 'Every time it was about to be evening, she would always dress up and go behind the house.'
    • 其家伺候，唯見有一株赤苋 - 'Her family waited, and they only saw a red amaranth plant.'
    • 女手指環掛其苋上 - 'The woman's finger ring was hanging on the amaranth.'
    • 芟之而女號泣，經宿遂死 - 'When it was cut down, the woman cried out and died after a night.'
    • 高祖永初中 - 'During the Yongchun period of the Eastern Jin dynasty.'
    • 張春為武昌太守 - 'Zhang Chun was the governor of Wuchang.'
    • 時人有嫁女，未及升車 - 'There was a woman who was about to get married, but she had not yet climbed into the carriage.'
    • 女忽然失怪 - 'The woman suddenly acted strangely.'
    • 出外殴擊人 - 'She went out and hit people.'
    • 乃自云已不樂嫁俗人 - 'She then said that she did not want to marry an ordinary person.'
    • 巫云是邪魅 - 'The witch said it was a demon.'
    • 將女至江際，遂擊鼓以術咒療 - 'They took the woman to the riverbank and hit a drum to perform rituals and spells to cure her.'
    • 春以為欺惑百姓，刻期須得妖魅 - 'Zhang Chun thought they were deceiving the people and set a deadline to catch the demon.'
    • 翼日有一青蛇來到坐所 - 'The next day, a green snake came to the place where they were sitting.'
    • 即以大釘釘其頭 - 'They immediately nailed a large nail into its head.'
    • 至日中時，復見大龜從江來，伏於巫前 - 'At noon, a large turtle came from the river and lay in front of the witch.'
    • 巫以朱書龜背作符，更遣入江 - 'The witch wrote a red charm on the turtle's back and sent it back into the river.'
    • 至暮，有大白鼍從江中出，乍沉乍浮，龜隨後催逼 - 'By evening, a large white alligator came out of the river, sometimes sinking and sometimes floating, with the turtle following and urging it on.'
    • 鼍自分死，冒來先入慢與女辭訣，女遂慟哭云，失其姻好 - 'The alligator knew it was going to die and came forward first, slowly saying goodbye to the woman, who then cried bitterly, saying she had lost her good match.'
    • 於是漸差 - 'She then gradually recovered.'
    • 或問巫曰：'魅者歸於一物，今安得有三巫。' - 'Someone asked the witch, 'The demon was supposed to be one thing, how could there be three demons?''
    • 云蛇是傳通，龜是媒人，鼍是其對 - 'The witch said the snake was the messenger, the turtle was the matchmaker, and the alligator was the counterpart.'
    • 所獲三物悉以示春，春始知靈驗，皆殺之 - 'The three things they caught were shown to Zhang Chun, who then knew they were effective and killed them all.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晉 - The Jin dynasty, specifically the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 鮮卑 - A nomadic people who lived in northern China during the Jin dynasty.
    • 赤苋 - Red amaranth, a plant that is often associated with evil spirits in Chinese folklore.
    • 高祖永初中 - The Yongchun period of the Eastern Jin dynasty, during the reign of Emperor Yuan.
    • 武昌 - A city in present-day Hubei Province, which was an important administrative center during the Jin dynasty.
    • 張春 - A historical figure who served as the governor of Wuchang during the Jin dynasty.
    • 巫 - A shaman or witch, often associated with performing rituals and spells to cure illnesses or exorcise demons.
    • 赤衣 - Red clothing, which can symbolize various things in Chinese culture, including nobility, but also sometimes evil or demonic figures.
    • 厕北 - To the north of the latrine, which is often associated with unclean or taboo areas in Chinese culture.
    • 青蛇 - Green snakes, which are often associated with evil or demonic spirits in Chinese folklore.
    • 大龜 - Large turtles, which are often associated with longevity and wisdom in Chinese culture, but can also be used in rituals to communicate with the spirit world.
    • 白鼍 - White alligators, which are rare and often associated with water spirits in Chinese folklore.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晉 - The Jin dynasty.
    • 鮮卑 - The Xianbei people.
    • 赤苋 - Red amaranth.
    • 高祖永初中 - The Yongchun period of the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 武昌 - Wuchang, a city in present-day Hubei Province.
    • 張春 - Zhang Chun, a historical figure who served as the governor of Wuchang.
    • 青蛇 - Green snake.
    • 大龜 - Large turtle.
    • 白鼍 - White alligator.

    There was a scholar in the Jin dynasty who bought a Xianbei woman named Huai Shun. She herself said that her cousin was bewitched by the red amaranth. She first saw a man, who was handsome and clean, wearing a red robe, and he said his home was to the north of the latrine. The woman then always sang and felt content, and every time it was about to be evening, she would always dress up and go behind the house. Her family waited, and they only saw a red amaranth plant. The woman's finger ring was hanging on the amaranth. When it was cut down, the woman cried out and died after a night.

    During the Yongchun period of the Eastern Jin dynasty, Zhang Chun was the governor of Wuchang. There was a woman who was about to get married, but she had not yet climbed into the carriage. The woman suddenly acted strangely and went out and hit people. She then said that she did not want to marry an ordinary person. The witch said it was a demon. They took the woman to the riverbank and hit a drum to perform rituals and spells to cure her. Zhang Chun thought they were deceiving the people and set a deadline to catch the demon. The next day, a green snake came to the place where they were sitting. They immediately nailed a large nail into its head. At noon, a large turtle came from the river and lay in front of the witch. The witch wrote a red charm on the turtle's back and sent it back into the river. By evening, a large white alligator came out of the river, sometimes sinking and sometimes floating, with the turtle following and urging it on. The alligator knew it was going to die and came forward first, slowly saying goodbye to the woman, who then cried bitterly, saying she had lost her good match. She then gradually recovered. Someone asked the witch, "The demon was supposed to be one thing, how could there be three demons?" The witch said the snake was the messenger, the turtle was the matchmaker, and the alligator was the counterpart. The three things they caught were shown to Zhang Chun, who then knew they were effective and killed them all. [85]

    元嘉初，建康大夏营寡妇严，有人称华督与严结好，街卒夜见一丈夫，行造护军府，府在建阳门内，街卒呵问，答曰：“我华督造府，径沿西墙而入。”街卒以其犯夜，邀击之，乃变为鼍，察其所出入处，甚莹滑，通府中池，池先有鼍窟，岁久因能为魅，杀之乃绝。
    　　文帝元嘉初，益州王双忽不欲见明，常取水沃地，以菰蒋覆上眠息，饮食悉入其中，云恒有一女子着青裙白{须巾}，来就其寝，每听闻荐下有声，历历发之，见一青色白缨蚯蚓，长二尺许，云此女常以一奁香见遗气甚清芬奁乃螺壳香则菖蒲根于时咸谓双暂同阜螽矣。 [86]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [86]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元嘉初 - Refers to the early years of the reign of Emperor Wen of Song during the Southern Dynasties period.
    • 建康大夏營寡婦嚴 - Describes a widow named Yan living in the Jiankang area, specifically in the Daxia camp.
    • 有人稱華督與嚴結好 - An idiom meaning someone claimed that Hua Du and Yan had formed a good relationship.
    • 街卒夜見一丈夫 - A descriptive phrase meaning a night watchman saw a man at night.
    • 行造護軍府 - Means 'went to the Military Guard Office.'
    • 府在建陽門內 - Indicates the location of the Military Guard Office inside the Jianyang Gate.
    • 街卒呵問 - Means 'the night watchman shouted and asked.'
    • 答曰：“我華督造府，徑沿西牆而入。” - A direct quote where Hua Du replied, 'I am Hua Du going to the office, I entered along the west wall.'
    • 街卒以其犯夜，邀擊之 - Means 'the night watchman attacked him for violating the curfew.'
    • 乃變為鼍 - Means 'he then turned into a crocodile.'
    • 察其所出入處，甚莹滑 - Describes the place where he entered and exited, noting it was very smooth.
    • 通府中池，池先有鼍窟 - Means 'it connected to a pond in the courtyard, which previously had a crocodile's den.'
    • 歲久因能為魅，殺之乃絕 - Means 'after a long time, it became a ghost, and it was only killed to stop it.'
    • 文帝元嘉初，益州王双忽不欲見明 - Refers to another incident in the early years of Emperor Wen's reign, involving a man named Wang Shuang in Yizhou who suddenly did not want to see the light.
    • 常取水沃地，以菰蒋覆上眠息 - Describes Wang Shuang's unusual behavior of pouring water on the ground and covering himself with grass while sleeping.
    • 飲食悉入其中 - Means 'he put all his food and drink inside.'
    • 云恒有一女子著青裙白{須巾}，來就其寝 - Describes a woman in a blue skirt and white headscarf who came to his bed.
    • 每聽聞荐下有声，历历发之，见一青色白缨蚯蚓，长二尺许 - Describes the sound heard under the bed, which revealed a green and white-banded earthworm about two feet long.
    • 云此女常以一奁香见遗气甚清芬奁乃螺壳香则菖蒲根 - Describes the woman leaving a box of incense, which was very fragrant, the box being made of a snail shell, and the incense being made from the root of the sweet flag.
    • 于时咸谓双暂同阜螽矣 - Means 'at that time, everyone thought Wang Shuang was temporarily the same as a locust.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉 - The era name of Emperor Wen of Song, indicating the time period.
    • 建康 - The capital of the Eastern Jin and Southern Dynasties, now known as Nanjing.
    • 大夏营 - A military camp in Jiankang.
    • 华督 - A name that may refer to a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 护军府 - The Military Guard Office, an important administrative institution.
    • 建阳门 - A gate in the city walls of Jiankang.
    • 鼍 - A type of crocodile or alligator, often associated with mythical creatures in Chinese folklore.
    • 益州 - A region in southwestern China, roughly corresponding to modern-day Sichuan Province.
    • 王双 - A name that may refer to a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 菰蒋 - A type of grass, often used in traditional Chinese medicine.
    • 阜螽 - A type of locust, often used metaphorically to describe something that is fleeting or transient.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • The sentence structure is straightforward but requires careful translation to maintain the nuance of the claim.
    • The sentence structure is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the sequence of events.
    • The sentence is simple but requires careful translation to maintain the transformation.
    • The sentence is descriptive and requires careful translation to maintain the smoothness of the description.
    • The sentence is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the connection between the pond and the crocodile's den.
    • The sentence is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the cause and effect relationship.
    • The sentence is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the temporal and spatial context.
    • The sentence is descriptive and requires careful translation to maintain the unusual behavior.
    • The sentence is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the containment of food and drink.
    • The sentence is descriptive and requires careful translation to maintain the appearance of the woman.
    • The sentence is descriptive and requires careful translation to maintain the sequence of events and the appearance of the earthworm.
    • The sentence is descriptive and requires careful translation to maintain the details of the incense.
    • The sentence is clear but requires careful translation to maintain the metaphorical comparison.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元嘉 - The era name of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 建康 - The capital of the Eastern Jin and Southern Dynasties.
    • 大夏营 - A military camp in Jiankang.
    • 华督 - A name that may refer to a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 护军府 - The Military Guard Office.
    • 建阳门 - A gate in the city walls of Jiankang.
    • 鼍 - A type of crocodile or alligator.
    • 益州 - A region in southwestern China.
    • 王双 - A name that may refer to a historical figure or a fictional character.
    • 菰蒋 - A type of grass.
    • 阜螽 - A type of locust.

    During the early years of the reign of Emperor Wen (Yuanjia era), there was a widow named Yan living in the Daxia camp in Jiankang. Someone claimed that Hua Du had formed a good relationship with Yan. One night, a watchman saw a man walking towards the Military Guard Office, which was located inside the Jianyang Gate. The watchman shouted and asked him, and the man replied, "I am Hua Du going to the office, I entered along the west wall." The watchman attacked him for violating the curfew, and the man then turned into a crocodile. The watchman observed where he entered and exited, noting that the path was very smooth and connected to a pond in the courtyard, which previously had a crocodile's den. After a long time, it became a ghost, and it was only killed to stop it.

    During the early years of the reign of Emperor Wen (Yuanjia era), a man named Wang Shuang in Yizhou suddenly did not want to see the light. He often poured water on the ground and covered himself with grass while sleeping, putting all his food and drink inside. He claimed that a woman in a blue skirt and white headscarf came to his bed. Each time he heard a sound under the bed, he would investigate and see a green and white-banded earthworm about two feet long. The woman would often leave a box of incense, which was very fragrant, the box being made of a snail shell, and the incense being made from the root of the sweet flag. At that time, everyone thought Wang Shuang was temporarily the same as a locust. [86]

    河东常丑奴将一小儿湖边拔蒲，暮恒宿空田舍中。时日向暝，见一少女子姿容极美，乘小船载莼，径前投丑奴舍寄住，因卧，觉有臊气，女已知人意，便求出户外，变为獭。
    　　陈郡殷家养子名琅，与一婢结好。经年，婢死后，犹来往不绝，心绪昏错，其母深察焉。后夕见大蜘蛛形如斗样，缘床就琅，便宴尔怡悦。母取而杀之，琅性理遂复。
    　　元嘉十八年，广陵下市县人张方女道香送其夫婿北行，日暮宿祠门下，夜有一物假作其婿来，云离情难遣，不能便去。道香俄昏惑失常，时有海陵王纂者能疗邪，疑道香被魅，请治之，始下一针，有一獭从女被内走入前港，道香疾便愈。
    　　元嘉十九年，长山留元寂曾捕得一，剖腹复得一，又破之更获一，方见五脏。三虽相包怀而大小不殊。元寂不以为怪，以皮挂于屋后。其夜有群绕之，号呼失皮所在，元寂家亦无他。 [87]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [87]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 河东常丑奴将一小儿湖边拔蒲 - 常丑奴 is a proper name, and 拔蒲 means to pull up reeds or rushes. The phrase describes a man named Chang Chounu taking a child to a lake to pull reeds.
    • 暮恒宿空田舍中 - 暮 means evening or night, and 恒宿 means to stay overnight. 空田舍 refers to an empty field hut.
    • 觉有臊气 - 觉 means to feel or sense, and 臊气 refers to a foul smell, often associated with animals.
    • 女已知人意，便求出户外，变为獭 - 女已知人意 means the woman understood the man's thoughts, and 变为獭 means she transformed into a weasel.
    • 陈郡殷家养子名琅 - 陈郡 is a place name, 殷家 refers to the Yin family, and 养子 means adopted son.
    • 与一婢结好 - 与一婢 means with a servant girl, and 结好 means to form a close relationship.
    • 经年，婢死后，犹来往不绝 - 经年 means after a long time, and 犹来往不绝 means the visits continued without stopping.
    • 心绪昏错 - 心绪 means mental state, and 昏错 means confused or disoriented.
    • 其母深察焉 - 其母 means his mother, and 深察 means to deeply observe or understand.
    • 夜有一物假作其婿来 - 夜有一物 means something appeared at night, and 假作其婿 means pretending to be his husband.
    • 云离情难遣，不能便去 - 云 means to say, and 离情难遣 means the feeling of separation is hard to express.
    • 道香俄昏惑失常 - 俄 means suddenly, and 昏惑失常 means becoming confused and losing one's usual state.
    • 海陵王纂者能疗邪 - 海陵王纂 is a proper name, and 能疗邪 means able to treat evil spirits.
    • 疑道香被魅，请治之 - 疑 means to suspect, and 被魅 means to be bewitched.
    • 始下一针，有一獭从女被内走入前港 - 始下一针 means he started to prick a needle, and 走入前港 means it entered the front harbor.
    • 元嘉十八年，广陵下市县人张方女道香送其夫婿北行 - 元嘉十八年 is a specific year, 广陵 is a place name, and 送其夫婿北行 means to send her husband north.
    • 日暮宿祠门下 - 日暮 means at dusk, and 宿祠门下 means to stay under the temple gate.
    • 始下一针，有一獭从女被内走入前港 - 始下一针 means he started to prick a needle, and 走入前港 means it entered the front harbor.
    • 道香疾便愈 - 疾 means illness, and 愈 means to recover.
    • 元嘉十九年，长山留元寂曾捕得一獭 - 元嘉十九年 is a specific year, 长山 is a place name, and 捕得一獭 means to catch a weasel.
    • 剖腹复得一獭 - 剖腹 means to cut open the belly, and 复得一獭 means another weasel was found inside.
    • 又破之更获一獭 - 又破之 means to cut it open again, and 更获一獭 means to find another weasel.
    • 方见五脏 - 方见 means to finally see, and 五脏 means the five internal organs.
    • 三獭虽相包怀而大小不殊 - 三獭 means three weasels, 相包怀 means to contain each other, and 大小不殊 means they were of similar size.
    • 元寂不以为怪 - 不以为怪 means not to find it strange.
    • 以皮挂于屋后 - 以皮 means using the skin, and 挂于屋后 means hanging it behind the house.
    • 其夜有群獭绕之 - 其夜 means that night, and 绕之 means to surround it.
    • 号呼失皮所在 - 号呼 means to cry out, and 失皮所在 means to lose the location of the skin.
    • 元寂家亦无他 - 亦无他 means nothing else happened.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉十八年 - The 18th year of the Yuanjia era, a period during the Southern Song Dynasty (424-453 CE).
    • 元嘉十九年 - The 19th year of the Yuanjia era, a period during the Southern Song Dynasty (424-453 CE).
    • 广陵 - A city in present-day Jiangsu Province, China.
    • 长山 - A place name, possibly referring to a region in present-day Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 海陵王纂 - A historical figure, likely a physician or exorcist known for treating supernatural ailments.

    Proper Nouns

    • 常丑奴 - A proper name.
    • 陈郡殷家 - The Yin family from Chen Prefecture.
    • 张方女道香 - A woman named Dao Xiang from Guangling.

    A man from Hedong named Chang Chounu took a young boy to the lake to pull reeds, and they often stayed overnight in an empty field hut. One evening, as the sun was setting, he saw a beautiful young woman riding in a small boat carrying water chestnuts. She came directly to Chang Chounu's hut and asked to stay for the night. As they lay down, Chang Chounu sensed a foul smell, and the woman, sensing his thoughts, asked to go outside and transformed into a weasel.

    In Chen Prefecture, the Yin family had an adopted son named Lang, who formed a close relationship with a servant girl. After a long time, even after the girl's death, the visits continued without stopping. The boy became confused and disoriented, and his mother deeply observed the situation. One night, she saw a large spider shaped like a winnowing basket climbing onto the bed and approaching Lang, who then became joyful. The mother killed the spider, and Lang's mental state returned to normal.

    In the 18th year of the Yuanjia era, a woman named Dao Xiang from Guangling sent her husband north. At dusk, she stayed under the temple gate. That night, something appeared pretending to be her husband, saying that the feeling of separation was hard to express and that it could not leave immediately. Dao Xiang suddenly became confused and lost her usual state. There was a physician named Hai Ling Wang Zuan who could treat evil spirits, and he suspected that Dao Xiang was bewitched. He was called to treat her, and he started to prick a needle. A weasel entered the front harbor from under Dao Xiang's bedding, and she recovered from her illness.

    In the 19th year of the Yuanjia era, a man named Liu Yuanji from Changshan once caught a weasel. When he cut open its belly, he found another weasel inside. Cutting it open again, he found yet another weasel. Finally, he saw the five internal organs. The three weasels were contained within each other, but they were of similar size. Liu Yuanji did not find this strange and hung the skin behind his house. That night, a group of weasels surrounded the house, crying out and losing the location of the skin. Nothing else happened in Liu Yuanji's household. [87]

    余姚县仓封印完全，既而开之，觉大损耗。后伺之，乃是富阳县桓王陵上双石龟所食，即密令毁龟口，于是不复损耗。
    　　琅琊费县民家恒患失物，谓是偷者，每以扃钥为意，常周行宅内，后果见篱一穿穴，可容人臂，甚滑泽，有踪迹，乃作绳氏放穿穴口。夜中忽闻有摆扑声，往掩得一髻，长三尺许，从此无复所失。
    　　永康舒寿夫与同里猎于远山，群犬吠深茂处，异而看之，见树下有一老公，长可三尺，头须蒙然，面皱齿落，通身黄服，裁能动摇。因问为是何人，而来在此。直云“我有三女，姿容兼多伎艺，弹琴歌诗，闲究五典。”寿夫等共缚束令出女，公曰：“我女居深房洞庭之中，非自往呼，不可得来。请解我绳，当呼女也。”猎人犹不置。俄而变成一兽，黄色四足，其形似皋，又复似狐，头长三尺，额生一角，耳高于顶，面如故。寿夫等大惧，狼狈放解，倏忽失处。 [88]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [88]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 封印完全 - Formal expression meaning 'the seal was intact,' indicating that the seal on the granary was unbroken.
    • 大损耗 - Means 'significant loss,' referring to the substantial reduction in grain.
    • 伺之 - Means 'to spy on it' or 'to observe it secretly,' indicating that someone was watching to find out the cause of the loss.
    • 双石龟 - Refers to 'two stone turtles,' which are traditional symbols of longevity and stability in Chinese culture.
    • 密令毁龟口 - Means 'secretly ordered to destroy the turtle's mouth,' indicating a covert action taken to stop the loss.
    • 恒患失物 - Means 'always suffered from losing things,' indicating a recurring problem.
    • 扃钥为意 - Means 'to be concerned about locks and keys,' suggesting that the family was worried about theft.
    • 周行宅内 - Means 'to walk around the house,' indicating a thorough search.
    • 穿穴 - Means 'a hole,' referring to a small opening in the fence.
    • 可容人臂 - Means 'large enough to fit an arm,' describing the size of the hole.
    • 滑泽 - Means 'smooth and shiny,' describing the condition of the hole.
    • 踪迹 - Means 'tracks' or 'signs,' indicating the presence of something.
    • 绳氏放穿穴口 - Means 'to place a rope trap at the hole,' indicating a method used to catch the thief.
    • 忽闻摆扑声 - Means 'suddenly heard a flapping sound,' indicating a sudden noise.
    • 一髻 - Means 'a tuft of hair,' likely referring to a creature or spirit.
    • 倏忽失处 - Means 'disappeared suddenly,' indicating a quick disappearance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 余姚县 - Refers to Yuyao County, a place in Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 富阳县 - Refers to Fuyang County, another place in Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 桓王陵 - Refers to the tomb of a king named Huán, likely a historical figure.
    • 琅琊费县 - Refers to Feixian County in Linyi City, Shandong Province, China.
    • 远山 - Refers to a distant mountain, indicating a remote location.
    • 深茂处 - Means 'deep and dense place,' describing a secluded area.
    • 老公 - Means 'old man,' but in this context, it likely refers to a supernatural being.
    • 三女 - Means 'three daughters,' indicating the old man's family.
    • 姿容兼多伎艺 - Means 'beautiful appearance and many skills,' describing the daughters.
    • 弹琴歌诗 - Means 'play the zither and sing poetry,' indicating the daughters' talents.
    • 闲究五典 - Means 'study the five classics in leisure,' referring to the study of classical texts.
    • 永康 - A place name, likely referring to Yongkang City, Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 舒寿夫 - A personal name, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 猎 - Means 'hunt,' indicating the activity of hunting.
    • 群犬吠 - Means 'dogs bark,' indicating a disturbance.
    • 异而看之 - Means 'found it strange and looked at it,' indicating curiosity.
    • 通身黄服 - Means 'wearing a yellow robe all over,' describing the old man's appearance.
    • 裁能动摇 - Means 'barely able to move,' indicating the old man's limited mobility.
    • 缚束 - Means 'to tie up,' indicating the act of binding.
    • 倏忽 - Means 'suddenly,' indicating a quick action.
    • 黄服 - Means 'yellow robe,' often associated with nobility or deities in Chinese culture.
    • 皋 - A mythical creature, often depicted as a deer with antlers.
