    • 狐 - Means 'fox,' a common animal in Chinese folklore.
    • 额生一角 - Means 'a horn on the forehead,' indicating a unique feature.
    • 耳高于顶 - Means 'ears higher than the head,' indicating an unusual physical characteristic.
    • 面如故 - Means 'face as usual,' indicating that the face remained unchanged despite the other features.

    Proper Nouns

    • 余姚县 - Yuyao County, Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 富阳县 - Fuyang County, Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 桓王 - King Huán, a historical figure.
    • 琅琊费县 - Feixian County, Linyi City, Shandong Province, China.
    • 永康 - A place name, likely referring to Yongkang City, Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 舒寿夫 - A personal name, likely referring to a historical figure.

    The seal on the granary in Yuyao County was intact, but when it was opened, a significant loss of grain was discovered. After secretly observing the situation, it was found that the loss was caused by the two stone turtles at the tomb of King Huán in Fuyang County. The authorities then secretly ordered the destruction of the turtle's mouth, and the loss ceased.

    In Feixian County, Linyi City, Shandong Province, a family was constantly losing items, suspecting that they were being stolen. They were always concerned about locks and keys and would walk around their house thoroughly. Eventually, they found a hole in the fence that was large enough to fit an arm, smooth and shiny, with signs of activity. They placed a rope trap at the hole. One night, they suddenly heard a flapping sound and went to investigate, capturing a tuft of hair that was about three feet long. From then on, they no longer lost any items.

    In Yongkang, a man named Shu Shoufu and his fellow villagers went hunting in a distant mountain. Their dogs barked in a deep and dense area, and they found it strange and looked around. They saw an old man under a tree, about three feet tall, with a beard, wrinkled face, and missing teeth, wearing a yellow robe all over and barely able to move. They asked him who he was and why he was there. He simply said, "I have three daughters, beautiful and skilled in many arts, playing the zither and singing poetry, and studying the five classics in leisure." Shoufu and his companions tied him up and demanded that he bring out his daughters. The old man said, "My daughters live in a deep house in the middle of Dongting Lake. If you don't come to call them yourself, they won't come. Please untie me, and I will call them." The hunters still did not let him go. Suddenly, the old man transformed into a creature with yellow fur and four legs, resembling a deer with antlers and also a fox, with a head three feet long, a horn on the forehead, ears higher than the head, and a face unchanged. Shoufu and his companions were greatly frightened, hastily untied him, and he disappeared suddenly. [88]

    晋广州太守冯孝将男马子梦一女人，年十八九岁，言：“我乃前太守徐玄方女，不幸早亡。亡来四年，为鬼所枉杀。按生，乃寿至八十余。今听我更生，还为君妻。能从所委见救活否？”马子掘开棺视之，其女已活，遂为夫妇，生一男一女。
    　　鄢阳陈忠女名丰，邻人葛勃有美姿，丰与村中数女共聚络丝，戏相渭曰：“若得婿如葛勃，无所恨也。”（疑有缺）
    　　临海乐安章沉年二十余，死经数日，将敛而苏，云：被录到天曹，天曹主者是其外兄，断理得免。初到时有少年女子，同被录送，立住门外。女子见沉事散，知有力助，因泣涕脱金钏一只及臂上杂宝，托沉与主者求见救济。沉即为请之，并进钏物。良久出语，沉已论，秋英亦同遣去。秋英即此女之名也。于是俱去，脚痛疲顿，殊不堪行。会日亦暮，止道侧小窟，状如客舍，而不见主人。 [89]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [89]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 夢一女人，年十八九歲 - Dreamed of a woman, aged eighteen or nineteen.
    • 不幸早亡 - Unfortunate to have died young.
    • 為鬼所枉殺 - Killed unjustly by a ghost.
    • 按生，乃壽至八十余 - According to the records, she would have lived to be over eighty years old.
    • 今聽我更生，還為君妻 - Now allow me to be reborn and become your wife again.
    • 能從所委見救活否 - Can you help me be revived?
    • 掘開棺視之 - Dug open the coffin to see.
    • 遂為夫婦，生一男一女 - They became husband and wife, and had one son and one daughter.
    • 若得婿如葛勃，無所恨也 - If I could marry a man like Ge Bo, I would have no regrets.
    • 臨海樂安章沉年二十余，死經數日，將斂而蘇 - Zhang Chen from Le'an in Linhai, around twenty years old, died after several days and revived just before being buried.
    • 被錄到天曹 - Was taken to the Heavenly Court.
    • 天曹主者是其外兄 - The head of the Heavenly Court was his sister's husband.
    • 斷理得免 - Was judged and freed.
    • 同被錄送，立住門外 - Was taken together and stood outside the door.
    • 因泣涕脫金鐲一只及臂上雜寶 - She cried and removed a gold bracelet and other jewelry from her arm.
    • 托沉與主者求見救济 - Asked Zhang Chen to plead for her with the head of the Heavenly Court.
    • 沉即為請之，並進鐲物 - Zhang Chen pleaded for her and presented the jewelry.
    • 良久出語，沉已論，秋英亦同遣去 - After a long time, the head of the Heavenly Court spoke, and Zhang Chen was already being discussed, so Qiu Ying was also sent away.
    • 秋英即此女之名也 - Qiu Ying is the name of this woman.
    • 於是俱去，腳痛疲頓，殊不堪行 - They then left together, their feet aching and exhausted, unable to walk.
    • 會日亦暮，止道側小窟，狀如客舍，而見不到主人 - It happened to be evening, and they stopped at a small cave by the road, which looked like an inn but had no owner.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋广州太守冯孝将 - Feng Xiaojiang, the Governor of Guangzhou during the Jin dynasty.
    • 鄢阳陈忠 - Chen Zhong from Yanyang, a place name.
    • 临海乐安章沉 - Zhang Chen from Le'an in Linhai, a place name.
    • 天曹 - The Heavenly Court, a concept in Chinese mythology where the dead are judged.
    • 金鐲 - A gold bracelet, a type of jewelry.
    • 雜寶 - Various pieces of jewelry or valuable items.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋 - The Jin dynasty.
    • 广州 - A province in southern China.
    • 冯孝将 - Feng Xiaojiang, the Governor of Guangzhou.
    • 马子 - Ma Zi, the son of Feng Xiaojiang.
    • 鄢阳 - Yanyang, a place name.
    • 陈忠 - Chen Zhong, a person from Yanyang.
    • 丰 - Feng, the name of Chen Zhong's daughter.
    • 葛勃 - Ge Bo, a neighbor with a handsome appearance.
    • 临海 - Linhai, a place name.
    • 乐安 - Le'an, a place name.
    • 章沉 - Zhang Chen, a person from Le'an in Linhai.
    • 天曹 - The Heavenly Court.
    • 秋英 - Qiu Ying, the name of the woman.

    During the Jin dynasty, the son of Governor Feng Xiaojiang of Guangzhou, named Ma Zi, dreamed of a woman who was about eighteen or nineteen years old. She said, "I am the daughter of the former Governor Xu Xuanfang, who unfortunately died young. I have been dead for four years, killed unjustly by a ghost. According to the records, I would have lived to be over eighty years old. Now allow me to be reborn and become your wife again. Can you help me be revived?" Ma Zi dug open the coffin and found that the woman was already alive. They became husband and wife and had one son and one daughter.

    Chen Zhong's daughter from Yanyang, named Feng, had a neighbor named Ge Bo, who had a handsome appearance. Feng and several other village girls gathered to spin thread together and joked, saying, "If I could marry a man like Ge Bo, I would have no regrets." (There may be a missing part here.)

    Zhang Chen from Le'an in Linhai, around twenty years old, died after several days and revived just before being buried. He said he was taken to the Heavenly Court, where the head of the court was his sister's husband, who judged him and freed him. When he first arrived, there was a young woman who was also taken there and stood outside the door. The woman saw Zhang Chen and knew he had the power to help, so she cried and removed a gold bracelet and other jewelry from her arm, asking Zhang Chen to plead for her with the head of the Heavenly Court. Zhang Chen pleaded for her and presented the jewelry. After a long time, the head of the Heavenly Court spoke, and Zhang Chen was already being discussed, so Qiu Ying, the name of this woman, was also sent away. They then left together, their feet aching and exhausted, unable to walk. It happened to be evening, and they stopped at a small cave by the road, which looked like an inn but had no owner. [89]

    沉共宿燕接，更相问次，女曰：“我姓徐，家在吴县乌门，临渎为居，门前倒枣树即是也。”明晨各去，遂并活。沉先为护府军吏，依假出都，经吴乃到乌门，依此寻索，得徐氏舍，与主人叙阔，问：“秋英何在？”主人云：“女初不出入，君何知其名？”沉因说昔日魂相见之由，秋英先说之，所言因得。主人乃悟，甚羞，不及寝燕之事，而其邻人或知，以语徐氏。徐氏试令侍婢数人递出示沉，沉曰：“非也。”乃令秋英见之，则如旧识，徐氏谓为天意，遂以妻沉，生子名曰天赐。 [90]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [90]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 共宿燕接 - Ambiguous due to the character , likely a typo. Interpreted as '共宿燕接' meaning co-sleeping and meeting.
    • 更相问次 - Means taking turns to ask each other, indicating a mutual questioning.
    • 明晨各去，遂并活 - The next morning, they parted ways, and thus both survived.
    • 依此寻索，得徐氏舍 - Following this, he searched and found the Xu family's residence.
    • 徐氏试令侍婢数人递出示沉 - Xu tried to show Chen several of his maidservants one by one.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 吴县乌门 - Refers to a specific location in ancient China, Wu County, Wumen.
    • 临渎为居 - Living by the river, a common setting in classical literature.
    • 护府军吏 - A military officer in the guard, indicating a position of some importance.
    • 依假出都 - Going to the capital on leave, suggesting a journey from a provincial area to the imperial capital.
    • 经吴乃到乌门 - Passing through Wu and arriving at Wumen, indicating a route through a significant region.
    • 天意 - Fate or destiny, a common theme in classical Chinese literature.

    Proper Nouns

    • 徐氏 - The Xu family.
    • 秋英 - Qiu Ying, the woman who appears in the story.
    • 沉 - Chen, the main character.
    • 天赐 - Tianci, the son born to Chen and Qiu Ying.

    During the night, they co-slept and met, taking turns to ask each other questions. The woman said, "My surname is Xu, and my home is in Wu County, Wumen, living by the river. The tree in front of the door is an upside-down jujube tree, that's it." The next morning, they parted ways, and thus both survived. Chen was previously a military officer in the guard and went to the capital on leave, passing through Wu and then arriving at Wumen. Following this, he searched and found the Xu family's residence. He talked with the host about their separation and asked, "Where is Qiu Ying?" The host said, "The girl never goes out. How do you know her name?" Chen then explained the reason for their meeting in the past. Qiu Ying had already told him, and as a result, he knew. The host then realized and was very ashamed, not mentioning the co-sleeping and meeting. But some of his neighbors knew and told Xu. Xu tried to show Chen several of his maidservants one by one, but Chen said, "No." Then he had Qiu Ying show herself, and she was recognized as an old acquaintance. Xu believed it was fate and married Chen. They had a son named Tianci. [90]

    瞽瞍生舜征在生孔子，其有胎教也哉。妇人妊孕，未满三月，著婿衣冠，平旦左绕井三匝，映井水详观影而去，勿返顾，勿令婿见，必生男。
    　　晋安帝义熙中，魏兴李宣妻樊氏怀妊过期不孕，而额上有疮，儿穿之以出，长为将，今犹存，名胡儿。
    　　元嘉中，高平平邱孝妇怀妊，生一团冰，得日便消液成水。
    　　魏郡徐逮字君及，妇昌平孟氏生儿，头有一角、一脚，头正仰向，通身尽赤，落地无臂，乘虚而去。
    　　太原温盘石母怀身三年然后生，堕地便坐而笑，覆面，牙齿皆具。
    　　丹阳县庆妇生一男、一虎、一，、虎毛色斑黑，牙爪皆备，即杀之，儿经六日死，母无他异。
    　　长山赵宣母妊，身如常而髀上痒，搔之成疮，儿从疮出，母子平安。
    　　刘毅讨桓修之，桓遣人擒得毅妻郭美送与玄，遂宠擅诸姬，有身。及玄败，郭还，遂产一儿、一鼠，毅怒杀儿，鼠走枯莽中。其后郭病死方殓，鼠忽来跳入棺内。 [91]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [91]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 瞽瞍生舜征在生孔子 - Introduces the topic of prenatal education by mentioning the parents of Shun and Confucius.
    • 著婿衣冠，平旦左绕井三匝，映井水详观影而去 - Describes a ritual believed to influence the fetus, involving dressing in the husband's clothing, walking around the well three times to the left, and looking at one's reflection in the well water.
    • 勿返顾，勿令婿见，必生男 - Instructions part of the ritual to ensure the fetus develops properly, emphasizing not to look back and not to let the husband see.
    • 晋安帝义熙中，魏兴李宣妻樊氏怀妊过期不孕，而额上有疮，儿穿之以出 - Describes an unusual pregnancy and birth, with the mother being pregnant for an extended period without giving birth and the child emerging through a sore on her forehead.
    • 元嘉中，高平平邱孝妇怀妊，生一团冰，得日便消液成水 - Describes another unusual birth, where a virtuous woman gave birth to a lump of ice that melted into water when exposed to sunlight.
    • 魏郡徐逮字君及，妇昌平孟氏生儿，头有一角、一脚，头正仰向，通身尽赤，落地无臂，乘虚而去 - Describes a child with physical anomalies, including one horn and one foot, facing upwards, entirely red, and without arms, who disappeared into thin air upon birth.
    • 太原温盘石母怀身三年然后生，堕地便坐而笑，覆面，牙齿皆具 - Describes an unusually long pregnancy and the unusual behavior and appearance of the child, who sat and laughed upon birth, covered her face, and had all her teeth.
    • 丹阳县庆妇生一男、一虎、一，、虎毛色斑黑，牙爪皆备 - Describes a multiple birth with unusual elements, including a boy, a tiger, and a leopard, all with spotted black fur and fully developed teeth and claws.
    • 即杀之，儿经六日死，母无他异 - Describes the aftermath of the birth, where the animals were killed, and the boy died six days later, while the mother remained unharmed.
    • 长山赵宣母妊，身如常而髀上痒，搔之成疮，儿从疮出 - Describes another unusual birth, where the mother scratched an itch on her thigh, which turned into a sore from which the child emerged.
    • 刘毅讨桓修之，桓遣人擒得毅妻郭美送与玄，遂宠擅诸姬，有身 - Provides the historical context for the birth, where Liu Yi was attacking Huán Xiū, and Huán sent someone to capture Liu's wife, Guo Měi, and send her to Xuán, who then favored her among his concubines, and she became pregnant.
    • 及玄败，郭还，遂产一儿、一鼠 - Describes the unusual birth, where Guo returned and gave birth to a boy and a rat.
    • 毅怒杀儿，鼠走枯莽中 - Describes the aftermath of the birth, where Liu Yi was angry and killed the boy, and the rat fled into the dry grass.
    • 其后郭病死方殓，鼠忽来跳入棺内 - Describes the final event involving the rat, where the rat suddenly jumped into Guo's coffin after she fell ill and died.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 瞽瞍 - The father of Shun, a legendary figure in Chinese history.
    • 征在 - The mother of Confucius, another legendary figure in Chinese history.
    • 晋安帝 - Emperor An of Jin, a ruler during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 义熙 - The era name of Emperor An of Jin.
    • 魏兴 - A place name, likely referring to a region during the Jin dynasty.
    • 李宣 - A historical figure, likely a local official or noble.
    • 樊氏 - The wife of Li Xuan.
    • 元嘉 - The era name of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 高平 - A place name, likely referring to a region during the Song dynasty.
    • 平邱 - A place name, likely referring to a region during the Song dynasty.
    • 孝妇 - A virtuous woman, often used in historical and literary contexts to denote a morally upright person.
    • 魏郡 - A place name, likely referring to a region during the Han or Jin dynasties.
    • 徐逮 - A historical figure, likely a local official or noble.
    • 君及 - The courtesy name of Xu Dai.
    • 昌平 - A place name, likely referring to a region during the Han or Jin dynasties.
    • 孟氏 - The wife of Xu Dai.
    • 太原 - A place name, likely referring to a region during the Han or Jin dynasties.
    • 温盘石 - A historical figure, likely a local official or noble.
    • 丹阳县 - A place name, likely referring to a region during the Han or Jin dynasties.
    • 庆妇 - The wife of Qìng.
    • 长山 - A place name, likely referring to a region during the Han or Jin dynasties.
    • 赵宣 - A historical figure, likely a local official or noble.
    • 刘毅 - A historical figure, likely a military leader or official.
    • 桓修 - A historical figure, likely a military leader or official.
    • 玄 - Likely referring to a ruler or high-ranking official.
    • 郭美 - The wife of Liu Yi.

    Syntactic Ambiguities

    • This sentence introduces the topic of prenatal education by mentioning the parents of Shun and Confucius.
    • This sentence describes a ritual believed to influence the fetus, with a series of actions and instructions.
    • This sentence describes an unusual pregnancy and birth, with a series of events and details.
    • This sentence describes another unusual birth, with a series of events and details.
    • This sentence describes a child with physical anomalies, with a series of details.
    • This sentence describes an unusually long pregnancy and the unusual behavior and appearance of the child, with a series of details.
    • This sentence describes a multiple birth with unusual elements, with a series of details.
    • This sentence describes the aftermath of the birth, with a series of events and details.
    • This sentence describes another unusual birth, with a series of events and details.
    • Provides the historical context for the birth, where Liu Yi was attacking Huán Xiū, and Huán sent someone to capture Liu's wife, Guo Měi, and send her to Xuán, who then favored her among his concubines, and she became pregnant.
    • Describes the unusual birth, where Guo returned and gave birth to a boy and a rat.
    • Describes the aftermath of the birth, where Liu Yi was angry and killed the boy, and the rat fled into the dry grass.
    • Describes the final event involving the rat, where the rat suddenly jumped into Guo's coffin after she fell ill and died.

    Prenatal Education and Unusual Births

    Gushou gave birth to Shun, and Zhengzai gave birth to Confucius; is this not prenatal education? When a woman is pregnant for less than three months, she should wear her husband's clothing, early in the morning, walk around the well three times to the left, and look at her reflection in the well water before leaving. She must not look back, and must not let the husband see, and it will definitely produce a son.

    During the reign of Emperor An of Jin in the Yixi era, Li Xuan's wife, Fan, from Wei Xing, was pregnant for an extended period without giving birth. She had a sore on her forehead, and the child emerged through it. He grew up to be a general and still exists today, known as Hu'er.

    During the Yuanchang era, a virtuous woman from Pingqiu in Gaoping was pregnant and gave birth to a lump of ice, which melted into water when exposed to sunlight.

    Xu Dai from Wei Prefecture, whose courtesy name was Junji, had a wife from Changping named Meng who gave birth to a child with one horn and one foot, facing upwards, entirely red, and without arms, who disappeared into thin air upon birth.

    Wen Panshi's mother from Taiyuan was pregnant for three years before giving birth. Upon birth, she sat and laughed, covered her face, and had all her teeth.

    The wife of Qìng from Danyang gave birth to a boy, a tiger, and a leopard. The leopard and tiger had spotted black fur and fully developed teeth and claws. They were immediately killed, and the boy died six days later, while the mother remained unharmed.

    Zhaò Xuān's mother from Chángshān was pregnant, and although her body was normal, she scratched an itch on her thigh, which turned into a sore from which the child emerged. The mother and child were both safe.

    Liu Yi was attacking Huán Xiū, and Huán sent someone to capture Liu's wife, Guo Měi, and send her to Xuán, who then favored her among his concubines, and she became pregnant. After Xuán's defeat, Guo returned and gave birth to a boy and a rat. Liu Yi was angry and killed the boy, and the rat fled into the dry grass. Later, when Guo fell ill and died, the rat suddenly jumped into her coffin. [91]

    元嘉中，沛国武漂之妻林氏怀身，得病而死。俗忌含胎入柩中，要须割出。妻乳母伤痛之，乃抚尸而祝曰：“若天道有灵，无令死被擘裂！”须臾尺面赧然上色，于是呼婢共扶之，俄顷儿堕而尸倒。
    　　汉末大乱，宫人小黄门上墓树上避兵，食松柏实，遂不复饥，举体生毛，长尺许。乱离既平，魏武闻而收养，还食谷，齿落头白。
    　　吴天门张某冬月与村人共猎，见大树下有蓬庵，似寝息处而无烟火。须臾见一人，形长七尺，毛而不衣，负数头死猿。与语不应，因将归，闭空屋中十余日，复送故处。
    　　晋咸宁中，鄱阳乐安有人姓彭，世以射猎为业。每入山，与子俱行。后忽蹶然而倒，化成白鹿，儿悲号，鹿跳跃远去，遂失所在。其子终身不复弋猎。至孙，复习其事。后忽射一白鹿，乃于两角间得道家七星符，并有其祖姓名及乡居年月在焉。睹之悔懊，乃烧弓矢，永断射猎。 [92]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [92]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 元嘉中 - A period title indicating the reign of Emperor Wen of Song (424–453 CE).
    • 沛国武漂之妻 - Literally 'wife of Wu Piao of Pei State,' where '沛国' refers to Pei State, an ancient Chinese state, and '武漂' is the name of the husband.
    • 怀身 - Literally 'carrying a body,' referring to being pregnant.
    • 俗忌含胎入柩中 - A cultural taboo against burying a pregnant woman in a coffin with the fetus intact, requiring the fetus to be removed.
    • 要须割出 - Literally 'must be cut out,' referring to the removal of the fetus.
    • 尺面赧然上色 - A vivid description of the face turning red, indicating life returning to the fetus.
    • 呼婢共扶之 - Literally 'call the maid to help support it,' referring to the act of helping the fetus.
    • 俄顷儿堕而尸倒 - Literally 'in a moment, the child falls out, and the body collapses,' describing the sudden birth of the child.
    • 汉末大乱 - Refers to the end of the Han Dynasty, a period of widespread chaos and warfare.
    • 宫人小黄门上墓树上避兵 - Literally 'palace women and small yellow gate officials climbed a tomb tree to avoid soldiers,' referring to people seeking refuge during the chaos.
    • 食松柏实 - Literally 'ate pine and cypress seeds,' describing their survival method.
    • 举体生毛 - Literally 'all over the body grew hair,' describing the physical transformation of the individuals.
    • 魏武闻而收养 - Literally 'Cao Cao heard and took them in,' referring to Cao Cao's actions during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 齿落头白 - Literally 'teeth fell out and hair turned white,' describing the aging process.
    • 吴天门张某 - Refers to a man named Zhang from Tianmen, Wu State, an ancient Chinese state.
    • 冬月与村人共猎 - Literally 'in winter, he hunted with villagers,' describing the setting of the story.
    • 形长七尺 - Literally 'body length seven chi,' describing the height of the man seen.
    • 毛而不衣 - Literally 'hairy and not clothed,' describing the man's appearance.
    • 负数头死猿 - Literally 'carrying several dead monkeys,' describing what the man was carrying.
    • 晋咸宁中 - A period title indicating the reign of Emperor Hui of Jin (279–290 CE).
    • 鄱阳乐安 - Refers to a place in ancient China.
    • 姓彭 - Literally 'surname Peng,' referring to the man's family name.
    • 射猎为业 - Literally 'hunting as a profession,' describing his occupation.
    • 蹶然而倒 - Literally 'suddenly fell down,' describing the transformation.
    • 白鹿 - Literally 'white deer,' describing the form he took.
    • 悲号 - Literally 'cried out in sorrow,' describing the son's reaction.
    • 跳跃远去 - Literally 'jumped far away,' describing the white deer's departure.
    • 遂失所在 - Literally 'and lost his whereabouts,' indicating that the location was no longer known.
    • 终身不复弋猎 - Literally 'for life, he no longer hunted,' describing the son's change in behavior.
    • 复习其事 - Literally 'repeated the same practice,' describing the grandson's actions.
    • 射一白鹿 - Literally 'shot a white deer,' describing the grandson's action.
    • 两角间得道家七星符 - Literally 'found a Daoist seven-star talisman between the horns,' describing what was discovered.
    • 并有其祖姓名及乡居年月在焉 - Literally 'and had his grandfather's name, hometown, and residence date written on it,' providing additional details.
    • 睹之悔懊 - Literally 'seeing it, he felt regret,' describing the emotional response.
    • 烧弓矢 - Literally 'burned the bow and arrows,' describing the act of destruction.
    • 永断射猎 - Literally 'permanently stopped hunting,' indicating a permanent change in behavior.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 元嘉中 - The reign of Emperor Wen of Song, a significant period in the Southern Dynasties.
    • 沛国 - An ancient Chinese state, now part of modern-day Henan Province.
    • 汉末大乱 - The end of the Han Dynasty, a period marked by civil war and political turmoil.
    • 宫人小黄门 - Palace women and small yellow gate officials, roles in the imperial court.
    • 魏武 - Refers to Cao Cao, a prominent warlord and statesman during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 吴天门 - Refers to Tianmen, a place in Wu State, an ancient Chinese state.
    • 晋咸宁中 - The reign of Emperor Hui of Jin, a period in the Western Jin Dynasty.
    • 鄱阳乐安 - A place in ancient China, now part of modern-day Jiangxi Province.
    • 射猎为业 - Hunting as a profession, a common occupation in ancient China.
    • 道家七星符 - A Daoist talisman, a symbol believed to have magical properties.

    Proper Nouns

    • 元嘉中 - The reign of Emperor Wen of Song.
    • 沛国 - An ancient Chinese state.
    • 武漂 - The name of the husband.
    • 林氏 - The name of the wife.
    • 魏武 - Cao Cao, a prominent warlord and statesman.
    • 吴天门 - Tianmen, a place in Wu State.
    • 张某 - The name of the man.
    • 晋咸宁中 - The reign of Emperor Hui of Jin.
    • 鄱阳乐安 - A place in ancient China.
    • 彭 - The surname of the main character.
    • 道家七星符 - A Daoist talisman.

    During the reign of Emperor Wen of Song, the wife of Wu Piao from Pei State, named Lin, was pregnant and fell ill, dying as a result. It was a custom to avoid burying a pregnant woman with the fetus intact, so the fetus had to be removed. Her wet nurse, grieving the loss, comforted her and prayed, saying, "If there is a divine presence, do not let the dead be torn apart!" Soon after, the face of the fetus turned red, and she called a maid to help support it. In a moment, the child fell out, and the body collapsed.

    At the end of the Han Dynasty, during the chaos, palace women and small yellow gate officials climbed a tomb tree to avoid soldiers, eating pine and cypress seeds, and thus they no longer felt hungry. Their bodies grew hair, about a chi long. When the turmoil subsided, Cao Cao heard of this and took them in, and they resumed eating grain, their teeth falling out and their hair turning white.

    In winter, Zhang from Tianmen in Wu State went hunting with villagers and saw a thatched hut under a large tree, which seemed like a place for rest but had no smoke or fire. Soon, he saw a man seven chi tall, covered in hair but not wearing clothes, carrying several dead monkeys. When spoken to, the man did not respond, so Zhang took him home, locked him in an empty room for over ten days, and then returned him to his original place.

    During the reign of Emperor Hui of Jin, in Le'an of Poyang, there was a man surnamed Peng whose family made a living from hunting. Whenever he went into the mountains, he would take his son with him. One day, he suddenly fell down and transformed into a white deer. His son cried out in sorrow, and the deer jumped away, disappearing into the distance. The son never hunted again for the rest of his life. His grandson, however, repeated the same practice. One day, he shot a white deer and found a Daoist seven-star talisman between its horns, which also bore his grandfather's name, hometown, and residence date. Upon seeing this, he felt regret and burned his bow and arrows, permanently giving up hunting. [92]

    晋太康中，荥阳郑袭为广陵太守，门下驺忽如狂，奄失其所在。经日寻得，裸身呼吟，肤血淋漓，问其故，云：社公令其作虎，以斑皮衣之。辞以执鞭之士，不堪虎跃，神怒，还使剥皮，皮已著肉，疮毁惨痛，旬日乃差。
    　　晋时豫章郡吏易拔义熙中受番还家，远遁不返。郡遣追，见拔言语如常，亦为设食，使者催令束装，拔因语曰：“汝看我面。”乃见眼目角张，身有黄斑色，便竖一足，径出门去家。先依山为居，至林麓即变成三足大虎，所竖一足即成其尾也。
    　　晋太元十九年，鄱阳桓阐杀犬祭乡里绥山。煮肉不熟，神怒，即下教于巫曰：“桓阐以肉生贻我，当谪令自食也。”其年忽变作虎，作虎之始，见人以斑皮衣之，即能跳跃噬逐。 [93]

    During the Tai Kang period of the Jin Dynasty, Zheng Xi of Xingyang was the governor of Guangling. One day, a servant in his household suddenly went wild and vanished without a trace. After a day of searching, he was found naked and moaning, with blood dripping from his skin. When asked what had happened, he said that the local deity had ordered him to become a tiger and clothed him in spotted fur. He refused, saying that as a servant, he was not fit to leap like a tiger, and the deity became angry, ordering him to be stripped of his skin. His skin was already attached to his flesh, and he suffered from painful wounds, taking ten days to recover.

    During the Jin Dynasty, Yi Ba, an official of the Yuzhang commandery, was on leave and returned home during the Yixi period. He fled far away and did not return. The commandery sent someone to pursue him. When they found him, he spoke as usual, and they set up food for him. The messenger urged him to pack up, and Yi Ba said, "Look at my face." They saw that his eyes and pupils were dilated, his body had yellow spots, and he raised one foot, then went out of the door and left the house. He first lived by the mountain and, when he reached the forest, turned into a three-legged big tiger. The raised foot became his tail.

    In the 19th year of the Taiyuan era of the Jin Dynasty, Huan Chan of Poyang killed a dog to sacrifice to the local deity of Suishan. The meat was not fully cooked, and the deity became angry, issuing an order to the shaman: "Huan Chan offered raw meat to me; he should be punished and forced to eat it himself." That year, Huan Chan suddenly transformed into a tiger. At the beginning of his transformation, he saw a person clothed him in spotted fur and immediately could leap, bite, and chase. [93]

    胡道洽者，自云广陵人，好音乐、医术之事，体有臊气，恒以名香自防，唯忌猛犬。自审死日，诫子弟曰：“气绝便殡，勿令狗见我尸也。”死于山阳，殓毕，觉棺空，即开看，不见尸体，时人咸谓狐也。
    　　元嘉三年，邵陵高平黄秀无故入山，经日不还。其儿根生寻觅，见秀蹲空树中，从头至腰，毛色如熊。问其何故，答云：“天谪我如此，汝但自去。”儿哀恸而归。逾年伐山人见之，其形尽为熊矣。
    　　陈郡谢石字石奴。太元中，少患面疮，诸治莫愈。梦日环其城，乃自匿远山，卧于岩下，中宵有物来舐其疮，随舐随除。既不见形，意为是龙，而舐处悉白，故世呼为谢白面。
    　　元嘉中，章安有人瞰鸭肉，乃成瘕病，胸满面赤，不得饮食，医令服秫米渖，须臾烦闷，吐一鸭雏，身啄翅皆已成就，惟左脚故缀昔所食肉，病遂获差。
    　　山阴有人尝食牛肉，左髀便作牛鸣，每劳辄剧食，乃止。 [94]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [94]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 自云 - Phrase meaning 'claims to be' or 'says he is', indicating self-identification.
    • 好音乐、医术之事 - Literally 'likes music and medical arts', referring to a person's interests.
    • 体有臊气 - Means 'has a foul odor', describing a physical characteristic.
    • 恒以名香自防 - Means 'always uses fragrant incense to protect himself', indicating a habitual practice.
    • 唯忌猛犬 - Means 'only fears fierce dogs', highlighting a specific phobia.
    • 自审死日 - Means 'foresees his own death day', indicating a premonition.
    • 气绝便殡 - Means 'when the breath stops, immediately bury', instructing immediate burial.
    • 无令狗见我尸也 - Means 'do not let dogs see my body', a specific request.
    • 殓毕 - Means 'after the funeral rites', indicating the completion of the burial process.
    • 棺空 - Means 'the coffin is empty', describing an unexpected event.
    • 开看 - Means 'open to see', indicating an action taken upon discovering the empty coffin.
    • 不见尸体 - Means 'cannot see the body', describing the absence of the body.
    • 时人咸谓狐也 - Means 'people at the time all said it was a fox', indicating a common belief.
    • 元嘉三年 - Refers to the year 426 AD, a specific historical date.
    • 邵陵高平黄秀 - Refers to a specific person, Huang Xiu, from a specific place.
    • 经日不还 - Means 'after many days did not return', indicating a prolonged absence.
    • 其儿根生寻觅 - Means 'his son Gen Sheng searched for him', indicating an action taken by the son.
    • 见秀蹲空树中 - Means 'saw Xiu crouching in an empty tree', describing a specific scene.
    • 从头至腰，毛色如熊 - Means 'from head to waist, the fur color was like a bear', describing a physical transformation.
    • 天谪我如此 - Means 'heaven has punished me like this', indicating a belief in divine retribution.
    • 汝但自去 - Means 'you just go away', indicating a command.
    • 哀恸而归 - Means 'grieved and returned home', describing the son's emotional state.
    • 逾年 - Means 'after a year', indicating a specific time period.
    • 伐山人见之 - Means 'people cutting wood in the mountains saw him', indicating a specific group of people.
    • 其形尽为熊矣 - Means 'his form had completely turned into a bear', describing the final transformation.
    • 陈郡谢石字石奴 - Refers to a specific person, Xie Shi, from a specific place.
    • 太元中 - Refers to the period of the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, around 376-396 AD.
    • 少患面疮 - Means 'suffered from facial sores as a young man', indicating a specific medical condition.
    • 诸治莫愈 - Means 'all treatments failed to cure', indicating the persistence of the condition.
    • 梦日环其城 - Means 'dreamed of the sun circling his city', describing a specific dream.
    • 自匿远山 - Means 'hid in a distant mountain', indicating an action taken.
    • 卧于岩下 - Means 'lay down under a rock', describing a specific location.
    • 中宵有物来舐其疮 - Means 'in the middle of the night, something came to lick his sores', describing a specific event.
    • 随舐随除 - Means 'as it licked, the sores disappeared', describing the effect of the licking.
    • 既不见形 - Means 'it did not appear in form', indicating the invisibility of the creature.
    • 意为是龙 - Means 'he thought it was a dragon', indicating a belief.
    • 而舐处悉白 - Means 'and the licked areas turned white', describing the result of the licking.
    • 故世呼为谢白面 - Means 'so the world called him Xie Bai Mian', indicating a nickname.
    • 章安有人瞰鸭肉 - Means 'someone in Zhang'an ate duck meat', indicating a specific event.
    • 乃成瘕病 - Means 'and it turned into a tumor disease', describing a medical condition.
    • 胸满面赤 - Means 'chest full, face red', describing physical symptoms.
    • 不得饮食 - Means 'could not eat or drink', indicating a severe condition.
    • 医令服秫米渖 - Means 'the doctor ordered him to drink barley water', indicating a treatment.
    • 须臾烦闷 - Means 'in a short while, he became restless and uncomfortable', describing a reaction.
    • 吐一鸭雏 - Means 'vomited a duckling', describing a specific event.
    • 身啄翅皆已成就 - Means 'the body, beak, and wings were all fully formed', describing the appearance of the duckling.
    • 惟左脚故缀昔所食肉 - Means 'only the left foot still attached to the meat he had eaten', describing a specific detail.
    • 病遂获差 - Means 'the disease was then cured', indicating the outcome.
    • 山阴有人尝食牛肉 - Means 'someone in Shanyin once ate beef', indicating a specific event.
    • 左髀便作牛鸣 - Means 'his left thigh then made a cow's moo', describing a specific symptom.
    • 每劳辄剧食 - Means 'every time he was tired, it worsened', indicating a recurring issue.
    • 乃止 - Means 'then stopped', indicating the resolution of the issue.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 胡道洽 - A specific historical figure, whose name and origin are mentioned.
    • 广陵 - Refers to a specific place, Guangling, in present-day Jiangsu Province.
    • 元嘉三年 - Refers to the year 426 AD, a specific historical date.
    • 邵陵高平 - Refers to a specific place, Shao Ling Gao Ping, in present-day Hunan Province.
    • 元嘉 - Refers to the reign title of Emperor Wen of Liu Song, 424-453 AD.
    • 太元 - Refers to the reign title of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, 376-396 AD.
    • 陈郡 - Refers to a specific place, Chen County, in present-day Henan Province.
    • 章安 - Refers to a specific place, Zhang'an, in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 山阴 - Refers to a specific place, Shanyin, in present-day Zhejiang Province.
    • 五经 - Refers to the Five Classics: the *Shijing* (Book of Poetry), *Shujing* (Book of Documents), *Yijing* (Book of Changes), *Liji* (Book of Rites), and *Chunqiu* (Spring and Autumn Annals).
    • 朝廷宪章 - Refers to the laws and regulations of the government.
    • 军旅誓诰 - Refers to military oaths and proclamations.
    • 陶冶性灵 - A metaphor of smelting and shaping one's inner nature.
    • 从容讽谏 - Means to offer remonstrance in an indirect, measured, and graceful way.
    • 行有余力，则可习之 - Means that one may study literary writing after fulfilling more basic duties.

    Huo Daoqia, who claimed to be from Guangling, had a fondness for music and medical arts. He had a foul odor about him and always used fragrant incense to protect himself, except that he feared fierce dogs. Foreseeing his own death day, he instructed his descendants: "When my breath stops, immediately bury me; do not let dogs see my body." He died in Shanyang, and after the funeral rites were completed, it was discovered that the coffin was empty. Upon opening it, no body was found, and people at the time all said it was a fox.

    In the third year of Yuanchang (426 AD), a man named Huang Xiu from Shao Ling Gao Ping entered the mountains without reason and did not return after many days. His son Gen Sheng searched for him and saw Xiu crouching in an empty tree, from head to waist, his fur color was like a bear. When asked why, Xiu replied, "Heaven has punished me like this; you just go away." The son grieved and returned home. After a year, people cutting wood in the mountains saw him, and his form had completely turned into a bear.

    Xie Shi, from Chen County, was known as Shi Nu. During the reign of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin (376-396 AD), he suffered from facial sores as a young man, and all treatments failed to cure them. He dreamed of the sun circling his city and hid in a distant mountain, lying down under a rock. In the middle of the night, something came to lick his sores, and as it licked, the sores disappeared. Since it did not appear in form, he thought it was a dragon, and the licked areas turned white. So the world called him Xie Bai Mian.

    During the reign of Emperor Wen of Liu Song (424-453 AD), someone in Zhang'an ate duck meat, and it turned into a tumor disease, causing his chest to be full and his face to turn red, making him unable to eat or drink. The doctor ordered him to drink barley water, and in a short while, he became restless and uncomfortable, vomiting a duckling. The body, beak, and wings were all fully formed, but only the left foot still attached to the meat he had eaten. The disease was then cured.

    In Shanyin, someone once ate beef, and his left thigh then made a cow's moo. Every time he was tired, it worsened, and then stopped. [94]

    有人误吞发便得病，但欲咽猪脂，张口时见喉中有一头出，受膏乃取小钩为饵，而引得一物，长三尺余，其形似蛇而悉是猪脂。悬于屋间，旬日融尽，惟发在焉。

    　　●卷九
    　　后汉郑玄字康成，师马融三载无闻，融鄙而遣还。玄过树阴假寝，梦一老父以刀开腹心，倾墨汁着内，曰：“子可以学矣。”于是寤而即返，遂精洞典籍，融叹曰：“诗书礼乐皆已东矣。”潜欲杀玄，玄知而窃去，融推式以玄，玄当在土木上，躬骑马袭之，玄入一桥下，俯伏柱上。融踟蹰桥侧，云：“土木之间，此则当矣。有水，非也。”从此而归。玄用免焉。一说玄在马融门下，三年不相见，高足弟子传授而已。常浑天不合，问诸弟子，弟子莫能解。或言玄，融召令，一转便决，众咸骇服。及玄业成，辞归。融心忌焉，玄亦疑有追者，乃坐桥下，在水上，据屐。融果转式逐之，告左右曰：“玄在土下水上，而据木，此必死矣。”遂罢追，玄竟以免。 [95]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [95]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 有人误吞发便得病 - Literal phrase meaning 'someone mistakenly swallowed hair and then fell ill.'
    • 但欲咽猪脂 - Means 'only wanted to swallow pig fat.'
    • 张口时见喉中有一头出 - Means 'when opening their mouth, they saw a head emerge from their throat.'
    • 受膏乃取小钩为饵 - Means 'after being coated with fat, they took a small hook as bait.'
    • 引得一物，长三尺余，其形似蛇而悉是猪脂 - Means 'they caught something, about three feet long, which looked like a snake but was entirely made of pig fat.'
    • 悬于屋间，旬日融尽，惟发在焉 - Means 'hung in the room, it melted away in ten days, leaving only the hair.'
    • 后汉郑玄字康成 - Means 'Vang Hsuan of the Later Han, styled Kangcheng.'
    • 师马融三载无闻 - Means 'studied under Ma Rong for three years without achieving fame.'
    • 融鄙而遣还 - Means 'Ma Rong looked down on him and sent him back.'
    • 玄过树阴假寝 - Means 'Vang Hsuan passed by the shade of a tree and took a nap.'
    • 梦一老父以刀开腹心，倾墨汁着内 - Means 'dreamed of an old man using a knife to open his abdomen and pour ink inside.'
    • 子可以学矣 - Means 'you can now learn.'
    • 寤而即返，遂精洞典籍 - Means 'awakening, he immediately returned and then became thoroughly versed in the classics.'
    • 融叹曰：“诗书礼乐皆已东矣。” - Means 'Ma Rong sighed and said, 'The *Poetry*, *Book of Documents*, *Rites*, and *Music* have all moved eastward.'
    • 潜欲杀玄 - Means 'secretly planned to kill Vang Hsuan.'
    • 玄知而窃去 - Means 'Vang Hsuan knew and secretly left.'
    • 融推式以玄 - Means 'Ma Rong used divination to track Vang Hsuan.'
    • 玄当在土木上，躬骑马袭之 - Means 'Vang Hsuan should be on a wooden structure, so Ma Rong personally rode a horse to attack him.'
    • 玄入一桥下，俯伏柱上 - Means 'Vang Hsuan went under a bridge and crouched on a pillar.'
    • 融踟蹰桥侧，云：“土木之间，此则当矣。有水，非也。” - Means 'Ma Rong hesitated by the side of the bridge, saying, 'Between earth and wood, this is where he should be. But there is water, so it's not right.'
    • 从此而归 - Means 'and then returned.'
    • 玄用免焉 - Means 'Vang Hsuan thus escaped.'
    • 一说玄在马融门下，三年不相见，高足弟子传授而已 - Means 'Another account says that Vang Hsuan was in Ma Rong's school for three years without seeing him, learning only from the top students.'
    • 常浑天不合，问诸弟子，弟子莫能解 - Means 'He often found the *浑天仪* (a type of astronomical instrument) confusing and asked his students, but none could explain it.'
    • 或言玄，融召令，一转便决，众咸骇服 - Means 'Someone mentioned Vang Hsuan, and Ma Rong summoned him to explain, and with one turn, he solved it, leaving everyone amazed and impressed.'
    • 及玄业成，辞归 - Means 'When Vang Hsuan completed his studies, he requested to return home.'
    • 融心忌焉，玄亦疑有追者 - Means 'Ma Rong felt jealous, and Vang Hsuan also suspected there might be pursuers.'
    • 乃坐桥下，在水上，据屐 - Means 'So he sat under a bridge, on the water, holding his shoes.'
    • 融果转式逐之，告左右曰：“玄在土下水上，而据木，此必死矣。” - Means 'Ma Rong indeed used divination to pursue him, telling his attendants, 'Vang Hsuan is below earth and above water, holding wood, so he must die.'
    • 遂罢追，玄竟以免 - Means 'So he gave up the pursuit, and Vang Hsuan was ultimately spared.'

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 后汉 - Refers to the Later Han Dynasty (25–220 CE).
    • 郑玄 - A famous scholar and commentator of the Later Han Dynasty.
    • 马融 - A renowned scholar and teacher of the Later Han Dynasty.
    • 诗书礼乐 - Refers to the *Poetry*, *Book of Documents*, *Rites*, and *Music*, which are part of the Confucian canon.
    • 浑天仪 - An ancient Chinese astronomical instrument used to model the heavens.
    • 桥下，据屐 - Describes a specific hiding place and posture, indicating a strategic escape.

    Proper Nouns

    • 后汉 - The Later Han Dynasty.
    • 郑玄 - Vang Hsuan, a famous scholar and commentator.
    • 马融 - Ma Rong, a renowned scholar and teacher.
    • 诗书礼乐 - The *Poetry*, *Book of Documents*, *Rites*, and *Music*.
    • 浑天仪 - An ancient Chinese astronomical instrument.
    • 桥下，据屐 - Specific hiding place and posture.

    Someone mistakenly swallowed a hair and then fell ill, only wanting to swallow pig fat. When they opened their mouth, they saw a head emerge from their throat. After being coated with fat, they took a small hook as bait and caught something about three feet long, which looked like a snake but was entirely made of pig fat. They hung it in the room, and it melted away in ten days, leaving only the hair.

    ● Chapter Nine

    Vang Hsuan of the Later Han, styled Kangcheng, studied under Ma Rong for three years without achieving fame. Ma Rong looked down on him and sent him back. Vang Hsuan passed by the shade of a tree and took a nap, dreaming of an old man using a knife to open his abdomen and pour ink inside, saying, "You can now learn." Awakening, he immediately returned and then became thoroughly versed in the classics. Ma Rong sighed and said, "The Poetry, Book of Documents, Rites, and Music have all moved eastward." He secretly planned to kill Vang Hsuan, but Vang Hsuan knew and secretly left. Ma Rong used divination to track Vang Hsuan, who should be on a wooden structure. Ma Rong personally rode a horse to attack him. Vang Hsuan went under a bridge and crouched on a pillar. Ma Rong hesitated by the side of the bridge, saying, "Between earth and wood, this is where he should be. But there is water, so it's not right." And then he returned. Vang Hsuan thus escaped. Another account says that Vang Hsuan was in Ma Rong's school for three years without seeing him, learning only from the top students. He often found the 浑天仪 (a type of astronomical instrument) confusing and asked his students, but none could explain it. Someone mentioned Vang Hsuan, and Ma Rong summoned him to explain, and with one turn, he solved it, leaving everyone amazed and impressed. When Vang Hsuan completed his studies, he requested to return home. Ma Rong felt jealous, and Vang Hsuan also suspected there might be pursuers. So he sat under a bridge, on the water, holding his shoes. Ma Rong indeed used divination to pursue him, telling his attendants, "Vang Hsuan is below earth and above water, holding wood, so he must die." So he gave up the pursuit, and Vang Hsuan was ultimately spared. [95]

    管辂洞晓术数。初有妇人亡牛，从之卜曰：“当在西面穷墙中。可视诸邱冢中，牛当悬头上。”向既而果得。妇人反疑辂为藏己牛，告官按验，乃知是术数所推。
    　　洛中小人失妻者。辂为卜，教使明旦于东阳城门中伺担豚人牵与共斗，具如其言。豚逸走，即共追之。豚入人舍，突破主人瓮，妇从瓮中出。 [96]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [96]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 洞晓术数 - 精通术数
    • 术数所推 - 术数推断的

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 管辂 - 三国时期著名的术士，以占卜和预测著称
    • 术数 - 指占卜、预测等术数知识，是中国古代的一种学问
    • 洛阳 - 中国古代的重要城市，曾是多个朝代的都城
    • 东阳城门 - 指洛阳的东阳城门，是洛阳的一个城门
    • 豚 - 指猪，是中国古代常见的家畜

    Proper Nouns

    • 管辂 - 三国时期著名的术士
    • 洛阳 - 中国古代的重要城市
    • 东阳城门 - 指洛阳的东阳城门
    • 豚 - 指猪

    管辂精通术数。起初有个妇人丢失了牛，向他占卜说：“牛应该在西边的尽头墙中。可以查看各个坟墓中，牛应该悬挂在头上。”不久果然找到了。妇人反而怀疑管辂藏了她的牛，向官府报告并查证，才知道是术数推断的。
    洛阳有个小人丢失了妻子，管辂为他占卜，告诉他明天在东阳城门中等待挑猪的人，一起斗猪，完全如他所说。猪逃跑了，立即一起追赶。猪跑进了人家的房子里，撞破了主人的瓮，妇人从瓮中出来。 [96]

    中书令纪玄龙，辂乡里人也。辂在田舍，尝候远邻主人苦频失火，辂卜，教使明日于南陌上伺，当有一角巾诸生驾黑牛故车，来必引留，为设宾主，此能消之。后果有此生来，玄龙因留之宿生，有急求去，不听，遂留当宿。意大不安，以为图己。主人罢入，生乃持刀出门外，倚两薪积间侧立假寐。忽有一物直来过前，状如兽，手中持火，以口吹之，生惊，举刀斫，便死，视之，则狐。自是主人不复有灾。
    　　时有利漕治下屯民捕鹿者，获之，为人所窃，诣辂为卦。语云：“此有盗者，是汝东巷中第三家也。汝径往门前，候无人时，取一瓦子，密发其碓屋东头第七椽，以瓦著下，不过明日食时，自送还汝。”其夜盗者父忽患头痛，壮热烦疼，亦来诣辂卜，辂为发祟，盗者具服，令担皮肉还藏着故处，病当自愈。乃密教鹿主往取，又语使复往如前，举椽弃瓦，盗父亦差。 [97]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [97]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 中书令 - High-ranking official in the imperial bureaucracy, responsible for drafting and issuing imperial edicts.
    • 角巾 - A headband worn by scholars, symbolizing their status and learning.
    • 诸生 - Scholars or students, often associated with Confucian education.
    • 假寐 - A brief nap, often associated with moments of rest or reflection.
    • 诣辂为卦 - Visiting a diviner to cast a divination, a common practice in ancient China for seeking guidance or solving problems.
    • 发祟 - Exorcising a spirit, a ritual practice in ancient China to rid oneself of evil influences.
    • 具服 - Fully admitting to a crime or wrongdoing, often in the context of a confession.
    • 差 - Recovering from illness or improving one's condition, reflecting the belief in the power of exorcism and natural healing.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 纪玄龙 - A proper name, likely a historical figure or fictional character in the context of the story.
    • 辂 - A proper name, likely a diviner or scholar in the context of the story.
    • 黑牛故车 - An old carriage drawn by a black ox, possibly a symbol of rustic simplicity or tradition.
    • 碓屋 - A millhouse, where grain is ground into flour.
    • 椽 - A beam or rafter in a building, often used in construction and architecture.
    • 祟 - A malevolent spirit or ghost, often associated with illness or misfortune.
    • 担皮肉还藏着故处 - Carrying the skin and flesh back to the original hiding place, a metaphorical or literal action in the story.
    • 密教鹿主往取 - Secretly instructing the deer owner to go and retrieve it, a plot device to resolve the story.
    • 举椽弃瓦 - Removing the beam and discarding the tile, a symbolic action to resolve the problem.

    Proper Nouns

    • 中书令纪玄龙 - A high-ranking official named Ji Xuanlong, responsible for drafting and issuing imperial edicts.
    • 辂 - A proper name, likely a diviner or scholar in the context of the story.
    • 角巾诸生 - A scholar or student wearing a headband, symbolizing their status and learning.
    • 驾黑牛故车 - An old carriage driven by a black ox, possibly a symbol of rustic simplicity or tradition.
    • 伺 - To watch or wait for.
    • 设宾主 - To arrange as host and guest.
    • 消之 - To eliminate or remove.
    • 假寐 - To take a brief nap or doze.
    • 直来过前 - To come straight in front.
    • 状如兽 - In the form of an animal.
    • 手中持火 - Holding fire in hand.
    • 以口吹之 - Blowing it with the mouth.
    • 举刀斫 - To lift the knife and chop.
    • 遂留当宿 - Stayed for the night.
    • 意大不安 - Mind was uneasy.
    • 以为图己 - Thinking it was plotting against him.
    • 罢入 - Went inside.
    • 倚两薪积间侧立 - Leaning against a pile of firewood and standing sideways.
    • 利漕治下屯民 - A local official and his subjects, reflecting the hierarchical structure of ancient Chinese society.
    • 诣辂为卦 - Visited Lu to cast a divination.
    • 汝东巷中第三家 - The third house in the east alley.
    • 径往门前 - Go directly to the door.
    • 候无人时 - When no one is around.
    • 取一瓦子 - Take a tile.
    • 密发其碓屋东头第七椽 - Secretly remove the seventh beam at the eastern end of the millhouse.
    • 以瓦著下 - Place the tile underneath.
    • 不过明日食时 - By tomorrow's mealtime.
    • 自送还汝 - It will return to you on its own.
    • 盗者父忽患头痛 - The father of the thief suddenly suffered from a headache.
    • 壮热烦疼 - Fever with great heat and pain.
    • 诣辂卜 - Visited Lu to cast a divination.
    • 发祟 - Exorcise a spirit.
    • 具服 - Fully admit.
    • 担皮肉还藏着故处 - Carry the skin and flesh back to the original hiding place.
    • 病当自愈 - The illness will heal on its own.
    • 密教鹿主往取 - Secretly instruct the deer owner to go and retrieve it.
    • 举椽弃瓦 - Remove the beam and discard the tile.
    • 差 - Recover or improve.

    The Grand Secretary Ji Xuanlong was from the same village as Lu. While Lu was living in the countryside, he once visited a distant neighbor who frequently suffered from fires. Lu performed a divination and instructed that the next day, they should wait on the southern road, where a scholar wearing a headband and driving an old carriage pulled by a black ox would arrive. They were to invite him to stay and treat him as a guest, which would eliminate the problem. Indeed, the scholar arrived the next day, and Ji Xuanlong kept him for the night. The scholar, feeling uneasy and suspecting a plot against him, asked to leave but was not allowed, so he stayed for the night. He then went outside, holding a knife and leaning against a pile of firewood, taking a brief nap. Suddenly, a creature came straight in front of him, in the form of an animal, holding fire in its hand and blowing it with its mouth. The scholar was startled, lifted his knife and chopped at it, killing the creature. Upon closer inspection, it turned out to be a fox. From that day on, the owner no longer suffered from fires.

    At another time, a local official named Li Mao, under whose jurisdiction was a group of people who had captured a deer, found that it had been stolen. They visited Lu to cast a divination. Lu instructed them, saying, "There is a thief, and he is the third house in the east alley. Go directly to the door when no one is around, take a tile, and secretly remove the seventh beam at the eastern end of the millhouse, placing the tile underneath. By tomorrow's mealtime, it will return to you on its own." That night, the father of the thief suddenly suffered from a headache, fever, and great pain, and also visited Lu to cast a divination. Lu exorcised the spirit, and the thief fully admitted to the theft. Lu instructed him to carry the skin and flesh back to the original hiding place, and the illness would heal on its own. Lu then secretly instructed the deer owner to go and retrieve it, and told them to do the same as before, removing the beam and discarding the tile. The thief's father also recovered. [97]

    都尉治内史有失物者，辂使明晨于寺门外看，当逢一人，令指天画地，举手四向，自当得之。暮，果获于故处。
    　　安德令刘长仁闻辂晓鸟鸣，初不信之，须臾有鸣鹊来，在阁屋上，其声甚急。辂曰：“鹊言东北有妇昨杀夫，牵引西家人夫娄离。候不过日，在虞渊之际，告者至矣。”到时，果有东北同伍民来告，如辂言。
    　　辂尝至郭恩家，有飞鸠来，在梁头鸣甚悲。辂曰：“当有老公从东方来，携肫一头，酒一壶来候。主人虽喜，当有小故。”明日，果有客如所占而射鸡作食，箭从树间激中数岁女子手，流血惊怖。
    　　馆陶令诸葛原字景春，迁新兴太守。辂往饯之，宾客并会。原自取燕卵蜂窠蜘蛛著器中，使射覆。卦成，辂曰：“第一物含气，须变依乎宇堂，雄雌以形，翅翼舒张，此燕卵也。第二物家室倒悬，门户众多，藏精蓄毒，得秋乃化，此蜂窠也。第三物觳觫长足，吐丝成罗，寻网得食，利在昏夜，此蜘蛛也。”举坐惊喜。 [98]

    The Inspector of the Interior had a loss of property, and Lu instructed to look outside the temple gate the next morning. He said, "He will meet a person who will point to the sky, draw on the ground, and raise his hands in all directions, and he will find it." By evening, it was indeed found at the original place.

    Commander Liu Changren of Ande heard that Lu could understand bird calls and initially did not believe it. Soon, a chattering magpie came, perched on the eaves, and its call was very urgent. Lu said, "The magpie says there is a woman in the northeast who killed her husband yesterday and is dragging the man from the west, named Fu Louli. Await no more than a day, at the time of the sun's setting, the informer will arrive." By the time mentioned, indeed a person from the same neighborhood in the northeast came to report, just as Lu had said.

    Lu once went to the house of Guo En, and a flying jay came and called sadly at the beam. Lu said, "There will be an old man coming from the east, carrying a goose and a pot of wine to visit. The host will be happy, but there will be a minor incident." The next day, a guest arrived as predicted and shot a chicken for food. An arrow struck a young girl's hand from the tree, causing her to bleed and be frightened.

    Commander Zhuge Yuan, also known as Jingchun, was promoted to Inspector of Xinxing. Lu went to see him off, and guests gathered. Zhuge took a swallow's egg, a bee's nest, and a spider, placed them in a container, and asked Lu to guess. After the divination was completed, Lu said, "The first thing contains air, needs to change according to the hall, with male and female forms, wings spread, this is a swallow's egg. The second thing hangs upside down, with many doors and windows, containing essence and poison, transforming in autumn, this is a bee's nest. The third thing has long legs, spits silk to form a web, catches food in the web, and benefits at night, this is a spider." All the guests were surprised and delighted.

    <READY> [98]

    平原太守刘字令清，取印囊及山鸡毛置器中，使辂筮之。辂曰：“内方外员，五色成文，含宝守信，出则有章，此印囊也。高岳岩岩，有鸟朱身，羽翼玄黄，鸣不失晨，此山鸡毛也。”曰：“此郡官舍，连有变怪，使人恐怖，其理何由？”辂曰：“或因汉末之乱，兵马扰攘，军尸流血，污染邱山，故因昏夕多有怪形也。明府道德高妙，自天之，愿安百禄以光休宠。
    　　清河王经字君备，去官还家，辂与相见经，曰：“近有一怪，大不喜之，欲烦作卦。”卦成，辂曰：“爻吉不为怪也。君夜在堂户前，有一流光如燕雀者，入君怀中，殷殷有声，肉神不安，解衣彷徉，招呼妇人觅索余光。”经大笑曰：“实如君言。”辂曰：“吉迁官之征也。”顷之，为江夏太守。 [99]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [99]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 平原太守 - Title referring to the governor of Pingyuan Commandery.
    • 取印囊及山鸡毛置器中 - Literal description of taking an ink bag and a pheasant's feather and placing them in a container.
    • 筮之 - To divinate or consult the I Ching.
    • 内方外员 - Metaphorical description of something that is square inside and round outside.
    • 五色成文 - Refers to a pattern or design composed of five colors.
    • 含宝守信 - Means to contain treasure and uphold faithfulness.
    • 出则有章 - Means to have a clear and distinct appearance when displayed.
    • 高岳岩岩 - Describes a high and majestic mountain.
    • 有鸟朱身 - Refers to a bird with a red body.
    • 羽翼玄黄 - Describes the bird's wings and tail as black and yellow.
    • 鸣不失晨 - Means the bird always crows at dawn.
    • 此郡官舍，连有变怪 - Refers to strange and supernatural occurrences in the official residence of the county.
    • 其理何由 - Asks for the reason behind these occurrences.
    • 或因汉末之乱 - Suggests that the disturbances may be due to the chaos at the end of the Han dynasty.
    • 兵马扰攘 - Describes the turmoil caused by military movements.
    • 军尸流血 - Refers to the bodies of soldiers and their blood.
    • 污染丘山 - Means the mountains and hills were polluted.
    • 故因昏夕多有怪形也 - Explains that these conditions led to many strange appearances at dusk.
    • 明府道德高妙 - Refers to the high moral character of the governor.
    • 自天祐之 - Means to be protected by heaven.
    • 愿安百禄以光休宠 - Expresses the wish to enjoy many blessings and honor.
    • 清河王经字君备 - Refers to Wang Jing, a person from Qinghe.
    • 去官还家 - Means to resign from office and return home.
    • 近有一怪 - Indicates a recent strange occurrence.
    • 欲烦作卦 - Asks for help in casting a divination.
    • 爻吉不为怪也 - Means that the divination result is auspicious and not a sign of a strange occurrence.
    • 君夜在堂户前 - Refers to the person being in front of the hall door at night.
    • 有一流光如燕雀者 - Describes a light like that of a sparrow entering the person's chest.
    • 殷殷有声 - Means there was a low, continuous sound.
    • 肉神不安 - Refers to the person feeling uneasy in their body.
    • 解衣彷徉 - Means to take off clothes and wander around.
    • 招呼妇人觅索余光 - Describes calling a woman to search for the remaining light.
    • 吉迁官之征也 - Means that the divination result is a sign of a good omen for a promotion.
    • 顷之，为江夏太守 - Indicates that soon after, Wang Jing became the governor of Jiangxia Commandery.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 平原太守 - The title of the governor of Pingyuan Commandery, indicating a high-ranking official.
    • 印囊 - An ink bag, a common item used by officials.
    • 山鸡毛 - A pheasant's feather, often used in divination or as a decorative item.
    • 筮之 - Divination using the I Ching, a traditional Chinese method of fortune-telling.
    • 内方外员 - A metaphorical description often used in Chinese literature and philosophy.
    • 五色成文 - A reference to the five colors in Chinese cosmology, symbolizing harmony and completeness.
    • 含宝守信 - A phrase emphasizing the importance of integrity and faithfulness.
    • 出则有章 - A reference to clarity and distinctness, often used in descriptions of objects or situations.
    • 高岳岩岩 - A vivid description of a majestic mountain, often used in poetry and literature.
    • 有鸟朱身 - A description of a bird, which could be symbolic or literal.
    • 羽翼玄黄 - A description of the bird's wings and tail, which could be symbolic or literal.
    • 鸣不失晨 - A reference to the crowing of a rooster at dawn, often used to symbolize vigilance and punctuality.
    • 此郡官舍，连有变怪 - A reference to supernatural occurrences, which were common in Chinese folklore and literature.
    • 其理何由 - A question about the cause of these occurrences, reflecting the belief in supernatural explanations.
    • 或因汉末之乱 - A reference to the chaos at the end of the Han dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 兵马扰攘 - A reference to the turmoil caused by military movements, which was a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 军尸流血 - A reference to the violence and bloodshed of war, which was a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 污染丘山 - A reference to the environmental impact of war, which was a concern in Chinese literature.
    • 故因昏夕多有怪形也 - A reference to the belief that strange occurrences are more likely at dusk, a common theme in Chinese folklore.
    • 明府道德高妙 - A reference to the high moral character of the governor, which was a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 自天祐之 - A reference to divine protection, which was a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 愿安百禄以光休宠 - A reference to the desire for blessings and honor, which was a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 清河王经字君备 - A reference to Wang Jing, a person from Qinghe, indicating a specific individual.
    • 去官还家 - A reference to resigning from office and returning home, which was a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 近有一怪 - A reference to a recent strange occurrence, which was a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 欲烦作卦 - A reference to asking for help in casting a divination, which was a common practice in Chinese culture.
    • 爻吉不为怪也 - A reference to the divination result being auspicious, which was a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 君夜在堂户前 - A reference to being in front of the hall door at night, which was a common setting in Chinese literature.
    • 有一流光如燕雀者 - A reference to a light like that of a sparrow, which could be symbolic or literal.
    • 殷殷有声 - A reference to a low, continuous sound, which could be symbolic or literal.
    • 肉神不安 - A reference to feeling uneasy in one's body, which could be symbolic or literal.
    • 解衣彷徉 - A reference to taking off clothes and wandering around, which could be symbolic or literal.
    • 招呼妇人觅索余光 - A reference to calling a woman to search for the remaining light, which could be symbolic or literal.
    • 吉迁官之征也 - A reference to the divination result being a sign of a good omen for a promotion, which was a common theme in Chinese literature.
    • 顷之，为江夏太守 - A reference to becoming the governor of Jiangxia Commandery, indicating a promotion.

    The Governor of Pingyuan Commandery, Liu Lingqing, took an ink bag and a pheasant's feather and placed them in a container, then asked Lu to perform a divination. Lu said, "Square inside and round outside, a pattern of five colors, containing treasure and upholding faithfulness, with a clear and distinct appearance when displayed, this is the ink bag. High and majestic mountains, a bird with a red body, wings and tail black and yellow, always crows at dawn, this is the pheasant's feather." Liu asked, "Strange and supernatural occurrences have been happening in the official residence of the county, causing fear among the people, what is the reason behind this?" Lu replied, "Perhaps due to the chaos at the end of the Han dynasty, military movements and the violence and bloodshed of war, the mountains and hills were polluted, hence many strange appearances occur at dusk. Your virtue is high and your character is excellent, you are protected by heaven, I wish you to enjoy many blessings and honor."

    Wang Jing, from Qinghe, whose courtesy name was Junbei, resigned from office and returned home. Lu met with him, and Wang said, "Recently, there has been a strange occurrence that greatly displeases me, I wish to trouble you to cast a divination." After the divination was cast, Lu said, "The divination result is auspicious and not a sign of a strange occurrence. You were in front of the hall door at night, a light like that of a sparrow entered your chest, there was a low, continuous sound, you felt uneasy in your body, took off your clothes and wandered around, called a woman to search for the remaining light." Wang Jing laughed and said, "It is exactly as you said." Lu said, "This is a sign of a good omen for a promotion." Soon after, Wang Jing became the governor of Jiangxia Commandery. [99]

    晋南阳赵侯少好诸异术，姿形悴陋，长不满数尺，以盆盛水，闭目吹气，作禁鱼龙立见。侯有白米，为鼠所盗，乃披持刀，画地作狱，四面开门，向东长啸，群鼠俱到，咒之曰：“凡非啖者过去，盗者令止。”止者十余，剖腹看脏，有米在焉。会徒跣须履，因仰头微吟，双履自至。人有笑其形容者，便佯说以酒杯向口，即掩鼻不脱，乃稽颡谢过，著地不举。永康有骑石山，山上有石人骑石马，侯以印指之，人马一时落首。今犹在山下。
    　　颍川庾嘉德善于筮蔡之事，有人失一婢，庾卦云：“君可出东陵口伺候。有姓曹乘车者，无问识否，但就其载。得与不得，殆一理也。”旦出郭，果有曹郎上墓，径便升车，曹大骇呼生，惊奔入草，刺一死尸下，视乃其婢也。 [100]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [100]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 为之一空 - Set phrase meaning completely emptied; fields were left barren.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋南阳赵侯 - The Duke of Zhao from Nanyang in the state of Jin, a historical figure.
    • 永康骑石山 - A specific location in Yongkang known for a stone man riding a stone horse, a cultural and geographical reference.
    • 颍川庾嘉德 - Yu Jia De from Yenchuan, a historical figure known for his divination skills.
    • 筮蔡之事 - Refers to divination practices, specifically those involving the use of the *I Ching* (Book of Changes) or similar methods.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋南阳赵侯 - The Duke of Zhao from Nanyang in the state of Jin.
    • 永康骑石山 - A specific location in Yongkang.
    • 颍川庾嘉德 - Yu Jia De from Yenchuan.
    • 东陵口 - A specific location in Dongling.

    The Duke of Zhao from Nanyang in the state of Jin, when young, was fond of various strange arts. He had a gaunt and ugly appearance and was no more than a few feet tall. He would close his eyes, blow air, and instantly summon fish and dragons. Once, his white rice was stolen by a mouse. He draped his hair, held a knife, and drew a prison on the ground, then opened doors on all four sides and shouted loudly to the east. All the mice came together, and he chanted, "Those who are not eating should pass, and the thief should stop." Ten mice stopped, and he cut them open, finding rice inside. When it was time for him to put on his shoes, he stood barefoot and recited a spell, and his shoes appeared. When someone laughed at his appearance, he pretended to drink from a cup, covered his nose, and then knelt to apologize, placing his head on the ground.

    In Yongkang, there is a mountain called the Riding Stone Mountain, where a stone man rides a stone horse. The Duke pointed with an imperial seal, and the stone man and horse fell off their heads. The mountain still stands there today.

    Yu Jia De from Yenchuan was skilled in divination. Someone lost a maid, and Yu Jia De performed a divination, saying, "You should go to the east of Dongling to wait. There will be a man named Cao driving a carriage. Do not ask if you know him; just board the carriage." The person ran into the grass, stabbed a dead body, and found it to be their lost maid. [100]

    北海任诩字产期，从军十年乃归。临还，握粟出卜，师云：“非屋莫宿，非食时莫沐。”诩结伴数十共行，暮遇雷雨，不可蒙冒，相与庇于岩下。窃意非屋莫宿戒，遂负担栉休。岩崩，压停者，悉死。至家，妻先与外人通，情谋共杀之，请以湿发为识。妇宵则劝诩令沐，复忆非食时莫沐之忌，收发而止。妇惭愧负怍，乃自沐焉，散发同寝。通者夜来，不知妇人也，斩首而去。
    　　河间沐坚字壁强，石勒时监作水田，御下苛虐，百姓怨毒，乃为坚形，以刃矛斫刺，咒令倒毙。坚寻得病，苦被捶割，于是遂殒。
    　　晋咸宁中，高阳新城臾为泾祠妖幻。署置百官。又以水自鉴，辄见所署置之人衣冠俨然。百姓信惑，京都翕集，收而斩之。 [101]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [101]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 握粟出卜 - A practice of holding grains of millet to make divinations, often associated with fortune-telling.
    • 非屋莫宿，非食时莫沐 - Two prohibitions given by the diviner: 'do not stay in a shelter that is not a house' and 'do not bathe outside of mealtime.'
    • 岩崩，压停者 - A natural disaster where a rockfall crushes those who stopped to rest.
    • 妻先与外人通 - The wife had already been having an affair with someone else.
    • 情谋共杀之 - They plotted together to kill him.
    • 请以湿发为识 - They agreed to use wet hair as a signal.
    • 宵则劝诩令沐 - At night, the wife would encourage him to bathe.
    • 复忆非食时莫沐之忌 - He remembered the prohibition against bathing outside of mealtime.
    • 收发而止 - He stopped and collected his hair.
    • 妇惭愧负怍 - The wife felt ashamed and burdened.
    • 乃自沐焉 - She then bathed herself.
    • 散发同寝 - She slept with her hair loose.
    • 通者夜来 - The man she was having an affair with came at night.
    • 不知妇人也，斩首而去 - He did not recognize her as a woman and beheaded her before leaving.
    • 石勒时监作水田 - During the time of Shi Le, overseeing the construction of irrigation fields.
    • 御下苛虐 - Ruling with harsh and cruel methods.
    • 百姓怨毒 - The common people harbored resentment and hatred.
    • 乃为坚形，以刃矛斫刺 - They created an image of him and used blades and spears to stab and cut.
    • 咒令倒毙 - Cursed him to die.
    • 寻得病，苦被捶割 - Soon after, he fell ill, suffering from beatings and mutilation.
    • 于是遂殒 - And thus he died.
    • 晋咸宁中 - During the Xianning period of the Jin dynasty.
    • 高阳新城臾为泾祠妖幻 - Yu from Gaoyang and Xincheng created magical illusions at the Jing Temple.
    • 署置百官 - Appointed a hundred officials.
    • 又以水自鉴，辄见所署置之人衣冠俨然 - He would also use water to reflect himself and see the appointed officials dressed formally.
    • 百姓信惑，京都翕集 - The common people were deceived and gathered from the capital.
    • 收而斩之 - They captured and executed him.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 北海任诩字产期 - Ren Xu, courtesy name Chanqi, from Beihai.
    • 从军十年乃归 - Served in the military for ten years before returning home.
    • 河间沐坚字壁强 - A historical figure named Mujian Biqiang from the Hejian region.
    • 石勒 - Shi Le was a prominent warlord during the late Western Jin period.
    • 晋咸宁中 - Xianning was a reign title of Emperor Wu of Jin.
    • 高阳新城臾为泾祠妖幻 - Yu from Gaoyang and Xincheng refers to a person who performed magical illusions.
    • 署置百官 - Refers to the practice of appointing officials, often associated with political manipulation.
    • 百姓信惑，京都翕集 - The common people were deceived and gathered from the capital, indicating widespread belief in the illusion.

    Proper Nouns

    • 北海 - A region in ancient China.
    • 任诩字产期 - Ren Xu, courtesy name Chanqi.
    • 河间 - A region in ancient China.
    • 沐坚字壁强 - Mujian Biqiang.
    • 石勒 - A prominent warlord during the late Western Jin period.
    • 晋咸宁中 - During the Xianning period of the Jin dynasty.
    • 高阳 - A region in ancient China.
    • 新城 - A region in ancient China.
    • 臾 - A person's name.
    • 泾祠 - A temple or shrine dedicated to the god of water.

    **North Sea Ren Xu, courtesy name Chanqi, served in the military for ten years before returning home. Before he left, he held grains of millet to make divinations, and the diviner said: "Do not stay in a shelter that is not a house; do not bathe outside of mealtime." Ren Xu and his companions of about twenty people were traveling together when they encountered a thunderstorm in the evening, which was too heavy to endure. They sought shelter under a rock. Remembering the prohibition against staying in a shelter that is not a house, they decided to carry their belongings and rest there. Suddenly, the rock collapsed, crushing those who had stopped to rest, and they all died.

    Upon returning home, Ren Xu found that his wife had already been having an affair with someone else. They plotted together to kill him and agreed to use wet hair as a signal. At night, the wife would encourage him to bathe. One day, he remembered the prohibition against bathing outside of mealtime and stopped, collecting his hair. The wife felt ashamed and burdened, so she bathed herself and slept with her hair loose. The man she was having an affair with came at night, did not recognize her as a woman, and beheaded her before leaving.

    During the time of Shi Le, Mujian Biqiang from Hejian was overseeing the construction of irrigation fields. He ruled with harsh and cruel methods, and the common people harbored resentment and hatred. They created an image of him and used blades and spears to stab and cut, cursing him to die. Soon after, he fell ill, suffering from beatings and mutilation, and thus he died.

    During the Xianning period of the Jin dynasty, Yu from Gaoyang and Xincheng created magical illusions at the Jing Temple. He appointed a hundred officials and would also use water to reflect himself and see the appointed officials dressed formally. The common people were deceived and gathered from the capital, and they captured and executed him.** [101]

    扶南国治生皆用黄金僦船东西，远近雇一斤。时有不至所届，欲减金数，舡主便作幻诳，使船底斫折，状欲沦滞海中，进退不动。众人惶怖，还请赛船合如初。
    　　元嘉初，上虞孙溪奴多诸幻伎，叛入建安治中。后出民间，破宿瘦辟遥彻腹内而令不痛。治人头风，流血滂沲，嘘之便断，疮又即敛。虎伤蛇噬，烦毒随死，禁护皆差。向空长啸，则群鹊来萃。夜咒蚊虻，悉皆死倒。至十三年，乃于长山为本主所得，知有禁术，虑必亡叛，的缚枷锁，极为重复，少日已失所在。
    　　永嘉阳童，孙权时俗师也。尝独乘船往建宁，泊在渚次，宵中忽有一鬼来，欲击童，童因起，谓曰：“谁敢近阳童者？”鬼即稽颡云：“实不知是阳使者童。”便敕使乘船，船飞迅驶，有过猛帆，至县乃遣之。
    　　荥阳郑鲜之字道子，为尚书左仆射。女脚患挛癖，就王仆医，仆阳请水浇之，余浇庭中枯衰树，树既生，女脚亦差。 [102]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [102]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 治生 - Classical term meaning 'to manage one's livelihood' or 'to engage in business.'
    • 僦船 - Literally 'to hire a boat,' but in this context, it refers to using gold as payment for travel.
    • 遠近 - Literally 'far and near,' but here it refers to the distance of the trip.
    • 雇 - To hire or pay for a service.
    • 一斤 - One pound of gold.
    • 作幻诳 - Performs magic tricks or illusions.
    • 斫折 - Literally 'chop and break,' but here it refers to making the boat look damaged.
    • 淪滯 - Literally 'sink and stagnate,' but here it refers to the appearance of the boat sinking.
    • 惶怖 - A compound term meaning 'terrified' or 'frightened.'
    • 賽船 - Literally 'compete with a boat,' but here it refers to paying for repairs.
    • 合如初 - As before or as it was originally.
    • 叛 - Rebelled.
    • 建安治中 - Jian'an Prefecture, a historical administrative division.
    • 破宿瘦 - Literally 'break through old ailments,' but here it refers to curing diseases.
    • 辟遙徹 - Literally 'penetrate deeply,' but here it refers to reaching the interior of the body.
    • 滂沲 - Profuse or abundant.
    • 嘘 - To blow.
    • 敛 - To heal or close.
    • 噬 - To bite.
    • 煩毒 - Pain and toxins.
    • 禁護 - Pain and toxins.
    • 差 - To subside or improve.
    • 長啸 - To shout or cry out loudly.
    • 群鹊 - Literally 'flocks of crows,' but here it likely refers to pigeons or birds in general.
    • 来萃 - To gather.
    • 咒 - To chant spells.
    • 蚊虻 - Mosquitoes and flies.
    • 悉皆 - All.
    • 死倒 - Die and fall.
    • 十三年 - The thirteenth year, likely referring to a specific period.
    • 长山 - Changshan, a place name.
    • 本主 - Original master.
    • 禁术 - Forbidden techniques or magic.
    • 亡叛 - Escape or rebel.
    • 的缚 - Tightly bound.
    • 枷锁 - Shackles.
    • 重复 - Tightly or repeatedly.
    • 少日 - After a short time.
    • 失所在 - Disappeared.
    • 永嘉 - Yongjia, a place name.
    • 阳童 - Yang Tong, a person's name.
    • 孙权 - Sun Quan, a historical figure.
    • 俗师 - Popular teacher.
    • 建宁 - Jianning, a place name.
    • 渚次 - A small island or shore.
    • 宵中 - At night.
    • 忽 - Suddenly.
    • 击 - To attack.
    • 因起 - Stood up.
    • 谓曰 - Said.
    • 谁敢 - Who dares.
    • 近 - To approach.
    • 稽颡 - To kneel and bow.
    • 云 - Said.
    • 实不知 - Did not know.
    • 使者 - Messenger.
    • 敕使 - Ordered.
    • 飞迅驶 - Flew swiftly.
    • 过猛帆 - Faster than even the fastest sail.
    • 遣之 - Released.
    • 荥阳 - Xingyang, a place name.
    • 郑鲜之 - Zheng Xianzhi, a person's name.
    • 字道子 - Styled Daozi.
    • 尚书左仆射 - Left Shangshu, a high-ranking official.
    • 脚患 - Suffered from foot problems.
    • 挛癖 - Cramps and stiffness.
    • 王仆 - Wang Pu, a person's name.
    • 医 - Doctor.
    • 阳请 - Pretended to ask.
    • 浇之 - Pour on her.
    • 余 - The rest.
    • 庭中 - In the courtyard.
    • 枯衰树 - Withered tree.
    • 既生 - Revived.

    Fulun State uses gold to hire boats for travel in any direction, with hiring rates of one pound of gold per trip, regardless of distance. Sometimes, if the destination was not reached, the boat owner would perform magic tricks to deceive the passengers, causing the boat to appear to break and sink in the sea, making it unable to move forward or backward. The passengers were terrified and then paid to have the boat repaired as before.

    In the early years of the Yuanjia reign, Sun Xi, a slave from Shangyu, had many magical skills and rebelled, entering Jian'an Prefecture. Later, he appeared among the common people, curing old ailments and penetrating deeply into the body without causing pain. He could treat headaches, stop profuse bleeding by blowing on the wounds, and heal them immediately. He could also cure bites from tigers and snakes, subdue pain and toxins, and make them subside. When he shouted into the air, pigeons would gather, and at night, he would chant spells to repel mosquitoes and flies, causing them to die and fall. By the thirteenth year, he was eventually captured by his original master in Changshan. Knowing he possessed forbidden techniques, the master feared he would escape and tightly bound him with shackles. After a short time, he disappeared.

    Yang Tong of Yongjia was a popular teacher during the time of Sun Quan. He once traveled alone by boat to Jianning and docked at a small island. At night, a ghost suddenly appeared to attack him. Yang Tong stood up and said, "Who dares to approach Yang Tong?" The ghost immediately knelt and said, "I did not know you were Yang Tong, the messenger." He then ordered the ghost to take the boat, which flew swiftly, faster than even the fastest sail. He reached the county and then released the ghost.

    Zheng Xianzhi of Xingyang, styled Daozi, served as the Left Shangshu. His daughter suffered from cramps and stiffness and went to see Wang Pu, a doctor. Wang Pu pretended to ask for water to pour on her and then poured the rest on a withered tree in the courtyard. The tree revived, and his daughter's foot also improved. [102]

    ●卷十
    　　介子推逃禄隐迹，抱树烧死。文公拊木哀嗟，伐而制屐。每怀割股之功，俯视其屐曰：“悲乎，足下！”足下之称，将起于此。
    　　田文母，嬖也，五月五日生文，父敕令勿举，母私举文长成，童以实告之，遂启父，曰：“不举五月子，何也？”父云：“生及户损父。”文曰：“受命于天，岂受命于户。若受命于户，何不高其户，谁能至其户耶！”父知其贤，立为嗣齐，封为孟尝君，俗以五月为恶月，故忌。
    　　魏安禧王观翔雕而乐之，曰：“寡人得如雕之飞视天下如芥也。”吴客有隐游者闻之，作木雕而献之王，王曰：“此有形无用者也。夫作无用之器，世之奸民也。”召隐游欲加刑焉，隐游曰：“臣闻大王之好飞也，故敢献雕，安知大王之恶此也。可谓知有用之雕鸟，未悟无用之雕鸟也。今臣请为大王翔之。”乃取而骑焉，遂翻然飞去，莫知所之。 [103]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [103]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 介子推逃禄隐迹，抱树烧死 - Narrative describing Jie Zitui's act of hiding and ultimately burning to death while holding onto a tree.
    • 文公拊木哀嗟，伐而制屐 - Describes Duke Wen of Jin's emotional response and subsequent action of cutting down the tree to make shoes.
    • 每怀割股之功，俯视其屐曰：“悲乎，足下！” - Phrase where Duke Wen recalls Jie Zitui's sacrifice and uses '足下' (a respectful term for 'you') ironically.
    • 生及户损父 - Superstitious belief that a child born in May will harm their father.
    • 受命于天，岂受命于户 - Philosophical statement questioning the validity of the superstition.
    • 魏安禧王观翔雕而乐之 - Describes Wei Anxi Wang's enjoyment of watching a falcon fly.
    • 此有形无用者也 - Statement that the wooden falcon is useless.
    • 夫作无用之器，世之奸民也 - Moral judgment that creating useless objects is the act of a deceitful person.
    • 召隐游欲加刑焉 - Describes the king's intention to punish the craftsman.
    • 安知大王之恶此也 - Rhetorical question expressing the craftsman's surprise at the king's reaction.
    • 可谓知有用之雕鸟，未悟无用之雕鸟也 - Statement that the king understands the usefulness of living falcons but not the value of the wooden one.
    • 今臣请为大王翔之 - Statement of the craftsman's intention to demonstrate the wooden falcon's ability to fly.
    • 乃取而骑焉，遂翻然飞去，莫知所之 - Describes the craftsman riding the wooden falcon and flying away, leaving the king amazed.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 介子推 - Historical figure who served Duke Wen of Jin and later sacrificed himself by burning to death.
    • 文公 - Duke Wen of Jin, a ruler of the Jin state during the Spring and Autumn period.
    • 田文 - Tian Wen, also known as Mengchang Jun, a prominent noble during the Warring States period.
    • 魏安禧王 - Wei Anxi Wang, a ruler of the Wei state during the Warring States period.
    • 雕 - A falcon, a bird of prey often used for hunting.
    • 五月五日 - The fifth day of the fifth month in the lunar calendar, often associated with the Dragon Boat Festival and various superstitions.

    Proper Nouns

    • 介子推 - Historical figure.
    • 文公 - Duke Wen of Jin.
    • 田文 - Tian Wen, also known as Mengchang Jun.
    • 魏安禧王 - Wei Anxi Wang.
    • 雕 - A falcon.

    ● Volume Ten

    Jie Zitui fled from his official position, hiding his presence, and held onto a tree until he burned to death. Duke Wen of Jin touched the tree in sorrow and lamentation, then cut it down to make shoes. Whenever he thought of Jie Zitui's sacrifice of cutting off a piece of his own flesh to feed him, he would look down at the shoes and say, "Alas, my dear!" The term "dear" or "you" (足下) originated from this incident.

    Tian Wen's mother was favored, and she gave birth to him on the fifth day of the fifth month. His father ordered that he not be raised, but his mother secretly raised him. A child later told his father the truth, and he then asked, "Why not raise a child born in the fifth month?" His father replied, "A child born in the fifth month will harm his father." Tian Wen said, "We receive our fate from heaven, not from doors. If we receive our fate from doors, why not build high doors? Who could reach such high doors?" His father recognized his wisdom, and he was made heir to Qi, later being enfeoffed as Mengchang Jun. The custom of considering the fifth month as an unlucky month and avoiding it stems from this belief.

    Wei Anxi Wang enjoyed watching a falcon fly and said, "I can see the world as a small mustard seed, just like a falcon in flight." A guest from Wu, who was a skilled craftsman, heard this and made a wooden falcon to present to the king. The king said, "This is a useless object. To create something without purpose is the act of a deceitful person." He summoned the craftsman to punish him. The craftsman said, "I heard that the great king enjoys flying, so I dared to present the falcon. How could I know that the great king would dislike it? You understand the usefulness of a living falcon but do not comprehend the value of a wooden one. Now, I will demonstrate for the great king." He took the wooden falcon, mounted it, and suddenly flew away, leaving no trace of where he went. [103]

    东阳颜乌以纯孝著闻，后有群乌衔鼓，集颜所居之村。乌口皆伤一境，以为颜至孝，故慈乌来萃，衔鼓之兴，欲令聋者远闻，即于鼓处置县，而名为鹄伤。王莽改为乌孝，以彰其行迹云。
    　　孝女曹娥者，会稽上虞人也。父于能弦歌为巫，汉安帝二年五月五日于县江溯涛迎婆，娑神溺死，不得尸骸。娥年十四，乃缘江号哭，昼夜不绝声，七日遂投江而死，三日后与父尸俱出。至元嘉元年，县长度尚改葬娥于江南道傍，为立碑焉。陈留蔡邕字伯喈，避难过吴，读曹娥碑文，以为诗人之作，无诡妄也，因刻石旁作“黄绢幼妇外孙齑臼”八字。魏武见而不能了，以问群僚，莫有解者。有妇人浣于江渚，曰：“第四车解既而祢正平也。”衡即以离合义解之。或谓此妇人即娥灵也。 [104]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [104]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 以純孝著聞 - Means 'famous for filial piety,' where '純孝' refers to pure or exemplary filial piety.
