    • 慈烏來萃 - Means 'kind-hearted crows gather,' where '慈烏' refers to crows, often associated with filial piety in Chinese culture.
    • 欲令聋者远闻 - Means 'to make the deaf hear from afar,' a metaphorical expression indicating the desire for the message to reach far and wide.
    • 改葬 - Means 'reburial,' referring to the act of moving a deceased person's remains to a new location.
    • 為立碑焉 - Means 'to erect a stele there,' where '立碑' refers to setting up a monument or tablet.
    • 避难过吴 - Means 'to avoid trouble by going to Wu,' where '避过' means 'to avoid' and '吴' refers to the region of Wu.
    • 無诡妄也 - Means 'not false or deceptive,' where '無' means 'not' and '诡妄' means 'false' or 'deceptive.'
    • 刻石旁作“黄绢幼妇外孙齑臼”八字 - Means 'carved eight characters 'yellow silk young woman's grandson's mortar' on the side of the stone,' where '黄绢' refers to yellow silk, '幼妇' means young woman, '外孙' means grandson, and '齑臼' means mortar.
    • 离合义解之 - Means 'to interpret it using the method of combining and separating characters,' where '离合' refers to the method of combining and separating characters to form new words or phrases.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东阳颜乌 - Refers to a historical figure named Yan Wu from Dongyang, known for his filial piety.
    • 鹄伤 - Refers to a place name, possibly derived from the story of Yan Wu and the crows.
    • 乌孝 - Refers to a place name, possibly derived from the story of Yan Wu and the crows.
    • 会稽上虞 - Refers to a region in present-day Zhejiang Province, China.
    • 曹娥 - Refers to a historical figure, a daughter known for her filial piety and sacrifice.
    • 汉安帝 - Refers to Emperor An of Han, a ruler of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 度尚 - Refers to a historical figure, a local official who reburied曹娥.
    • 蔡邕 - Refers to Cai Yong, a famous scholar and calligrapher of the Eastern Han Dynasty.
    • 黄绢幼妇外孙齑臼 - Refers to a cryptic message carved by Cai Yong, which was later interpreted by Liu Bei and his advisors.
    • 祢正平 - Refers to Mi Zhenping, a scholar who interpreted the cryptic message.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东阳颜乌 - Yan Wu from Dongyang.
    • 鹄伤 - A place name.
    • 乌孝 - A place name.
    • 会稽上虞 - A region in Zhejiang Province.
    • 曹娥 - A historical figure.
    • 汉安帝 - Emperor An of Han.
    • 度尚 - A local official.
    • 蔡邕 - Cai Yong.
    • 黄绢幼妇外孙齑臼 - A cryptic message.
    • 祢正平 - Mi Zhenping.

    Yan Wu from Dongyang was famous for his filial piety. Later, a group of crows carrying drums gathered at the village where Yan lived. The mouths of the crows were all injured, and it was believed that Yan's great filial piety had attracted these kind-hearted crows. The crows brought the drums to raise a commotion, intending to make the deaf hear from afar. Thus, a county was established at the site of the drum, and it was named Huoshang. Wang Mang later renamed it Wuxiao to commemorate Yan's deeds.

    As for the filial daughter Cao E, she was from Shangyu, Kuaiji. Her father, Cao Yu, was a shaman skilled in stringed music and singing. On the fifth day of the fifth month of the second year of Emperor An of Han, he drowned while crossing the river in the county to welcome the goddess of the waves, and his body was never recovered. At the age of fourteen, Cao E began to wail along the riverbank, crying day and night without ceasing. After seven days, she threw herself into the river and died. Three days later, she emerged with her father's body. In the first year of Yuangjia, the local official Du Shang reburied Cao E by the roadside in the south of the county and erected a stele there. Chenliu's Cai Yong, whose courtesy name was Bojiao, avoided trouble by going to Wu, where he read the inscription on Cao E's stele. He believed it to be the work of a poet, free from deceit, and thus carved eight characters "yellow silk young woman's grandson's mortar" on the side of the stone. Cao Cao saw this but could not understand it, so he asked his officials, none of whom could explain it. A woman washing at the riverbank said, "The fourth carriage is resolved, and Mi Zhenping follows." Cao Cao then interpreted it using the method of combining and separating characters. Some say this woman was the spirit of Cao E. [104]

    管宁字幼安，避难辽东，后还泛海遭风，船垂倾没，宁潜思良久，曰：“吾尝一朝科头，三晨晏起。今天怒猥集，过恐在此。”
    　　魏徐邈字景山，为尚书郎，时禁酒而邈私饮，至于沈醉。从事赵达问以曹事，邈曰：“中圣人。”达白太祖，太祖甚怒，渡辽，鲜于辅进曰：“醉客谓清酒为圣人，浊酒为贤人。邈性修慎，偶醉言耳。”由是得免。后文帝幸许昌，见邈，问曰：“颇复中圣人否？”对曰：“昔子反毙于谷阳，御叔罚于饮酒。臣嗜同二子，不能自惩。时复中之。”帝大笑，顾左右曰：“名不虚立。”
    　　贾充字公闾，平阳襄陵人也。妻郭氏为人凶妒。生儿犁民，年始三岁，乳母抱之当阁，犁民见充外入，喜笑，充就乳母怀中不呜撮。郭遥见，谓充爱乳母，即鞭杀之。儿恒啼泣不食他乳，经日遂死，郭于是终身无子。 [105]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [105]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 避難 - Seek refuge or flee for safety, often due to political or social unrest.
    • 泛海 - Sail across the sea, indicating a journey by sea.
    • 垂 - About to or on the verge of, emphasizing the imminent danger.
    • 傾没 - Capsize and sink, describing the near-catastrophic situation.
    • 潛思良久 - Ponder deeply for a long time, indicating a period of deep reflection.
    • 吾嘗一朝科頭，三晨晏起 - I once went bareheaded for one morning and woke late for three mornings, indicating a moment of carelessness.
    • 今天怒猥集 - The anger of heaven has suddenly gathered, suggesting divine retribution.
    • 過恐在此 - The fault probably lies here, indicating a self-reflective acknowledgment of guilt.
    • 中聖人 - Euphemism for being drunk, where '聖人' (sage) is used humorously to refer to alcohol.
    • 賢人 - Another euphemism for strong drink, where '賢人' (virtuous person) is used to refer to strong alcohol.
    • 修慎 - Diligent and cautious, describing a person's character.
    • 偶醉言耳 - Just a slip of the tongue when drunk, indicating that the statement was made in a moment of intoxication.
    • 時復中之 - Occasionally I do get drunk, indicating a recurring habit.
    • 顧左右 - Look around at those present, indicating a gesture to draw attention.
    • 名不虚立 - His reputation is well-deserved, indicating that the person's character matches their reputation.
    • 凶妒 - Cruel and jealous, describing *De Guo*'s character.
    • 乳母 - Nurse or wet nurse.
    • 喜笑 - Smiling with joy, indicating the child's reaction.
    • 就乳母懷中不鳴撮 - Climbed into the nurse's lap and did not cry, indicating the child's comfort.
    • 鞭殺之 - Whipped to death, indicating the harsh punishment.
    • 啼泣不食他乳 - Cried and refused to nurse from anyone else, indicating the child's attachment.
    • 經日遂死 - After a few days, he died, indicating the child's eventual death.
    • 郭於是終身無子 - De Guo thus remained childless for the rest of her life, indicating the long-term consequence of her actions.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 管寧 - Prominent scholar and official during the Three Kingdoms period, known for his integrity and wisdom.
    • 徐邈 - High-ranking official during the Wei dynasty, known for his literary talents and political acumen.
    • 郭氏 - De Guo, the wife of Jia Chung, known for her cruelty and jealousy.
    • 趙達 - Subordinate of Cao Cao, who questioned Xu Miao about state affairs.
    • 曹 - Refers to Cao Cao, the powerful warlord and founder of the Wei dynasty.
    • 子反 - Historical figure from the Zuo Zhuan (Leftist Commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals), who died after drinking too much.
    • 谷陽 - The place where Zi Fan died.
    • 御叔 - Another historical figure from the Zuo Zhuan, who was punished for excessive drinking.
    • Jia Chung - High-ranking official during the Wei dynasty, known for his political and military achievements.
    • 鮮于辅 - General and advisor to Cao Cao, who intervened on behalf of Xu Miao.
    • 文帝 - Cao Pi, the first emperor of the Wei dynasty, who succeeded Cao Cao.
    • 許昌 - The capital of the Wei dynasty, where Cao Pi held court.

    Proper Nouns

    • 管寧 - Prominent scholar and official during the Three Kingdoms period.
    • 徐邈 - High-ranking official during the Wei dynasty.
    • 郭氏 - De Guo, the wife of Jia Chung.
    • 趙達 - Subordinate of Cao Cao.
    • 曹 - Refers to Cao Cao.
    • 子反 - Historical figure from the Zuo Zhuan.
    • 谷陽 - The place where Zi Fan died.
    • 御叔 - Another historical figure from the Zuo Zhuan.
    • Jia Chung - High-ranking official during the Wei dynasty.
    • 鮮于辅 - General and advisor to Cao Cao.
    • 文帝 - Cao Pi.
    • 許昌 - The capital of the Wei dynasty.
    • 平陽襄陵 - The hometown of Jia Chung.
    • 犁民 - Jia Chung's son.

    Guan Ning, whose courtesy name was You'an, sought refuge in Liaodong during a time of turmoil. Later, he returned by sea and encountered a storm that nearly capsized and sank the ship. After pondering deeply for a long time, Guan Ning said, "I once went bareheaded for one morning and woke late for three mornings. Now, the anger of heaven has suddenly gathered, and the fault probably lies here."

    During the Wei dynasty, Xu Miao, whose courtesy name was Jingshan, served as a Secretary of the Imperial Secretariat. At a time when alcohol was banned, Xu Miao drank privately and became heavily intoxicated. When an official named Zhao Da asked him about state affairs, Xu Miao replied, "I have become a sage." Zhao Da reported this to Cao Cao, who was very angry. Cao Cao then crossed the river to Liaodong, and Xian Yu Fu interceded, saying, "Drunkards call clear wine 'sage' and turbid wine 'virtuous person.' Xu Miao is diligent and cautious by nature; he merely spoke in a moment of intoxication." Because of this, Xu Miao was spared. Later, when Emperor Wen of Wei visited Xuchang, he met Xu Miao and asked, "Have you become a sage again?" Xu Miao replied, "In the past, Zi Fan died in Guyang, and Yu Shu was punished for excessive drinking. I share their fondness for alcohol and cannot discipline myself. I do get drunk occasionally." The emperor laughed heartily and looked around at those present, saying, "His reputation is well-deserved."

    Jia Chung, whose courtesy name was Gonglu, was from Pingyang Xiangling. His wife, De Guo, was cruel and jealous. They had a son named Lemin, who was only three years old. One day, the wet nurse was holding Lemin on the veranda when he saw Jia Chung returning home and smiled with joy. Jia Chung climbed into the wet nurse's lap and did not cry. De Guo, seeing this from afar, thought Jia Chung loved the wet nurse and whipped her to death. Lemin cried and refused to nurse from anyone else, and after a few days, he died. De Guo thus remained childless for the rest of her life. [105]

    晋司隶校尉高平满奋字武秋，丰肥肉溃肤裂，每至暑夏，辄膏汗流溢。其有爱妾夜取以燃照，炎灼发于屋表，奋大恶之，悉盛而埋之。暨永嘉之乱，奋为所烧，皎若烛光。
    　　晋滕放，太元初，夏枕文石枕卧，忽暴雨雷震其枕，枕四解傍人，莫不怖惧，而放独自若，云微觉有声，不足为惊。
    　　浔阳周字孟威，晋宁康中镇于巴西，为苻坚所获，守节不屈。坚使使者迫清道，躬治逵陌，谓使者曰：“烦君与语氐贼苻坚，何至仰烦国士如此。”又潜图袭坚，坚闻之曰：“貉子正欲觅死，杀之适足成其名耳。”乃若加拷楚，不食而卒。敛已经旬，坚怒犹未歇，剖棺临视，尸回眸断，齿鬓髭张，列睛瞳明亮，回盼瞩坚，坚睹而喜称，乃厚加赠赙。 [106]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [106]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 晋司隶校尉 - A title referring to a high-ranking official in the Jin dynasty, specifically the Inspector of the Capital Region.
    • 丰肥肉溃肤裂 - A vivid description of being extremely overweight, with the skin splitting due to the weight.
    • 辄膏汗流溢 - A phrase describing the excessive sweating that occurs frequently, with '膏汗' referring to thick, oily sweat.
    • 夜取以燃照 - A phrase indicating that the person's body fat was used as fuel for lighting at night.
    • 炎灼发于屋表 - A phrase describing the intense heat that emanated from the burning body fat, causing the house to smoke.
    • 悉盛而埋之 - A phrase indicating that the body fat was collected and buried.
    • 永嘉之乱 - Refers to the Yongjia Rebellion, a period of political instability and chaos in the Jin dynasty.
    • 太元初 - Refers to the early years of the Taiyuan era, a period during the Eastern Jin dynasty.
    • 文石枕 - A type of pillow made from carved stone, often used for its aesthetic or symbolic value.
    • 炎雷震其枕 - A phrase describing the pillow being struck by lightning, causing it to break apart.
    • 躬治逵陌 - A phrase indicating that the person personally cleaned the streets and paths.
    • 烦君与语氐贼苻坚 - A phrase where the person asks the messenger to convey a message to the enemy leader, 'Fu Jian,' using a derogatory term '氐贼' (Di rebel).
    • 何至仰烦国士如此 - A phrase expressing frustration at being treated as a national hero for such trivial tasks.
    • 潜图袭坚 - A phrase indicating a plan to ambush or attack Fu Jian.
    • 剖棺临视 - A phrase describing the act of opening the coffin to view the body.
    • 尸回眸断 - A phrase describing the corpse's eyes opening and staring, with '回眸断' suggesting a sudden, intense gaze.
    • 齿鬓髭张 - A phrase describing the corpse's teeth, eyebrows, and beard standing out prominently.
    • 列睛瞳明亮 - A phrase describing the eyes being wide open and bright.
    • 回盼瞩坚 - A phrase describing the corpse's eyes turning to look at Fu Jian.
    • 厚加赠赙 - A phrase indicating that Fu Jian provided generous gifts and compensation for the funeral.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 晋 - Refers to the Jin dynasty, a significant period in Chinese history.
    • 永嘉之乱 - A period of political turmoil and rebellion during the Jin dynasty.
    • 太元 - A reign title of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin, indicating the early years of his rule.
    • 浔阳 - A place name, likely referring to a region in modern-day Jiangxi province.
    • 周 - A proper name, likely referring to a historical figure.
    • 晋宁康中 - Refers to the Ningkang period of the Jin dynasty.
    • 巴西 - A place name, likely referring to a region in modern-day Sichuan province.
    • 苻坚 - A historical figure, the emperor of the Former Qin dynasty.
    • 氐贼 - A derogatory term used to refer to the Di people, a minority group that played a significant role in the Former Qin dynasty.

    Proper Nouns

    • 晋 - The Jin dynasty.
    • 司隶校尉 - A high-ranking official position.
    • 高平满奋 - A historical figure.
    • 字武秋 - The courtesy name of 高平满奋.
    • 永嘉之乱 - The Yongjia Rebellion.
    • 太元 - A reign title of Emperor Xiaowu of Jin.
    • 文石枕 - A type of pillow.
    • 浔阳 - A place name.
    • 周 - A historical figure.
    • 晋宁康中 - A period during the Jin dynasty.
    • 巴西 - A place name.
    • 苻坚 - A historical figure.
    • 氐贼 - A derogatory term for the Di people.

    The Inspector of the Capital Region of the Jin dynasty, Gao Ping Man Fen, courtesy name Wuqiu, was extremely overweight to the point that his skin split. Whenever it was hot in summer, he would sweat profusely. He had a beloved concubine who, at night, used his body fat as fuel for lighting. The intense heat from the burning fat caused smoke to fill the room, which Man Fen greatly disliked. He collected and buried the body fat. During the Yongjia Rebellion, Man Fen was burned, and his body glowed brightly like a candle.

    In the early years of the Taiyuan era, Jin Ten Fang slept on a carved stone pillow in the summer. Suddenly, a thunderstorm struck the pillow, causing it to break apart into four pieces. The people around him were terrified, but Fang remained calm, saying he only felt a slight sound and was not alarmed.

    In the Ningkang period of the Jin dynasty, Zhou Mengwei of Xunyang was stationed in Bashu. He was captured by Fu Jian of the Former Qin dynasty but remained steadfast and did not yield. Fu Jian sent an envoy to force Zhou to clear the roads, but Zhou personally cleaned the streets and paths. He told the envoy, "Please convey a message to the Di rebel Fu Jian: why should a national hero be involved in such trivial tasks?" Zhou also secretly planned to ambush Fu Jian. Upon hearing this, Fu Jian said, "The fox is just looking for death; killing him will only complete his reputation." He then subjected Zhou to torture, and Zhou died without eating. After a week, Fu Jian's anger had not subsided. He opened Zhou's coffin to view the body, which had its eyes open and staring, with teeth, eyebrows, and beard standing out prominently. The eyes were wide and bright, turning to look at Fu Jian. Fu Jian saw this and was pleased, so he provided generous gifts and compensation for the funeral. [106]

    汝南殷陶市同县张南宅，掘地得钱百万金千斤，即以还南。南曰：“君至德感神，宝为君出。”终不肯受。陶送付县。
    　　河内荀儒字君林，乘冰省舅氏，陷河而死。兄伦字君文，求尸积日不得，设祭冰侧，又笺与河伯投笺。一宿岸冰开，尸手执笺浮出，伦又笺谢。
    　　蜀郡张贞行船覆溺死，贞妇黄因投江就之。积十四日，执夫手俱浮出。
    　　顺阳南乡杨丰与息名香于田获粟，因为虎所噬。香年十四，手无寸刃，直扌益虎颈，丰遂得免。香以诚孝至感，猛兽为之逡巡。太守平昌孟肇之赐贷之谷，旌其门闾焉。
    　　崔景贤为平昌郡守，有惠民政，尝悬一蒲鞭而未尝用。
    　　任城王六月沉饮，忽失所在，人以为中酒毒而化。 [107]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [107]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 汝南殷陶市同县张南宅 - Literally means 'Yin Tao from Runan bought a house in the same county as Zhang Nan's residence.' Introduces the main characters and their relationship.
    • 掘地得钱百万金千斤 - Means 'dug up and found a million dollars in gold and a thousand pounds of silver.' Describes a significant discovery.
    • 即以还南 - Means 'immediately returned it to Zhang Nan.' Indicates the immediate action taken by Yin Tao.
    • 君至德感神，宝为君出 - Means 'Your great virtue has moved the spirits, and the treasure has appeared for you.' Poetically praises Yin Tao's moral character.
    • 送付县 - Means 'sent it to the county office.' Indicates the formal handover of the treasure.
    • 河内荀儒字君林 - Means 'Xun Ru from Henei, courtesy name Junlin.' Introduces another character with his place of origin and courtesy name.
    • 乘冰省舅氏 - Means 'rode on the ice to visit his uncle.' Describes a specific action taken by Xun Ru.
    • 陷河而死 - Means 'fell into the river and died.' Indicates the tragic outcome of Xun Ru's journey.
    • 兄伦字君文 - Means 'his brother Lun, courtesy name Junwen.' Introduces Xun Ru's brother.
    • 求尸积日不得 - Means 'sought his body for many days but could not find it.' Indicates the prolonged search for Xun Ru's body.
    • 设祭冰侧 - Means 'set up a sacrifice by the side of the ice.' Describes a ritual performed by Xun Ru's brother.
    • 又笺与河伯投笺 - Means 'also wrote a letter to the River God and threw it in.' Indicates a further ritual action.
    • 一宿岸冰开，尸手执笺浮出 - Means 'after one night, the ice on the shore opened, and the body, holding the letter, floated out.' Describes the miraculous recovery of Xun Ru's body.
    • 伦又笺谢 - Means 'Lun also wrote a letter to thank the River God.' Indicates a final act of gratitude.
    • 蜀郡张贞行船覆溺死 - Means 'Zhang Zhen from Shu Prefecture drowned when his boat capsized.' Introduces another character and the circumstances of his death.
    • 贞妇黄因投江就之 - Means 'Zhang Zhen's wife Huang threw herself into the river to join him.' Describes the wife's act of devotion.
    • 积十四日，执夫手俱浮出 - Means 'after fourteen days, she held her husband's hand and they both floated out.' Describes the miraculous reunion of the couple.
    • 顺阳南乡杨丰与息名香于田获粟 - Means 'Yang Feng from Nanxiang, Shunyang, and his son Xiang were harvesting grain in the fields.' Introduces another character and his son.
    • 因为虎所噬 - Means 'were attacked by a tiger.' Describes the attack.
    • 香年十四，手无寸刃，直扌益虎颈，丰遂得免 - Means 'Xiang, aged fourteen, without any weapon, struck the tiger's neck, and Yang Feng was saved.' Describes the son's heroic act.
    • 香以诚孝至感，猛兽为之逡巡 - Means 'Xiang's sincere filial piety moved even the fierce beasts, causing them to hesitate.' Emphasizes the power of Xiang's virtue.
    • 太守平昌孟肇之赐贷之谷，旌其门闾焉 - Means 'Governor Meng Zhao of Pingchang granted them grain as a loan and honored their gate.' Indicates the recognition and reward given to the family.
    • 崔景贤为平昌郡守，有惠民政，尝悬一蒲鞭而未尝用 - Means 'Cui Jingxian, as the governor of Pingchang Prefecture, had benevolent governance and once hung a willow whip but never used it.' Describes Cui Jingxian's wise and gentle leadership.
    • 任城王六月沉饮，忽失所在，人以为中酒毒而化 - Means 'Prince Rencheng drank and disappeared in June, and people believed he was transformed by alcohol poisoning.' Describes a mysterious disappearance.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 汝南 - Refers to Runan, a historical region in present-day Henan Province.
    • 河内 - Refers to Henei, a historical region in present-day Henan Province.
    • 蜀郡 - Refers to Shu Prefecture, a historical region in present-day Sichuan Province.
    • 顺阳南乡 - Refers to Nanxiang, Shunyang, a historical region in present-day Henan Province.
    • 平昌 - Refers to Pingchang, a historical region in present-day Shandong Province.
    • 任城王 - Refers to Prince Rencheng, a historical figure.
    • 河伯 - Refers to the River God, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 太守 - Refers to a governor or prefect, a high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 蒲鞭 - Refers to a willow whip, a symbol of authority and discipline.

    Proper Nouns

    • 汝南 - A historical region in present-day Henan Province.
    • 河内 - A historical region in present-day Henan Province.
    • 蜀郡 - A historical region in present-day Sichuan Province.
    • 顺阳南乡 - A historical region in present-day Henan Province.
    • 平昌 - A historical region in present-day Shandong Province.
    • 任城王 - A historical figure.
    • 河伯 - The River God, a deity in Chinese mythology.
    • 太守 - A high-ranking official in ancient China.
    • 蒲鞭 - A willow whip, a symbol of authority and discipline.

    In Runan, Yin Tao bought a house in the same county as Zhang Nan's residence. While digging, he discovered a treasure worth a million dollars in gold and a thousand pounds of silver. He immediately returned it to Zhang Nan. Zhang Nan said, "Your great virtue has moved the spirits, and the treasure has appeared for you." He refused to accept it. Yin Tao then sent the treasure to the county office.

    Xun Ru from Henei, courtesy name Junlin, rode on the ice to visit his uncle and fell into the river, dying. His brother Lun, courtesy name Junwen, sought his body for many days but could not find it. He set up a sacrifice by the side of the ice and wrote a letter to the River God, throwing it in. After one night, the ice on the shore opened, and the body, holding the letter, floated out. Lun also wrote a letter to thank the River God.

    Zhang Zhen from Shu Prefecture drowned when his boat capsized. His wife Huang threw herself into the river to join him. After fourteen days, she held her husband's hand, and they both floated out.

    Yang Feng from Nanxiang, Shunyang, and his son Xiang were harvesting grain in the fields when they were attacked by a tiger. Xiang, aged fourteen, without any weapon, struck the tiger's neck, and Yang Feng was saved. Xiang's sincere filial piety moved even the fierce beasts, causing them to hesitate. Governor Meng Zhao of Pingchang granted them grain as a loan and honored their gate.

    Cui Jingxian, as the governor of Pingchang Prefecture, had benevolent governance and once hung a willow whip but never used it.

    Prince Rencheng drank and disappeared in June, and people believed he was transformed by alcohol poisoning. [107]

    东莞刘邕性嗜食疮痂，以为味似鳆鱼。尝诣孟灵休，灵休先患炙，疮痂落在床，邕取食之。灵休大惊，痂未落者悉褫取饴邕。南康国吏二百许人不问有罪无罪，递与鞭，疮痂常以给膳。
    　　太原孙广头上不得有虱，大者便遭期丧、大功，小则小功、缌服。
    　　有人姓刘，在朱方人，不得共语。若与之言，必遭祸难，或本身死疾。惟一士谓无此理，偶值人有屯塞耳，刘闻之，忻然而往，自说被谤，君能见明。答云：“世人雷同，亦何足恤！”须臾火燎，资蓄服玩荡尽，于是举世号为刘鸺鹞。脱遇诸涂，皆闭车走马，掩目奔避。刘亦杜门自守，岁时一出，则人惊散，过于见鬼。
    　　晋陵曲阿扬<贝免>财数千万，三吴人多取其直，为商贾治生，辄得倍直。或行长江，卒遇暴风，及劫盗者。若投<贝免>钱，多获免济。<贝免>死后，先所埋金皆移去，邻人陈家陈尝晨起，见门外忽有百许万镪，封题是扬<贝免>姓字，然后知财物聚散，必由天运乎！ [108]

    Relevant Explanation

    Explanation

    [108]

    Idiomatic Expressions

    • 嗜食疮痂 - Literal phrase meaning 'enjoys eating scabs,' describing an unusual and repulsive habit.
    • 味似鳆鱼 - Means 'tastes like abalone,' providing a comparison to explain the strange taste.
    • 先患炙 - Refers to someone who has previously suffered from a burn or scald.
    • 褫取 - Means 'to take off' or 'to remove,' used here to describe the removal of scabs.
    • 递与鞭 - Means 'to beat in turn,' indicating a repetitive and indiscriminate punishment.
    • 大者便遭期丧、大功 - Refers to a severe form of mourning (期丧) or a major mourning period (大功) for larger lice.
    • 小则小功、缌服 - Refers to a minor mourning period (小功) or a light mourning period (缌服) for smaller lice.
    • 不得共语 - Means 'cannot speak to him,' indicating a social taboo.
    • 遭祸难 - Means 'to suffer misfortune or disaster.'
    • 本身死疾 - Means 'to die of illness' or 'to suffer from a serious disease.'
    • 脱遇诸涂 - Means 'if encountered on the road,' describing a hypothetical situation.
    • 闭车走马 - Means 'to close the carriage and ride away quickly,' indicating a reaction of fear or avoidance.
    • 掩目奔避 - Means 'to cover the eyes and run away,' further emphasizing the fear.
    • 杜门自守 - Means 'to shut oneself in and guard against others,' indicating isolation.
    • 岁时一出 - Means 'to come out once a year,' describing a rare occurrence.
    • 人惊散 - Means 'people scatter in fear,' indicating a strong reaction.
    • 过于见鬼 - Means 'more frightening than seeing a ghost,' emphasizing the extreme fear.
    • 数千万 - Means 'several million,' indicating a large sum of money.
    • 三吴人 - Refers to people from the Three Wu regions, a historical area in southern China.
    • 取其直 - Means 'to take his money,' indicating a transaction.
    • 商贾治生 - Means 'to engage in business and make a living,' describing economic activities.
    • 得倍直 - Means 'to get double the value,' indicating a profitable outcome.
    • 长江 - Refers to the Yangtze River, one of the longest rivers in Asia.
    • 暴风 - Means 'typhoon' or 'strong wind,' describing a natural disaster.
    • 劫盗者 - Means 'robbers' or 'thieves,' indicating criminal activity.
    • 投贝免钱 - Means 'to throw out the money of Yang Beimian,' referring to a specific person's money.
    • 埋金皆移去 - Means 'the buried gold all moved away,' indicating a mysterious phenomenon.
    • 邻人陈家 - Refers to a neighbor named Chen, indicating a specific individual.
    • 陈尝晨起 - Means 'Chen often got up in the morning,' describing a habitual action.
    • 忽有百许万镪 - Means 'suddenly had about a hundred million coins,' indicating a sudden appearance of wealth.
    • 封题是扬贝免姓字 - Means 'the seal was marked with Yang Beimian's name,' indicating ownership.
    • 财物聚散，必由天运 - Means 'the gathering and dispersing of wealth must be due to fate,' indicating a belief in destiny.

    Cultural and Historical Allusions

    • 东莞刘邕 - Refers to Liu Yong from Dongguan, a specific individual known for his unusual habit.
    • 孟灵休 - Refers to Meng Lingxiu, another specific individual who experienced the unusual behavior of Liu Yong.
    • 南康国吏 - Refers to officials from Nan'gang, a historical region in southern China.
    • 太原孙广 - Refers to Sun Guang from Taiyuan, a specific individual known for his aversion to lice.
    • 朱方人 - Refers to people from Zhufang, a specific location.
    • 刘鸺鹞 - Refers to Liu Xiu'ao, a derogatory nickname given to the person who could not be spoken to.
    • 晋陵曲阿 - Refers to Qu'ā in Jinling, a specific location.
    • 三吴人 - Refers to people from the Three Wu regions, a historical area in southern China.
    • 三吴 - Refers to the historical regions of Wu, Ou, and Hai, located in southern China.
    • 贝免 - Refers to Yang Beimian, a specific individual known for his wealth and mysterious occurrences.

    Proper Nouns

    • 东莞 - A historical region in southern China.
    • 刘邕 - A specific individual known for his unusual habit.
    • 孟灵休 - A specific individual who experienced the unusual behavior of Liu Yong.
    • 南康国 - A historical region in southern China.
    • 太原 - A historical region in northern China.
    • 孙广 - A specific individual known for his aversion to lice.
    • 朱方 - A specific location.
    • 刘鸺鹞 - A derogatory nickname given to a specific individual.
    • 晋陵 - A historical region in southern China.
    • 曲阿 - A specific location.
    • 三吴 - Historical regions in southern China.
    • 贝免 - A specific individual known for his wealth and mysterious occurrences.
    • 陈家 - A specific family or individual.

    A Man from Dongguan Named Liu Yong Had a Strange Appetite for Eating Scabs, Believing They Tasted Like Abalone. Once, He Visited Meng Lingxiu, Who Had Previously Suffered from Burns, and Found Scabs Falling on His Bed. Liu Yong Took and Ate Them. Meng Lingxiu Was Shocked and Removed All Remaining Scabs to Give to Liu Yong. Officials from the Nan'gang Kingdom, Around Two Hundred in Number, Were Punished Indiscriminately with Floggings, and Their Scabs Were Often Used as Food.

    In Taiyuan, Sun Guang Was Averse to Lice on His Head. Larger Lice Would Prompt Him to Observe the Severe Mourning Periods of qishang or dagong, while Smaller Lice Would Prompt Him to Observe the Minor Mourning Periods of xiaogong or sifu.

    There Was a Man Named Liu from Zhufang Who Could Not Be Spoken To. If One Spoke to Him, Misfortune or Disaster Would Follow, or He Might Die of Illness. Only One Scholar Believed This Was Not True, Saying It Was Just a Coincidence of Hardship. Upon Hearing This, Liu Went to the Scholar with Joy, Claiming He Had Been Slandered and Asking for the Scholar's Clarification. The Scholar Replied, "People Are All the Same, Why Worry About It?" Soon After, a Fire Destroyed His Wealth, and He Was Left with Nothing. Thus, the World Came to Call Him Liu Xiu'ao. If Encountered on the Road, People Would Close Their Carriages and Ride Away Quickly, Covering Their Eyes and Fleeing. Liu Also Shut Himself In and Guarded Against Others, Coming Out Only Once a Year, When People Would Scatter in Fear, More Frightening Than Seeing a Ghost.

    Yang Beimian from Qu'ā in Jinling Was Wealthy, With Several Million in Assets. People from the Three Wu Regions Often Took His Money to Engage in Business and Make a Living, Often Doubling Their Profits. Sometimes While Traveling the Yangtze River, They Would Sudden [108]

    东莞刘邕性嗜食疮痂，以为味似鳆鱼。尝诣孟灵休，灵休先患炙，疮痂落在床，邕取食之。灵休大惊，痂未落者悉褫取饴邕。南康国吏二百许人不问有罪无罪，递与鞭，疮痂常以给膳。
    　　太原孙广头上不得有虱，大者便遭期丧、大功，小则小功、缌服。
    　　有人姓刘，在朱方人，不得共语。若与之言，必遭祸难，或本身死疾。惟一士谓无此理，偶值人有屯塞耳，刘闻之，忻然而往，自说被谤，君能见明。答云：“世人雷同，亦何足恤！”须臾火燎，资蓄服玩荡尽，于是举世号为刘鸺鹞。脱遇诸涂，皆闭车走马，掩目奔避。刘亦杜门自守，岁时一出，则人惊散，过于见鬼。
    　　晋陵曲阿扬<贝免>财数千万，三吴人多取其直，为商贾治生，辄得倍直。或行长江，卒遇暴风，及劫盗者。若投<贝免>钱，多获免济。<贝免>死后，先所埋金皆移去，邻人陈家陈尝晨起，见门外忽有百许万镪，封题是扬<贝免>姓字，然后知财物聚散，必由天运乎！

    A Man from Dongguan Named Liu Yong Had a Strange Appetite for Eating Scabs, Believing They Tasted Like Abalone. Once, He Visited Meng Lingxiu, Who Had Previously Suffered from Burns, and Found Scabs Falling on His Bed. Liu Yong Took and Ate Them. Meng Lingxiu Was Shocked and Removed All Remaining Scabs to Give to Liu Yong. Officials from the Nan'gang Kingdom, Around Two Hundred in Number, Were Punished Indiscriminately with Floggings, and Their Scabs Were Often Used as Food.

    In Taiyuan, Sun Guang Was Averse to Lice on His Head. Larger Lice Would Prompt Him to Observe the Severe Mourning Periods of qishang or dagong, while Smaller Lice Would Prompt Him to Observe the Minor Mourning Periods of xiaogong or sifu.

    There Was a Man Named Liu from Zhufang Who Could Not Be Spoken To. If One Spoke to Him, Misfortune or Disaster Would Follow, or He Might Die of Illness. Only One Scholar Believed This Was Not True, Saying It Was Just a Coincidence of Hardship. Upon Hearing This, Liu Went to the Scholar with Joy, Claiming He Had Been Slandered and Asking for the Scholar's Clarification. The Scholar Replied, "People Are All the Same, Why Worry About It?" Soon After, a Fire Destroyed His Wealth, and He Was Left with Nothing. Thus, the World Came to Call Him Liu Xiu'ao. If Encountered on the Road, People Would Close Their Carriages and Ride Away Quickly, Covering Their Eyes and Fleeing. Liu Also Shut Himself In and Guarded Against Others, Coming Out Only Once a Year, When People Would Scatter in Fear, More Frightening Than Seeing a Ghost.

    Yang Beimian from Qu'ā in Jinling Was Wealthy, With Several Million in Assets. People from the Three Wu Regions Often Took His Money to Engage in Business and Make a Living, Often Doubling Their Profits. Sometimes While Traveling the Yangtze River, They Would Sudden
